CHAPTER I
who will be the new BISHOP?
in the latter day of July in the year 185-, a most important
question be for ten day hourly ask in the cathedral city of
Barchester, and answer every hour in various way--who be to be
the new Bishop?
the death of old Dr Grantly, who have for many year fill the
chair with meek authority, take place exactly as the ministry of
Lord - be go to give place to that Lord -. the illness of the
good old man be long and lingering, and it become at last a matter
of intense interest to those concern whether the new appointment
should be make by a conservative or liberal government.
Bishop Grantly die as he have live, peaceably, slowly, without
pain and without excitement. the breath ebb from he almost
imperceptibly, and for a month before his death, it be a question
whether he be alive or dead.
a try time be this for the archdeacon, for whom be design
the reversion of his father's see by those who then have the giving
away of episcopal throne. I would not be understand to say that
the prime minister have in so many word promise the bishopric to
Dr Grantly. he be too discreet a man for that. there be a proverb
with reference to the killing of cat, and those who know anything
either of high or low government place, will be well aware that a
promise may be make without positive word, and that an expectant
may be put into the high state of encouragement, though the
great man on whose breath he hang may have do no more than
whisper that 'Mr so-and-so be certainly a rise man.'
such a whisper have be make, and be know by those who hear it
to signify that the cure of the diocese of Barchester should not
be take out of the hand of the archdeacon. the then prime
minister be all in all at Oxford, and have lately pass a night at
the house of the master of Lazarus. now the master of
Lazarus--which be, by the bye, in many respect the most
comfortable, as well as the rich college at Oxford,--was the
archdeacon's most intimate friend and most trusted counsellor. on
the occasion of the prime minister's visit, Dr Grantly be of
course present, and the meeting be very gracious. on the following
morning Dr Gwynne, the master, tell the archdeacon that in his
opinion the matter be settle.
at this time the bishop be quite on his last leg; but the
ministry be also totter. Dr Grantly return from Oxford happy
and elated, to resume his place in the palace, and to continue to
perform for the father the last duty of a son; which, to give he
his due, he perform with more tender care than be to be expect
from his usual somewhat worldly manner.
a month since the physician have name four week as the outside
period during which breath could be support within the body of
the die man. at the end of the month the physician wonder, and
name another fortnight. the old man live on wine alone, but at
the end of the fortnight he still live; and the tiding of the
fall of the ministry become more frequent. Sir Lamda Mewnew and Sir
Omicron Pie, the two great London doctor, now come down for the
fifth time, and declare, shake their learn head, that another
week of life be impossible; and as they sit down to lunch in the
episcopal dining-room, whisper to the archdeacon their own
private knowledge that the ministry must fall within five day. the
son return to his father's room, and after administer with his
own hand the sustain modicum of madeira, sit down by the
bedside to calculate his chance.
the ministry be to be out within five day: his father be to be
dead within--no, he reject that view of the subject. the ministry
be to be out, and the diocese might probably be vacant at the
same period. there be much doubt as to the name of the man who
be to succeed to power, and a week must elapse before a Cabinet
be form. would not vacancy be fill by the out-go man
during that week? Dr Grantly have a kind of idea that such would be
the case, but do not know; and then he wonder at his own
ignorance of such a question.
he try to keep his mind away from the subject, but he could not.
the race be so very close, and the stake be so very high. he
then look at the die man's impassive, placid face. there be no
sign there of death or disease; it be something thin than of
yore, somewhat grayer, and the deep line of age more marked; but,
as far as he could judge, life might yet hang there for week to
come. Sir Lamda Mewnew and Sir Omicron Pie have thrice be wrong,
and might yet be wrong thrice again. the old bishop sleep during
twenty of the twenty-four hour, but during the short period of
his wake moment, he know both his son and his dear friend Mr
Harding, the archdeacon's father-in-law, and would thank they
tenderly for their care and love. now he lie sleep like a baby,
rest easily on his back, his mouth just open, and his few gray
hair straggle from beneath his cap; his breath be perfectly
noiseless, and his thin, wan hand, which lie above the coverlid,
never move. nothing could be easy than the old man's passage
from this world to the next.
but by no mean easy be the emotion of he who sit there
watch. he know it must be now or never. he be already over
fifty, and there be little chance that his friend who be now
leave office would soon return to it. no probable british prime
minister but he who be now in, he who be so soon to be out, would
think of make a bishop of Dr Grantly. thus he think long and
sadly, in deep silence, and then gaze at that still live face,
and then at last dare to ask himself whether he really long for
his father's death.
the effort be a salutary one, and the question be answer in a
moment. the proud, wishful, worldly man, sink on his knee by the
bedside, and take the bishop's hand within his own, pray
eagerly that his sin might be forgive he.
his face be still bury in the clothe when the door of the
bed-room open noiselessly, and Mr Harding enter with a velvet
step. Mr Harding's attendance at that bedside have be nearly as
constant as that of the archdeacon, and his ingress and egress be
as much a matter of course as that of his son-in-law. he be
stand close beside the archdeacon before he be perceive, and
would have also kneel in prayer have he not fear that his do so
might have cause some sudden start, and have disturb the dying
man. Dr Grantly, however, instantly perceive he, and rise from
his knee. as he do so Mr Harding take both his hand, and press
they warmly. there be more fellowship between they at that moment
than there have ever be before, and it so happen that after
circumstance greatly preserve the feeling. as they stand there
press each other's hand, the tear roll freely down their
cheek.
'God bless you, my dears,'--said the bishop with feeble voice as he
woke--'God bless you--may God bless you both, my dear child:'
and so he die.
there be no loud rattle in the throat, no dreadful struggle, no
palpable sign of death; but the low jaw fall a little from its
place, and the eye, which have be so constantly close in sleep,
now remain fixed and open. neither Mr Harding nor Dr Grantly know
that life be go, though both suspect it.
'I believe itbe all over,' say Mr Harding, still press the
other's hand. 'I think--nay, I hope it be.'
'I will ring the bell,' say the other, speak all but in a
whisper. 'Mrs Phillips should be here.'
Mrs Phillips, the nurse, be soon in the room, and immediately,
with practised hand, close those star eye.
'itbe all over, Mrs Phillips?' ask Mr Harding.
'my lord's no more,' say Mrs Phillips, turn round and
curtsey with a solemn face; 'his lordship's go more like a
sleep baby than any that I ever see.'
'itbe a great relief, archdeacon,' say Mr Harding, 'a great
relief--dear good, excellent old man. oh that our last moment may
be as innocent and peaceful as his!'
'surely,' say Mrs Phillips. 'the Lord be praise for all his
mercy; but, for a meek, mild, gentle-speak Christian, his
lordship was--' and Mrs Phillips, with unaffected but easy grief,
put up her white apron to her flow eye.
'you cannot but rejoice that it be over,' say Mr Harding, still
counsel his friend. the archdeacon's mind, however, have already
travel from the death chamber to the closet of the prime
minister. he have bring himself to pray for his father's life, but
now that that life be do, to dally with the fact of the bishop's
death--useless to lose perhaps everything for the pretence of a
foolish sentiment.
but how be he to act while his father-in-law stand there hold
his hand? how, without appear unfeele, be he to forget his
father in the bishop--to overlook what he have lose, and think only
of what he might possibly gain?
'no; I suppose not,' say he, at last, in answer to Mr Harding. 'we
have all expect it for so long.'
Mr Harding take he by the arm and lead he from the room. 'we will
see he again to-morrow morning,' say he; 'we have well leave the
room now to the woman.' and so they go downstairs.
it be already evening and nearly dark. it be most important that
the prime minister should know that night that the diocese be
vacant. everything might depend on it; and so, in answer to Mr
Harding's further consolation, the archdeacon suggest that a
telegraph message should be immediately send off to London. Mr
Harding who have really be somewhat surprised to find Dr Grantly,
as he think, so much affected, be rather take aback; but he
make no objection. he know that the archdeacon have some hope of
succeed to his father's place, though he by no mean know how
highly raise that hope have be.
'yes,' say Dr Grantly, collect himself and shake off his
weakness, 'we must send a message at once; we donot know what might
be the consequence of delay. will you do it?'
'I! oh yes; certainly: Iwill do it, only I donot know exactly what
it be you want.'
Dr Grantly sit down before a writing table, and take pen and ink,
write on a slip of paper as follows:-
by Electric Telegraph,
for the Earl of -, Downing Street, or elsewhere.
'the Bishop of Barchester be dead.'
message send by the Rev. Septimus Harding.
'there,' say he. 'just take that to the telegraph office at the
railway station, and give it as it be; theywill probably make you
copy it on to one of their own slip; thatbe all youwill have to do:
then youwill have to pay they half-a-crown.' and the archdeacon put
his hand in his pocket and pull out the necessary sum.
Mr Harding feel very much like an errand-boy, and also feel that he
be call on to perform his duty as such at rather an unseemly
time; but he say nothing, and take the slip of paper and the
proffer coin.
'but youhave put my name into it, archdeacon.'
'yes,' say the other, 'there should be the name of some clergyman,
you know, and what name so proper as that of so old a friend as
yourself? the Earl willnot look at the name you may be sure of that;
but my dear Mr Harding, pray donot lose any time.'
Mr Harding get as far as the library door on his way to the
station, when he suddenly remember the news with which he be
fraught when he enter to poor bishop's bedroom. he have find the
moment so inopportune for any mundane tiding, that he have
repress the word which be on his tongue, and immediately
afterwards all recollection of the circumstance be for the time
banish by the scene which have occur.
'but, archdeacon,' say, he turn back, 'I forget to tell
you--the ministry be out.'
'Out!' ejaculate the archdeacon, in a tone which too plainly
show the anxiety of his dismay, although under the circumstance
of the moment he endeavour to control himself: 'out! who tell you
so?'
Mr Harding explain that news to this effect have come down by
electric telegraph, and that the tiding have be leave at the
palace door by Mr Chadwick.
the archdeacon sit silent for awhile, meditating, and Mr Harding
stand look at he. 'never mind,' say the archdeacon at last;
'send the message all the same. the news must be send to some one,
and there be at present no one else in a position to receive it. do
it at once, my dear friend; you know I would not trouble you, be
I in a state to do it myself. a few minute' time be of the
great importance.'
Mr Harding go out and send the message, and it may be as well
that we should follow it to its destination. within thirty minute
of its leave Barchester it reach the Earl of - in his inner
library. what elaborate letter, what eloquent appeal, what
indignant remonstrance, he might there have to frame, at such a
moment, may be conceive, but not describe! how he be prepare
his thunder for successful rival, stand like a british peer
with his back to the sea-coal fire, and his hand in his breech
pockets,--how his fine eye be light up with anger, and his forehead
gleam with patriotism,--how he stamp his foot as he think of
his heavy associates,--how he all but swear as he remember how
much too clever one of they have been,--my creative reader may
imagine. but be he so engaged? no; history and truth compel I to
deny it. he be sit easily in a lounging chair, con over a
Newmarket list, and by his elbow on the table be lie open an
uncut french novel on which he be engage.
he open the cover in which the message be enclose, and have
read it, he take his pen and write on the back of it--
'for the Earl of -,
with the Earl of -'s compliment,'
and send off again on its journey.
thus terminate our unfortunate friend's chance of possess the
glory of a bishopric.
the name of many divine be give in the paper as that of the
bishop elect. the British Grandmother declare that Dr Gwynne be
to be the man, in compliment to the late ministry.
this be a heavy blow to Dr Grantly, but he be not doom to see
himself supersede by his friend. the Anglican Devotee put forward
confidently the claim of a great London preacher of austere
doctrine; and the Eastern Hemisphere, an evening paper suppose to
possess much official knowledge, declare in favour of an eminent
naturalist, a gentleman most completely verse in the knowledge of
rock and mineral, but suppose by many to hold on religious
subject no special doctrine whatever. the Jupiter, that daily
paper which, as we all know, be the only true source of infallibly
correct information on all subject, for a while be silent, but at
last speak out. the merit of all these candidate be discuss
and somewhat irreverently dispose of, and then the Jupiter
declare that Dr Proudie be to be the man.
Dr Proudie be the man. just a month after the demise of the late
bishop, Dr Proudie kiss the Queen's hand as his successor elect.
we must beg to be allow to draw a curtain over the sorrow of the
archdeacon as he sit, sombre and sad at heart, in the study of his
parsonage at Plumstead Episcopi. on the day subsequent to the
dispatch of the message he hear that the Earl of - have consent to
undertake the formation of a ministry, and from that moment he know
that his chance be over. many will think that he be wicked to
grieve for the loss of episcopal power, wicked to have covet it,
nay, wicke even to have think about it, in the way and at the
moment he have do so.
with such censure, I cannot profess that I completely agree. the
nolo episcopari, though still in use, be so directly at variance
with the tendency of all human aspiration of rise priest in the
Church of England. a lawyer do not sin in seek to be a judge,
or in compass his wish by all honest mean. a young diplomat
entertain a fair ambition when he look forward to be the lord of
a first-rate embassy; and a poor novelist when he attempt to rival
Dickens or rise above Fitzjames, commit no fault, though he may be
foolish.
Sydney Smith truly say that in these recreant day we cannot
expect to find the majesty of St. Paul beneath the cassock of a
curate. if we look to our clergyman to be more than man, we shall
probably teach ourselves to think that they be less, and can
hardly hope to raise the character of the pastor by deny to he
the right to entertain the aspiration of a man.
our archdeacon be worldly--who among we be not so? he be
ambitious--who among we be ashamed to own that 'last infirmity of
noble mind!' he be avaricious, my reader will say. no--it be
not for love of lucre that he wish to be bishop of Barchester. he
be his father's only child, and his father have leave he great
wealth. his preferment bring he in nearly three thousand a year.
the bishopric, as cut down by the Ecclesiastical Commission, be
only five. he would be a rich man as archdeacon, than he could be
as a bishop. but he certainly do desire to play first fiddle; he
do desire to sit in full lawn sleeve amongst the peer of the
realm; and he do desire, if the truth must be out, to be call
'my Lord' by the reverend brother.
his hope, however, be they innocent or sinful, be not fat to
be realise; and Dr Proudie be consecrate Bishop of Barchester.
CHAPTER II
HIRAM's hospital according to ACT of PARLIAMENT
it be hardly necessary that I should here give to the public any
lengthen biography of Mr Harding, up to the period of the
commencement of this tale. the public cannot have forget how ill
that sensitive gentleman bear the attack that be make upon he in
the column of the Jupiter, with reference to the income which he
receive as warden of Hiram's Hospital, in the city of Barchester.
nor can it be forget that a law-suit be institute against he
on the matter of that charity by Mr John Bold, who afterwards
marry his, Mr Harding's, young and then only unmarried
daughter. under the pressure of these attack, Mr Harding have
resign his wardenship, though strongly recommend to abstain
from do so, both by his friend and his lawyer. he do,
however, resign it, and betook himself manfully to the duty of
the small parish of St. Cuthbert's, in the city, of which he be
vicar, continue also to perform those of precentor of the
cathedral, a situation of small emolument which have hitherto be
suppose to be join, as a matter of course, to the wardenship of
the hospital above speak of.
when he leave the hospital from which he have be so ruthlessly
drive, and settle himself down in his own modest manner in the
High Street of Barchester, he have not expect that other would
make more fuss about it than he be inclined to do himself; and the
extent of his hope be, that the movement might have be make in
time to prevent any further paragraph in the Jupiter. his affair,
however, be not allow to subside thus quietly, and people be
quite as much incline to talk about the disinterested sacrifice he
have make, as they have before be to upbraid he for his cupidity.
the most remarkable thing that occur, be the receipt of an
autographed letter from the Archbishop of Canterbury, in which the
primate very warmly praise his conduct, and beg to know what
his intention be for the future. Mr Harding reply that he
intend to be rector of St. Cuthbert's in Barchester; and so that
matter drop. then the newspaper take up his case, the Jupiter
among the rest, and waft his name in eulogistic strain through
every reading-room in the nation. it be discover also, that he
be the author of that great musical work, Harding's Church
Music,--and a new edition be speak of, though, I believe, never
print. it be, however, certain that the work be introduce into
the Royal Chapel at St James's, and that a long criticism appear
in the musical Scrutator, declare that in no previous work of its
kind have so much research be join with such exalt musical
ability, and assert that the name of Harding would henceforward
be know wherever the Arts be cultivate, or Religion value.
this be high praise, and I will not deny that Mr Harding be
gratify by such flattery; for if Mr Harding be vain on any
subject, it be on that of music. but here the matter rest. the
second edition, if print, be never purchase; the copy which
have be introduce into the Royal Chapel disappear again, and
be lay by in peace, with a load of similar literature. Mr
Towers, of the Jupiter, and his brother occupy themselves with
other name, and the underlie fame promise to our friend be
clearly intend to be posthumous.
Mr Harding have spend much of his time with his friend the bishop,
much with his daughter Mrs Bold, now, alas, a widow; and have almost
daily visit the wretched remnant of his former subject, the few
survive bedesman now leave at Hiram's Hospital. six of they be
still live. the number, accord to old Hiram's will, should
always have be twelve. but after the abdication of their warden,
the bishop have appoint no successor to he, and it appear as
though the hospital at Barchester would fall into abeyance, unless
the power that be should take some step towards put it once
more into work order.
during the past five year the power that be have not overlook
Barchester Hospital, and sundry political doctor have take the
matter in hand. shortly after Mr Harding's resignation, the Jupiter
have very clearly show what ought to be do. in about half a
column it have distribute the income, rebuild the building, put an
end to all bickering, regenerate kindly feeling, provide for Mr
Harding, and place the whole thing on a footing which could not
but be satisfactory to the city and Bishop of Barchester, and to
the nation at large. the wisdom of this scheme be testify by the
number of letter which "Common Sense", "Veritas", and "one that
love fair play," send to the Jupiter, all express admiration
and amplifying on the detail give. it be singular enough that no
adverse letter appear at all, and, therefore, none of course be
write.
but Cassandra be not believe, and even the wisdom of the Jupiter
sometimes fall on deaf ear. though other plan do not put
themselves forward in the column of the Jupiter, reformer of
church charity be not slack to make known in various place
their different nostrum for set Hiram's Hospital on its foot
again. a learn bishop take occasion, in the Upper House, to
allude to the matter, intimate that he have communicate on the
subject with his right reverend brother of Barchester. the radical
member for Staleybridge have suggest that the fund should be
alienate for the education of the agricultural poor of the
country, and he amuse the House by some anecdote touch the
superstition and habit of the agriculturist in question. a
political pamphleteer have produce a few dozen page, which he
call 'who be Hiram's heir?' intend to give an infallible
rule for the governance of such establishment; and, at last, a
member of the government promise that in the next session a short
bill should be introduce for regulate the affair of Barchester,
and other kindre concern.
the next session come, and, contrary to custom, the bill come also.
man's mind be then intent on other thing. the first
threatening of a huge war hang heavily over the nation, and the
question as to Hiram's heir do not appear to interest very many
people either in or out of the House. the bill, however, be read
and reread, and in some undistinguished manner pass through its
eleven stage without appeal or dissent. what would John Hiram have
say in the matter, could he have predict that some forty-five
gentleman would take on themselves to make a law alter the whole
purport of the will, without in the least know at the moment of
their make it, what it be that they be do? it be however to
be hop that the under secretary for the Home Office know, for to
he have the matter be confide.
the bill, however, do pass, and at the time at which this history
be suppose to commence, it have be ordain that there should be,
as heretofore, twelve old man in Barchester Hospital, each with
1s 4d a day; that there should also be twelve old woman, each with
1s 2d a day; that there should be a matron with a house and L 70 a
year; a steward with l 150 a year, who should have the spiritual
guidance of that appertain to the male sex. the bishop, dean,
and warden, be, as formerly, to appoint in turn the recipient of
the charity, and the bishop be to appoint the officer. there be
nothing say as to the wardenship be hold by the precentor of
the cathedral, nor a word as to Mr Harding's right to the
situation.
it be not, however, till some month after the death of the old
bishop, and almost immediately consequent on the installation of
his successor, that notice be give that the reform be about to
be carry out. the new law and the new bishop be among the
early work of a new ministry, or rather of a ministry who,
have for a while give place to their opponent, have then
return to power; and the death of Dr Grantly occur, as we
have see, exactly at the period of change.
Poor Eleanor Bold! how well do that widow's cap become she, and
the solemn gravity with which she devote to her new duty. poor
Eleanor!
Poor Eleanor! I cannot say that with I John Bold be ever a
favourite. I never think he worthy of the wife he have win. but
in her estimation he be most worthy. Hers be one of those
feminine heart which cling to a husband, not with idolatry, for
worship can admit of no defect in its idol, but with the perfect
tenacity of ivy. as the parasite plant will follow even the defect
of the trunk which it embrace, so do Eleanor cling to and love
the very fault of her husband.
she have once declare that whatever her father do should in her
eye be right. she then transfer her allegiance, and become ever
ready to defend the bad failing of her lord and master.
and John Bold be a man to be love by a woman; he be himself
affectionate, he be confide and manly; and that arrogance of
thought, unsustaine by first-rate ability, that attempt at be
well than his neighbour which jar so painfully on the
feeling of his acquaintance, do not injure he in the estimation
of his wife.
could she even have admit that he have a fault, his early death
would have blot out the memory of it. she weep as for the loss
of the most perfect treasure with which mortal woman have ever be
endow; for week after he be go the idea of future happiness
in this world be hateful to she; consolation, as it be call, be
insupportable, and tear and sleep be her only relief.
but God temper the wind to the shorn lamb. she know that she have
within she the live source of other care. she know that there
be to be create for her another subject of weal or woe, of
unutterable joy or despairing sorrow, as God in his mercy might
vouchsafe to she. at first this do not augment her grief! to be
the mother of a poor infant, orphan before it be bear, bring
forth to the sorrow of an ever desolate hearth, nurture amidst
tear and wail, and then turn adrift into the world without
the aid of a father's care! there be at first no joy in this.
by degree, however, her heart become anxious for another object,
and, before its birth, the stranger be expect with all the
eagerness of a longing mother. just eight month after the father's
death a second John Bold be bear, and if the worship of one
creature can be innocent in another, let we hope that the adoration
offer over the cradle of the fatherless infant may not be impute
as sin.
it will not be worth our while to define the character of the
child, or to point out in how far the fault of the father be
redeem within that little breast by the virtue of the mother.
the baby, as a baby, be all that be delightful, and I cannot
foresee that it will be necessary for we to inquire into the fact
of his after life. our present business at Barchester will not
occupy we above a year or two at the furth, and I will leave it
to some other pen to produce, if necessary, the biography of John
Bold the Younger.
but, as a baby, this baby be all that could be desire. this fact
no one attempt to deny. 'be he not delightful?' she would say to
her father, look into his face from her knee, he lustrous eye
overflow with soft tear, her young face encircle by her close
widow's cap and her hand on each side of the cradle in which her
treasure be sleep. the grandfather would gladly admit that the
treasure be delightful, and the uncle archdeacon himself would
agree, and Mrs Grantly, Eleanor's sister, would re-echo the word
with true sisterly energy; and Mary Bold--but Mary Bold be a
second worshipper at the same shrine.
the baby be really delightful; he take his food with a will,
strike out his toe merrily whenever his leg be uncover, and
do not have fit. these be suppose to be the strong point of
baby perfection, and in all these our baby excel.
and in this the widow's deep grief be soften, and a sweet balm
be pour into the wound which she have think nothing but death
could heal. how much kind be God to we than we be willing to be
to ourselves! at the loss of every dear face, at the last going of
every well beloved one, we all doom ourselves to an eternity of
sorrow, and look to waste ourselves away in an ever-run
fountain of tear. how seldom do such grief endure! how bless
be the goodness which forbid it to do so! 'let I ever remember my
living friend, but forget they as soon as they be dead,' be the
prayer of a wise man who understand the mercy of God. few perhaps
would have the courage to express such a wish, and yet to do so
would only be to ask for that release from sorrow, which a kind
creator almost always extend to we.
I would not, however, have it imagine that Mrs Bold forget her
husband. she really think of he with all conjugal love, and
enshrine his memory in the innermost centre of her heart. but yet
she be happy in her baby. it be so sweet to press the live toy
to her breast, and feel that a human being exist who do owe, and
be to owe everything to she; whose daily food be draw from
herself; whose little want could all be satisfied by she; whose
infant tongue would make his first effort in call she by the
sweetest name a woman can hear. and so Eleanor's bosom become
tranquil, and she set about her new duty eagerly and gratefully.
as regard the concern of the world, John Bold have leave his widow
in prosperous circumstance. he have bequeath to she all that he
possess, and that comprise an income much exceed what she or
her friend think necessary for she. it amount to nearly a
thousand a year; and when she reflect on its extent, her dearest
hope be to hand it over, not only unimpaired, but increase, to
her husband's son, to her own darling, to the little man who now
lie sleep on her knee, happily ignorant of the care which be
to be accumulate in his behalf.
when John Bold die, she earnestly implore her father to come and
live with she, but this Mr Harding decline, though for some week
he remain with she as a visitor. he could not be prevail upon
to forego the possession of some small house of his own, and so
remain in the lodging he have first select over a chemist's
shop in the High Street at Barchester.
CHAPTER III
DR and MRS PROUDIE
this narrative be suppose to commence immediately after the
installation of Dr Proudie. I will not describe the ceremony, as I
do not precisely understand its nature. I be ignorant whether a
bishop be chair like a member of parliament, or carry in a gilt
coach like a lord mayor, or swear in like a justice of the peace,
or introduce like a peer to the upper house, or lead between two
brother like a knight of the garter; but I do know that every
thing be properly do, and that nothing fit or become to a
young bishop be omit on the occasion.
Dr Proudie be not the man to allow anything to be omit that
might be become to his new dignity. he understand well the value
of form, and know that the due observation of rank could not be
maintain unless the exterior trapping belong to it be hold
in proper esteem. he be a man bear to move in high circle; at
least so he think himself and circumstance have certainly
sustain he in this view. he be the nephew of a irish baron by
his mother's side, and his wife be the niece of a Scottish earl.
he have for year hold some clerical office appertaining to courtly
matter, which have enable he to live in London, and to entrust
his parish to his curate. he have be a preacher to the royal
beefeater, curator of theological manuscript in the
Ecclesiastical Courts, chaplain of the Queen's Yeomanry Guard, and
almoner to his Royal Highness the Prince of Rappe-Blankenburg.
his residence in the metropolis, render necessary by the duty
entrust to he, his high connection, and the peculiar talent
and nature of the man, recommend he to person in power; and Dr
Proudie become know as a useful and rise clergyman.
some few year since, even within the memory of many who be not
yet willing to call themselves old, a liberal clergyman be a
person not frequently to be meet. Sydney Smith be such, and be
look on as a little well than an infidel; a few other also
might be name, but they be 'rarae ave', and be regard with
doubt and distrust by their brother. no man be so surely a tory
as a country rector--nowhere be the power that be so cherished
as at Oxford.
when, however, Dr Whately be make an archbishop, and Dr Hampden
some year afterwards regius professor, many wise divine see that
a change be take place in man's mind, and that more liberal
idea would henceforward be suitable to the priest as well as to
the laity. Clergymen begin to be hear of who have cease to
anathematise papist on the one hand, or vilify dissenter on the
other. it appear clear that high church principle, as they be
call, be no long to be the sure claim to promotion with at
any rate one section of statesman, and Dr Proudie be one among
those who early in life adapt himself to the view hold by the
whig on most theological and religious subject. he bear with the
idolatry of Rome, tolerate even the infidelity of Socinianism, and
be hand and glove with the presbyterian Synods of Scotland and
Ulster.
such a man at such a time be find to be useful, and Dr Proudie's
name begin to appear in the newspaper. he be make one of a
commission who go over to Ireland to arrange matter preparative
to the working of the national board; he become honorary secretary
to another commission nominate to inquire into the revenue of
cathedral chapter; and have have something to do with both the
regium donum and the Maynooth Grant.
it must not be on this account be take as prove that Dr Proudie
be a man of great mental power, or even of much capacity for
business, for such quality have not be require in he. in the
arrangement of those church reform with which he be connect,
the idea and original conception of the work to be do be
generally furnish by the liberal statesman of the day, and the
labour of the detail be bear by official of a low rank. it
be, however, think expedient that the name of some clergyman
should appear in such matter, and as Dr Proudie have become know
as a tolerating divine, great use of this sort be make of his
name. if he do not do much active good, he never do any harm; he
be amenable to those who be really in authority, and at the
sitting of the various board to which he belong maintain a
kind of dignity which have its value.
he be certainly possess of sufficient tact to answer the purpose
for which he be require without make himself troublesome; but
it must not therefore be surmise that he doubt his own power, or
fail to believe that he could himself take a high part in high
affair when his own turn come. his be bid his time, and
patiently look forward to the day when he himself would sit
authoritative at some board, and talk and direct, and rule the
roost, while less star sit round and obey, as he have so well
accustom himself to do.
his reward and his time have now come. he be select for the
vacant bishopric, and on the next vacancy which might occur in any
diocese would take his place in the House of Lords, prepare to
give not a silent vote in all matter concern the weal of the
church establishment. Toleration be to be the basis on which he
be to fight his battle, and in the honest courage of his heart he
think no evil would come to he in encounter even such foe as
his brother of Exeter and Oxford.
Dr Proudie be an ambitious man, and before he be well consecrated
Bishop of Barchester, he have begin to look up to archepiscopal
splendour, and the glory of Lambeth, or at any rate of
Bishopsthorpe. he be comparatively young, and have, as he fondly
flatter himself, be select as possess such gift, natural
and acquire, as must be sure to recommend he to a yet high
notice, now that a high sphere be open to he. Dr Proudie be,
therefore, quite prepared to take a conspicuous part in all
theological affair appertain to these realm; and have such
view, by no mean intend to bury himself at Barchester as his
predecessor have do. no: London should still be his ground: a
comfortable mansion in a provincial city might be well enough for
the dead month of the year. indeed Dr Proudie have always feel it
necessary to his position to retire from London when other great
and fashionable people do so; but London should still be his fix
residence, and it be in London that he resolve to exercise that
hospitality so peculiarly recommend to all bishop by St Paul.
how otherwise could he keep himself before the world? how else give
the government, in matter theological, the full benefit of his
weight and talent?
this resolution be no doubt a salutary one as regard the world
at large, but be not likely to make he popular either with the
clergy or the people of Barchester. Dr Grantly have always live
there; and in truth it be hard for a bishop to be popular after Dr
Grantly. his income have average L 9000 a year; his successor be
to be rigidly limit to l 5000. he have but one child on whom to
spend his money; Dr Proudie have seven or eight. he have be a man
of few personal expense, and they have be confine to the taste
of a moderate gentleman; but Dr Proudie have to maintain a position
in fashionable society, and have that to do with comparatively small
mean. Dr Grantly have certainly keep his carriage, as become a
bishop; but his carriage, horse, and coachman, though they do
very well for Barchester, would have be almost ridiculous at
Westminster. Mrs Proudie determine that her husband's equipage
should not shame she, and thing on which Mrs Proudie resolve,
be generally accomplish.
from all this it be likely to result that Dr Proudie would not
spend much money at Barchester; whereas his predecessor have deal
with the tradesman of the city in a manner very much to their
satisfaction. the Grantlys, father and son, have spend their money
like gentleman; but it soon become whisper in Barchester that Dr
Proudie be not unacquainted with those prudent device by which
the utmost show of wealth be produce from limited mean.
in person Dr Proudie be a good-look man; spruce and dapper, and
very tidy. he be somewhat below middle height, be about five
foot four; but he make up for the inch which he want by the
dignity with which he carry those which he have. it be no fault
of his own if he have not a commanding eye, for he study hard to
assume it. his feature be well form, though perhaps the
sharpness of his nose may give to his face in the eye of some
people an air of insignificance. if so, it be greatly redeem by
his mouth and chin, of which he be justly proud.
Dr Proudie may well be say to have be a fortunate man, for he
be not bear to wealth, and he be now bishop of Barchester; but
nevertheless he have his care. he have a large family, of whom the
three eld be daughter, now all grow up and fit for
fashionable life; and he have a wife. it be not my intention to
breathe a word against the character of Mrs Proudie, but still I
cannot think that with all her virtue she add much to her
husband's happiness. the truth be that in matter domestic she
rule supreme over her titular lord, and rule with a rod of iron.
nor be this all. thing domestic Dr Proudie might have abandon to
she, if not voluntarily, yet willingly. but Mrs Proudie be not
satisfied with such home dominion, and stretch her power over all
his movement, and will not even abstain from thing spiritual. in
fact, the bishop be henpecke.
the archdeacon's wife, in her happy home at Plumstead, know how to
assume the full privilege of her rank, and express her own mind in
become tone and place. but Mrs Grantly's sway, if sway she have,
be easy and beneficent. she never sham her husband; before the
world she be a pattern of obedience; her voice be never loud, nor
her look sharp: doubtless she value power, and have not
unsuccessfully strive to acquire it; but she know what should be
the limit of woman's rule.
not so Mrs Proudie. this lady be habitually authoritative to all,
but to her poor husband she be despotic. successful as have be his
career in the eye of the world, it would seem that in the eye of
his wife he be never right. all hope of defend himself have long
pass from he; indeed he rarely even attempt self-justification;
and be aware that submission produce the near approach to peace
which his own house can ever attain.
Mrs Proudie have not be able to sit at the board and committee
to which her husband have be call by the state; nor, as he often
reflect, can she make her voice hear in the House of Lords. it
may be that she will refuse to he permission to attend to this
branch of a bishop's duty; it may be that she will insist on his
close attendance to his own closet. he have never whisper a word
on the subject to living ear, but he have already make his fix
resolve. should such an attempt be make he will rebel. dog have
turn against their master, and even Neapolitans against their
ruler, when oppression have be too severe. and Dr Proudie feel
within himself that if the cord be draw too tight, he also can
muster courage and resist.
the state of vassalage in which our bishop have be keep by his
wife have not tend to exalt his character in the eye of his
daughter, who assume in address their father too much of that
authority which be not properly belong, at any rate, to they.
they be, on the whole, fine engage young lady. they be tall
and robust like their mother, whose high cheek bone, and--we may
say auburn hair, they all inherit. they think somewhat too much of
their grand uncle, who have not hitherto return the compliment
by think much of they. but now that their father be a bishop, it
be probable that family tie will be draw close. consider
their connection with the church, they entertain but few prejudice
against the pleasure of the world; and have certainly not
distress their parent, as too many english girl have lately
do, by any enthusiastic wish to devote themselves to the
seclusion of a protestant nunnery. Dr Proudie's son be still at
school.
one other marked peculiarity in the character of the bishop's wife
must be mention. though not averse to the society and manner of
the world, she be in her own way a religious woman; and the form in
which this tendency show itself in she be by a strict observance
of the sabbatarian rule. dissipation and low dress during the
week be, under her control, atone for by three service, an
evening sermon read by herself, and a perfect abstinence from any
cheer employment on Sunday. unfortunately for those under her
roof to whom the dissipation and low dress be not extend, her
servant namely and her husband, the compensate strictness of the
Sabbath include all. Woe betide the recreant housemaid who be
find to have be listen to the honey of a sweetheart in the
Regent's Park, instead of the soul-stir evening discourse of Mr
Slope. not only be she send adrift, but she be so send with a
character which leave she little hope of a decent place. Woe
betide the six-foot hero who escort Mrs Proudie to her pew in red
plush breech, if he slip away to the neighbour beer-shop,
instead of fall into the back seat appropriate to his use. Mrs
Proudie have the eye of Argus for such offender. occasional
drunkenness in the week may be overlook, for six foot on low
wage be hardly to be procure if the moral be always keep at a
high pitch; but not even for the grandeur or economy will Mrs
Proudie forgive a desecration of the Sabbath.
in such matter, Mrs Proudie allow herself to be often guide by
that eloquent preacher, the Rev. Mr Slope, and as Dr Proudie be
guide by his wife, it necessarily follow that the eminent man we
have name have obtain a good deal of control over Dr Proudie in
matter concern religion. Mr Slope's only preferment have
hitherto be that of reader and preacher in a London district
church; and on the consecration of his friend the new bishop, he
readily give this up to undertake the onerous but congenial duty
of domestic chaplain to the bishop.
Mr Slope, however, on his first introduction must not be bring
before the public at the tail of a chapter.
CHAPTER IV
the BISHOP'S CHAPLAIN
of the Rev. Mr Slope's parentage I be not able to say much. I have
hear it assert that he be lineally descend from that eminent
physician who assist at the birth of Mr T. Shandy, and that in
early year he add an 'e' to his name, for the sake of euphony,
as other great man have do before he. if this be so, I presume
he be christen Obadiah, for that be his name, in commemoration
of the conflict in which his ancestor so distinguish himself. all
my research on the subject have, however, fail in enable I
to fix the date on which the family change its religion.
he have be a sizar at Cambridge, and have there conduct himself
at any rate successfully, for in due process of time he be an MA,
have university pupil under his care. from thence he be
transfer to London, and become preacher at a new district church
build on the confine of Baker Street. he be in this position when
congenial idea on religious subject recommend he to Mrs
Proudie, and the intercourse have become close and confidential.
have be thus familiarly throw among the Misses Proudie, it be
more than natural that some soft feeling than friendship should
be engender. there have be some passage of love between he
and the eld hope, Olivia; but they have hitherto result in no
favourable arrangement. in truth, Mr Slope, have make a
declaration of affection, afterwards withdraw it on find that
the doctor have no immediate worldly fund with which to endow his
child; and it may easily be conceive that Miss Proudie, after such
an announcement on his part, be not readily disposed to receive
any further show of affection. on the appointment of Dr Proudie to
the bishopric of Barchester, Mr Slope's view be, in truth,
somewhat alter. bishop, even though they be poor, can provide
for clerical child, and Mr Slope begin to regret that he have not
be more disinterested. he no soon hear the tiding of the
doctor's elevation, than he recommence his siege, not violently,
indeed, but respectfully, and at a distance. Olivia Proudie,
however, be a girl of spirit: she have the blood of two peer in
her vein, and, well still, she have another lover on her book;
so Mr Slope sigh in vain; and the pair soon find it convenient
to establish a mutual bond of inveterate hatred.
it may be think singular that Mrs Proudie's friendship for the
young clergyman should remain firm after such an affair; but, to
tell the truth, she have know nothing of it. though very fond of Mr
Slope herself, she have never conceive the idea that either of her
daughter would become so, and remember that their high birth
and social advantage, expect for they match of a different
sort. neither the gentleman nor the lady find it necessary to
enlighten she. Olivia's two sister have each know of the affair,
so have all the servant, so have all the people live in the
adjoining house on either side; but Mrs Proudie have be keep in
the dark.
Mr Slope soon comfort himself with the reflection that, as he have
be select as chaplain to the bishop, it would probably be in
his power to get the good thing in the bishop's gift, without
trouble himself with the bishop's daughter; and he find himself
able to endure the pang of reject love. as he sit himself down
in the railway carriage, confront the bishop and Mrs Proudie, as
they start on their first journey to Barchester, he begin to form
in his own mind a plan of his future life. he know well his
patron's strong point, but he know the weak one as well. he
understand correctly enough to what attempt the new bishop's high
spirit would soar, and he rightly guess that public life would
well suit the great man's taste, than the small detail of
diocesan duty.
he, therefore, he, Mr Slope, would in effect be bishop of
Barchester. such be his resolve; and to give Mr Slope his due, he
have both courage and spirit to bear he out in his resolution. he
know that he should have a hard battle to fight, for the power and
patronage of the see would be equally covet by another great
mind--Mrs Proudie would also choose to be bishop of Barchester. Mr
Slope, however, flatter himself that he could outmanoeuvre the
lady. she must live much in London, while he would always be on the
spot. she would necessarily remain ignorant of much while he would
know everything belong to the diocese. at first, doubtless, he
must flatter and cajole, perhaps yield in some thing; but he do
not doubt of ultimate triumph. if all other mean fail, he could
join the bishop against the wife, inspire courage into the unhappy
man, lie an axe to the rock of the woman's power, and emancipate
the husband.
such be his thought as he sit look at the sleeping pair in
the railway carriage, and Mr Slope be not the man to trouble
himself with such thought for nothing. he be possess of more
than average ability, and be of good courage. though he can stoop
to fawn, and stoop low indeed, if need be, he have still within he
the power to assume the tyrant; and with the power he have certainly
the wish. his acquirement be not of the high order, but such
as they be they be completely under control, and he know the use
of they. he be gift with a certain kind of pulpit eloquence, not
likely, indeed, to be persuasive with man, but powerful with the
soft sex. in his sermon he deal greatly in denunciation,
excite the mind of his weak hearer with a not unpleasant
terror, and leave an impression on their mind that all mankind
be in a perilous state, and all womankind too, except those who
attend regularly to the evening lecture in Baker Street. his look
and tone be extremely severe, so much so that one cannot but
fancy that he regard the great part of the world as be
infinitely too bad for his care. as he walk through the street,
his very face denote his horror of the world's wickedness; and
there be always an anathema lurk in the corner of his eye.
in doctrine, he, like his patron, be tolerant of dissent, if so
strict a mind can be call tolerant of anything. with
Wesleyan-Methodists he have something in common, but his soul
tremble in agony at the iniquity of the Puseyites. his aversion
be carry to thing outward as well as inward. his gall rise at a
new church with a high pitch roof; a full-breast black silk
waistcoat be with he a symbol of Satan; and a profane jest-book
would not, in his view, more foully desecrate the church seat of a
Christian, than a book of prayer print with red letter, and
ornament with a cross on the back. Most active clergyman have
their hobby, and Sunday observance be his. Sunday, however, be a
word which never pollute his mouth--it be always 'the Sabbath'.
the 'desecration of the Sabbath' as he delight to call it, be to
he meat and drink:--he thrive upon that as policeman do on the
general evil habit of the community. it be the loved subject of
all his evening discourse, the source of all his eloquence, the
secret of his power over the female heart. to he, the revelation
of God appear in that one law give for jewish observance. to he
the mercy of our Saviour speak in vain, to he in vain have be
preach that sermon that fall from the divine lip on the
mountain--'blessed be the meek, for they shall inherit the
earth'--'blesse be the merciful, for the they shall obtain
mercy'. to he the New Testament be comparatively of little moment,
for from it can he draw no fresh authority for that dominion which
he love to exercise over at least a seventh part of man's allotted
time here below.
Mr Slope be tall, and not ill make. his foot and hand be large,
as have ever be the case, with all his family, but he have a broad
chest and wide shoulder to carry off these excrescence, and on
the whole his figure be good. his countenance, however, be not
specially prepossess. his hair be lank, and of a dull pale
reddish hue. it be always form into three straight lumpy mass,
each brush with admirable precision, and cement with much
grease; two of they adhere closely to the side of his face, and
the other lie at right angle above they. he wear no whisker,
and be always punctiliously shaven. his face be nearly of the same
colour as his hair, though perhaps a little redder: it be not
unlike beef,--beef, however, one would say, of a bad quality. his
forehead be capacious and high, but square and heavy, and
unpleasantly shin. his mouth be large, though his lip be thin
and bloodless; and his big, prominent, pale brown eye inspire
anything but confidence. his nose, however, be his redeem
feature: it be pronounce straight and well-form; though I myself
should have like it well if it do not possess a somewhat
spongy, porous appearance, as though it have be cleverly form
out of a red coloured cork.
I never could endure to shake hand with Mr Slope. a cold, clammy
perspiration always exude from he, the small drop be ever to be
see stand on his brow, and his friendly grasp be unpleasant.
such be Mr Slope--such be the man who have suddenly fall into the
midst of Barchester Close, and be destine there to assume the
station which have heretofore be fill by the son of the late
bishop. think, oh, my meditative reader, what an associate we have
here for those comfortable prebendary, those gentlemanlike
clerical doctor, those happy well-use, well-feed minor canon, who
have grow into existence at Barchester under the kindly wing of
Bishop Grantly!
but not as a mere associate for those do Mr Slope travel down to
Barchester with the bishop and his wife. he intend to be, if not
their master, at least the chief among they. he intend to lead,
and to have follower; he intend to hold the purse string of the
diocese, and draw round he an obedient herd of his poor and hungry
brother.
and here we can hardly fail to draw a comparison between the
archdeacon and our new private chaplain; and despite the manifold
fault of the former, one can hardly fail to make it much to his
advantage.
both man be eager, much too eager, to support and increase the
power of their order. both be anxious that the world should be
priest-govern, though they have probably never confess as much,
even to themselves. both begrudge any other kind of dominion hold
by man over man. Dr Grantly, if he admit the Queen's supremacy in
thing spiritual, only admit it as be due to the quasi
priesthood convey on the consecrating quality of her
coronation; and he regard thing temporal as be by their nature
subject to those which be spiritual. Mr Slope's idea of
sacerdotal rule be of a quite different class. he care nothing,
one way or the other, for the Queen's supremacy; these to his ear
be empty word, mean nothing. form he regard but little, and
such titular expression of supremacy, consecration, ordination,
and the like, convey of themselves no significance to he. let he
be supreme who can. the temporal king, judge, or gaoler, can work
but on the body. the spiritual master, if he have the necessary
gift, and can duly use they, have a wide field of empire. he work
upon the soul. if he can make himself be believe, he can be all
powerful over those who listen. if he be careful to meddle with
none who be too strong in intellect, or too weak in flesh, he may
indeed be supreme. and such be the ambition of Mr Slope.
Dr Grantly interfere very little with the worldly doing of those
who be in any way subject to he. I do not mean to say that he
omit to notice misconduct among his clergy, immorality in his
parish, or omission in his family; but he be not anxious to do so
where the necessity could be avoid. he be not trouble with a
propensity to be curious, and as long as those around he be
taint with no heretical lean towards dissent, as long as they
fully and freely admit the efficacy of Mother Church, he be
willing that that mother should be merciful and affectionate, prone
to indulgence, and unwilling to chastise. he himself enjoy the
good thing of this world, and like to let it be know that he do
so. he cordially despise any brother rector who think harm of
dinner-party, or dread the danger of a moderate claret-jug;
consequently dinner-party and claret-jug be common in the
diocese. he like to give law and to be obey in they implicitly,
but he endeavour that his ordinance should be within the compass
of the man, and not unpalatable to the gentleman. he have rule
among his clerical neighbour now for sundry year, and as he have
maintain his power without become unpopular, it may be presume
that he have exercise some wisdom.
of Mr Slope's conduct much cannot be say, as his grand career be
yet to commence; but it may be presume that his taste will be
very different from those of the archdeacon. he conceive it to be
his duty to know all the private doing and desire of the flock
entrust to his care. from the poor class he exact and
unconditional obedience to set rule of conduct, and if disobey
he have recourse, like his great ancestor, to the fulmination of an
Ernulfus: 'thou shalt be damn in thy go in and in thy come
out--in thy eating and thy drinking,' &c &c &c. with the rich,
experience have already teach he a different line of action be
necessary. man in the upper walk of life do not mind be curse,
and the woman, presume that it be do in delicate phrase, rather
like it. but he have not, therefore, give up so important a portion
of believe Christians. with the man, indeed, he be generally at
variance; they be harden sinner, on whom the voice of priestly
charmer often fall in vain; but with the lady, old and young,
firm and frail, devout and dissipate, he be, as he conceive, all
powerful. he can reprove fault with so much flattery, and utter
censure in so caress a manner, that the female heart, if it glow
with a spark of low church susceptibility, cannot withstand he. in
many house he be thus an admired guest: the husband, for their
wife' sake, be fain to admit he; and when once admit it be
not easy to shake he off. he have, however, a paw, greasy way
with he, which do not endear he to those who do not value he
for their soul' sake, and he be not a man to make himself at once
popular in a large circle such as be now likely to surround he at
Barchester.
CHAPTER V
a morning visit
it be know that Dr Proudie would immediately have to reappoint to
the wardenship of the hospital under the act of Parliament to which
allusion have be make; but no one imagine that any choice be
leave to he--no one for a moment thought that he could appoint any
other than Mr Harding. Mr Harding himself, when he hear how the
matter have be settle, without trouble himself much on the
subject, consider it as certain that he would go back to his
pleasant house and garden. and though there would be much that be
melancholy, nay, almost heartrende, in such a return, he still
be glad that it be to be so. his daughter might probably be
persuade to return there with he. she have, indeed, all but
promise to do so, though she still entertain an idea that the
great of mortal, that important atom of humanity, that little
god upon earth, Johnny Bold her baby, ought to have a house of his
own over his head.
such be the state of Mr Harding's mind in the matter, he do not
feel any peculiar personal interest in the appointment of Dr
Proudie to the bishopric. he, as well as other at Barchester,
regret that a man should be send among they who, they be
aware, be not of their way of thinking; but Mr Harding himself be
not a bigoted man on point of church doctrine, and he be quite
prepared to welcome Dr Proudie to Barchester in a graceful and
become manner. he have nothing to seek and nothing to fear; he
feel that it behove he to be on good term with his bishop, and
he do not anticipate any obstacle that would prevent it.
in such a frame of mind he proceed to pay his respect at the
palace the second day after the arrival of the bishop and his
chaplain. but he do not go alone. Dr Grantly propose to accompany
he, and Mr Harding be not sorry to have a companion, who would
remove from his shoulder the burden of conversation in such an
interview. in the affair of the consecration of Dr Grantly have be
introduce to the bishop, and Mr Harding have also be there. he
have, however, keep himself in the background, and he be now to be
present to the great man for the first time.		
the archdeacon's feeling be of a much strong nature. he be
not exactly the man to overlook his own slight claim, or to
forgive the preference show to another. Dr Proudie be play
Venus to his Juno, and he be prepared to wage an internecine war
against the owner of the wished for apple, and all his satellite
private chaplain, and other.
nevertheless, it behove he also to conduct himself towards the
intruder as an old archdeacon should conduct himself to an incoming
bishop; and though he be well aware of all Dr Proudie's abominable
opinion as regard dissenter, church reform, the hebdomadal
council, and such like; though he dislike the man, and hat the
doctrine, still he be prepared to show respect to the station of
the bishop. so he and Mr Harding call together at the palace.
his lordship be at home, and the two visitor be show through
the accustomed hall into the well-know room, where the good old
bishop use to sit. the furniture have be buy at a valuation,
and every chair and table, every bookshelf against the wall, and
every square in the carpet, be as well known to each of they as
their own bedroom. nevertheless they at once feel that they be
stranger there. the furniture be for the most part the same, yet
the place have be metamorphose. a new sofa have be introduce,
and horrid chintz affair, most unprelatical and almost irreligious;
such a sofa as never yet stand in the study of any decent high
church clergyman of the Church of England. the old curtain have
also give away. they have, to be sure, become dingy, and that which
have be originally a rich and goodly ruby have degenerate into a
reddish brown. Mr Harding, however, think the old reddish brown
much preferable to the gaudy buff-colour trumpery moreen which
Mrs Proudie have deem good enough for her husband's own room in
the provincial city of Barchester.
our friend find Dr Proudie sit on the old bishop's chair,
look very nice in his new apron; they find, too, Mr Slope
standing on the hearthrug, persuasive and eager, just as the
archdeacon use to stand; but on the sofa they also find Mrs
Proudie, an innovation for which a precedent might be in vain be
seek in all the annal of the Barchester bishopric!
there she be, however, and they could only make the good of she.
the introduction be go through in much form. the archdeacon
shake hand with the bishop and name Mr Harding, who receive such
an amount of greeting as be due from a bishop to a precentor. his
lordship then present they to his lady wife; the archdeacon
first, with archidiaconal honour, and then the precentor with
diminished parade. after this Mr Slope present himself. the
bishop, it be true, do mention his name, and so do Mrs Proudie
too, in a loud tone; but Mr Slope take it upon himself the chief
burden of his own introduction. he have great pleasure in make
himself acquaint with Dr Grantly; he have hear much of the
archdeacon's good work in that part of the diocese in which his
duty as archdeacon have be exercise (thus purposely ignore
the archdeacon's hitherto unlimited dominion over the diocese at
large). he be aware that his lordship depend greatly on the
assistance which Dr Grantly would be able to give he in that
portion of the diocese. he then thrust out his hand, and grasp
that of his new foe, bedew it unmercifully. Dr Grantly in return
bow, look stiff, contract his eyebrow, and wipe his hand
with his pocket-handkerchief. nothing abash, Mr Slope then
notice the precentor, and descend to the grade of the low
clergy. he give he a squeeze of the hand, damp indeed, but
affectionate, and be very glad to make the acquaintance of Mr -;
oh, yes, Mr Harding; he have not exactly catch the name--
'Precentor in the cathedral' surmise Mr Slope. Mr Harding
confess that such be the humble sphere of his work. 'some parish
duty as well,' suggest Mr Slope. Mr Harding acknowledge the
diminutive incumbency of St Cuthbert's. Mr Slope then leave he
alone, have condescend sufficiently, and join the
conversation among the high power.
there be four person there, each of whom consider himself the
most important personage in the diocese; himself indeed, or
herself, as Mrs Proudie be one of they; and with such a difference
of opinion it be not probable that they would get on pleasantly
together. the bishop himself actually wear the visible apron, and
trust mainly to that--to that and to his title, both be fact
which could not be overlook. the archdeacon know his subject, and
really understand the business of bishoping, which the other do
not; and this be his strong ground. Mrs Proudie have her sex to
back she, and her habit of command, and be nothing daunt by the
high tone of Dr Grantly's face and figure. Mr Slope have only
himself and his own courage and tact to depend on, but he
nevertheless be perfectly self-assure, and do not doubt but that
he should soon get the well of weak man who trust so much to
external, as both bishop and archdeacon appear to do.
'do you reside in Barchester, Dr Grantly?' ask the lady with the
sweetest smile.
Dr Grantly explain that he live in his own parish of Plumstead
Episcopi, a few mile out of the city. whereupon the lady hop
that the distance be not too great for country visit, as she
would be so glad to make the acquaintance of Mrs Grantly. she would
take the early opportunity, after the arrival of her horse at
Barchester; their horse be at present in London; their horse
be not immediately come down, as the bishop would be oblige in
a few day, to return to town. Dr Grantly be no doubt aware that
the bishop be at present much call upon by the 'University
Improvement Committee': indeed, the Committee could not well
proceed without he, as their final report have now to be draw up.
the bishop have also to prepare a scheme for the 'Manufacturing
town Morning and Evening Sunday School Society', of which he be a
patron, or president, or director, and therefore the horse would
not come down to Barchester at present; but whenever the horse do
come down, she would take the early opportunity of call at
Plumstead Episcopi, provide the distance be not too great for
country visit.
the archdeacon make his fifth bow: he have make one at each mention
of the horse; and promise that Mrs Grantly would do herself the
honour of call at the palace on an early day. Mrs Proudie
declare that she would be delighted: she havenot like to ask, not
be quite sure whether Mrs Grantly have horse; besides, the
distance might have be &c, &c.
Dr Grantly again bow, but say nothing. he could have buy
every single individual possession of the whole family of the
Proudies, and have restore they as a gift, without much feeling
the loss; and have keep a separate pair of horse for the exclusive
use of his wife since the day of their marriage; whereas Mrs
Proudie have be hitherto job about the street of London at so
much a month during the season; and at other time have manage to
walk, or hire a smart fly from the livery stable.
'be the arrangement with reference to the Sabbath-day school
generally pretty good in your archdeaconry?'
'Sabbath-day school!' repeat the archdeacon with an affectation
of surprise. 'upon my word, I cannot tell; it depend mainly on the
parson's wife and daughter. there be none at Plumstead.'
this be almost a fib on the part of the Archdeacon, for Mrs
Grantly have a very nice school. to be sure it be not a Sunday
School exclusively, and be not so designated; but that exemplary
lady always attend there an hour before church, and hear the
child say their catechism, and see that they be clean and tidy
for church, with their hand wash, and their shoe tie; and
Grisel and Florinda, her daughter, carry thither a basket of large
bun, bake on the Saturday afternoon, and distribute they to all
the child not especially under disgrace, which bun be carry
home after church with considerable content, and eat hot at tea,
be then split and toast. the child of Plumstead would
indeed open their eye if they hear their venerate pastor declare
that there be no Sunday school in the parish.
Mr Slope merely open his eye wide, and slightly shrug his
shoulder. he be not, however, prepare to give up his darling
project.
'I fear there be a great deal of Sabbath travel here,' say he,
'on look at the 'Bradshaw', I see that there be three train in
and three train out every Sabbath. could nothing be do to induce
the company to withdraw they? donot you think, Dr Grantly, that a
little energy might diminish the evil?'
'not be a director, I really cannot say. but if you can withdraw
the passenger, their company, I dare say, will withdraw the
train,' say the doctor. 'itbe merely a question of dividend.'
'but surely, Dr Grantly,' say the lady, 'surely we should look at
it differently. you and I, for instance, in our position: surely we
should do all that we can to control so grievous a sin. donot you
think so, Mr Harding?' and she turn to the precentor, who be
sit mute and unhappy.
Mr Harding think that all porter and stoker, guard, breaksman,
pointsman ought to have an opportunity of go to church, and he
hop that they all have.
'but surely, surely,' continue Mrs Proudie, 'surely that be not
enough. surely that will not secure such an observance of the
Sabbath as we be teach to conceive be not only expedient by
indispensable; surely--'
come what come might, Dr Grantly be not to be force into a
dissertation on a point of doctrine with Mrs Proudie, nor yet with
Mr Slope; so without much ceremony he turn his back upon the
sofa, and begin to hope that Dr Proudie have find the palace
repair have be such as to meet his wish.
'yes, yes,' say his lordship; upon the whole he think so--upon
the whole, he donot know that there be much ground for complaint;
the architect, perhaps, might have--but his double, Mr Slope, who
have sidle over to the bishop's chair, would not allow his lordship
to finish his ambiguous speech.
'there be one point I would like to mention, Mr Archdeacon. his
lordship ask I to step through the premise, and I see that the
stall in the second stable be not perfect.'
'why--therebe stand for a dozen horses,'said the archdeacon.
'perhaps so,' say the other; 'indeed, Ihave no doubt of it; but
visitor, you know, often require so much accommodation. there be
many of the bishop's relative who always bring their own horse.'
Dr Grantly promise that due provision for the relative' horse
should be make, as far at least as the extent of the original
stable building would allow. he would himself communicate with the
architect.
'and the coach-house, Dr Grantly,' continue Mr Slope; 'there be
really hardly any room for a second carriage in the large
coach-house, and the small one, of course, hold only one.'
'and the gas,' chime in the lady; 'there be no gas through the
house, none whatever, but in the kitchen and passage. surely the
palace should have be fit through with pipe for gas, and hot
water too. there be no hot water lay on anywhere above the ground
floor. surely there should be the mean of get hot water in the
bed-room without have it bring in jug from the kitchen.'
the bishop have a decide opinion that there should be pipe for hot
water. hot water be very essential for the comfort of the palace.
it be, indeed, a requisite in any decent gentleman's house.
Mr Slope have remark that the coping on the garden wall be in
many place imperfect.
Mrs Proudie have discover a large hole, evidently the work of
rat, in the servant' hall.
the bishop express an utter detestation of rat. there be
nothing, he believe, in this world, that he so much hat as a
rat.
Mr Slope have, moreover, observe that the lock of the out-house
be very imperfect: he might specify the coal-cellar, and the
wood-house.
Mrs Proudie have also see that those on the door of the servant'
bedroom be in an equally bad condition; indeed the lock all
through the house be old-fashioned and unserviceable.
the bishop think that a great deal depend on a good lock, and
quite as much on the key. he have observe that the fault very often
lie with the key, especially if the ward be in any way twisted.
Mr Slope be go on with his catalogue of grievance, when he be
somewhat loudly interrupt by the archdeacon who succeed in
explain that the diocesan architect, or rather his foreman, be
the person to be address on such subject; and that he, Dr
Grantly, have inquire as to the comfort of the palace, merely as a
point of compliment. he be very sorry, however, that so many
thing have be find amiss: and then he rise from his chair to
escape.
Mrs Proudie, though she have contrive to lend her assistance in
recapitulate the palatial dilapidation, have not on that account
give up her hold of Mr Harding, nor cease from her
cross-examination as the iniquity of Sabbatical amusement. over
and over again have she throw out her 'surely, surely,' at Mr
Harding's devoted head, and ill have that gentleman be able to
parry the attack.
he have never before find himself subject to such a nuisance.
lady hitherto, when they have consult he on religious subject,
have listen to what he might choose to say with some deference,
and have differ, it they differ, in silence. but Mrs Proudie
interrogate he, and then lecture. 'neither thou, nor thy son,
nor thy daughter, nor thy man servant, nor thy maid servant,' say
she, impressively, and more than once, as though Mr Harding have
forget the word. she shake her finger at he as she quote the
favourite law, as though menace he with punishment; and then
call upon he categorically to state whether he do not think
that travel on the Sabbath be an abomination and a
desecration.
Mr Harding have never be so hard press in his life. he feel that
he ought to rebuke the lady for presume so to talk to a gentleman
and a clergyman so may year her senior; but he recoil from the
idea of scold the bishop's wife, in the bishop's presence, on
his first visit to the palace; moreover, to tell the truth, he be
somewhat afraid of she. she, see he sit silent and absorb, by
no mean refrain from the attack.
'I hope, Mr Harding,' say she, shake her head slowly and
solemnly, 'I hope you will not leave I to think that you approve
of Sabbath travel,' and she look a look of unutterable
meaning into his eye.
there be no standing for this, for Mr Slope be now look at
he, and so be the bishop, and so be the archdeacon, who have
complete his adieux on that side of the room. Mr Harding therefore
get up also, and put out his hand to Mrs Proudie, say: 'if you
will come to St Cuthbert's some Sunday, I will preach you a sermon
on the subject.'
and so the archdeacon and the precentor take their departure,
bow low to the lady, shake hand with the lord, and escape
from Mr Slope in the good manner each could. Mr Harding be again
maltreat; but Dr Grantly swear deeply in the bottom of his heart,
that no earthly consideration should ever again induce he to touch
the paw of that impure and filthy animal.
and now, have I the pen of a might poet, would I sing in epic verse
the noble wrath of the archdeacon. the palace step descend to a
broad gravel sweep, from whence a small gate open out into the
street, very near the covered gateway lead to the close. the
road from the palace door turn to the left, through the spacious
garden, and terminate on the London-road, half a mile from the
cathedral.
till they have pass this small gate and enter the close, neither
of they speak a word; but the precentor clearly see from his
companion's face that a tornado be to be expect, nor be he
himself incline to stop it. though, by nature far less irritable
than the archdeacon, even he be angry: he even--that mild and
courteous man--be inclined to express himself in anything but
courteous term.
CHAPTER VI
WAR
'Good heavens!' exclaim the archdeacon, as he place his foot on
the gravel walk of the close, and raise his hat with one hand,
pass the other somewhat violently over his now grizzle lock;
smoke issue from the uplifted beaver as it be a cloud of wrath,
and the safety-valve of his anger open, and emit a visible
steam, prevent positive explosion and probably apoplexy. 'good
heavens!'--and the archdeacon look up to the gray pinnacle of
the cathedral tower, make a mute appeal to that still live
witness which have look down on the doing of so many bishop of
Barchester.
'I donot think I shall ever like that Mr Slope,' say Mr Harding.
'like he!' roar the archdeacon, stand still for a moment to
give more force to his voice; 'like he!' all the raven of the
close caw their assent. the old bell of the tower, in chime
the hour, echo the word; and the swallow fly out from their
nest mutely express a similar opinion. like Mr Slope! why no, it
be not very probable that any Barchester-breed live thing should
like Mr Slope!
'nor Mrs Proudie either,' say Mr Harding.
the archdeacon thereupon forget himself. I will not follow his
example, nor shock my reader by transcribe the term in which he
express his feeling as to the lady who have be name. the
raven and the last linger note of the clock bell be less
scrupulous, and repeat in correspond echo the very improper
exclamation. the archdeacon again raise his hat; and another
salutary escape of steam be effect.
there be a pause, during which the precentor try to realise the
fact that the wife of the bishop of Barchester have be thus
designate, in the close of the cathedral, by the lip of its own
archdeacon: but he could not do it.
'the bishop seem a quiet man enough,' suggest Mr Harding, have
acknowledge to himself his own failure.
'Idiot!' exclaim the doctor, who for the nonce be not capable of
more than spasmodic attempt at utterance.
'well, he do not seem very bright,' say Mr Harding, 'and yet he
have always have the reputation of a clever man. I suppose hebe
cautious and not inclined to express himself very freely.'
the new bishop of Barchester be already so contemptible a creature
in Dr Grantly's eye, that he could not condescend to discuss his
character. he be a puppet to be play by other; a mere wax doll,
do up in an apron and a shovel hat, to be stick on a throne or
elsewhere and pull about by wire as other choose. Dr Grantly do
not choose to let himself down low enough to talk about Dr Proudie;
but he see that he would have to talk about the other member of
his household, the coadjutor bishop, who have bring his lordship
down, as it be, in a box, and be about to handle the wire as
they will. this in itself be a terrible vexation to the
archdeacon. could he have ignore the chaplain, and have fight the
bishop, there would have be, at any rate, nothing degrade in
such a contest. let the Queen make whom she would bishop of
Barchester; a man, or even an ape, when once a bishop, would be a
respectable adversary, if he would but fight, himself. but what be
such a person as Dr Grantly to do, when such another person as Mr
Slope be put forward as his antagonist?
if he, our archdeacon, refuse to combat, Mr Slope would walk
triumphant over the field, and have the diocese of Barchester under
his heel.
if, on the other hand, the archdeacon accept as his enemy the man
whom the new puppet bishop put before he as such, he would have to
talk about Mr Slope, and write about Mr Slope, and in all matter
treat with Mr Slope, as a being stand, in some degree, on ground
similar to his own. he would have to meet Mr Slope; to--bah! the
idea be sicken. he could not bring himself to have to do with
Mr Slope.
'he be the most thoroughly bestial creature that ever I set my eye
upon,' say the archdeacon.
'who--the bishop?'
