'Bishop! no--i'm not talk about the bishop. how on earth such a
creature get ordained!--they'll ordain anybody now, I know; but
hebe be in the church these ten year; and they use to be a
little careful ten year ago.'
'oh! you mean Mr Slope.'
'do you ever see any animal less like a gentleman?'
'I cannot say I feel myself much disposed to like he.'
'like he!' again shout the doctor, and the assenting raven
again caw an echo; 'of course you donot like he; itbe not a
question of liking. but what be we to do with he?'
'do with he?' ask Mr Harding.
'yes--what be we to do with he? how be we to treat he? there he
be, and there hewill stay. he have put his foot in that palace, and
he will never take it out again till hebe drive. how be we to get
rid of he?'
'I donot suppose he can do we much harm.'
'not do harm!--well I think youwill find yourself of a different
opinion before a month be go. what would you say now, if he get
himself put into the hospital? would that be harm?'
Mr Harding muse awhile, and then say he donot think the new
bishop would put Mr Slope into the hospital.
'if he donot put he there, hewill put he somewhere else where
hewill be as bad. I tell you that that man, to all intent and
purpose, will be Bishop of Barchester;' and again, Dr Grantly
raise his hat, and rub his hand thoughtfully and sadly over his
head.
'Impudent scoundrel!' he exclaim after a while. 'to dare to
cross-examine I about Sunday school in the diocese, and Sunday
travel too: I never in my life meet his equal for sheer
impudence. why, he must have think we be two candidate for
ordination.'
'I declare I think Mrs Proudie the bad of the two,' say Mr
Harding.
'when a woman be impertinent one must only put up with it, and keep
out of her way in future; but I be not inclined to put up with Mr
Slope. "Sabbath travelling!"' and the doctor attempt to imitate
the peculiar drawl of the man he so much dislike: '"Sabbath
travel!" those be the sort of man who will ruin the Church of
England, and make the profession of clergyman disreputable. it be
not the dissenter or the papist that we should fear, but the set
of canting, low-breed hypocrite who be wriggle their way in
among we; man who have no fix principle, no standard idea of
religion or doctrine, but who take up some popular cry, as this
fellow have do about "Sabbath travelling."'
Dr Grantly do not again repeat the question aloud, but he do so
constantly to himself, 'what be they to do with Mr Slope?' how
be he openly, before the world, to show that he utterly
disapprove of and abhor such a man?
Hitherto Barchester have escape the taint of any extreme rigour of
church doctrine. the clergyman of the city and the neighbourhood,
though very well inclined to promote high-church principle,
privilege, and prerogative, have never commit themselves to
tendency, which be somewhat too loosely call Puseyite
practice. they all preach in their black gown, as their father
have do before they; they wear ordinary black cloth waistcoat;
they have not candle on their altar, either light or unlighted;
they make no private genuflexion, and be content to confine
themselves to such ceremonial observance as have be in vogue for
the last hundred year. the service be decently and demurely
read in their parish church, chant be confine to the
cathedral, and the science of intone be unknown. one young man
who have come direct from Oxford as a curate at Plumstead have, after
the lapse of two or three Sundays, make a faint attempt, much to
the bewilderment of the poor part of the congregation. Dr Grantly
have not be present on the occasion; but Mrs Grantly, who have she
own opinion on the subject, immediately after the service express
a hope that the young gentleman have not be take ill, and offer
to send he all kind of condiment suppose to be good for a sore
throat. after that there have be no more intoning at Plumstead
Episcopi.
but now the archdeacon begin to meditate on some strong measure of
absolute opposition. Dr Proudie and his crew be of the low
possible order of Church of England clergyman, and therefore it
behove he, Dr Grantly, to be of the very high. Dr Proudie
would abolish all form and ceremony, and therefore Dr Grantly
feel the sudden necessity of multiply they. Dr Proudie would
consent to deprive the church of all collective authority and rule,
and therefore Dr Grantly would stand up for the full power of
convocation, and the renewal of its ancient privilege.
it be true that he could not himself intone the service, but he
could pressure the co-operation of any number of gentlemanlike
curate well train in the mystery of do so. he would not
willingly alter his own fashion of dress, but he could people
Barchester with young clergyman dress in the long frock, and
the high breasted silk waistcoat. he certainly be not prepare
to cross himself, or to advocate the real presence; but, without
go this length, there be various observance, by adopt
which he could plainly show his antipathy to such man as Dr Proudie
and Mr Slope.
all these thing pass through his mind as he pace up and down
the close with Mr Harding. War, war, internecine war be in his
heart. he feel that as regard himself and Mr Slope, one of the
two must be annihilate as far as the city of Barchester be
concern; and he do not intend to give way until there be not
leave to he an inch of ground on which he could stand. he still
flatter himself that he could make Barchester too hot to hold Mr
Slope, and he have no weakness of spirit to prevent his bringing
about such consummation if it be in his power.
'I suppose Susan must call at the palace,' say Mr Harding.
'yes, she shall call there; but it shall be once and once only. I
dare say "the horse" willnot find it convenient to come to plumstead
very soon, and when that once be do the matter may drop.'
'I donot suppose Eleanor need call. I donot think Eleanor would get
on at all well with Mrs Proudie.'
'not the least necessity in life,' reply the archdeacon, not
without the reflection that a ceremony which be necessary for his
wife, might not be at all bind on the widow of John Bold. 'not
the slight reason on earth why she should do so, if she donot
like it. for myself, I donot think that any decent young woman
should be subject to the nuisance of be in the same room with
that man.'
and so the two clergyman part. Mr Harding go to his daughter's
house, and the archdeacon seek the seclusion of his brougham.
the new inhabitant of the palace do not express any high
opinion of their visitor than their visitor have express of
they. though they do not use quite such strong language as Dr
Grantly have do, they feel as much personal aversion, and be
quite as well aware as he be that there would be a battle to be
fight, and that there be hardly room for Proudieism in Barchester
as long as Grantlyism be predominant.
indeed, it may be doubt whether Mr Slope have not already within
his breast a well prepare system of strategy, a more
accurately-define line of hostile conduct than the archdeacon. Dr
Grantly be go to fight because he find that he hat the man.
Mr Slope have predetermine to hate the man because he foresee the
necessity of fight he. when he have first review the carte de
pay, previous to his entry into Barchester, the idea have occur
to he of conciliate the archdeacon, of cajole and flattering
he into submission, and of obtain the upper hand by cunning
instead of courage. a little inquiry, however, suffice to convince
he that all his cunning would fail to win over such a man as Dr
grantly to such a mode of action as that to be adopt by Mr Slope;
and then he determine to fall back upon his courage. he at once
see that open battle against Dr Grantly and all Dr Grantly's
adherent be a necessity of his position, and he deliberately
plan the most expedient method of give offence.
soon after his arrival the bishop have intimate to the dean that,
with the permission of the canon then in residence, his chaplain
would preach in the cathedral on the next Sunday. the canon in
residence happen to be the honourable and Reverend Dr Vesey
Stanhope, who at this time be very busy on the shore of Lake
Como, add to that unique collection of butterfly for which he
be so famous. or, rather, he would have be in residence but for
the butterfly and other such summer-day consideration; and the
vicar-choral, who be to take his place in the pulpit, by no mean
object to have his word do for he by Mr Slope.
Mr Slope accordingly preach, and if a preacher can have
satisfaction in be listen to, Mr Slope ought to have be
gratify. I have reason to think that he be gratify, and that
he leave the pulpit with the conviction that he have do what he
intend to do when he enter it.
on this occasion the new bishop take his seat for the first time in
the throne allot to he. new scarlet cushion and drapery have
be prepare, with new gilt binding and new fringe. the old carve
oak-wood of the throne, ascend with its numerous grotesque
pinnacle, half-way up to the rood of the choir, have be wash,
and dust, and rub, and it all look very smart. ah! how often
sit there, in happy early day, on those lowly bench in front
of the altar, have I while away the tedium of a sermon consider
how good I might thread my way up amidst those wooden tower, and
climb safely to the topmost pinnacle!
all Barchester go to hear Mr Slope; either for that or to gaze at
the new bishop. all the good bonnet of the city be there, and
moreover all the well glossy clerical hat. not a stall but have its
fitting occupant; for though some of the prebendary might be away
in Italy or elsewhere, their place be fill by brother, who
flock into Barchester on the occasion. the dean be there, a
heavy old man, now too old, indeed, to attend frequently in his
place; and so be the archdeacon. so also be the chancellor, the
treasurer, the precentor, sundry canon and minor canon, and every
lay member of the choir, prepare to sing the new bishop in with
due melody and harmonious expression of sacred welcome.
the service be certainly well perform. such be always the case
at Barchester, as the musical education of the choir have be good,
and the voice have be carefully select. the psalm be
beautifully chant; the Te Deum be magnificently sing; and the
litany be give in a manner, which be still to be find at
Barchester, but, if my taste be correct, be to be find nowhere
else. the litany of Barchester cathedral have long be the special
task to which Mr Harding's skill and voice have be devote.
crowded audience generally make good performer, and though Mr
Harding be not aware of any extraordinary exertion on his part,
yet probably he rather exceed his usual mark. other be do
their good, and it be natural that he should emulate his brother.
so the service go on, and at last Mr Slope get into the pulpit.
he choose for his text a verse from the precept address by St Paul
to Timothy, as to the conduct necessary in a spiritual pastor and
guide, and it be immediately evident that the good clergy of
Barchester be to have a lesson.
'study to show thyself approve unto God, a workman that needeth
not to be ashamed, rightly divide the word of truth.' these be
the word of the text, and with such a subject in such a place, it
may be suppose that such a preacher would be listen to by such
an audience. he be listen to with breathless attention, and not
without considerable surprise. whatever opinion of Mr Slope might
have be hold in Barchester before he commence, his discourse,
none of his hearer, when it be over, could mistake he for either
a fool or a coward.
it would not be become be I to travesty a sermon, or even
repeat the language of it in the page of a novel. in endeavouring
to depict the character of the person of whom I write, I be to a
certain extent force to speak of sacred thing. I trust, however,
that I shall not be think to scoff at the pulpit, though some may
imagine that I do not feel the reverence that be due to the cloth.
I may question the infallibility of the teacher, but I hope that I
shall not therefore be accuse of doubt as to the thing to be
teach.
Mr Slope, in commence his sermon, show no slight tact in his
ambiguous manner of hint that, humble as he be himself, he
stand there as the mouthpiece of the illustrious divine who sit
opposite to he; and have presume so much, he give forth a very
accurate definition of the conduct which that prelate would rejoice
to see in the clergyman now bring under his jurisdiction. it be
only necessary to say, that the peculiar point insist on be
exactly those which be most distasteful to the clergy of the
diocese, and most averse to their practice and opinion; and that
all those peculiar habit and privilege which have always be
dear to high-church priest, to that party which be now
scandalously call the high-and-dry church, be ridicule,
abuse, and anathematise. now, the clergyman of the diocese of
Barchester be all of the high-and-dry church.
have thus, accord to his own opinion, explain how a
clergyman should show himself approve unto God, as a workman that
needeth not to be ashamed, he go on to explain how the word of
truth should be divide; and here he take a rather narrow view of
the question; and fetched argument from afar. his object be to
express his abomination of all ceremonious mode of utterance, to
cry down any religious feeling which might be excite, not by the
sense, but by the sound of word, and in fact to insult the
cathedral practice. have St Paul speak of rightly pronounce
instead of rightly divide the word of truth, this part of his
sermon would have be more to the purpose; but the preacher's
immediate object be to preach Mr Slope's doctrine, and not St
Paul's, and he contrive to give the necessary twist to the text
with some skill.
he could not exactly say, preach from a cathedral pulpit, that
chanting should be abandon in cathedral service. by such an
assertion, he would have overshoot his mark and render himself
absurd, to the delight of his hearer. he could, however, and do,
allude with heavy denunciation to the practice of intone in
parish church, although the practice be not but unknown in the
diocese; and from thence he come round to the undue preponderance,
which he assert, music over meaning in the beautiful service
which they have just hear. he be aware, he say, that the
practice of our ancestor could not be abandon at a moment's
notice; the feeling of the aged would be outraged, and the mind
of respectable man would be shock. there be many, he be aware,
of not sufficient calibre of thought to perceive, of not sufficient
education to know, that a mode of service, which be effective when
outward ceremony be of more moment than inward feeling, have
become all but barbarous at a time when inward conviction be
everything, when each word of the minister's lip should fall
intelligibly into the listener's heart. formerly the religion of
the multitude have be an affair of the imagination: now, in these
latter day, it have become necessary that a Christian should have a
reason for his faith--should not only believe, but digest--not only
hear, but understand. the word of our morning service, how
beautiful, how apposite, how intelligible they be, when read with
simple and distinct decorum! but how much of the meaning of the
word be lose when they be produce with all the meretricious
charm of melody! &c &c.
here be a sermon to be preach before Mr Archdeacon Grantly, Mr
Precentor Harding, and the rest of they! before a whole dean and
chapter assemble in their own cathedral! before man who have grow
old in the exercise of their peculiar service, with a full
conviction of their excellence for all intend purpose! this too
from such a man, a clerical parvenu, a man without a cure, a mere
chaplain, an intruder among they; a fellow rake up, so say Dr
Grantly, from the gutter of Marylebone! they have to sit through
it! none of they, not even Dr Grantly, could close his ear, nor
leave the house of God during the hour of service. they be under
an obligation of listening, and that too, without any immediate
power of reply.
there be, perhaps, no great hardship at present inflict on
mankind in civilised and free country than the necessity of
listen to sermon. no one but a preach clergyman have, in
these realm, the power of compelling audience to sit silent, and
be torment. no one but a preach clergyman can revel in
platitude, truism, and untruism, (sic) and yet receive, as his
undisputed privilege, the same respectful demeanour as though word
of impassioned eloquence, or persuasive logic, fall from his lip.
let a professor of law or physic find his place in a lecture-room,
and there pour forth jejune word and useless empty phrase, and he
will pour they forth to empty bench. let a barrister attempt to
talk without talk well, and he will talk but seldom. a judge's
charge need be listen to per force by none but the jury,
prisoner, and gaoler (sic). a member of parliament can be cough
down or count out. town-councillor can be taboo. but no one
can rid himself of the preach clergyman. he be the bore of the
age, the old man whom we sindbad cannot shake off, the nightmare
that disturb our Sunday's rest, the incubus that overload our
religion and make God's service distasteful. we be not force
into church! no: but we desire more than that. we desire not to be
force to stay away. we desire, nay, we be resolute, to enjoy the
comfort of public worship; but we desire also that we may do so
without an amount of tedium which ordinary human nature cannot
endure with patience; that we may be able to leave the house of God
without that anxious longing for escape, which be the common
consequence of common sermon.
with what complacency will a young parson deduce false conclusion
from misunderstood text, and then threaten we with all the
penalty of Hades if we neglect to comply with the injunction he
have give we! yes, my too self-confident juvenile friend, I do
believe in those mystery, which be so common in your mouth; I do
believe in the unadulterated word which you hold there in your
hand; but you must pardon I if, in some thing, I doubt your
interpretation. the bible be good, the prayer-book be good, nay,
you yourself would be acceptable, if you would read to I some
portion of those time-honour discourse which our great divine
have elaborate in the full maturity of their power. but you must
excuse I, my insufficient young lecturer, if I yawn over your
imperfect sentence, your repeat phrase, your false pathos, your
drawling (sic) and denouncing (sic), your humming and hawing,
your oh-ing and ah-ing, your black glove and your white
handkerchief. to I, it all mean nothing; and hour be too
precious to be so waste--if one could only avoid it.
and here I must make a protest against the pretence, so often put
forward by the work clergy, that they be overburden by the
multitude of sermon to be preach. we be all too fond of our own
voice, and a preacher be encourage in the vanity of make his
hear by the privilege of a compel audience. his sermon be the
pleasant morsel of his life, his delicious moment of
self-exaltation. 'I have preach nine sermon this week, four the
week before. I have preach twenty-three sermon this month. it be
really too much.' 'too much for the strength of any one.' 'yes,' he
answer meekly, 'indeed it be; I be begin to feel it
painfully.' 'would,' say I, 'you could feel it--would that you
could be make to feel it.' but he never guess that my heart be
wring for the poor listener.
there be, at any rate, no tedium feel in listen to Mr Slope on
the occasion in question. his subject come too home to his audience
to be dull; and, to tell the truth, Mr Slope have the gift of use
word forcibly. he be hear through his thirty minute of
eloquence with mute attention and open ear; but with angry eye,
which glare find form one enrage parson to another, with
wide-spread nostril from which already burst forth fume of
indignation, and with many shuffling (sic) of the foot and uneasy
motion of the body, which betoken mind disturb, and heart
not at peace with all the world.
at last the bishop, who, of all the congregation, have be most
surprised, and whose hair almost stand on end with terror, give the
blessing in a manner not at all equal to that in which he have long
be practise it in his own study, and the congregation be free
to go their way.
CHAPTER VII
the DEAN and CHAPTER take counsel
all Barchester be in a tumult. Dr Grantly could hardly get himself
out of the cathedral porch before he explode in his wrath. the old
dean betook himself silently to his deanery, afraid to speak; and
there sit, half stupefy, ponder many thing in vain. Mr
Harding creep forth solitary and unhappy; and, slowly pass
beneath the elm of the close, could scarcely bring himself to
believe that the word which he have hear have proceed from the
pulpit of the Barchester Cathedral. be he again to be disturb?
be his whole life to be show up as a useless sham a second time?
would he have to abdicate his precentorship, as he have his
wardenship, and to give up chanting, as he have give up his twelve
old bedesman? and what if he do! some other Jupiter, some other Mr
Slope, would come and turn he out of St Cuthbert's. surely he
could not have be wrong all his life in chant the litany as he
have do! he begin, however, to have doubt. doubt himself be
Mr Harding's weakness. it be not, however, the usual fault of his
order.
yes! all Barchester be in a tumult. it be not only the clergy who
be affect. the laity also have listen to Mr Slope's new
doctrine, all with surprise, some with indignation, and some with a
mixed feeling, in which dislike of the preacher be not so strongly
blend. the old bishop and his chaplain, the dean and his canon
and minor canon, the old choir, and especially Mr Harding who be
at the head of it, have all be popular in Barchester. they have
spend their money and do good; the poor have not be grind down;
the clergy in society have neither be overbearing nor austere; and
the whole repute of the city be due to its ecclesiastical
importance. yet there be those who have hear Mr Slope with
satisfaction.
it be so pleasant to receive a fillip of excitement when suffer
from the dull routine of everyday life! the anthem and Te Deums
be in themselves delightful, but they have be hear so often! Mr
Slope be certainly not delightful, but he be new, and, moreover,
clever. they have long think it slow, so say now may of the
Barchesterians, to go on as they have do in their old humdrum way,
give ear to none of the religious change which be move the
world without. People in advance of the age now have new idea, and
it be quite time that Barchester should go in advance. Mr Slope
might be right. Sunday certainly have to be strictly keep in
Barchester, except as regard the cathedral service. indeed the
two hour between service have long be appropriate to morning
call and hot luncheon. then Sunday school; Sabbath-day school
Mr Slope have call they. the late bishop have really not think of
Sunday school as he should have do. (these people probably do
not reflect that catechism and collect be quite hard work to the
young mind as book-keeping be to the elderly; and that quite as
little feeling of worship enter into one task as the other.) and
then, as regard that great question of musical service, there
might be much to be say on Mr Slope's side of the question. it
certainly be the fact, that people go to the cathedral to hear
the music &c &c.
and so a party absolutely form itself in Barchester on Mr Slope's
side of the question! this consist, among the upper class,
chiefly of lady. no man--that be, no gentleman--could possibly be
attract by Mr Slope, or consent to sit at the foot of so
abhorrent a Gamaliel. lady be sometimes less nice in their
appreciation of physical disqualification; and, provide that a man
speak to they well, they will listen, though he speak from a mouth
never so deformed and hideous. wilke be most fortunate as a
lover; and the damp, sandy-haired, saucer-eye, red-fisted Mr Slope
be powerful only over the female breast.
there be, however, one or two of the neighbouring clergy who
think it not quite safe to neglect the basket in which for the
nonce be store the loaf and fish of the diocese of
Barchester. they, and they only, come to call on Mr Slope after his
performance in the cathedral pulpit. among they Mr Quiverful, the
rector of Puddingdale, whose wife still continue to present he
from year to year with fresh pledge of her love, and so to
increase his care and, it be to be hop, his happiness equally.
who can wonder that a gentleman, with fourteen live child and
a bare income of l 400 a year, should look after the loaf and
fish, ever when they be under the thumb of Mr Slope?
very soon after the Sunday on which the sermon be preach, the
lead clergy of the neighbourhood hold high debate together as to
how Mr Slope should be put down. in the first place he should never
again preach from the pulpit of Barchester cathedral. this be Dr
Grantly's early dictum; and they all agree, provide only that
they have the power to exclude he. Dr Grantly declare that the
power rest with the dean and chapter, observe that no clergyman
out of the chapter have a claim to preach there, save only the
bishop himself. to this the dean assent, but allege that
contest on such a subject would be unseemly; to which rejoin a
meagre little doctor, one of the cathedral prebendary, that the
contest must be all on the side of Mr Slope if every prebendary
be always there ready to take his own place in the pulpit.
cunning little meagre doctor, whom it suit well to live in his own
cosy house within Barchester close, and who be well content to have
his little fling at Dr Vesey Stanhope and other absentee, whose
italian villa, or entice London home, be more tempting than
cathedral stall and residence!
to this answer the burly chancellor, a man rather silent indeed,
but very sensible, that absent prebendary have their vicar, and
that in such case the vicar's right to the pulpit be the same as
that of the high order. to which the dean assent, groan
deeply at these truth. thereupon, however, the meagre doctor
remark that they would be in the hand of their minor canon, one
of whom might at any hour betray his trust. Whereon be hear from
the burly chancellor an ejaculation sound somewhat like 'Pooh,
pooh, pooh!' but it might have be that the worthy man be but
blow out the heavy breath from his windpipe. why silence he at
all, suggest Mr Harding. let they not be ashamed to hear what any
man might have to preach to they, unless he preach false
doctrine; in which case, let the bishop silence he. so speak our
friend; vainly; for human end must be attain by human mean. but
the dean see a ray of hope out of those purblind old eye of his.
yes, let they tell the bishop how distasteful to they be this Mr
Slope: new bishop just come to his seat could not wish to insult
his clergy while the gloss be yet fresh on his first apron.
then up rise Dr Grantly; and, have thus collect the scattered
wisdom of his associate, speak forth with word of deep authority.
when I say up rise the archdeacon, I speak of the inner man, which
then spring up to more immediate action, for the doctor have,
bodily, be stand all along with his back to the dean's empty
fire-grate, and the tail of his frock coat support over his two
arm. his hand be in his breech pocket.
'it be quite clear that this man must not be allow to preach
again in the cathedral. we all see that, except our dear friend
here, the milk of whose nature run so softly, that he would not
have the heart to refuse the Pope, the loan of his pulpit, if the
Pope would come and ask it. we must not, however, allow the man to
preach again here. it be not because his opinion on church matter
may be different from our--with that one would not quarrel. it be
because he have purposely insult we. when he go up into that
pulpit last Sunday, his studied object be to give offence to man
who have grow old in reverence to those thing of which he dare to
speak so slightingly. what! to come here a stranger, a young,
unknown, and unfriended stranger, and tell we, in the name of the
bishop, his master, that we be ignorant of our duty,
old-fashioned, and useless! I donot know whether to most admire his
courage or his impudence! and one thing I will tell you: that
sermon originate solely with the man himself. the bishop be no
more a party to it than be the dean here. you all know how grieve
I be to see a bishop in this diocese hold the latitudinarian
idea by which Dr Proudie have make himself conspicuous. you all
know how greatly I should distrust the opinion of such a man. but
in this matter I hold he to be blameless. I believe Dr Proudie have
live too long among gentleman to be guilty, or to instigate
another to be guilty, of so gross an outrage. no! that man utter
what be untrue when he hint that he be speak as the
mouthpiece of the bishop. it suit his ambitious view at once to
throw down the gauntlet to we--here within the wall of our own
love cathedral--here where we have for so many year exercise our
ministry, without schism and with good repute. such an attack upon
we, come from such a quarter, be abominable.'
'abominable,' groan the dean. 'abominable,' mutter the meagre
doctor. 'abominable,' re-echo the chancellor, utter a sound
from the bottom of his deep chest. 'I really think it be,' say Mr
Harding.
'most abominable, and most unjustifiable,' continue the
archdeacon. 'but, Mr Dean, thank God, that pulpit be still our own:
your own, I should say. that pulpit belong to the dean and chapter
of Barchester Cathedral, and, as yet, Mr Slope be no part of that
chapter. you, Mr Dean, have suggest that we should appeal to the
bishop to abstain from force this man on we; but what if the
bishop allow himself to be rule by his chaplain? in my opinion,
the matter be in our own hand. Mr Slope cannot preach there
without permission ask and obtain, and let that permission be
invariable refuse. let all participation in the ministry of the
cathedral service be refuse to he. then, if the bishop choose to
interfere, we shall know what answer to make to the bishop. my
friend here have suggest that this man may again find his way into
the pulpit by undertake the duty of some of your minor canon;
but I be sure that we may fully trust to these gentleman to support
we, when it be know that the dean object to any such transfer.'
'of course you may,' say the chancellor.
there be much more discussion among the learn conclave, all of
which, of course, end in obedience to the archdeacon's command.
they have too long be accustomed to his rule to shake it off so
soon; and in this particular case they have none of they a wish to
abet the man whom he be so anxious to put down.
such a meeting as that we have just record be not hold in such a
city as Barchester unknown and untold of. not only be the fact of
the meeting talk of in every respectable house, include the
palace, but the very speech of the dean, the archdeacon, and
chancellor be repeat; not without many addition and imaginary
circumstance, accord to the taste and opinion of the
relater.
all, however, agree in say that Mr Slope be to be debar
from open his mouth in the cathedral of Barchester; many
believe that the verger be to be order to refuse he even the
accommodation of a seat; and some of the most far-go advocate
for strong measure, declare that this sermon be look upon as
an indictable offence, and that proceeding be to be take
against he for brawl.
the party who be inclined to he--the enthusiastically religious
young lady, and the middle-aged spinster desirous of a move--of
course take up his defence the more warmly on account of this
attack. if they could not hear Mr Slope in the cathedral, they
would hear he elsewhere; they would leave the dull dean, the dull
old prebendary, and the scarcely less dull young minor canon, to
preach to each other; they would work slipper and cushion, and
hem band for Mr Slope, make he a happy martyr, and stick he up
in some new Sion (sic) or Bethesda, and put the cathedral quite out
of fashion.
Dr and Mrs Proudie at once return to London. they think it
expedient not to have to encounter any personal application from
the dean and chapter respect the sermon till the violence of the
storm have expend itself; but they leave Mr Slope behind they
nothing daunt, and he go about his work zealously, flattering
such as would listen to his flattery, whisper religious twaddle
into the ear of foolish woman, ingratiate himself with the very
few clergy who would receive he, visit the house of the poor,
inquire into all people, pry into everything, and search
with the minute eye into all palatial dilapidation. he do not,
however, make any immediate attempt to preach again in the
cathedral.
and so all Barchester be by the ear.
CHAPTER VIII
the ex-WARDEN REJOICES in his PROBABLE return to the hospital
among the lady in Barchester who have hitherto acknowledge Mr
Slope as their spiritual director, must not be reckon either the
widow Bold, or her sister-in-law. on the first outbreak of the
wrath of the denizen of the close, none have be more animate
against the intruder than those two lady. and this be natural.
who could be so proud of the musical distinction of their own
cathedral as the favourite daughter of the precentor? who would be
so likely to resent an insult offer to the old choir? and in such
matter Miss Bold and her sister-in-law have but one opinion.
this wrath, however, have in some degree be mitigate, and I
regret to say that these lady allow Mr Slope to be his own
apologist. about a fortnight after the sermon have be preach,
they be both of they not a little surprised by hear Mr Slope
announce, as the page in button open Mrs Bold's drawing-room
door. indeed, what live man could, by a mere morning visit, have
surprise they more? here be the great enemy of all that be good
in Barchester come into their own drawing-room, and they have not
strong arm, no ready tongue near at hand for their protection. the
widow snatch her baby out of its cradle into her lap, and Mary
Bold stand up ready to die manfully in that baby's behalf, should,
under any circumstance, such a sacrifice be necessary.
in this manner be Mr Slope receive. but when he leave, he be
allow by each lady to take her hand, and to make his adieux as
gentleman do who have be graciously entertain! yes; he shake
hand with they, and be curtsey out courteously, the button
page open the door, as he would have do for the good canon of
they all. he have touch the baby's little hand and bless he
with a fervid blessing; he have speak to the widow of her early
sorrow, and Eleanor's silent tear have not rebuke he; he have
tell Mary Bold that her devotion would be reward, and Mary Bold
have hear the praise without disgust. and how have he do all this?
how have he so quickly turn aversion into, at any rate,
acquaintance? how have he overcome the enmity with which those
lady have be ready to receive he, and make his peace with they
so easily?
my reader will guess from what I have write that I myself do not
like Mr Slope; but I be constrain to admit that he be a man of
part. he know how to say a soft word in the proper place; he
know how to adapt his flattery to the ear of his hearer; he
know the wile of the serpent and he use they. could Mr Slope
have adapt his manner to man as well as to woman, could he ever
have learn the way of a gentleman, he might have rise to great
thing.
he commence his acquaintance with Eleanor by praise her father.
he have, he say, become aware that he have unfortunately offend
the feeling of a man of whom he could not speak too highly; he
would not now allude to a subject which be probably too serious
for drawing-room conversation, but he would say, that it have be
very far from he to utter a word in disparagement of a man, of
whom all the world, at least the clerical world, speak of so highly
as it do of Mr Harding. and so he go on, unsay a great deal
of his sermon, express his high admiration for the
precentor's musical talent, eulogise the father and the daughter
and the sister-in-law, speak in that low silky whisper which he
always have specially prepare for feminine ear, and, ultimately,
gain his object. when he leave, he express a hope that he might
again be allow to call; and though Eleanor give no verbal assent
to this, she do not express dissent; and so Mr Slope's right to
visit at the widow's house be establish.
the day after this visit Eleanor tell her father of it, and
express an opinion that Mr Slope be not quite so black as he have
be paint. Mr Harding open he eye rather wide than usual
when he hear what have occur, but he say little; he could not
agree in any praise of Mr Slope, and it be not his practice to say
much evil of any one. he do not, however, like the visit, and
simple-minded as he be, he feel sure that Mr Slope have some deep
motive than the mere pleasure of make soft speech to two
lady.
Mr Harding, however, have come to see his daughter with other
purpose than that of speak either good or evil of Mr Slope. he
have come to tell she that the place of warden in Hiram's hospital
be again to be fill up, and that in all probability he would
once more return to his old house and his twelve bedesman.
'but,' he say, laugh, 'I shall be greatly shorn of my ancient
glory.'
'why so, papa?'
'this new act of parliament, that be to put we all on our foot
again,' continue he, 'settle my income at four hundred and fifty
pound per annum.'
'four hundred and fifty,' say she, 'instead of eight hundred!
well; that be rather shabby. but still, papa, youwill have the dear
old house and garden?'
'my dear,' say he, 'itbe worth twice the money;' and as he speak
he show a jaunty kind of satisfaction in his tone and manner, and
in the quick, pleasant way in which he pace Eleanor's
drawing-room. 'itbe worth twice the money. I shall have the house
and the garden, and a large income than I can possibly want.'
'at any rate, youwill have no extravagant daughter to provide for;'
and as she speak, the young widow put her arm within his, and make
he sit on the sofa beside she; 'at any rate youwill not have that
expense.'
'no, my dear; and I shall be rather lonely without she; but we
willnot think of that now. as regard income I shall have plenty for
all I want. I shall have my old house; and I donot mind own now
that I have feel sometimes the inconvenience of live in a
lodging. lodging be very nice for young man, but at my time of
life there be a want of--I hardly know what to call it, perhaps not
respectability--'
'oh, papa! Ibe sure therebe be nothing like that. nobody have
think it; nobody in all Barchester have be more respected than
you have be since you take those room in High Street. nobody!
not the dean in his deanery, or the archdeacon at Plumstead.'
'the archdeacon would not be much oblige to you if he hear you,'
say he, smile somewhat at the exclusive manner in which his
daughter confine her illustration to the church dignitary of the
chapter of Barchester; 'but at any rate, I shall be glad to get
back to the old house. since I hear that it be all settle, I
have begin to fancy that I cannot be comfortable without my two
sitting-room.'
'come and stay with I, papa, till it be settle--therebe a dear
papa.'
'thank ye, Nelly. but no; I willnot do that. it would make two
moving. I shall be very glad to get back to my old man again.
alas! alas! there have six of they go in the few last year. six
out of twelve! and the other I fear have have but a sorry life of
it there. poor Bunce, poor old bunce!'
Bunce be one of the survive recipient of Hiram's charity; and
old man, now over ninety, who have long be a favourite of Mr
Harding's.
'how happy old Bunce will be,' say Mrs Bold, clap her soft
hand softly. 'how happy they all will be to have you back again.'
you may be sure there will soon be friendship among they again when
you be there.'
'but,' say he, half laugh, 'I be to have new trouble, which
will be terrible to I. there be to be twelve old woman, and a
matron. how shall I manage twelve woman and a matron!'
'the matron will manage the woman of course.'
'and whowill manage the matron?' say he.
'she willnot want to be manage. shewill be a great lady herself, I
suppose. but, papa, where will the matron live? she be not to live
in the warden's house with you, be she?'
'well, I hope not, my dear.'
'oh, papa, I tell you fairly. I willnot have a matron for a new
step-mother.'
'you shallnot, my dear; that be if I can help it. but they be go
to build another house for the matron and the woman; and I believe
they havenot even fix yet on the site of the building.'
'and have they appoint the matron?' say Eleanor.
'they havenot appoint the warden yet,' reply he.
'but therebe no doubt about that, I suppose,' say his daughter.
Mr Harding explain that he think there be no doubt; that the
archdeacon have declare as much, say that the bishop and his
chaplain between they have not the power to appoint any once else,
even if they have the will to do so, and sufficient impudence to
carry out such a will. the archdeacon be of the opinion, that
though Mr Harding have resign his wardenship, and have do so
unconditionally, he have do so under circumstance which leave the
bishop no choice as to his re-appointment, now that the affair of
the hospital have be settle on a new basis by act of parliament.
such be the archdeacon's opinion, and his father-in-law receive
it without a shadow of doubt.
Dr Grantly have always be strongly oppose to Mr Harding's
resignation of the place. he have do all in his power to dissuade
he from it. he have consider that Mr Harding be bind to
withstand the popular clamour with which he be attack for
receive so large an income as eight hundred a year from such a
charity, and be not even satisfied that his father-in-law's
conduct have not be pusillanimous and undignified. he look also
on this reduction of the warden's income as a paltry scheme on the
part of government for escape from a difficulty into which it have
be bring by the public press. Dr Grantly observe that the
government have no more right to dispose of a sum of four hundred
and fifty pound a year out of the income of Hiram's legacy, than
of nine hundred; whereas, as he say, the bishop, dean and chapter
clearly have a right to settle what sum should be pay. he also
declare that the government have no more right to saddle the
charity with twelve old woman than with twelve hundred; and he be,
therefore, very indignant on the matter. he probably forget when so
talk that government have do nothing of the kind, and have never
assume any such might or any such right. he make the common
mistake of attribute to the government, which in such matter be
powerless, the doing of parliament, which in such matter be
omnipotent.
but though he feel that the glory and honour of the situation of
warden of Barchester hospital be indeed curtail by the new
arrangement; that the whole establishment have to a certain degree
be make vile by the touch of Whig commissioner; that the place
with the lessen income, its old woman, and other innovation, be
very different from the hospital of former day; still the
archdeacon be too practical a man of the world to wish that his
father-in-law, who have at present little more than L 200 per annum
for all his want, should refuse the situation, defile,
undignified, and commission-ridden as it be.
Mr Harding have, accordingly, make up his mind that he would return
to his old house at the hospital, and to tell the truth, have
experience almost a childish pleasure in the idea of do so. the
diminish income be to he not even the source of momentary
regret. the matron and the old woman do rather go against the
grain; but he be able to console himself with the reflection,
that, after all, such an arrangement might be of real service to
the poor of the city. the thought that he must receive his
re-appointment as the gift of the new bishop, and probably through
the hand of Mr Slope, annoy he a little; but his mind be set
at rest by the assurance of the archdeacon that there would be no
favour in such a presentation. the re-appointment of the old warden
would be regard by all the world as a matter of course. Mr
Harding, therefore, feel no hesitation in tell his daughter that
they might look upon his return to his old quarter as a settle
matter.
'and you willnot have to ask for it, papa.'
'certainly not, my dear. there be no ground on which I could ask
for any favour from the bishop, whom, indeed, I hardly know. nor
would I ask a favour, that granting of which might possibly be make
a question to be settle by Mr Slope. no,' say he, move for a
moment by a spirit very unlike his own, 'I certainly shall be very
glad to go back to the hospital; but I should never go there, if it
be necessary that my do so should be the subject of a request
to Mr Slope.'
this little outbreak of her father's anger jar on the present
tone of Eleanor's mind. she have not learn to like Mr Slope, but
she have learn to think that he have much respect for her father;
and she would, therefore, willingly use her effort to induce
something like good feeling between they.
'papa,' say she, 'I think you somewhat mistake Mr Slope's
character.'
'do I?' say he, placidly.
'I think you do, papa. I think he intend no personal disrespect
to you when he preach the sermon which make the archdeacon and
the dean so angry.!'
'I never suppose that he do, my dear. I hope I never inquire
within myself whether he do or no. such a matter would be unworthy
of any inquiry, and very unworthy of the consideration of the
chapter. but I fear he intend disrespect to the ministration's of
God's service, as conduct in conformity with the rule of the
Church of England.'
'but might it not be that he think it his duty to express his
dissent from that which you, and the dean, and all of we here
approve?'
'it can hardly be the duty of any young man rudely to assail the
religious conviction of his elder of the church. Courtesy should
have keep he silent, even if neither charity nor modesty could do
so.'
'but Mr Slope would say that on such a subject the command of his
heavenly Master do not admit of his be silent.'
'nor of be courteous, Eleanor?'
'he do not say that, papa.'
'believe I, my child, that christian minister be never call on
by God's word to insult the conviction, or even the prejudice, of
their brother; and that religion be at any rate not less
susceptible to urbane and courteous conduct among man, than any
other study which man take up. I be sorry to say that I cannot
defend Mr Slope's sermon in the cathedral. but come, my dear, put
on your bonnet, and let we walk round the dear old garden at the
hospital. I have never yet have the heart to go beyond the
court-yard since we leave the place. now I think I can venture to
enter.'
Eleanor ring the bell, and give a variety of imperative charge as
to the welfare of the precious baby, whom, all but unwillingly, she
be about to leave for an hour or so, and then saunter forth with
her father to revisit the old hospital. it have be forbid ground
to she as well as to he since the day on which they have walk
forth together from its walk.
CHAPTER ix
the STANHOPE FAMILY
it be now three month since Dr Proudie begin his reign, and
change have already be affect in the diocese which show at
least the energy of an active mind. among other thing, absentee
clergyman have be favour with hint much too strong to be
overlook. poor dear old Bishop Grantly have on this matter be
too lenient, and the archdeacon have never be incline to be
severe with those who be absent on reputable pretence, and who
provide for their duty in a liberal way.
among the great of the diocesan sinner in this respect be Dr
Vesey Stanhope. year have now pass since he have do a day's
duty; and yet there be no reason against his do duty except a
want of inclination on his own part. he hold a prebendal stall in
the diocese; one of the good residence in the close; and the two
large rectory of Crabtree Canonicorum, and Stogpingum. indeed, he
have the cure of three parish, for that of Eiderdown be join to
Stogpingum. he have reside in Italy for twelve year. his first
go there have be attribute to a sore throat; and that sore
throat, though never repeat in any violent manner have stand he
in such stead, that it have enable he to live in easy idleness
ever since.
he have now be summon home,--not indeed, with rough violence, or
by any peremptory command, but by a mandate which he find himself
unable to disregard. Mr Slope have write to he by the bishop's
desire. in the first place, the bishop much want the valuable
co-operation of Dr Vesey Stanhope in the diocese; in the next, the
bishop think it his imperative duty to become personally
acquaint with the most conspicuous of his diocesan clergy; then
the bishop think it essentially necessary for Dr Stanhope's own
interest, that Dr Stanhope should, at any rate for a time, return
to Barchester; and lastly, it be say that so strong a feeling be
at the present moment evince by the hierarch of the church with
reference to the absence of its clerical member, that it behove
Dr Vesey Stanhope not to allow his name to stand among those which
would probably in a few month be submit to the council of the
nation.
there be something so ambiguously frightful in this last threat
that Dr Stanhope determine to spend two or three summer month at
his residence in Barchester. his rectory be inhabit by his
curate, and he feel himself from disuse to be unfit for parochial
duty; but his prebendal home be keep empty for he, and he think
it probable that he might be able now and again to preach a
prebendal sermon. he arrive, therefore, with all his family at
Barchester, and he and they must be introduce to my reader.
the great family characteristic of the Stanhopes might probably be
say to be heartlessness; but the want of feeling be, in most of
they, accompany by so great an amount of good nature that their
neighbour fail to perceive how indifferent to they be the
happiness and well-being of those around they. the Stanhopes would
visit you in your sickness (provide it be not contagious), would
bring you orange, french novel, and the last new bit of scandal,
and then hear of your death or your recovery with an equally
indifferent composure. their conduct to each other be the same as
to the world; they bore and forbore: and there be sometimes, as
will be see, much necessity for forbearing: but their love among
themselves rarely reach above this. it be astonish how much
each of the family be able to do, and how much each do, to
prevent the well-being of the other four.
for there be five in all; the doctor, namely, and Mrs Stanhope,
two daughter, and one son. the doctor, perhaps, be the least
singular and most estimable of they all, and yet such good
quality as he possess be all negative. he be a good look
rather plethoric gentleman of about sixty year of age. his hair
be snow white, very plentiful, and somewhat like wool of the
fine description. his whisker be large and very white, and
give to his face the appearance of a benevolent sleepy old lion.
his dress be always unexceptionable. although he have live so many
year in Italy it be invariably of a decent clerical hue, but it
never be hyperclerical. he be a man not give to much talking,
but what little he do say be generally well say. his reading
seldom go beyond romance and poetry of the light and not
always most moral description. he be thoroughly a bon vivant; an
accomplish judge of wine, though he never drink to excess; and a
most inexorable critic in all affair touch the kitchen. he have
have much to forgive in his own family, since a family have grow up
around he, and have forgive everything--except inattention to his
dinner. his weakness in that respect be now fully understand, and
his temper but seldom try. as Dr Stanhope be a clergyman, it may
be suppose that his religious conviction make up a considerable
part of his character; but this be not so. that he have religious
conviction must be believe; but he rarely obtrude they, even on
his child. this abstinence on his part be not systematic, but
very characteristic of the man. it be not that he have
predetermine never to influence their thought; but he be so
habitually idle that his time for do so have never come till the
opportunity for do so be go forever. whatever conviction the
father may have have, the child be at any rate but indifferent
member of the church from which he draw his income.
such be Dr Stanhope. the feature of Mrs Stanhope's character be
even less plainly marked than those of her lord. the far niente of
her italian life have enter into her very soul, and bring she to
regard a state of inactivity as the only earthly good. in manner
and appearance she be exceedingly prepossess. she have be a
beauty, and even now, at fifty-five, she be a handsome woman. her
dress be always perfect: she never dress but once in the day,
and never appear till between three and four; but when she do
appear, she appear at her good. whether the toil rest partly
with she, or wholly with she handmaid, it be not for such a one as
the author to imagine. the structure of her attire be always
elaborate, and yet never over laboured. she be rich in apparel,
but not bedizen with finery; her ornament be costly, rare, and
such as could not fail to attract notice, but they do not look as
though wear with that purpose. she well know the great
architectural secret of decorate her construction, and never
condescend to construct a decoration. but when we have say that
Mrs Stanhope know how to dress, and use her knowledge daily, we
have say all. other purpose in life she have none. it be
something, indeed, that she do not interfere with the purpose of
other. in early life she have undergo great trial with reference
to the doctor's dinner; but for the last ten or twelve year her
eld daughter Charlotte have take that labour off her hand, and
she have have little to trouble her;--little, that is, till the edict
for this terrible english journey have go forth; since, then,
indeed, her life have be laborious enough. for such a one, the
toil of be carry from the shore of Como to the city of
Barchester be more than labour enough, let the care of the
carrier be ever so vigilant. Mrs Stanhope have be oblige to have
every one of her dress take in from the effect of the journey.
Charlotte Stanhope be at this time about thirty-five year old;
and, whatever may have be her fault, she have none of those which
belong particularly to old young lady. she neither dress young,
nor talk young, nor indeed look young. she appear to be
perfectly content with her time of life, and in no way affect the
grace of youth. she be a fine young woman; and have she be a man,
would have be a very fine young man. all that be do in the
house, and that be not do by servant, be do by she. she give
the order, pay the bill, hire and dismiss the domestic, make
the tea, carve the meat, and manage everything in the Stanhope
household. she, and she alone, could ever induce her father to look
into the state of his worldly concern. she, and she alone, could
in any degree control the absurdity of her sister. she, and she
alone, prevent the whole family from fall into utter disrepute
and beggary. it be by her advice that they now find themselves
very unpleasantly situate in Barchester.
so far, the character of Charlotte Stanhope be not unprepossesse.
but it remain to be say, that the influence which she have in her
family, though it have be use to a certain extent for their
worldly well-being, have not be use to their real benefit, as it
might have be. she have aid her father in his indifference to
his professional duty, counsel he that his living be as
much as his individual property as the estate of his eld brother
be the property of that worthy peer. she have for year past
stifle every little rise wish for a return to England which the
doctor have from time to time express. she have encourage she
mother in her idleness in order that she herself might be mistress
and manager of the Stanhope household. she have encourage and
foster the folly of her sister, though she be always willing,
and often able, to protect she from their probable result. she have
do she good, and have thoroughly succeed in spoil she
brother, and turn he loose upon the world an idle man without a
profession, and without a shilling that he could call his own.
Miss Stanhope be a clever woman, able to talk on most subject,
and quite indifferent as to what the subject be. she pride
herself on her freedom from english prejudice, and she might have
add, from feminine delicacy. on religion she be a pure
freethinker, and with much want of true affection, delight to
throw out her own view before the troubled mind of her father. to
have shake what remain of his Church of England faith would have
gratify she much; but the idea of his abandon his preferment
in the church have never once present itself to her mind. how
could he indeed, when he have no income from any other source?
but the two most prominent member of the family still remain to be
describe. the second child have be christen Madeline, and have
be a great beauty. we need not say have be, for she be never
more beautiful than at the time of which we write, though her
person for many year have be disfigure by an accident. it be
unnecessary that we should give in detail the early history of
Madeline Stanhope. she have go to Italy when seventeen year of
age, and have be allow to make the most of her surpass beauty
in the saloon of Milan, and among the crowded villa along the
shore of the Lake of Como. she have become famous for adventure in
which her character be just not lose, and have destroy the heart
of a dozen cavalier without once be touch in her own. blood
have flow in quarrel about her charm, and she hear of these
encounter with pleasurable excitement. it have be tell of she
that on one occasion she have stand by in the disguise of a page,
and have see her lover fall.
as be so often the case, she have marry the very bad of those
who seek her hand. why she have choose Paulo Neroni, a man of no
birth and no property, a mere captain in the pope's guard, one who
have come up to Milan either simply as an adventurer or as a spy, a
man of harsh temper and oily manner, mean in figure, swarthy in
face, and so false in word as to be hourly detect, need not now
be tell. when the moment for do so come, she have probably no
alternative. he, at any rate, have become her husband; and after a
prolong honeymoon among the lake, they have go together to
Rome, the papal captain have vainly endeavour to induce his
wife to remain behind he.
six month afterwards she arrive at her father's house a cripple
and a mother. she have arrive without even notice, with hardly
clothe to cover she, and without one of those many ornament which
have grace her bridal trousseaux. her baby be in the arm of a
poor girl from Milan, whom she have take in exchange for the Roman
maid who have accompany she thus far, and who have then, as she
mistress say, become homesick and have return. it be clear that
the lady have determine that there should be no witness to tell
story of her life in Rome.
she have fall, she say, in ascend a ruin and have fatally
injure the sinew of her knee; so fatally, that when she stand she
lose eight inch of her accustomed height; so fatally, that when
she essay to move, she could only drag herself painfully along,
with protrude hip and extended foot in a manner less graceful than
that of a hunchback. she have consequently make up her mind, once
and for ever, that she would never stand, and never attempt to move
herself.
story be not slow to follow she, aver that she have be
cruelly ill-use by Neroni, and that to his violence have she owe
her accident. be that as it may, little have be say about her
husband, but that little have make it clearly intelligible to the
family that Signor Neroni be to be see and hear of no more.
there be no question as to re-admit the poor ill-use beauty
to her old family right, no question as to adopt her infant
daughter, beneath the Stanhope roof tree. though heartless, the
Stanhopes be not selfish. the two be take in, pet, make
much of, for a time all but adore, and then feel by the two
parent to be great nuisance in the house. but in the house the
lady be, and there she remain, have her own way, though that
way be not very comfortable with the customary usage of an
english clergyman.
Madame Neroni, though force to give up all motion in the world,
have no intention whatever of give up the world itself. the beauty
of her face be uninjured, and that beauty be of a peculiar kind.
her copious rich brown hair be wear in Grecian bandeaux round her
head, display as much as possible of her forehead and cheek.
her forehead, though rather low, be very beautiful from its
perfect contour and pearly whiteness. her eye be long and large,
and marvellously bright; might I venture to say, bright as
Lucifer's, I should perhaps well express the depth of their
brilliancy. they be dreadful eye to look at, such as would
absolutely deter any man of quiet mind and easy spirit from
attempt a passage of arm with such foe. there be talent in
they, and the fire of passion and the play of wit, but there be no
love. Cruelty be there instead, and courage, a desire for
masterhood, cunning, and a wish for mischief. and yet, as eye,
they be very beautiful. the eyelash be long and perfect, and
the long steady unabashed gaze, with which she would look into the
face of her admirer, fascinated while it frighten he. she be a
basilisk from whom an ardent lover of beauty could make no escape.
her nose and mouth more so at twenty-eight than they have be at
eighteen. what wonder that with such charm still glow in her
face, and with such deformity destroy her figure, she should
resolve to be see, but only to be see recline on a sofa.
her resolve have not be carry out without difficulty. she have
still frequent the opera at Milan; she have still be see
occasionally in the saloon of the noblesse; she have cause herself
to be carry in and out from her carriage, and that in such a
manner as in no wise to disturb her charm, disarrange her dress,
or expose her deformity. her sister always accompany she and a
maid, a manservant also, and on state occasion, two. it be
impossible that her purpose could have be achieve with less: and
yet, poor as she be, she have achieve her purpose. and then again
the more dissolute italian youth of Milan frequent the Stanhope
villa and surround her couch, not greatly to her father's
satisfaction. sometimes his spirit would rise, a dark spot would
show itself on his cheek, and he would rebel; but Charlotte would
assuage he with some peculiar triumph of her culinary art, and all
again would be smooth for a while.
Madeline affect all manner of rich and quaint device in the
garniture of her room, her person, and her feminine belonging. in
nothing be this more apparent than in the visit card which she
have prepare for her use. for such an article one would say that
she, in her present state, could have but small need, see how
improbable it be that she should make a morning call; but not such
be her own opinion. her card be surround by a deep border of
gilding; on this she have imprint, in three lines:-
La Signora Madeline
Vesey Neroni.
- Nata Stanhope.
and over the name she have a bright gilt coronet, which certainly
look very magnificent. how she have come to concoct such a name
for herself it would be difficult to explain. her father have be
christen vesey, as another man be christen Thomas; and she have
no more right to assume it than would have the daughter of a Mr
Josiah Jones to call herself Mrs Josiah Smith, on marry a man of
the latter name. the gold coronet be equally out of place, and
perhaps insert with even less excuse. Paul Neroni have not the
faint title to call himself a scion of even italian nobility.
have the pair meet in England Neroni would probably have be a
count; but they have meet in Italy, and any such pretence on his part
would have be simply ridiculous. a coronet, however, be a pretty
ornament, and if it could solace a poor cripple to have such on her
card, who could begrudge it to she?
of her husband, or of his individual family, she never speak; but
with her admirer she would often allude in a mysterious way to she
married life and isolated state, and, point to her daughter,
would call she the last of the blood of the emperor, thus
refer Neroni's extraction to the old roman family from which
the bad of the Caesars sprang.
the 'Signora' be not without talent, and not without a certain
sort of industry; she be an indomitable letter writer, and her
letter be worth the postage: they be full of wit, mischief,
satire, love, latitudinarian philosophy, free religion, and,
sometimes, alas! loose ribaldry. the subject, however, depend
entirely on the recipient, and she be prepared to correspond with
any one, but moral young lady or stiff old woman. she write also
a kind of poetry, generally in italian, and short romance,
generally in French. she read much of a desultory sort of
literature, and as a modern linguist have really make great
proficiency. such be the lady who have now come to wound the heart
of the man of Barchester.
Ethelbert Stanhope be in some respect like his young sister,
but he be less inestimable as a man than she be as a woman. his
great fault be an entire absence of that principle which should
have induce he, as the son of a man without fortune, to earn his
own bread. many attempt have be make to get he to do so, but
these have all be frustrate, not so much by idleness on his part,
as by a disinclination to exert himself in any way not to his
taste. he have be educate at Eton, and have be intend for the
Church, but have leave Cambridge in disgust after a single term, and
notify to his father his intention to study for the bar.
preparatory to that, he think it well that he should attend a
german university, and consequently go to Leipzig. there he
remain two year, and bring away a knowledge of German and a
taste for the fine art. he still, however, intend himself for
the bar, take chamber, engage himself to sit at the foot of a
learn pundit, and spend a season in London. he there find that
all his aptitude incline he to the life of an artist, and he
determine to live by paint. with this object he retire to
Milan, and have himself rig out for Rome. as a painter he might
have earn his bread, for he want only diligence to excel; but
when at Rome his mind be carry away by other thing: he soon
write home for money, say that he have be convert to the
Mother Church, that he be already an acolyte of the Jesuits, and
that he be about to start with other to Palestine on a mission
for convert Jews. he do go to Judea, but be unable to
convert the Jews, be convert by they. he again write home, to
say that Moses be the only giver of perfect law to the world,
that the coming of the true Messiah be at hand, that great thing
be do in Palestine, and that he have meet one of the family of
Sidonis, a most remarkable man, who be now on his way to Western
Europe, and whom he have induce to deviate from his route with the
object of call at the Stanhope villa. Ethelbert then express
his hope that his mother and sister would listen to this wonderful
prophet. his father he know could not do so from pecuniary
consideration. this Sidonia, however, do not take so strong a
fancy to he as another of that family once do to a young English
nobleman. at least he provide he with no hope of gold as large as
lion; so that the Judaised Ethelbert be again oblige to draw on
the revenue of the Christian Church.
it be needless to tell how the father swear that he would send no
more money and receive no Jew; nor how Charlotte declare that
Ethelbert could not be leave penniless in Jerusalem; and how 'La
Signora Neroni' resolve to have Sidonia at her foot. the money be
send, and the Jew do come. the Jew do come, but he be not at all
to the taste of 'la Signora'. he be a dirty little old man, and
though he have provide no golden lion, he have, it seem, relieve
young Stanhope's necessity. he positively refuse to leave the
villa till he get a bill from the doctor on his London banker.
Ethelbert do not long remain a Jew. he soon reappear at the
villa without prejudice on the subject of his religion, and with a
firm resolve to achieve fame and fortune as a sculptor. he bring
with he some model which he have originate at Rome, and which
really give much fair promise that his father be induce to go to
further expense in further these view. Ethelbert open an
establishment, or rather take lodging and workshop, at Carrara,
and there spoil much marble, and make some few pretty image.
since that period, now four year ago, he have alternate between
Carrara and the villa, but his sojourn at the workshop become
short and short, and those at the villa long and long.
'twas no wonder; for Carrara be not a spot in which an Englishman
would like to dwell.
when the family start for England he have resolve not to be leave
behind, and with the assistance of his eld sister have earn his
point against his father's wish. it be necessary, he say, that
he should come to England for order. how otherwise be he to bring
his profession to account?
in personal appearance Ethelbert Stanhope be the most singular of
being. he be certainly very handsome. he have his sister
Madeline's eye without their stare, and without their hard cunning
cruel firmness. they be also very much light, and of so light
and clear a blue as to make his face remarkable, if nothing else
do so. on enter a room with he, Ethelbert's blue eye would be
the first thing you would see, and on leave it almost the last
thing you would forget. his light hair be very long and silky,
come down over his coat. his beard have be prepare in the holy
land, and be patriarchal. he never shave, and rarely trim it.
it be glossy, soft, clean, and altogether not unprepossesse. it
be such that lady might desire to reel it off and work it into
their pattern in lieu of floss silk. his complexion be fair and
almost pink, he be small in height, and slender in limb, but
well-make, and his voice be of particular sweetness.manner and
dress he be equally remarkable. he have none of the mauvaise honte
of an Englishman. he require no introduction to make himself
agreeable to any person. he habitually address stranger, lady
as well as man, without any such formality, and in do so never
seem to meet with rebuke. his costume cannot be describe,
because it be so various; but it be always totally opposed in
every principle of colour and construction to the dress of those
with whom he for the time consort.
he be habitually addict to make love to lady, and do so
without scruple of conscience, or any idea that such a practice be
amiss. he have no heart to touch himself, and be literally unaware
that humanity be subject to such infliction. he have not think
much about it; but, have he be ask, would have say, that
ill-treat a lady's heart mean injure her promotion in the
world. his principle therefore forbid he to pay attention to a
girl, if he think any man be present whom it might suit she to
marry. in this manner, his good nature frequently interfere with
his amusement; but he have no other motive in abstain from the
full declaration of love to every girl that please his eye.
Bertie Stanhope, as he be generally call, be, however, popular
with both sex; and with Italians as well as English. his circle
of acquaintance be very large, and embrace people of all sort.
he have not respect for rank, and no aversion to those below he. he
have live on familiar term with english peer, german shopkeeper,
and roman priest. all people be nearly alike to he. he be
above, or rather below, all prejudice. no virtue could charm he,
no vice shock he. he have about he a natural good manner, which
seem to qualify he for the high circle, and yet he be never
out of place in the low. he have no principle, no regard for
other, no self-respect, no desire to be other than a drone in a
hive, if only he could, as a drone, get what honey be sufficient
for he. of honey, in his latter day, it may probably be presage,
that he will have but short allowance.
such be the family of the Stanhopes, who, at this period, suddenly
join themselves to the ecclesiastical circle of Barchester close.
any stranger union, it would be impossible perhaps to conceive. and
it be not as though they all fall down into the cathedral
precinct hitherto unknown and untalked of. in such case no
amalgamation would have be at all probable between the new comer
and either the Proudie set or the grantly set. but such be far
from be the case. the Stanhopes be all know by name in
Barchester, and Barchester be prepared to receive they with open
arm. the doctor be one of the prebendary, one of her rector,
one of her pillar of strength; and be, moreover, count on, as a
sure ally, both by Proudies and Grantlys.
he himself be the brother of one peer, and his wife be the sister
of another--and both these peer be lord of whiggish tendency,
with whom the new bishop have some sort of alliance. this be
sufficient to give to Mr Slope high hope that he might enlist Dr
Stanhope on his side, before his enemy could out-manoeuvre he.
on the other hand, the old dean have many many year ago, in the
day of the doctor's clerical energy, be instrumental in
assist he in his view as to preferment; and many many year
ago also, the two doctor, Stanhope and Grantly, have, as young
parson, be joyous together in the common room of Oxford. Dr
grantly, consequently, do not doubt but that the new comer would
range himself under his banner.
Little do any of they dream of what ingredient the Stanhope
family be now compose.
CHAPTER X
MRS PROUDIE's reception--COMMENCED
the bishop and his wife have only spend three or four day in
Barchester on the occasion of their first visit. his lordship have,
as we have see, take his seat on his throne; but his demeanour
there, into which it have be his intention to infuse much
hierarchical dignity, have be a good deal disarrange by the
audacity of his chaplain's sermon. he have hardly dare to look his
clergy in the face, and to declare by the severity of his
countenance that in truth he mean all that his factotum be say
on his behalf; nor yet do he dare throw Mr Slope over, and show to
those around he that he be no party to the sermon, and would
resent it.
he have accordingly bless his people in a shambling manner, not at
all to his own satisfaction, and have walk back to his palace with
his mind very doubtful as to what he would say to his chaplain on
the subject. he do not remain long in doubt. he have hardly doff
his lawn when the partner of all his toil enter his study, and
exclaim even before she have seat herself--
'Bishop, do you ever hear a more sublime, more spirit-move, more
appropriate discourse than that?'
'well, my love; ha-hum-he!' the bishop do not know what to say.
'I hope, my lord, you donot mean to say you disapprove?'
there be a look about the lady's eye which do not admit of my
lord's disapprove at that moment. he feel that if he intend to
disapprove, it must be now or never; but he also feel that it could
not be now. it be not in he to say to the wife of his bosom that
Mr Slope's sermon be ill-time, impertinent and vexatious.
'no, no,' reply the bishop. 'no, I cannot say I disapprove--a very
clever sermon and very well intend, and I dare say will do a
great deal of good.' this last praise be add, see that what
he have already say by no mean satisfy Mrs Proudie.
'I hope it will,' say she. 'I be sure it be well deserve. do
you ever in your life, bishop, hear anything so like play-act as
the way in which Mr Harding sing the litany? I shall beg Mr Slope
to continue a course of sermon on the subject till all that be
alter. we will have at any rate, in our cathedral, a decent,
godly, modest morning service. there must be no more play-act
here now;' and so the lady ring for lunch.
this bishop know more about cathedral and dean, and precentor
and church service than his wife do, and also more of the
bishop's power. but he think it well at present to let the
subject drop.
'my dear,' say he, 'I think we must go back to London on Tuesday.
I find that my staying here will be very inconvenient to the
Government.'
the bishop know that to this proposal his wife would not object;
and he also feel that by thus retreat from the ground of battle,
the heat of the fight might be get over in his absence.
'Mr Slope will remain here, of course,' say the lady.
'oh, of course,' say the bishop.
thus, after less than a week's sojourn in his palace, do the
bishop fly from Barchester; nor do he return to it for two month,
the London season be then over. during that time Mr Slope be
not idle, but he do not again assay to preach in the cathedral. in
answer to Mrs Proudie's letter, advise a course of sermon, he
have plead that he would at any rate wish to put off such an
undertaking till she be there to hear they.
he have employ his time in consolidate a Proudie and Slope
party--or rather a Slope and Proudie party, and he have not employ
his time in vain. he do not meddle with the dean and chapter,
except by give they little teasing intimation of the bishop's
wish about this and the bishop's feeling about that, in a manner
which be to they sufficiently annoying, but which they could not
resent. he preach once or twice in a distant church in the
suburb of the city, but make no allusion to the cathedral service.
he commence the establishment of the 'Bishop of Barchester's
Sabbath-day school,' give notice of a propose 'Bishop of
Barchester Young Men's Sabbath Evening Lecture Room,'--and write
three or four letter to the manager of the Barchester branch
railway, inform he how anxious the bishop be that the Sunday
train should be discontinue.
at the end of two month, however, the bishop and the lady
reappear; and as a happy harbinger of their return, herald
their advent by the promise of an evening party on the large
scale. the ticket of invitation be send out from London--they
be date from Bruton Street, and be dispatch by the odious
Sabbath-break railway, in a huge brown paper parcel to Mr Slope.
everybody call himself a gentleman, or herself a lady, within
the city of Barchester, and a circle of two mile round it, be
include. ticket be send to all the diocesan clergy, and also to
many other person of priestly note, of whose absence the bishop,
or at least the bishop's wife, feel tolerably confident. it be
intended, however, to be a thronged and noticeable affair, and
preparation be make for receive some hundred.
and now there arise considerable agitation among the Grantleyites
whether or not they would attend the bidding. the first feeling
with they all be to send the brief excuse both for themselves
and their wife and daughter. but by degree policy prevail over
passion. the archdeacon perceive that he would be make a false
step if he allow the cathedral clergy to give the bishop just
ground of umbrage. they all meet in conclave and agree to go. the
old dean would crawl in, if it be but for half an hour. the
chancellor, treasurer, archdeacon, prebendary, and minor canon
would all go, and would take their wife. Mr Harding be especially
bid to go, resolve in his heart to keep himself remove from
Mrs Proudie. and Mrs Bold be determined to go, though assure by
her father that there be no necessity for such a sacrifice on her
part. when all Barchester be to be there, neither Eleanor nor Mary
Bold understand why they should stay away. have they not be
invite separately? and have not a separate little note from the
