chaplain couch in the most respectful language, be enclose
with the huge episcopal card?
and the Stanhopes would be there, one and all. even the lethargic
mother would so far bestir herself on such an occasion. they have
only just arrive. the card be at the residence wait for they.
no one in Barchester have see they; and what well opportunity
could they have of show themselves to the Barchester world? some
few old friend, such as the archdeacon and his wife, have call,
and have find the doctor and his eld daughter; but the elite of
the family be not yet know.
the doctor indeed wish in his heart to prevent the signora from
accept the bishop's invitation; but she herself have fully
determine that she would accept it. if her father be ashamed of
have his daughter carry into a bishop's palace, she have no such
feeling.
'indeed, I shall,' she say to her sister who have greatly
endeavour to dissuade she, by say that the company would
consist wholly of parson and parson' wife. 'parson, I suppose,
be much the same as other man, if you strip they of their black
coat; and as to their wife, I dare say they willnot trouble I. you
may tell papa I donot mean to be leave at home.'
Papa be tell, and feel that he could do nothing but yield. he also
feel that it be useless of he now to be ashamed of his child.
such as they be, they have become such under his auspex; as he
have make his bed, so he must lie upon it; as he have sow his seed,
so must he reap his corn. he do not indeed utter such reflection
in such language, but such be the gist of his thought. it be not
because Madeline be a cripple that he shrink from see she make
one of the bishop's guest; but because he know that she would
practise her accustomed lur, and behave herself in a way that
could not fail of be distasteful to the propriety of
Englishwomen. these thing have annoy but not shock he in
Italy. there they have shock no one; but here in Barchester, here
among his fellow parson, he be ashamed that they should be see.
such have be his feeling, but he repress they. what if his
brother clergyman be shock! they could not take it from his
preferment because the manner of his married daughter be too
free.
La Signora Neroni have, at any rate, no fear that she would shock
anybody. her ambition be to create a sensation, to have parson at
her foot, see that the manhood of Barchester consist mainly of
parson, and to send, if possible, every parson's wife home with a
green fit of jealousy. none could be too old for she, and hardly
any too young. none too sanctify, and none too worldly. she be
quite prepared to entrap the bishop himself, and then to turn up
her nose at the bishop's wife. she do not doubt of success, for
she have always succeed; but one thing be absolutely necessary,
she must secure the entire use of a sofa.
the card send to Dr and Mrs Stanhope and family, have be send in
an envelope, have on the cover Mr Slope's name. the signora soon
learn that Mrs Proudie be not yet at the palace, and that the
chaplain be manage everything. it be much more in her line to
apply to he than to the lady, and she accordingly write to he the
prettiest little billet in the world. in five line she explain
everything, declare how impossible it be for she not to be
desirous to make the acquaintance of such person as the bishop of
Barchester and his wife, and she might add also of Mr Slope,
depict her own grievous state, and conclude by be assure
that Mrs Proudie would forgive her extreme hardihood in petitioning
to be allow to be carry to a sofa. she then enclose one of her
beautiful card. in return she receive as polite an answer from Mr
Slope--a sofa should be keep in the large drawing-room, immediately
at the top of the grand stair, especially for her use.
and now the day of the party have arrive. the bishop and his wife
come down from town, only on the morning of the eventful day, as
behove such great people to do; but Mr Slope have toil day and
night to see that everything should be in right order. there have
be much to do. no company have be see in the palace since
heaven know when. new furniture have be require, new pot and
pan, new cup and saucer, new dish and plate. Mrs Proudie have
first declare that she would condescend to nothing so vulgar as
eat and drink; but Mr Slope have talk, or rather write she
out of economy!--bishop should be give to hospitality, and
hospitality mean eat and drink. so the supper be concede;
the guest, however, be to stand as they consume it.
there be four room open into each other on the first floor of
the house, which be denominate the drawing-room, the
reception-room, and Mrs Proudie's boudoir. in olden day one of
these have be Bishop Grantly's bed-room, and another his common,
sit-room and study. the present bishop, however, have be move
down into a back parlour, and have be give to understand that he
could very well receive his clergy in the dining-room, should they
arrive in too large a flock to be admit to his small sanctum. he
have be unwilling to yield, but after a short debate have yield.
Mrs Proudie's heart beat high as she inspect her suite of room.
they be really very magnificent, or at least would be so by
candlelight; and they have nevertheless be get up with commendable
economy. large room when full of people, and full of light look
well, because they be large, and be full, and be light. small
room be those which require costly fitting and rich furniture.
Mrs Proudie know this, and make the most of it; she have therefore a
huge gas lamp with a dozen burner hang from each of the
ceiling.
People be to arrive at ten, supper be to last from twelve to
one, and at half-past one everybody be to be go. carriage be
to come in at the gate in the town and depart at the gate outside.
they be desire to take up at a quarter before one. it be
manage excellently, and Mr Slope be invaluable.
at half-past nine the bishop and his wife and their three daughter
enter the great reception-room, and very grand and very solemn
they be. Mr Slope be down-stair give the last order about
the wine. he well understand that curate and country vicar with
their belonging do not require so generous an article as the
dignitary of the close. there be a useful gradation in such
thing, and Marsala at 20 a dozen do very well for the exterior
supplementary table in the corner.
'Bishop,' say the lady, as his lordship sit himself down, 'donot
sit on that sofa, if you please; it be to be keep separate for a
lady.'
the bishop jump up and seat himself on a cane-bottom chair.
'a lady?' he inquire meekly; 'do you mean one particular lady, my
dear?'
'yes, bishop, one particular lady,' say his wife, disdain to
explain.
'she have get no leg, papa,' say the young daughter, titter.
'no leg!' say the bishop, open his eye.
'Nonsense, Netta, what stuff you talk,' say Olivia. 'she have get
leg, but she cannot use they. she have always to be keep lie down,
and three or four man carry she about everywhere.'
'law, how odd!' say Augusta. 'always carry about by four man!
Ibe quite sure I wouldnot like it. be I right behind, mama? I feel
as if I be open;' and she turn she back to her anxious parent.
'open! to be sure you be,' say she, 'and a yard of petticoat
string hang out. I donot know why I pay such high wage to Mrs
Richards, if she cannot take the trouble to see whether or no you
be fit to be look at,' and Mrs Proudie poke the string here,
and twitch the dress there, and give her daughter a shove and a
shake, and then pronounce it all right.
'but,' rejoin the bishop, who be die with curiosity about the
mysterious lady and her leg, 'who be it that be to have the sofa?
what be her name, Netta?'
a thunder rap at the front door interrupt the conversation.
Mrs Proudie stand up and shake herself gently, and touch her cap
on each side as she look in the mirror. each of the girl stand
on tiptoe, and re-arrange the bow on their bosom; and Mr Slope
rush up stair three step at a time.
'but who be it, Netta?' whisper the bishop to his young
daughter.
'La Signora Madeline Vesey Neroni,' whisper back the daughter;
'and mind you donot let any one sit upon the sofa.'
'La Signora Madeline Vicinironi!' mutter, to himself, the
bewildered prelate. have he be tell that the Begum of Oude be to
be there, or Queen Pomara of the Western Isles, he could not have
be more astonished. La Signora Madeline Vicinironi, who, have
no leg to stand on, have bespeak a sofa in his drawing-room!--who
could she be? he however could now make no further inquiry, as Dr
and Mrs Stanhope be announce. they have be send on out of the
way a little before the time, in order that the signora might have
plenty of time to get herself conveniently pack into the
carriage.
the bishop be all smile for the prebendary's wife, and the
bishop's wife be all smile for the prebendary. Mr Slope be
present, and be delighted to make the acquaintance of one of
whom he have hear so much. the doctor bow very low, and then
look as though he could not return the compliment as regard Mr
Slope, of whom, indeed, he have hear nothing. the doctor, in spite
of his long absence, know an english gentleman when he see he.
and then the guest come in shoal; Mr and Mrs Quiverful and their
three grown daughter. Mr and Mrs Chadwick and their three
daughter. the burly chancellor and his wife and clerical son from
Oxford. the meagre little doctor without encumbrance. Mr Harding
with Eleanor and Miss Bold. the dean lean on a gaunt spinster,
his only child now live with he, a lady very learn in stone,
fern, plant, and vermin, and who have write a book about petal.
a wonderful woman in her way be Miss Trefoil. Mr Finnie, the
attorney, with his wife, be to be see, much to the dismay of many
who have never meet he in a drawing-room before. the five Barchester
doctor be all there, and old Scalpen, the retire apothecary and
toothdrawer, who be first teach to consider himself as belong
to the high order by the receipt of the bishop's card. then come
the archdeacon and his wife, with their eld daughter Griselda, a
slim pale retire girl of seventeen, who keep close to her mother,
and look out on the world with quiet watchful eye, one who give
promise of much beauty when time should have ripen it.
and so the room become full, and knot be form, and every new
comer pay his respect to my lord and pass on, not presume to

hand very heartily with Dr Stanhope, and Mrs Grantly seat

regulated grace, measure out the quantity of she favour to the

'why donot she come?' say the bishop to himself. his mind be so
preoccupy with the signora, that he hardly remember how to
behave himself en bishop.
at last a carriage dash up to the hall step with a very
different manner of approach from that of any other vehicle that
have be there that evening. a perfect commotion take place. the
doctor, who hear it as he be stand in the drawing-room, know
that his daughter be come, and retire to the furth corner,
where he might not see her entrance. Mrs Proudie park herself up,
feel that some important piece of business be in hand. the
bishop be instinctively aware, that La Signora Vicinironi be come
at last, and Mr Slope hurry to the hall to give his assistance.
he be, however, nearly knock down and trample on by the cortege
that he encounter on the hall step. he get himself pick up as
well as he could, and follow the cortege up stair. the signora
be carry head foremost, her head be the care of her brother
and an italian man-servant who be accustom to the work; her foot
be in the care of the lady's maid and the lady's italian page;
and Charlotte Stanhope follow to see that all be do with due
grace and decorum. in this manner they climb easily into the
drawing-room, and a broad way through the crowd have be open,
the signora rest safely on her couch. she have send a servant
beforehand to learn whether it be a right or a left hand sofa, for
it require that she should dress accordingly, particularly as
regard her bracelet.
and very become her dress be. it be white velvet, without any
other garniture than rich white lace work with pearl across her
bosom, and the same round the armlet of her dress. across her brow
she wear a band of red velvet, on the centre of which shine a
magnificent Cupid in mosaic, the tint of whose wing be of the
most lovely azure, and the colour of his chubby cheek the clear
pink. on the one arm which her position require she to expose she
wear three magnificent bracelet, each of different stone. beneath
she on the sofa, and over the cushion and head of it, be spread a
crimson silk mantle or shawl, which go under her whole body and
conceal her foot. dress as she be and look as she do, so
beautiful and yet so motionless, with the pure brilliancy of her
white dress bring out and strengthen by the colour beneath it,
with that lovely head, and those large bold bright star eye, it
be impossible that either man or woman should do other than look
at she.
neither man nor woman for some minute do do other.
her bearer too be worthy of note. the three servant be
italian, and though perhaps not peculiar in their own country, be
very much so in the palace at Barchester. the man, especially
attract notice, and create a doubt in the mind of some whether
he be a friend or a domestic. the same doubt be feel as to
Ethelbert. the man be attire in a loose-fitting common black
cloth morning coat. he have a jaunty well-please clean face, on
which no atom of beard appear, and he wear round his neck a loose
black silk neckhandkerchief. the bishop assay to make he a bow,
but the man, who be well-train, take no notice of he, and
walk out of the room, quite at his ease, follow by the woman
and the boy.
Ethelbert Stanhope be dress in light blue from head to foot. he
have on the loose possible blue coat, cut square like a shooting
coat, and very short. it be line with silk of azure blue. he have
on a blue satin waistcoat, a blue handkerchief which be fasten
beneath his throat with a coral ring, and very loose blue trouser
which almost conceal his foot. his soft glossy beard be soft
and more glossy than ever.
the bishop, who have make one mistake, think that he also be a
servant, and therefore try to make way for he to pass. but
Ethelbert soon correct the error.
CHAPTER XI
MRS PROUDIE's RECEPTION--CONCLUDED
'Bishop of Barchester, I presume?' say Bertie Stanhope, put
out his hand, frankly; 'I be delighted to make your acquaintance.
we be in rather close quarter here, a'nt we?'
in truth they be. they have be crowd up behind the head of the
sofa: the bishop in wait to receive his guest, and the other in
carry she; and they now have hardly room to move themselves.
the bishop give his hand quickly, and make a little studied bow,
and be delighted to make--. he couldnot go on, for he do not know
whether his friend be a signor, or a count, or a prince.
'my sister really put you all to great trouble,' say Bertie.
'not at all!' the bishop be delighted to have the opportunity of
welcome the Signora Vicinironi--so at least he say--and
attempt to force his way round to the front of the sofa. he have,
at any rate, learn that his strange guest be brother and
sister. the man, he presume, must be Signor Vicinironi--or count,
or prince, as it might be. it be wonderful what good English he
speak. there be just a twang of foreign accent, and no more.
'do you like Barchester on the whole?' ask Bertie.
the bishop, look dignify, say that he do like Barchester.
'youhave not be here very long, I believe,' say Bertie.
'no--not long,' say the bishop, and try again to make his way
between the back of the sofa and a heavy rector, who be star
over it at the grimace of the signora.
'you benot a bishop before, be you?'
Dr Proudie explain that this be the first diocese he have hold.
'ah--I think so,' say Bertie; 'but you be change about
sometimes, a'nt you?'
'translation be occasionally make,' say Dr Proudie; 'but not so
frequently as in former day.
'theyhave cut they all down to pretty nearly the same figure,
havenot they?' say Bertie.
to this the bishop could not bring himself to make any answer, but
again try to move the rector.
'but the work, I suppose, be different?' continue Bertie. 'be
there much to do here at Barchester?' this be say exactly in the
tone that a young Admiralty clerk might use in ask the same
question of a brother acolyte in the Treasury.
'the work of a bishop of the Church of England,' say Dr Proudie,
with considerable dignity, 'be not easy. the responsibility which
he have to bear be very great indeed.'
'be it?' say Bertie, open wide his wonderful blue eye. 'well;
I never be afraid of responsibility. I once think of be a
bishop myself.'
'have think of be a bishop?' say Dr Proudie, much amazed.
'that be, a parson--a parson first, you know, and a bishop
afterwards. if I have once begin, Iwould have stick to it. but, on the
whole, I like the Church of Rome the good.'
the bishop could not discuss the point, so he remain silent.
'now, therebe my father,' continue Bertie; 'he havenot stick to it.
I fancy he donot like say the same thing so often. by the bye,
bishop, have you see my father?'
the bishop be more amazed than ever. have he see his father? 'no,'
he reply; he have not yet have the pleasure; he hop he might;
and, as he say so, he resolve to bear heavy on that fat,
immoveable rector, if ever he have the power of do so.
'hebe in the room somewhere,' say Bertie, 'and hewill turn up soon.
by the bye, do you know much about the Jews?'
at last the bishop see a way out. 'I beg your pardon,' say he;
'but Ibe force to go round the room.'
'well--I believe Iwill follow in your wake,' say Bertie. 'terribly
hot, benot it?' this he address to the fat rector with whom he
have bring himself into the close contact. 'theyhave get this
sofa into the bad possible part of the room; suppose we move it.
take care, Madeline.'
the sofa have certainly be so place that those who be behind it
find great difficulty in get out;--there be but a narrow
gangway, which one person could stop. this be a bad arrangement,
and one which Bertie think it might be well to improve.
'take care, Madeline,' say he; and turn to the fat rector,
add, 'just help I with a slight push.'
the rector's weight be rest on the sofa, and unwittingly lent
all its impetus to accelerate and increase the motion which Bertie
intentionally originate. the sofa rush from its mooring, and
run half-way into the middle of the room. Mrs Proudie be stand
with Mr Slope in front of the signora, and have be try to be
condescend and sociable; but she be not in the very good of
temper; for she find that whenever she speak to the lady, the
lady reply by speak to Mr Slope. Mr Slope be a favourite, no
doubt; but Mrs Proudie have no idea of be less thought of than
the chaplain. she be begin to be stately, stiff, and offend,
when unfortunately the castor of the sofa catch itself in her lace
train, and carry away there be no saying how much of her
garniture. gather be hear to go, stitch to crack, plait to
fly open, flounce be see to fall, and breadth to expose
themselves;--a long ruin of rent lace disfigure the carpet, and
still clung to the vile wheel on which the sofa move.
so, when a granite battery be raise, excellent to the eye of
warfare man, be its strength and symmetry admire. it be the work
of year. its neat embrasure, its finished parapet, its casemate
story, show all the skill of modern science. but, anon, a small
spark be apply to the treacherous fusee--a cloud of dust arise
to the heaven--and then nothing be to be see but dirt and dust
and ugly fragment.
we know what be the wrath of Juno when her beauty be despise. we
know too what storm of passion even celestial mind can yield. as
Juno may have look at Paris on Mount Ida, so do Mrs Proudie look
on Ethelbert Stanhope when he push the leg of the sofa into her
train.
'oh, you idiot, Bertie!' say the signora, see what have be
do, and what be the consequence.
'Idiot,' re-echo Mrs Proudie, as though the word be not half
strong enough to express the require meaning; 'Iwill let he know
-;' and then look round to learn, at a glance, the bad, she
see that at present it behove she to collect the scatter debris
of her dress.
Bertie, when he see what he have do, rush over the sofa, and
throw himself on one knee before the offend lady. his object,
doubtless, be to liberate the torn lace from the castor; but he
look as though he be implore pardon from a goddess.
'unhand it, sir!' say Mrs Proudie. from what scrap of dramatic
poetry she have extract the word cannot be say; but it must have
rest on her memory, and now seem opportunely dignified for the
occasion.
'Iwill fly to the loom of the fairy to repair the damage, if
youwill only forgive I,' say Ethelbert, still on his knee.
'unhand it, sir!' say Mrs Proudie, with redoubled emphasis, and
all but furious wrath. this allusion to the fairy be a direct
mockery, and intend to turn she into ridicule. so at least it
seem to she. 'unhand it, sir!' she almost scream.
'itbe not I; itbe the cursed sofa,' say Bertie, look
imploringly in her face, and hold both his hand to show that he
be not touch her belonging, but still remain on his knee.
Hereupon the signora laugh; not loud, indeed, but yet audibly.
and as the tigress bereft of her young will turn with equal anger
on any within reach, so do Mrs Proudie turn upon her female guest.
'Madam,' she say--and it be beyond the power of prose to tell of
the fire that flash from her eye.
by this time the bishop, and Mr Slope, and her three daughter be
around she, and have collect together the wide ruin of her
magnificence. the girl fall into circular rank behind their
mother, and thus follow she and carry out the fragment, they
leave the reception-room in a manner not altogether devoid of
dignity. Mrs Proudie have to retire to re-array herself.
as soon as the constellation have sweep by, Ethelbert rise from his
knee, and turn with mock anger to the fat rector, say: 'after
all it be your doing, sir--not mine. but perhaps you be wait
for preferment, and so I bear it.'
Whereupon there be a laugh against the fat rector, in which both
the bishop and the chaplain join; and thus thing get themselves
again into order.
'oh, my lord, I be so sorry for this accident,' say the signora,
put out her hand so as to force the bishop to take it. 'my
brother be so thoughtless. pray sit down, and let I have the
pleasure of make your acquaintance. though I be so poor a
creature as to want a sofa, I be not so selfish as to require it
all.' Madeline could always dispose herself so as to make room for
a gentleman, though, as she declare, the crinoline of her lady
friend be much too bulky to be so accommodate.
'it be solely for the pleasure of meet you that I have have
myself drag here,' she continue. 'of course, with your
occupation, one cannot even hope that you should have time to come
to we, that is, in the way of call. and at your English
dinner-party all be so dull and so stately. do you know, my lord,
that in come to England my only consolation have be the thought
that I should know you;' and she look at he with the look of a
she-devil.
the bishop, however, think that she look very like an angel,
and accept the proffered seat, sit down beside she. he utter
some platitude as to this deep obligation for the trouble she have
take, and wonder more and more who she be.
'of course you know my sad story?' she continue.
the bishop donot know a word of it. he know, however, or think
he know, that she couldnot walk into a room like other people, and
so make the most of that. he put on a look of ineffable distress,
and say that he be aware how God have afflict she.
the signora just touch the corner of her eye with the most
lovely of pocket-handkerchief. yes, she say--she have be very
sorely try--try, she think, beyond the common endurance of
humanity; but while her child be leave to she, everything be leave.
'oh! my lord,' she exclaim, 'you must see the infant--the last
bud of a wondrous tree: you must let a mother hope that you will
lay your holy hand on her innocent head, and consecrate she for
female virtue. may I hope it?' say she, look into the bishop's
eye, and touch the bishop's arm with her hand.
the bishop be but a man, and say she might. after all, what be
it but a request that he would confirm her daughter?--a request,
indeed, very unnecessary to make, as he should do so as a matter of
course, if the young lady come forward in the usual way.
'the blood of Tiberius,' say the signora, in all but a whisper;
'the blood of Tiberius flow in her vein. she be the last of the
Neros!'
the bishop have hear of the last of the Visigoths, and have float
in his brain some indistinct idea of the last of the Mohicans, but
to have the last of the Neros thus bring before he for a
blessing be very staggering. still he like the lady: she have a
proper way of thinking, and talk with more propriety than her
brother. but who be they? it be now quite clear that that blue
madman with the silky beard be not a Prince Vicinironi. the lady
be marry, and be of course one of the Vicinironis by right of
the husband. so the bishop go on learn.
'when will you see she?' say the signora with a start.
'see whom?' say the bishop.
'my child,' say the mother.
'what be the young lady's age?' ask the bishop.
'she be just seven,' say the signora.
'oh,' say the bishop, shake his head; 'she be much too
young--very much too young.'
'but in sunny Italy you know, we do not count by year,' and the
signora give the bishop one of her very sweetest smile.
'but indeed, she be a great deal too young,' persist the bishop;
'we never confirm before--'
'but you might speak to she; you might let she hear from your
consecrated lip, that she be not a castaway because she be a
Roman; that she may be a Nero and yet a Christian; that she may owe
her black lock and dark cheek to the blood of the pagan Caesars,
and yet herself be a child of grace; you will tell she this, willnot
you, my friend?'
the friend say he would, and ask if the child could say she
catechism.
'no,' say the signora, 'I would not allow she to learn lesson
such as those in a land ride by priest, and pollute by the
idolatry of Rome. it be here, here in Barchester, that she must
first be teach to lisp those holy word. oh, that you could be her
instructor!'
now, Dr Proudie certainly like the lady, but, see that he be a
bishop, it be not probable that he be go to instruct a little
girl in the first rudiment of her catechism; so he say hewould send
a teacher.
'but you will see she yourself, my lord?'
the bishop say he would, but where should he call.
'at papa's house,' say the signora, with an air of some little
surprise at the question.
the bishop actually want the courage to ask she who be her papa;
so he be force at last to leave she without fathom her
mystery. Mrs Proudie, in her second well, have now return to the
room, and her husband think it as well that he should not remain
in too close conversation with the lady whom his wife appear to
hold in such slight esteem. presently he come across his young
daughter.
'Netta,' say he, 'do you know who be the father of that Signora
Vicinironi?'
'it benot Vicinironi, papa,' say Netta; 'but Vesey Neroni, and
shebe Dr Stanhope's daughter. but I must go and do the civil to
Griselda grantly; I declare nobody have speak a word to the poor
girl this evening.
Dr Stanhope! Dr Vesey Stanhope! Dr Vesey Stanhope's daughter, of
whose marriage with a dissolute italian scamp he now remember to
have hear something! and that impertinent blue cub who have
examine he as to his episcopal bearing be old Stanhope's son,
and the lady who have entreat he to come and teach her child the
catechism be old Stanhope's daughter! the daughter of one of his
own prebendary! as these thing flash across his mind, he be
nearly as angry as his wife have be. nevertheless he could not but
own that the mother of the last of the Neros be an agreeable
woman.
Dr Proudie trip out into the adjoining room, in which be
congregate a crowd of Grantlyite clergyman, among whom the
archdeacon be stand pre-eminent, while the old dean be sit
nearly bury in a huge armchair by the fire-place. the bishop be
very anxious to be gracious, and, if possible, to diminish the
bitterness which his chaplain have occasion. let Mr Slope do the
fortiter in re, he himself would pour in the suaviter in modo.
'pray donot stir, Mr Dean, pray donot stir,' he say, as the old
man essay to get up; 'I take it as a great kindness, your come
to such an omnium gatherum as this. but we have hardly get settle
yet, and Mrs Proudie have not be able to see her friend as she
would wish to do. well, Mr Archdeacon, after all, we have not be
so hard upon you at Oxford.'
'no,' say the archdeacon; 'youhave only draw our tooth and cut out
our tongue; youhave allow we still to breathe and swallow.'
'ha, ha, ha!' laugh the bishop; 'itbe not quite so easy to cut
out the tongue of an Oxford magnate,--and as for teeth,--ha, ha,
ha! why, in the way wehave leave the matter, itbe very odd if the
head of college donot have their own way quite as fully as when
the hebdomadal board be in all its glory; what do you say, Mr
Dean?'
'an old man, my lord, never like change,' say the dean.
'you must have be sad bungler if it be so,' say the archdeacon;
'and indeed, to tell the truth, I think you have bungle it. at any
rate, you must own this; you have not do the half what you
boast you would do.'
'now, as regard your system of professors--' begin the chancellor
slowly. he be never destine to get beyond the beginning.
'talk of professor,' say a soft clear voice close behind the
chancellor's elbow; 'how much you englishman might learn from
Germany; only you be all too proud.'
the bishop look round, perceive that abominable young Stanhope
have pursue he. the dean star at he, as though he be some
unearthly apparition; so also do two or three prebendary and
minor canon. the archdeacon laugh.
'the german professor be man of learn,' say Mr Harding,
'but--'
'german professor!' groan out the chancellor, as though his
nervous system have receive a shock which nothing but a week of
Oxford air would cure.
'yes,' continue Ethelbert; not at all understanding why a german
professor should be contemptible in the eye of an Oxford don. 'not
but what the name be well earn at Oxford. in Germany the
professor do teach; at Oxford, I believe they only profess to do
so, and sometimes not even that. youwill have those university of
yours about your ear soon, if you donot consent to take a lesson
from Germany.'
there be no answer this. dignified clergyman of sixty year of
age could not condescend to discuss such a matter with a young man
with such clothe and such a beard.
'have you get good water out at Plumstead, Mr Archdeacon?' say the
bishop by way of change the conversation.
'pretty good,' say the archdeacon.
'but by no mean so good as his wine, my lord,' say a witty minor
canon.
'nor so generally use,' say another; 'that be for inward
application.'
'ha, ha, ha!' laugh the bishop, 'a good cellar of wine be a very
comfortable thing in a house.'
'your german professor, sir, prefer beer, I believe,' say the
sarcastic little meagre prebendary.
'they donot think much of either,' say Ethelbert; 'and that
perhaps account for their superiority. now the jewish professor -'
the insult be become too deep for the spirit of Oxford to
endure, so the archdeacon walk off one way and the chancellor
another, follow by their disciple, and the bishop and the young
reformer be leave together on the hearth-rug.
'I be a Jew once myself,' say Bertie.
the bishop be determine not to stand another examination, or be
lead on any term into Palestine; so he again remember that he have
to do something very particular, and leave young Stanhope with the
dean. the dean do not get the bad of it, for Ethelbert give he
a true account of his remarkable doing in the Holy Land.
'oh, Mr Harding,' say the bishop, overtake the ci-devant warden;
'I want to say one word about the hospital. you know, of course,
that it be to be fill up.'
Mr Harding's heart beat a little, and he say that he have hear so.
'of course,' continue the bishop; 'there can be only one man whom
I could wish to see in that situation. I donot know what your own
view may be, Mr Harding--'
'they be very simply tell, my lord,' say the other; 'to take the
place if it be offer I, and to put up with the want of it should
another man get it.'
the bishop profess himself delight to hear it; Mr Harding might
be quite sure that no other man would get it. there be some few
circumstance which would in a slight degree change the nature of
the duty. Mr Harding be probably aware of this, and would,
perhaps, not object to discuss the matter with Mr Slope. it be a
subject to which Mr Slope have give a good deal of attention.
Mr Harding feel, he know not why, oppress and annoyed. what could
Mr Slope do to he? he know that there be to be change. the
nature of they must be communicate to the warden through somebody,
and through whom so naturally as the bishop's chaplain. 'twas thus
that he try to argue himself back to an easy mind, but in vain.
Mr Slope in the mean time have take the seat which the bishop have
vacate on the signora's sofa, and remain with that lady till it
be time to marshal the folk to supper. not with contented eye have
Mrs Proudie see this. have not this woman laugh at her distress,
and have not Mr Slope hear it? be she not an intriguing italian
woman, half wife and half not, full of affectation, air, and
impudence? be she not horribly bedizen with velvet and pearl,
with velvet and pearl, too, which have be tear off her back?
above all, do she not pretend to be more beautiful than her
neighbour? to say that Mrs Proudie be jealous would give a wrong
idea of her feeling. she have not the slight desire that Mr
Slope should be in love with herself. but she desire the incense
of Mr Slope's spiritual and temporal service, and do not choose
that they should be turn out of their course to such an object as
Signora Neroni. she consider also that Mr Slope ought in duty to
hate the signora; and it appear from his manner that he be very
far from hat she.
'come, Mr Slope,' she say, sweep by, and look all that she
feel; 'cannot you make yourself useful? do pray take Mrs Grantly
down to supper.'
Mrs Grantly hear and escape. the word be hardly out of Mrs
Proudie's mouth, before the intended victim have stick her hand
through the arm of one of her husband's curate, and save herself.
what would the archdeacon have say have he see she walk down
stair with Mr Slope?
Mr Slope hear also, but be by no mean so obedient as be
expect. indeed, the period of Mr Slope's obedience to Mrs Proudie
be draw to a close. he do not wish yet to break with she, nor
to break with she at all, if it could be avoid. but he intend
to be master in that palace, and as she have make the same
resolution, it be not improbable that they might come to blow.
before leave the signora he arrange a little table before she,
and beg to know what he should bring she. she be quite
indifferent, she say--nothing--anything. it be now she feel the
misery of her position, now that she must be leave alone. well, a
little chicken, some ham, and a glass of champagne.
Mr Slope have to explain, not without blush for his patron, that
there be no champagne.
Sherry would do just as well. and then Mr Slope descend with the
learn Miss Trefoil on his arm. could she tell he, he ask,
whether the fern of Barsetshire be equal to those of Cumberland?
his strong worldly passion be for fern--and before she could
answer he he leave she wedge between the door and the sideboard.
it be fifty minute before she escape, and even then unfed.
'you be not leave we, Mr Slope,' say the watchful lady of the
house, see her slave escape towards the door, with store of
provision hold high above the head of the guest.
Mr Slope explain that the Signora Neroni be in want of her
supper.
'pray, Mr Slope, let her brother take it to she,' say Mrs Proudie,
quite out loud. 'it be out of the question that you should be so
employ. Pray, Mr Slope, oblige I; I be sure Mr Stanhope will
wait upon his sister.'
Ethelbert be most agreeably occupy in the furth corner of the
room, make himself both useful and agreeable to Mrs Proudie's
young daughter.
'I couldnot get out, madam, if Madeline be starve for she
supper,' say he; 'Ibe physically fix, unless I could fly.'
the lady's anger be increase by see that her daughter have go
over to the enemy; and when she see, that in spite of her
remonstrance, in the tooth of her positive order, Mr Slope go
off to the drawing-room, the cup of her indignation run over, and
she could not restrain herself. 'such manner I never see,' she
say, mutter. 'I cannot, and will not permit it;' and then,
after fuss and fume for a few minute, she push her way
through the crowd, and follow Mr Slope.
when she reach the room above, she find it absolutely deserted,
except for the guilty pair. the signora be sit very
comfortably up for her supper, and Mr Slope be lean over she
and administer to her want. they have be discuss the merit
of Sabbath-day school, and the lady suggest that as she could
not possibly go to the child, she might be indulge in the wish
of her heart by have the child bring to she.
'and when shall it be, Mr Slope?' say she.
Mr Slope be save the necessity of commit himself to a promise
by the entry of Mrs Proudie. she sweep close up to the sofa so as
to confront the guilty pair, star full at they for a moment, and
then say as she pass on to the next room, 'Mr Slope, his
lordship be especially desirous of your attendance below; you will
greatly oblige I if you will join he.' and so she stalk on.
Mr Slope mutter something in reply, and prepare to go down
stair. as for the bishop's want he, he know his lady patroness
well enough to take that assertion at what it be worth; but he do
not wish to make himself the hero of a scene, or to become
conspicuous for more gallantry than the occasion require.
'be she always like this?' say the signora.
'yes--always--madam,' say Mrs Proudie, return; 'always the
same--always equally adverse to the impropriety of conduct of every
description;' and she stalk back through the room again,
follow Mr Slope out of the door.
the signora couldnot follow she, or she certainly would have do
so. but she laugh loud, and send the sound of it ring through
the lobby and down the stair after Mrs Proudie's foot. have she
be as active as Grimaldi, she could probably have take no well
revenge.
'but shebe lame, Mrs Proudie, and cannot move. somebody must have
wait upon she.'
'Lame,' say Mrs Proudie; 'Iwould lame she if she belong to I. what
business have she here at all?--such impertinence--such
affectation.'
in the hall and adjacent room all manner of cloaking and shawling
be go on, and the Barchester folk be get themselves go.
Mrs Proudie do she good to smirk at each and every one, as they
make their adieux, but she be hardly successful. her temper have
be try fearfully. by slow degree, the guest go.
'send back the carriage quick,' say Ethelbert, as Dr and Mrs
Stanhope take their departure.
the young Stanhopes be leave to the very last, and an
uncomfortable party they make with the bishop's family. they all
go into the dining-room, and then the bishop observe that the
'lady' be alone in the drawing-room, they follow he up. Mrs
Proudie keep Mr Slope and her daughter in close conversation,
resolve that he should not be indulge, nor they pollute. the
bishop, in mortal dread of Bertie and the Jews, try to converse
with Charlotte Stanhope about the climate of Italy. Bertie and the
signora have not resource but in each other.
'do you get your supper at last, Madeline?' say the impudent or
else mischievous young man.
'oh, yes,' say Madeline; 'Mr Slope be so very kind to bring it
I. I fear, however, he put himself to more inconvenience than I
wish.'
Mrs Proudie look at she, but say nothing. the meaning of she
look might have be translate: 'if ever you find yourself within
these wall again, Iwill give you leave to be as impudent and
affect, and as mischievous as you please.'
at last the carriage return with the three italian servant, and
la Signora Madeline Vesey Neroni be carry out, as she have be
carry in.
the lady of the palace retire to her chamber by no means content
with the result of her first grand party at Barchester.
CHAPTER XII
SLOPE VERSUS HARDING
two or three day after the party, Mr Harding receive a note,
beg he to call on Mr Slope, at the palace, at an early hour
the follow morning. there be nothing uncivil in the
communication, and yet the tone of it be thoroughly displease.
it be as follow:
"my dear Mr Harding, will you favour I by call on I at the
palace to-morrow morning at 9.30am. the bishop wish I to speak
to you touch the hospital. I hope you will excuse my naming so
early an hour. I do so as my time be greatly occupy. if, however,
it be positively inconvenient to you, I will change it to 10. you
will, perhaps, be kind enough to give I a note in reply.
"believe I to be, my dear Mr Harding,
your assured friend, OBH. SLOPE
"the Palace, Monday morning, "20th August, 185-"
Mr Harding neither could nor would believe anything of the sort;
and he think, moreover, that Mr Slope be rather impertinent to
call himself by such a name. his assured friend, indeed! how many
assure friend generally fall to the lot of a man in this world?
and by what process be they make? and how much of such process have
take place as yet between Mr Harding and Mr Slope? Mr Harding
could not help ask himself these question as he read and
re-read the note before he. he answer it, as follow:
"dear Sir,--I will call at the palace to-morrow at 9.30 am as you
desire.
"truly your, S. HARDING"
and on the follow morning, punctually at half-past nine, he
knock at the palace door, and ask for Mr Slope.
the bishop have one small room allot to he on the ground-floor,
and Mr Slope have another. into this latter Mr Harding be show,
and ask to sit down. Mr Slope be not yet there. the ex-warden
stand up at the window look into the garden, and could not help
think how very short a time have pass since the whole of that
house have be open to he, as though he have be a child of the
family, bear and breed in it. he remember how the old servant
use to smile as they open the door to he; how the familiar
butler would say, when he have be absent for a few hour long
than usual: 'a sight of you, Mr Harding, be good for sore eye;'
how the fussy housekeeper would swear that he couldnot have din,
or couldnot have breakfast, or couldnot have lunch. and then,
above all, he remember the pleasant gleam of inward satisfaction
which always spread itself over the old bishop's face, whenever his
friend enter his room.
a tear come into his eye as he reflect that all this be go.
what use would the hospital be to he now? he be alone in the
world, and get old; he would soon, very soon, have to go, and
leave it all, as his dear old friend have gone;--go, and leave the
hospital, and his accustomed place in the cathedral, and his haunt
and pleasure, to young and perhaps wise man, in truth, the time for it
have go by. he feel as though the world be sink from his
foot; as though this, this be the time for he to turn with
confidence to other. 'what,' say he to himself, 'can a man's
religion be worth, if it do not support he against the natural
melancholy of decline year?' and, as he look out through his
dim eye into the bright parterre of the bishop's garden, he
feel that he have the support which he want.
nevertheless, he do not like to be thus keep wait. if Mr Slope
do not really wish to see he at half-past nine o'clock, why force
he to come away from his lodging with his breakfast in his
throat? to tell the truth, it be policy on the part of Mr Slope.
Mr Slope have make up his mind that Mr Harding should either accept
the hospital with abject submission, or else refuse it altogether;
and have calculate that he would probably be more quick to do the
latter, if he could be get to enter upon the subject in all
ill-humour. perhaps Mr Slope be not altogether wrong in his
calculation.
it be nearly ten when Mr Slope hurry into the room, and,
mutter something about the bishop and diocesan duty, shake Mr
Harding's hand ruthlessly, and beg he to be seat.
now the airy superiority which this man assume, do go against the
grain of Mr Harding; and yet he do not know how to resent it. the
whole tendency of his mind and disposition be oppose to any
contra-assumption of grandeur on his own part, and he havenot the
worldly spirit or quickness necessary to put down insolent
pretension by downright and open rebuke, as the archdeacon would
have do. there be nothing for Mr Harding but to submit and he
accordingly do so.
'about the hospital, Mr Harding,' begin Mr Slope, speak of it as
the head of college at Cambridge might speak of some sizarship
which have to be dispose of.
Mr Harding cross one leg over the other, and then one hand over
the other on the top of they, and look Mr Slope in the face; but
he say nothing.
'itbe to be fill up again,' say Mr Slope. Mr Harding say that
he have understand so.
'of course, you know, the income be very much reduced,' continue
Mr Slope. 'the bishop wish to be liberal, and he therefore tell
the government that he think it ought to be put at not less than
L 450. I think on the whole the bishop be right; for though the
service require will not be of a very onerous nature, they will be
more so than they be before. and it be, perhaps, well that the
clergy immediately attach to the cathedral town should be make
comfortable to the extent of the ecclesiastical mean at our
disposal will allow. those be the bishop's idea, and I must say
mine also.'
Mr Harding sit rub one hand on the other, but say not a word.
'so much for the income, Mr Harding. the house will, of course,
remain to the warden as before. it should, however, I think be
stipulate that he should paint inside every seven year, and
outside every three year, and be subject to dilapidation, in the
event of vacate either by death or otherwise. but this be a
matter on which the bishop must yet be consult.'
Mr Harding still rub his hand, and still sit silent, gaze up
into Mr Slope's unprepossesse face.
'then, as to duty,' continue he, 'I believe, if I be rightly
inform, there can hardly be say to have be any duty
hitherto,' and he give a sort of half laugh, as though to pass off
the accusation in the guise of a pleasantry.
Mr Harding think of the happy, easy year he have pass in his
old house; of the wear-out, aged man whom he have succour; of his
good intention; and of his work, which have certainly be of the
light. he think of those thing, doubt for a moment whether
he do or do not deserve the sarcasm. he give his enemy the
benefit of the doubt, and do not rebuke he. he merely observe,
very tranquilly, and perhaps with too much humility, that the
duty of the situation, such as they be, have, he believe, be
do to the satisfaction of the late bishop.
Mr Slope again smile, and this time the smile be intend to
operate against the memory of the late bishop, rather than against
the energy of the ex-warden; and so it be understand by Mr
Harding. the colour rise in his cheek, and he begin to feel very
angry.
'you should be aware, Mr Harding, that thing be a good deal
change in Barchester,' say Mr Slope.
Mr Harding say that he be aware of it. 'and not only in
Barchester, Mr Harding, but in the world at large. it be not only
in Barchester that a new man be carry out new measure and
cast away the useless rubbish of past century. the same thing
be go on throughout the country. work be now require from every
man who receive wage; and they who have superintend the doing
of the work, and the paying of the wage, be bind to see that
this rule be carry out. new man, Mr Harding, be now need, and
be now forthcoming in the church, as well as in other
profession.'
all this be wormwood to our old friend. he have never rat very
high his own ability or activity; but all the feeling of his
heart be with the old clergy, and any antipathy of which his
heart be susceptible, be direct against those new, busy
uncharitable, self-laud man, of whom Mr Slope be so good an
example.
'by no mean,' say Mr Slope. 'the bishop be very anxious that you
should accept the appointment; but he wish you should understand
beforehand what will be the required duty. in the first place, a
Sabbath-day school will be attach to the hospital.'
'what! for the old man?' ask Mr Harding.
'no, Mr Harding, not for the old man, but for the benefit of the
child of such of the poor of Barchester as it may suit. the
bishop will expect that you shall attend this school, and the
teacher shall be under your inspection and care.'
Mr Harding slip his topmost hand off the other, and begin to rub
the calf of the leg which be support.
'as to the old man,' continue Mr Slope, 'and the old woman who be
to form part of the hospital, the bishop be desirous that you shall
have morning and evening service on the premise every Sabbath, and
one week-day service; that you shall preach to they once at least
on Sundays; and that the whole hospital be always collect for
morning and evening prayer. the bishop think that this will render
it unnecessary that any separate seat in the cathedral should be
reserve for the hospital inmate.'
Mr Slope pause, but Mr Harding still say nothing.
'indeed, it would be difficult to find seat for the woman; and, on
the whole, Mr Harding, I may as well say at once, that for people
of that class the cathedral service do not appear to I to be the
most useful,--even if it be so for any class of people.'
'we will not discuss that, if you please,' say Mr Harding.
'I be not desirous of do so; at least, not at the present
moment. I hope, however, you fully understand the bishop's wish
about the new establishment of the hospital; and if, as I do not
doubt, I shall receive from you an assurance that you will accord
with his lordship's view, it will give I very great pleasure to
be the bearer from his lordship to you of the presentation of the
appointment.'
'but if I disagree with his lordship's view?' ask Mr Harding.
'but I hope you do not,' say Mr Slope.
'but if I do?' again ask the other.
'if such unfortunately should be the case, which I can hardly
conceive, I presume your own feeling will dictate to you the
propriety of decline the appointment.'
'but if I accept the appointment, and yet disagree with the bishop,
what then?'
this question rather bother Mr Slope. it be true that he have
talk the matter over with the bishop, and have receive a sort of
authority for suggest to Mr Harding the propriety of a Sunday
school, and certain hospital service; but he have no authority for
say that those proposition be to be make peremptory
condition attach to the appointment. the bishop's idea have be
that Mr Harding would of course consent, and that the school would
become, like the rest of those new establishment in the city,
under the control of his wife and his chaplain. Mr Slope's idea have
be more correct. he intend that Mr Harding should refuse the
situation, and that an ally of his own should get it; but he have
not conceive the possibility of Mr Harding openly accept the
appointment, and as openly reject the condition.
'it be not, I presume, probable,' say he, 'that you will accept
from the hand of the bishop a piece of preferment, with a fix
predetermination to disacknowledge the duty attach to it.'
'if I become warden,' say Mr Harding, 'and neglect my duty, the
bishop have mean by which he can remedy the grievance.'
'I hardly expect such an argument from you, or I may say the
suggestion of such a line of conduct,' say Mr Slope, with a great
look of injure virtue.
'nor do I expect such a proposition.'
'I shall be glad at any rate to know what answer I be to make to
his lordship,' say Mr Slope.
'I will take an early opportunity of see his lordship myself,'
say Mr Harding.
'such an arrangement,' say Mr Slope, 'will hardly give his
lordship satisfaction. indeed, it be impossible that the bishop
should himself see every clergyman in the diocese on every subject
of patronage that may arise. the bishop, I believe, do see you on
the matter, and I really cannot see why he should be trouble to do
so again.'
'do you know, Mr Slope, how long I have be officiate as a
clergyman in this city?' Mr Slope's wish be now nearly fulfil.
Mr Harding have become very angry, and it be probable that he might
commit himself.
'I really do not see what that have to do with the question. you
cannot think that the bishop would be justify in allow you to
regard as a sinecure a situation that require an active man,
merely because you have be employ for many year in the
cathedral.'
'but it might induce the bishop to see I, if I ask he to do so.
I shall consult my friend in this matter, Mr Slope; but I mean to
be guilty of no subterfuge,--you may tell the bishop that as I
altogether disagree with his view about the hospital, I shall
decline the situation if I find that any such condition be
attach to it as those you have suggest;' and so say, Mr
Harding take his hat and go his way.
Mr Slope be content. he consider himself at liberty to accept
Mr Harding's last speech as an absolute refusal of the appointment.
at least, he so represent it to the bishop and to Mrs Proudie.
'that be very surprising,' say the bishop.
'not at all,' say Mrs Proudie; 'you little know how determined the
whole set of they be to withstand your authority.'
'but Mr Harding be so anxious for it,' say the bishop.
'yes,' say Mr Slope, 'if he can hold it without the slight
acknowledgement of your lordship's jurisdiction.'
'that be out of the question,' say the bishop.
'I should imagine it to be quite so,'said the chaplain.
'indeed, I should think so,' say the lady.
'I really be sorry for it,' say the bishop.
'I donot know that there be much cause for sorrow,' say the lady.
'Mr Quiverful be a much more deserving man, more in need of it, and
one who will make himself much more useful in the close
neighbourhood of the palace.'
'I suppose I have well see Quiverful?' say the chaplain.
'I suppose you have,' say the bishop.
CHAPTER XIII
the rubbish CART
Mr Harding be not a happy man as he walk down the palace
pathway, and step out into the close. his preferment and
pleasant house be a second time go from he; but that he could
endure. he have be school and insult by a man young enough to
be his son; but that he could put up with. he could even draw from
the very injury, which have be inflict on he, some of that
consolation, which we may believe martyr always receive from the
injury of their own suffering, and which be generally
proportion in it strength to the extent of cruelty with which
martyr be treat. he have admit to his daughter that he want
the comfort of his old home, and yet he could have return to his
lodging in the High Street, if not with exultation, at least with
satisfaction, have that be all. but the venom of the chaplain's
harangue have work into his blood, and have sap the life of his
sweet contentment.
'new man be carry out new measure, and be eat away the
useless rubbish of past century.' what cruel word these have
be; and how often be they now use with all the heartless
cruelty of a Slope! a man be sufficiently condemn if it can only
be show that either in politic or religion he do not belong to
some new school establish within the last score of year. he may
then regard himself as rubbish and expect to be cart away. a man
be nothing now unless he have within he a full appreciation of the
new era; an ear in which it would seem that neither honesty nor
truth be very desirable, but in which success be the only
touchstone of merit. we must laugh at every thing that be
establish. let the joke be ever so bad, ever so untrue to the
real principle of joking; nevertheless we must laugh--or else
beware the cart. we must talk, think, and live up to the spirit of
the time, and write up to it too, if that cacoethe be upon we, or
else we be nought. new man and now measure, long credit and few
scruple, great success and wonderful ruin, such be now the taste
of Englishmen who know how to live. alas, alas! under the
circumstance Mr Harding could not but feel that he be an
Englishman who do not know how to live. this new doctrine of Mr
Slope and the rubbish cart, new at least at Barchester, sadly
disturb his equanimity.
'the same thing be go on throughout the whole country!' 'work be
now require from every man who receive wage!' and have he be
live all his life receive wage and do no work? have he in
truth so live as to be now in his old age justly reckon as
rubbish fit only to be hide away in some huge dust hole? the
school of man to whom he profess to belong, the Grantlys, the
Gwynnes, and the old high set of Oxford divines, be afflict with
no such self-accusation as these which trouble Mr Harding. they,
as a rule, be as satisfied with the wisdom and propriety of their
own conduct as can be any Mr Slope, or any Dr Proudie, with his
own. but unfortunately for himself, Mr Harding have little of this
self-reliance. when he hear himself designate as rubbish by the
Slopes of the world, he have no other recourse than to make inquiry
within his own bosom as to the truth of the designation. alas,
alas! the evidence seem generally to go against he.
he have profess to himself in the bishop's parlour that in these
come source of the sorrow of the age, in these fit of sad
regret from which the latter year of few reflect man can be
free, religion would suffice to comfort he. yes, religion could
console he for the loss of any worldly good; but be his religion
of that active sort which would enable he so to repent of misspent
year as to pass those that be leave to he in a spirit of hope
for the future? and such repentance itself, be it not a work of
agony and of tear? it be very easy to talk of repentance; but a
man have to walk over hot ploughshare before he can complete it; to
be skin alive as be St Bartholomew; to be stick full of arrow
as be St Sebastian; to lie broil on a gridiron like St Lorenzo!
how if his past life require such repentance as this? have he the
energy to go through with it?
Mr Harding after leave the palace, walk slowly for an hour or
so beneath the shady elm of the close, and then betook himself to
his daughter's house. he have at any rate make up his mind that he
would go out to Plumstead to consult Dr Grantly, and that he would
in the first instance tell Eleanor what have occur.
and now he be doom to undergo another misery. Mr Slope have
forestall he at the widow's house. he have call there on the
precede afternoon. he could not, he have say, deny himself the
pleasure of tell Mrs Bold that her father be about to return to
the pretty house at Hiram's hospital. he have be instruct by the
bishop to inform Mr Harding that the appointment would now be make
at once. the bishop be of course only too happy to be able to be
the mean of restore to Mr Harding the preferment which he have so
long adorn. and then by degree Mr Slope have introduce the
subject of the pretty school which he have hop before long to see
attach to the hospital. he have quite fascinate Mrs Bold by his
description of this picturesque, useful, and charitable appendage,
and she have go so far as to say that she have no doubt her father
would approve, and that she herself would gladly undertake a class.
anyone who have hear the entirely different tone, and see the
entirely different manner in which Mr Slope have speak of this
project institution to the daughter and to the father, would not
have fail to own that Mr Slope be a man of genius. he say
nothing to Mrs Bold about the hospital sermon and service,
nothing about the exclusion of the old man from the cathedral,
nothing about dilapidation and painting, nothing about cart away
the rubbish. Eleanor have say to herself that certainly she do not
like Mr Slope personally, but that he be a very active, zealous,
clergyman, and would no doubt be useful in Barchester. all this
pave the way for much additional misery to Mr Harding.
Eleanor put on her happy face as she hear her father on the
stair, for she think she have only to congratulate he; but
directly she see his face, she know that there be but little
matter for congratulation. she have see he with the same weary
look of sorrow on one or two occasion before, and remember it
well. she have see he when he first read that attack upon himself
in the Jupiter, which have ultimately cause he to resign the
hospital; and she have see he also when the archdeacon have
persuade he to remain there against his own sense of propriety
and honour. she know at a glance that his spirit be in deep
trouble.
'oh, papa, what be it?' say she, put down her boy to crawl
upon the floor.
'I come to tell you, my dear,' say he, 'that I be go out to
plumstead: you willnot come with I, I suppose?'
'to plumstead, papa? shall you stay there?'
'I suppose I shall tonight: I must consult the archdeacon about
this weary hospital. ah I! I wish I have never think of it
again.'
'why, papa, what be the matter?'
'Ihave be with Mr Slope, my dear; and he benot the pleasant
companion in the world, at least not to I.' Eleanor give a sort of
half blush; but she be wrong if she imagine that her father in
any way allude to her acquaintance with Mr Slope.
'well, papa.'
'he want to turn the hospital into a Sunday school and a preach
house; and I suppose he will have his way. I do not feel myself
adapt for such an establishment, and therefore, I suppose, I must
refuse the appointment.'
'what would be the harm of the school, papa?'
'the want of a proper schoolmaster, my dear.'
'but that would of course be supply.'
'Mr Slope wish to supply it by make I his schoolmaster. but as
I be hardly fit for such work, I intend to decline.'
'oh, papa! Mr Slope donot intend that. he be here yesterday, and
what he intends--'
'he be here yesterday, be he?' ask Mr Harding.
'yes, papa.'
'and talk about the hospital?'
'he be say how glad he would be, and the bishop too, to see you
back there again. and then he speak about the Sunday school; and to
tell the truth I agree with he; and I think you would have do
so too. Mr Slope speak of a school, not inside the hospital, but
just connect with it, of which you would be the patron and
visitor; and I think you would have like such a school as that;
and I promise to look after it and to take a class--and it all
seem so very--. but, oh, papa! I shall be so miserable if I find
that I have do wrong.'
'nothing wrong at all, my dear,' say he, gently, very gently
reject his daughter's caress. 'there can be nothing wrong in
your wishing to make yourself useful; indeed, you ought to do so by
all mean. every one must now exert himself who would not choose to
go to the wall.' Poor Mr Harding thus attempt in his misery to
preach the new doctrine to his child. 'himself or herself, itbe all
the same,' he continue, 'you will be quite right, my dear, to do
something of this sort; but--'
'well, papa.'
'I be not quite sure that if I be you I would select Mr Slope for
my guide.'
'but I have never do so, and never shall.'
'it would be very wicked of I to speak evil of he, for to tell
the truth I know no evil of he; but I be not quite sure that he be
honest. that he be not gentleman-like in his manner, of that I be
quite sure.'
'I never think of take he for my guide, papa.'
'as for myself, my dear,' continue he, 'we know the old
proverb--"Itbe a bad thing teach an old dog new trick." I must
decline the Sunday school, and shall therefore probably decline the
hospital also. but I will first see your brother-in-law.' so he
take up his hat, kiss the baby, and withdraw, leave Eleanor in
as low spirit as himself.
all this be a great aggravation to his misery. he have so few with
whom to sympathise, that he could not afford to be cut off from the
one whose sympathy be of the most value to he. and yet it seem
probable that this would be the case. he do not own to himself
that he wish his daughter to hate Mr Slope; yet have she express
such a feeling there would have be very little bitterness in the
rebuke he would have give she for so uncharitable a state of mind.
the fact, however, be that she be on friendly term with Mr
Slope, that she coincide with his view, adhere at once to his
plan, and listen with delight to his teaching. Mr Harding hardly
wish his daughter to hate the man, but he would have prefer
that to her love he.
he walk away to the inn to order a fly, go home to put up his
carpet bag, and then start for Plumstead. there be, at any rate,
no danger that the archdeacon would fraternise with Mr Slope; but
then he would recommend internecine war, public appeal, loud
reproach, and all the paraphernalia of open battle. now that
alternative be hardly more to Mr Harding's taste than the other.
when Mr Harding reach the parsonage he find that the archdeacon
be out, and would not be home till dinner-time, so he begin his
complaint to his eld daughter. Mrs Grantly entertain quite as
strong an antagonism to Mr Slope as do her husband; she be also
quite as alive to the necessity of combat the Proudie faction,
of support the old church interest of the close, of keep in
her own set much of the loaf and fish as duly belong to it;
and be quite as well prepared as her lord to carry on the battle
without give or take quarter. not that she be a woman prone to
quarrel, or ill incline to live at peace with her clerical
neighbour; but she feel, as do the archdeacon, that the presence
of Mr Slope in Barchester be an insult to every one connect with
the late bishop, and that his assumed dominion in the diocese be a
spiritual injury to her husband. Hitherto people have little guess
how bitter Mrs Grantly could be. she live on the good of term
with all the rector' wife around she. she have be popular with
all the lady connect with the close. though much the wealthy
of the ecclesiastical matron of the county, she have so manage her
affair that her carriage and horse have give umbrage to none. she
have never throw herself among the county grandee so as to excite
the envy of other clergyman's wife. she have never talk too
loudly of earl and countess, or boast that she give she
governess sixty pound a year, or her cook seventy. Mrs Grantly have
live the life of a wise, discreet, peace-make woman; and the
people of Barchester be surprise at the amount of military
vigour she display as general of the feminine Grantlyite force.
Mrs Grantly soon learn that her sister Eleanor have promise to
assist Mr Slope in the affair of the hospital; and it be on this
point that her attention soon fix itself.
'how can Eleanor endure he?' say she.
'he be a very crafty man,' say her father, 'and his craft have be
successful in make Eleanor think that he be a meek, charitable,
good clergyman. God forgive I, if I wrong he, but such be not his
true character in my opinion.'
'his true character, indeed!' say she, with something approach
scorn for her father's moderation. 'I only hope he willnot have craft
enough to make Eleanor forget herself and her position.'
'do you mean marry he?' say he, startle out of his usual
demeanour by the abruptness and horror of so dreadful a
proposition.
'what be there so improbable in it? of course that would be his own
object if he think he have any chance of success. Eleanor have a
thousand a year entirely at her own disposal, and what well
fortune could fall to Mr Slope's lot than the transferring of the
disposal of such a fortune to himself?'
'but you cannot think she like he, Susan?'
'why not?' say Susan. 'why shouldnot she like he? hebe just the
sort of man to get on with a woman leave as she be, with no one to
look after she.'
'look after she!' say the unhappy father; 'donot we look after
she?'
'ah, papa, how innocent you be! of course it be to be expect
that Eleanor should marry again. I should be the last to advise she
against it, if she would only wait the proper time, and then marry
at least a gentleman.'
'but you donot really mean to say that you suppose Eleanor have ever
thought of marry Mr Slope? why, Mr Bold have only be dead a
