'eighteen month,' say his daughter. 'but I donot suppose Eleanor
have ever think about it. it be very probable, though, that he
have, and that he will try and make she do so; and that he will
succeed too, if we donot take care what we be about.'
this be quite a new phase of the affair to poor Mr Harding. to
have thrust upon he as his son-in-law, as the husband of his
favourite child, the only man in the world whom he really
positively dislike, would be a misfortune which he feel he would
not know how to endure patiently. but then, could there be any
ground for so dreadful a surmise? in all worldly matter he be apt
to look upon the opinion of his eld daughter, as one generally
sound and trustworthy. in her appreciation of character, of
motif, and the probable conduct both of man and woman, she be
usually not far wrong. she have early foresee the marriage of
Eleanor and John Bold; she have at a glance decipher the character
of the new bishop and his chaplain; could it possibly be that her
present surmise should ever come forth as true?'
'but you donot think that she like he,' say Mr Harding again.
'well, papa, I cannot say that I think she dislike he as she ought
to do. why be he visit there as a confidential friend, when he
never ought to have be admit inside the house? why be it that
she speak to he of about your welfare and your position, as she
clearly have do? at the bishop's party the other night, I see she
talk to he for half an hour at the stretch.'
'I think Mr Slope seem to talk to nobody there but that
daughter of Stanhope's,' say Mr Harding, wish to defend his
child.
'oh, Mr Slope be a cleverer man than you think of, papa, and keep
more than one iron in the fire.'
to give Eleanor her due, any suspicion as to the slight
inclination on her part towards Mr Slope be a wrong to she. she
have no more idea of marry Mr Slope than she have of marry the
bishop; and the idea that Mr Slope would present himself as a
suitor have never occur to she. indeed, to give she her due
again, she have never think about suitor since her husband's
death. but nevertheless it be true that she have overcome all that
repugnance to the man which be so strongly feel for he by the
rest of the grantly faction. she have forgive he his sermon. she
have forgive he his low church tendency, his Sabbath school,
and puritanical observance. she have forgive his pharisaical
arrogance, and even his greasy face and oily vulgar manner. have
agree to overlook such offence as these, why should she not in
time be teach to regard Mr Slope as a suitor?
and as to he, it must be affirm that he be hitherto equally
innocent of the crime impute to he. how it have come to pass that
a man whose eye be generally widely open to everything have not
perceive that this young widow be rich as well as beautiful,
cannot probably now be explain. but such be the fact. Mr Slope
have ingratiate himself with Mrs Bold, merely as he have do with
other lady, in order to strengthen his party in the city. he
subsequently attend his error; but it be not till after the
interview with he and Mr Harding.
CHAPTER XIV
the new CAMPAIGN
the archdeacon do not return to the parsonage till close upon the
hour of dinner, and there be therefore no time to discuss matter
before that important ceremony. he seem to be in an especial good
humour, and welcome his father-in-law with a sort of jovial
earnestness that be usual with he when thing on which be intent
be go on as he would have they.
'itbe all settle, my dear,' say he to his wife as he wash his
hand in his dressing-room, while she, accord to she willnot, sit
listen in the bedroom; 'Arabin have agree to accept the living.
hewill be here next week.' and the archdeacon scrub his hand and
rub his face with a violent alacrity, which show that Arabin's
come be a great point gain.
'will he come here to plumstead?' say the wife.
'he have promise to stay a month with we,' say the archdeacon, 'so
that he may see what his parish be like. youwill like Arabin very
much. hebe a gentleman in every respect, and full of good humour.'
'hebe very queer, benot he?' ask the wife.
'well--he be a little odd in some of his fancy; but therebe
nothing about he you willnot like. he be as staunch a churchman as
there be at Oxford. I really donot know what we should do without
Arabin. itbe a great thing for I to have he so near I; and if
anything can put Slope down, Arabin will do it.'
the Reverend Francis Arabin be a fellow of Lazarus, the favour
disciple of the great Dr Gwynne, a high churchman at all point; so
high, indeed, that at one period of his career he have all but
topple over into the cesspool of Rome; a poet and also a polemical
writer, a great pet in the common room at Oxford, an eloquent
clergyman, a droll, odd, humorous, energetic, conscientious man,
and, as the archdeacon have boast of he, a thorough gentleman. as
he will hereafter be bring more closely to our notice, it be now
only necessary to add, that he have just be present to the
vicarage of St Ewold by Dr Grantly, in whose gift as archdeacon the
living lay. St Ewold's be a parish lie just without the city of
Barchester. the suburb of the new town, indeed, be partly within
its precinct, and the pretty church and parsonage be not much
above a mile distant from the city gate.
St Ewold be not a rich piece of preferment--it be worth some three
or four hundred a year, at most, and have generally be hold by a
clergyman attach to the cathedral choir. the archdeacon, however,
feel, when the living on this occasion become vacant, that it
imperatively behove he to aid the force of his party with some
tower of strength, if any such tower could be get to occupy St
Ewold's. he have discuss the matter with his brother in
Barchester; not in any weak spirit as the holder of patronage to be
use for his own or his family's benefit, but as one to whom be
commit a trust, on the due administration of which much of the
church's welfare might depend. he have submit to they the name of
Mr Arabin, as though the choice have rest with they all in
conclave, and they have unanimously admit that, if Mr Arabin
would accept St Ewold's no well choice could possibly be make.
if Mr Arabin would accept St Ewold's! there lie the difficulty. Mr
Arabin be a man stand somewhat prominently before the world,
that be, before the Church of England world. he be not a rich man,
it be true, for he hold no preferment but his fellowship; but he
be a man not over anxious for rich, not married of course, and
one whose time be greatly take up in discuss, both in print
and on platform, the privilege and practice of the church to
which he belong. as the archdeacon have do battle for its
temporality, so do Mr Arabin do battle for its spirituality;
and both have do so conscientiously; that be, not so much each for
his own benefit as for that of other.
hold such a position as Mr Arabin do, there be much reason to
doubt whether he would consent to become the parson of St Ewold's,
and Dr Grantly have take the trouble to go himself to Oxford on the
matter. Dr Gwynne and Dr Grantly together have succeed in
persuade this eminent divine that duty require he to go
Barchester. there be wheel within wheel in this affair. for
some time past Mr Arabin have be engage in a tremendous
controversy with no less a person than Mr Slope, respect the
apostolic succession. these two gentleman have never see each
other, but they have be extremely bitter in print. Mr Slope have
endeavour to strengthen his cause by call Mr Arabin an owl,
and Mr Arabin have retaliate by hint that Mr Slope be an
infidel. this battle have be commence in the column of the daily
Jupiter, a powerful newspaper, the manager of which be very
friendly to Mr Slope's view of the case. the matter, however, have
become too tedious for the reader of the Jupiter, and a little
note have therefore be append to one of Mr Slope's most tell
rejoinder, in which it have be state that no further letter
from the reverend gentleman could be insert except as
advertisement.
other method of publication be, however, find less expensive
than advertisement in the Jupiter; and the war go on merrily. Mr
Slope declare that the main part of the consecration of a
clergyman be the self-devotion of the inner man to the duty of
the ministry. Mr Arabin contend that a man be not consecrate at
all, have, indeed, no single attribute of a clergyman, unless he
become so through the imposition of some bishop's hand, who have
become a bishop through the imposition of other hand, and so on in
a direct line to one of the apostle. each have repeatedly hang the
other on the horn of a dilemma; but neither seem to a whit the
bad for the hanging; and so the war go on merrily.
whether or no the near neighbourhood of the foe may have act in
any way as an inducement to Mr Arabin to accept the living of St
Ewold, we will not pretend to say; but it have at any rate be
settle in Dr Gwynne's library, at Lazarus, that he would accept
it, and that he would lend his assistance towards drive the enemy
out of Barchester, or, at any rate, silence he while he remain
there. Mr Arabin intend to keep his room at Oxford, and to have
the assistance of a curate at St Ewold; but he promise to give as
much time as possible to the neighbourhood of Barchester, and from
so great a man Dr Grantly be quite satisfied with such a promise.
it be no small part of the satisfaction derivable from such an
arrangement that Dr Proudie would be force to institute into a
living, immediately under his own nose, the enemy of his favourite
chaplain.
all through the dinner the archdeacon's good humour shine brightly
in his face. he eat of the good thing heartily, he drink wine with
his wife and daughter, he talk pleasantly of his doing at
Oxford, tell his father-in-law that he ought to visit Dr Gwynne at
Lazarus, and launch out again in praise of Dr Arabin.
'be Mr Arabin marry, papa?' ask Griselda.
'no, my dear; the fellow of a college be never marry.'
'be he a young man, papa?'
'about forty, I believe,' say the archdeacon.
'oh!' say Griselda. have her father say eighty, Mr Arabin would
not have appear to she to be very much old.
when the two gentleman be leave alone over their wine, Mr Harding
tell his tale of woe. but even this, sad as it be, do not much
diminish the archdeacon's good humour, though it greatly add to
his pugnacity.
'he cannot do it,' say Dr Grantly over and over again, as his
father-in-law explain to he the term on which the new warden of
the hospital be to be appoint; 'he cannot do it. what he say be
not worth the trouble of listen to. he cannot alter the duty of
the place.'
'who cannot?' ask the ex-warden.
'neither can the bishop nor the chaplain, nor yet the bishop's
wife, who, I take it, have really more to say to such matter than
either of the other two. the whole body corporate of the palace
together have no power to turn the warden of the hospital into a
Sunday schoolmaster.'
'but the bishop have the power to appoint whom he please, and--'
'I donot know that; I rather think hewill find he have no such power.
let he try it, and see what the press will say. for once we shall
have the popular cry on our side. but Proudie, ass as he be, know
the world too well to get such a hornet's nest about his ear.'
Mr Harding wince at the idea of the press. he have have enough of
that sort of publicity, and be unwilling to be show up a second
time either as a monster or as a martyr. he gently remark that he
hop the newspaper would not get hold of his name again, and then
suggest that perhaps it would be well that he should abandon
his object. 'I be get old,' say he; 'and after all I doubt
whether I be fit to undertake new duty.'
'new duty!' say the archdeacon: 'donot I tell you there shall be
no new duty?'
'or, perhaps, old duty either,' say Mr Harding; 'I think I will
remain content as I be.' the picture of Mr Slope cart away the
rubbish be still present to his mind.
the archdeacon drink off his glass of claret, and prepare himself
to be energetic. 'I do hope,' say he, 'that you be not go to
be so weak as to allow such a man as Mr Slope to deter you from
do what you know be your duty to do. you know that it be your
duty to resume your place at the hospital now that parliament have
so settle the stipend as to remove those difficulty which
induce you to resign it. you cannot deny this; and should your
timidity now prevent you from do so, your conscience will
hereafter never forgive you;' and as he finish this clause of his
speech, he push over the bottle to his companion.
'your conscience will never forgive you,' he continue. 'you
resign the place from conscientious scruple, scruple which I
greatly respect, though I do not share they. all your friend
respect they, and you leave your old house as rich in reputation
as you be ruin in fortune. it be now expect that you will
return. Dr Gwynne be say only the other day--'
'Dr Gwynne do not reflect how much old a man I be now than when
he last see I.'
'old--nonsense!' say the archdeacon; 'you never think yourself
old till you listen to the impudent trash of that coxcomb at the
palace.'
'I shall be sixty-five if I live till November,' say Mr Harding.
'and seventy-five if you live till November ten year,' say the
archdeacon. 'and you bid fair to be as efficient then as you be
ten year ago. but for heaven's sake let we have no pretence in
this matter. your plea of old age be only a pretence. but yoube
not drink your wine. it be only a pretence. the fact be, you be
half afraid of this Slope, and would rather subject yourself to
comparative poverty and discomfort, than come to blow with a man
who will trample on you, if you let he.'
'I certainly donot like come to blow, if I can help it.'
'nor I neither--but sometimes we cannot help it. this man's object
be to induce you to refuse the hospital, that he may put some
creature of his own into it; that he may show his power, and insult
we all by insult you, whose cause and character be so
intimately bind up with that of the chapter. you owe it to we all
to resist he in this, even if you have no solicitude for yourself.
but surely, for your own sake, you will not be so lily-livere as
to fall into this trap which he have bait for you, and let he
take the very bread out of your mouth without a struggle.'
Mr Harding do not like be call lily-livere, and be rather
inclined to resent it. 'I doubt there be any true courage,' say
he, 'in squabble for money.'
'if honest man do not squabble for money, in this world of ours,
the dishonest man would get it all; and I do not see that the cause
of virtue would be much improve. no,--we must use the mean which
we have. if we be to carry your argument home, we might give away
every shilling of revenue which the church have; and I presume you
be not prepared to say that the church would be strengthen by
such a sacrifice.' the archdeacon fill his glass and then empty
it, drink with much reverence a silent toast to the well-being
and permanent security of those temporality which be so dear to
his soul.
'I think all quarrel between a clergyman and his bishop should be
avoid,' say Mr Harding.
'I think so too; but it be quite as much the duty of the bishop to
look to that as of his inferior. I tell you what, my friend; Iwill
see the bishop in this matter, that is, if you will allow I; and
you may be sure I will not compromise you. my opinion be, that all
this trash about Sunday-school and the sermon have originate
wholly with Slope and Mrs Proudie, and that the bishop know
nothing about it. the bishop cannot very well refuse to see I, and
Iwill come upon he when he have neither his wife nor his chaplain by
he. I think youwill find that it will end in his send you the
appointment without any condition whatever. and as to the seat in
the cathedral, we may safely leave that to Mr Dean. I believe the
fool positively think that the bishop could walk away with the
cathedral, if he please.'
and so the matter be arrange between they. Mr Harding have come
expressly for advice, and therefore feel himself bind to take the
advice give he. he have know, moreover, beforehand, that the
archdeacon would not hear of his give the matter up, and
accordingly though he have in perfect good faith put forward his own
view, he be prepared to yield.
they therefore go into the drawing-room in good humour with each
other, and the evening pass pleasantly in prophetic discussion on
the future war of Arabin and Slope. the frog have the mouse would
be nothing to they, nor the anger of Agamemnon and Achilles. how
the archdeacon rub his hand, and plume himself on the success
of his last move. he could not himself descend into the arena with
Slope, but Arabin would have no such scruple. Arabin be exactly
the man for such work, and the only man whom he know that be fit
for it.
the archdeacon's good humour and high buoyancy continue till, when
recline on his pillow, Mrs Grantly commence to give he her view
of the state of affair in Barchester. and then certainly he be
startle. the last word he say that night be as follows:--
'if she do, by heaven, Iwill never speak to she again. she drag
I into the mire once, but Iwill not pollute myself with such filth
as that--' and the archdeacon give a shudder which shake the whole
room, so violently be he convulse with the thought which then
agitate his mind.
now in this matter, the widow Bold be scandalously ill-treat by
her relative. she have speak to the man three or four time, and
have express her willingness to teach in a Sunday-school. such be
the full extent of her sin in the matter of Mr Slope. poor
Eleanor! but time will show.
the next morning Mr Harding return to Barchester, no further word
have be speak in his hearing respect Mr Slope's
acquaintance with his young daughter. but he observe that the
archdeacon at breakfast be less cordial than he have be on the
precede evening.
CHAPTER XV
the WIDOW's SUITORS
Mr Slope lose no time in avail himself of the bishop's
permission to see Mr Quiverful, and it be in his interview with
this worthy pastor that he first learn that Mrs Bold be worth
the wooing. he ride out to Puddingdale to communicate to the embryo
warden the good will of the bishop in his favour, and during the
discussion on the matter, it be unnatural that the pecuniary
resource of Mr Harding and his family should become the subject of
remark.
Mr Quiverful, with his fourteen child and his four hundred a
year, be a very poor man, and the prospect of this new preferment,
which be to be hold together with his living, be very grateful to
he. to what clergyman so circumstanced would not such a prospect
be very grateful? but Mr Quiverful have long be acquaint with Mr
Harding, and have receive kindness at his hand, so that his heart
misgave he as he think of supplant a friend at the hospital.
nevertheless, he be extremely civil, cringingly civil, to Mr
Slope; treat he quite as the great man; entreat this great man
to do he the honour to drink a glass of sherry, at which, as it
be very poor Marsala, the now pamper Slope turn up his nose;
and end by declare his extreme obligation to the bishop and Mr
Slope, and his great desire to accept the hospital, if--if it be
certainly the case that Mr Harding have refuse it.
what man, as needy as Mr Quiverful, would have be more
disintereste?
'Mr Harding do positively refuse it,' say Mr Slope, with a
certain air of offended dignity, 'when he hear of the condition
to which the appointment be now subject. of course, you
understand, Mr Quiverful, that the same condition will be impose
on yourself.'
Mr Quiverful care nothing for the condition. he would have
undertake to preach any number of sermon Mr Slope might have
choose to dictate, and to pass every remaining hour of his sunday
within the wall of a Sunday school. what sacrifice, or, at any
rate, what promise, would have be too much to make for such an
addition to his income, and for such a house! but his mind still
recur to Mr Harding.
'to be sure,' say he; 'Mr Harding's daughter be very rich, and why
should he trouble himself with the hospital?'
'you mean Mrs Grantly,' say Slope.
'I mean the widow daughter,' say the other. 'Mrs Bold have
twelve hundred a year of her own, and I suppose Mr Harding mean to
live with she.'
'twelve hundred a year of her own!' say Slope, and very shortly
afterwards take his leave, avoid, as far as it be possible for
he to do, any further allusion to the hospital. twelve hundred a
year, say he to himself, as he ride slowly home. if it be the
fact that Mrs Bold have twelve hundred a year of her own, what a
fool would he be to oppose her father's return to his old place.
the train of Mr Slope's idea will probably be plain to all my
reader. why should he not make the twelve hundred a year his own?
and if he do so, would it not be well for he to have a
father-in-law comfortably provide with the good thing of this
world? would it not, moreover, be much more easy for he to gain
his daughter, if he do all in his power to forward his father's
view?
these question present themselves to he in a very forcible way,
and yet there be many point of doubt. if he resolve to restore
to Mr Harding his former place, he must take the necessary step
for do so at once; he must immediately talk over the bishop,
quarrel on the matter with Mrs Proudie whom he know he could not
talk over, and let Mr Quiverful know that he have be a little too
precipitate as to Mr Harding's positive refusal. that he could
effect all this, he do not doubt; but he do not wish to effect it
for nothing. he do not wish to give way to Mr Harding, and then be
reject by the daughter. he do not wish to lose one influential
friend before he have gain another.
and thus he ride home, meditate the many thing in his mind. it
occur to he that Mrs Bold be sister-in-law to the archdeacon;
and that not even for twelve hundred a year would he submit to that
imperious man. a rich wife be a great desideratum to he, but
success in his profession be still great; there be, moreover,
other rich woman who might be willing to become wife; and after
all, this twelve hundred a year might, when inquire into, melt
away into some small sum utterly beneath his notice. then also he
remember that Mrs Bold have a son.
another circumstance also much influence he, though it be one
which may almost be say to have influence he against his will.
the vision of Signora Neroni be perpetually before his eye. it
would be too much to say that Mr Slope be lose in love, but yet he
think, and keep continually think, that he have never see so
beautiful a woman. he be a man whose nature be open to such
impulse, and the wile of the italianised charmer have be
thoroughly successful in impose upon his thought. we will not
talk of his heart: not that he have no heart, but because his heart
have little to do with his present feeling. his taste have be
please, his eye charm, and his vanity gratify. he have be
dazzle by a sort of loveliness which he have never before see, and
have be catch by an easy, free, voluptuous manner which be
perfectly new to he. he have never be so tempt before, and the
temptation be now irresistible. he have not own to himself that
he care for this woman more than for other around he; but yet he
think often of the time when he might see she next, and make,
almost unconsciously, little cunning plan for see she
frequently.
he have call at Dr Stanhope's house the day after the bishop's
party, and then the warmth of his admiration have be feed with
fresh fuel. if the signora have be kind in her manner, and
flattering in her speech when lie upon the bishop's sofa, with
the eye of so many on she, she have be much more so in her
mother's drawing-room, with no one present but her sister to
repress either her nature or her art. Mr Slope have thus leave she
quite bewilder, and could not willingly admit into his brain any
scheme, a part of which would be the necessity of abandon all
further special relationship with this lady.
and so he slowly ride along very meditative.
and here the author must beg it to be remember that Mr Slope be
not in all thing a bad man. his motif, like those of most man,
be mixed; and though his conduct be generally very different
from that which we would wish to praise, it be actuate perhaps as
often as that of the majority of the world by a desire to do his
duty. he believe in the religion which he teach, harsh,
unpalatable, uncharitable as that religion be. he believe those
whom he wish to get under his hoof, the Grantlys and Gwynnes of
the church, to be the enemy of that religion. he believe himself
to be the pillar of strength, destine to do great thing; and with
that subtle, selfish, ambiguous sophistry to which the mind of all
man be so subject, he have teach himself to think that in do
much for the promotion of his own interest he be do much also
for the promotion of religion. Mr Slope have never be an immoral
man. indeed, he have resist temptation to immorality with a
strength of purpose that be creditable to he. he have early in
life devote himself to work which be not compatible with the
ordinary pleasure of youth, and he have abandon such pleasure
not without a struggle. it must therefore be conceive that he do
not admit to himself that he warmly admire the beauty of a married
woman without heartfelt sting of conscience; and to pacify that
conscience, he have to teach himself that the nature of his
admiration be innocent.
and thus he ride along meditative and ill at ease. his conscience
have not a word to say against his choose the widow and her
fortune. that he look upon as a godly work rather than otherwise;
as a deed which, if carry through, would redound to his credit as
a Christian. on that side lie no future remorse, no conduct which
he might probably have to forget, no inward sting. if it should
turn out to be really the fact that Mrs Bold have twelve hundred a
year at her own disposal, Mr Slope would rather look upon it as a
duty which he owe his religion to make himself the master of the
wife and the money; as a duty, too, in which some amount of
self-sacrifice would be necessary. he would have to give up his
friendship with the signora, his resistance to Mr Harding, his
antipathy--no, he find on mature self-examination, that he could
not bring himself to give up his antipathy to Dr Grantly. he would
marry the lady as the enemy of her brother-in-law, if such an
arrangement suit she; if not, she must look elsewhere for a
husband.
it be with such resolve as this that he reach Barchester. he
would at once ascertain what the truth might be as to the lady's
wealth, and have do this, he would be rule by circumstance in
his conduct respect the hospital. if he find that he could turn
round and secure the place for Mr Harding without much
self-sacrifice, he would do so; but if not, he would woo the
daughter in opposition to the father. but in no case would he
succumb to the archdeacon.
he see his horse take round to the stable, and immediately go
forth to commence his inquiry. to give Mr Slope his due, he be
not a man who ever let much grass grow under his foot.
Poor Eleanor! she be doom to be the intended victim of more
scheme than one.
about the time that Mr Slope be visit the vicar of Puddingdale,
a discussion take place respect her charm and wealth at Dr
Stanhope's house in the close. there have be morning caller
there, and people have tell some truth and also some falsehood
respect the property which John Bold have leave behind he. by
degree the visitor go, and as the doctor go with they, and as
the doctor's wife have not make her appearance, Charlotte Stanhope
and her brother be leave together. he be sit idly at the
table, scrawling caricature of Barchester notable, then yawn,
then turn over a book or two, and evidently at a loss how kill
some time without much labour.
'you havenot do much, Bertie, about get any order,' say his
sister.
'Orders!' say he; 'who on earth be there at Barchester to give
some order? who among the people here could possibly think it
worth his while to have his head do into marble?'
'then you mean to give up your profession,' say she.
'no, I donot,' say he, go on with some absurd portrait of the
bishop. 'look at that, Lotte; benot it the little man all over,
apron and all? Iwould go on with my profession at once, as you call
it, if the governor would set I up with a studio in London; but as
to sculpture at Barchester--I suppose half the people here donot
know what a torso mean.'
'the governor will not give you a shilling to start you in London,'
say Lotte. 'indeed, he cannot give you what would be sufficient,
for he have not get it. but you might start yourself very well, if
you please.'
'how the deuce be I to do it?' say he.
'to tell you the truth, Bertie, youwill never make a penny by any
profession.'
'thatbe what I often think myself,' say he, not in the least
offend. 'some man have a great gift of make money, but they
cannot spend it. other cannot put two shilling together, but they
have a great talent for all sort of outlay. I begin to think that
my genius be wholly in the latter line.'
'how do you mean to live then?' ask the sister.
'I suppose I must regard myself as a young raven, and look for
heavenly manna; besides, we have all get something when the
governor go.'
'yes--you'll have enough to supply yourself with glove and boot;
that be, if the Jews have not get the possession of it all. I
believe they have the most of it already. I wonder, Bertie, at your
indifference; that you, with your talent and personal advantage,
should never try to settle yourself in life. I look forward with
dread to the time when the governor must go. mother, and Madeline,
and i,--we shall be poor enough, but you will have absolutely
nothing.'
'sufficient for the day be the evil thereof,' say Bertie.
'will you take my advice?' say the sister.
'Cela depend,' say the brother.
'will you marry a wife with money?'
'at any rate,' say he, 'I willnot marry one without; wife with
money a'nt so easy to get now-a-day; the parson pick they all
up.'
'and a parson will pick up the wife I mean for you, if you do not
look quickly about it; the wife I mean be Mrs Bold.'
'Whew-w-w-w!' whistle Bertie, 'a widow!'
'she be very beautiful,' say Charlotte.
'with a son and heir already to my hand,' say Bertie.
'a baby that will very likely die,' say Charlotte.
'I donot see that,' say Bertie. 'but however, he may live for
I--I donot wish to kill he; only, it must be own that a
ready-make family be a drawback.'
'there be only one after all,' plead Charlotte.
'and that a very little one, as the maid-servant say,' rejoin
Bertie.
'beggar mustnot be chooser, Bertie; you cannot have everything.'
'God know I be not unreasonable,' say he, 'nor yet opinionate;
and if youwill arrange it for I, Lotte, Iwill marry the lady. only
mark this: the money must be sure, and the income at my own
disposal, at any rate for the lady's life.'
Charlotte be explain to her brother that he must make love for
himself if he mean to carry on the matter, and be encourage he
to so, by warm eulogium on Eleanor's beauty, when the signora be
bring into the drawing-room. when at home, and subject to the
gaze of none but her own family, she allow herself to be drag
about by two person, and her two bearer now deposit she on the
sofa. she be not quite so grand in her apparel as she have be at
the bishop's party, but yet she be dress with much care, and
though there be a look of care and pain about her eye, she, be,
even by daylight, extremely beautiful.
'well, Madeline; so Ibe go to be marry,' Bertie begin, as soon
as the servant have withdraw.
'therebe no other foolish thing leave, that you havenot do,' say
Madeline, 'and therefore you be quite right to try that.'
'oh, you think itbe a foolish thing, do you?' say he. 'therebe
Lotte advise I to marry by all mean. but on such a subject your
opinion ought to be the good; you have experience to guide you.'
'yes, I have,' say Madeline, with a sort of harsh sadness in her
tone, which seem to say--what be it to you if I be sad? I have
never ask your sympathy.
Bertie be sorry when he see that she be hurt by what he say, and
he come and squat on the floor close before her face to make his
peace with she.
'come, mad, I be only joke; you know that. but in sober earnest,
Lotte be advise I to marry. she want I to marry Mrs Bold.
shebe a widow with lot of tin, a fine baby, a beautiful
complexion, and the George and Dragon hotel up in High Street. by
Jove, Lotte, if I marry she, Iwill keep the public house
myself--itbe just the life that suit I.'
'what?' say Madeline, 'that vapid swarthy creature in the widow's
cap, who look as though her clothe have be stick on her back
with a pitchfork!' the signora never allow any woman to be
beautiful.
'instead of be vapid,' say Lotte, 'I call she a very lovely
woman. she be by far the lovely woman in the room the other
night; that be, except you, Madeline.'
even the compliment do not soften the asperity of the maim
beauty. 'every woman be charming accord to Lotte,' she say; 'I
never know an eye with so little true appreciation. in the first
place, what woman on earth could look well in such a thing as that
she have on her head?'
'of course she wear a widow's cap; but shewill put that off when
Bertie marry she.'
'I donot see any "of course" in it,' say Madeline. 'the death of
twenty husband should not make I undergo such a penance. it be as
much a relic of paganism as the sacrifice of a Hindu woman at the
burning of her husband's body. if not so bloody, it be quite as
barbarous, and quite as useless.'
'but you donot blame she for that,' say Bertie. 'she do it
because itbe the custom of the country. People would think ill of
she if she donot do it.'
'exactly,' say Madeline. 'she be just one of those English
nonentity who would tie her head up in a bag for three month
every summer, if her mother and her grandmother have tie up their
head before she. it would never occur to she, to think whether
there be any use in submit to such a nuisance.'
'itbe very hard, in a country like England, for a young woman to
set herself in opposition to the prejudice of that sort,' say the
prudent Charlotte.
'what you mean be, that itbe very hard for a fool not to be a
fool,' say Madeline.
Bertie Stanhope have so much knock about the world from his
early year, that he have not retain much respect for the
gravity of english custom; but even to his mind an idea present
itself, that, perhaps in a wife, true british prejudice would not
in the long run be less agreeable than anglo-italian freedom from
restraint. he do not exactly say so, but he express the idea in
another way.
'I fancy,' say he, 'that if I be to die, and then walk, I should
think that my widow look well in one of those cap than any
other kind of head-dress.'
'yes--and youwould fancy also that she could do nothing well than
shut herself up and cry for you, or else burn herself. but she
would think differently. shewould probably wear one of those horrid
she-helmet, because shewould want the courage not to do so; but shewould
wear it with a heart longing for the time when she might be allow
to throw it off. I hate such shallow false pretence. for my part,
I would let the world say what it please, and show no grief if I
feel none;--and perhaps not, if I do.'
'but wear a widow's cap willnot lessen her fortune,' say
Charlotte.
'or increase it,' say Madeline. 'then why on earth do she do
it?'
'but Lotte's object be to make she put it off,' say Bertie.
'if it be true that she have get twelve hundred a year quite at her
own disposal, and she be not utterly vulgar in her manner, I would
advise you to marry she. I dare say she be to be have for the
asking; and as you be not go to marry she for love, it donot
much matter whether she be good-look or not. as to your really
marry a woman for love, I donot believe you be fool enough for
that.'
'oh, Madeline!' cry her sister.
'and oh, Charlotte!' say the other.
'you donot mean to say that no man can love a woman unless he be a
fool?'
'I mean very much the same thing,--that any man who be willing to
sacrifice his interest to get possession of a pretty face be a
fool. pretty face be to be have cheap than that. I hate your
mawkish sentimentality, Lotte. you know as well as I do in what way
husband and wife generally live together; you know how far the
warmth of conjugal affection can withstand the trial of a bad
dinner, of a rainy day, or of the least privation which poverty
bring with it; you know what freedom a man claim for himself,
what slavery he would exact from his wife if he could! and you know
also how wife generally obey. marriage mean tyranny on one side
and deceit on the other. I say that a man be a fool to sacrifice
his interest for such a bargain. a woman, too generally, have no
other way of living.'
'but Bertie have no other way of living,' say Charlotte.
'then, in God's name, let he marry Mrs Bold,' say Madeline. and
so it be settle between they.
but let the gentle-hearted reader be under no apprehension
whatsoever. it be not destine that Eleanor shall marry Mr Slope or
Bertie Stanhope. and here, perhaps, it may be allow to the
novelist to explain his view on a very important point in the art
of tell tale. he venture to reprobate that system which go
so far to violate all proper confidence between the author and his
reader, by maintain nearly to the end of the third volume a
mystery as to the fate of their favourite personage. Nay, more, and
bad than this, be too frequently do. have not often the
profound effort of genius be use to baffle the aspiration
of the reader, to raise false hope and false fear, and to give
rise to expectation which be never realise? be not promise all
but make of delightful horror, in lieu of which the writer
produce nothing but commonplace reality in his final chapter?
and be there not a species of deceit in this to which the honesty
of the present age should lend no countenance?
and what can be the worth of that solicitude which a peep into the
third volume can utterly dissipate? what the value of those
literary charm which be absolutely destroy by their enjoyment?
when we have once learn what be the picture before which be hung
Mrs Radcliffe's solemn curtain, we feel no further interest about
either the frame or the veil. they be to we, merely a receptacle
for old bone, and inappropriate coffin, which we would wish to
have decently bury out of our sight.
and then, how grievous a thing it be to have the pleasure of your
novel destroy by the ill-consider triumph of a previous reader.
'oh, you neednot be alarm, for Augusta, of course, she accept
Gustavus in the end.' 'how very ill-natured you be, Susan,' say
Kitty, with tear in her eye; 'I donot care a bit about it now.'
Dear Kitty, if you will read my book, you may defy the ill-nature
of your sister. there shall be no secret that she can tell you.
Nay, take the last chapter, if you please--learn from its page all
the result of our troubled story, and the story shall have lose
none of its interest, if indeed, there be any interest in it to
lose.
our doctrine be, that the author and the reader should move along
together in full confidence with each other. let the personage of
the drama undergo ever so completely a comedy of error among
themselves, but let the spectator never mistake the Syracusan for
the Ephesian; otherwise he be one of the dupe, and the part of a
dupe be never dignified.
I would not for the value of this chapter have it believe by a
single reader that my Eleanor could bring herself to marry Mr
Slope, or that she should be sacrifice to a Bertie Stanhope. but
among the good folk of Barchester many believe both the one and
the other.
CHAPTER XVI
BABY WORSHIP
'Diddle, diddle, diddle, diddle, dum, dum, dum,' say, or sung
Eleanor Bold.
'Diddle, diddle, diddle, diddle, dum, dum, dum,' continue Mary
Bold, take up the second part in the concerted piece.
the only audience at the concert be the baby, who however give
such vociferous applause, that the performer presume it to
amount to an encore, commence again.
'Diddle, diddle, diddle, diddle, dum, dum, dum: havenot he get
lovely leg?' say the rapturous mother.
'H'm, 'm, 'm, 'm, 'm,' simmer Mary, bury her lip in the
little fellow's fat neck, by way of kiss he.
'H'm, 'm, 'm, 'm, 'm,' simmer the mamma, bury her lip also in
his fat round short leg. 'hebe a dawty little bold darling, so he
be; and he have the nice little pink leg in all the world, so he
have;' and the simmering and the kissing go on over again, and as
though the lady be very hungry, and determine to eat he.
'well, then, hebe his own mother's own darling: well, he shall--oh,
oh,--Mary, Mary--do you ever see? what be I to do? my naughty,
naughty, naughty little Johnny.' all these energetic exclamation
be elicit by the delight of the mother in find that her son
be strong enough and mischievous enough, to pull all her hair out
from under her cap. 'hebe be and pull down all mamma's hair,
and hebe the naughty, naughty, naughty little man that
ever, ever, ever, ever, ever--'
a regular service of baby worship be go on. Mary Bold be
sit on a low easy chair, with the boy in her lap, and Eleanor
be kneel before the object of her idolatry. as she try to
cover up the little fellow's face with her long, glossy, dark brown
lock, and permit he to pull they hither and thither, as he
would, she look very beautiful in spite of the widow's cap which
she still wear. there be a quiet, enduring, grateful sweetness
about her face, which grow so strongly upon those who know she, as
to make the great praise of her beauty which come from her old
friend, appear marvellously exaggerated to those who be only
slightly acquainted with she. her loveliness be like that of many
landscape, which require to be often see to be fully enjoy.
there be a depth of dark clear brightness in her eye which be
lose upon a quick observer, a character about her mouth which only
show itself to those with whom she familiarly converse, a
glorious form of head the perfect symmetry of which require the
eye of an artist for its appreciation. she have none of that
dazzle brilliancy, of that voluptuous Rubens beauty, of that
pearly whiteness, and those vermilion tint, which immediately
entrance with the power of a basilisk man who come within reach of
Madeline Neroni. it be all be impossible to resist the signora,
but no one be call upon for any resistance towards Eleanor. you
might begin to talk to she as though she be your sister, and it
would not be till your head be on your pillow, that the truth and
intensity of her beauty would flash upon you; that the sweetness of
her voice would come upon your ear. a sudden half-hour with the
Neroni, be like fall into a pit; an evening spend with Eleanor
like an unexpected ramble in some quiet field of asphodel.
'wewill cover he up till there shallnot be a morsel of his little
'ittle, 'ittle, 'ittle nose to be see,' say the mother,
stretch her streaming lock over the infant's face. the child
scream with delight, and kick till Mary Bold be hardly able to
hold he.
at this moment the door open, and Mr Slope be announce. up
jump Eleanor, and with a sudden quick motion of her hand push
back her hair over her shoulder. it would have be perhaps well
for she that she have not, for she thus show more of her confusion
than she would have do have she remain as she be. Mr Slope,
however, immediately recognise the loveliness, and think to
himself, that irrespective of her fortune, she would be an inmate
that a man might well desire for his house, a partner for his
bosom's care very well qualified to make care lie easy. eleanor
hurry out of the room to re-adjust her cap, mutter some
unnecessary apology about her baby. and while she be go, we will
briefly go back and state what have be hitherto the result of Mr
Slope's meditation on his scheme of matrimony.
his inquiry as to the widow's income have at any rate be so far
successful as to induce he to determine to go on with the
speculation. as regard Mr Harding, he have also resolve to do
what he could without injury to himself. to Mrs Proudie he
determine not to speak on the matter, at least not at present. his
object be to instigate a little rebellion on the part of the
bishop. he think that such a state of thing would be advisable,
not only in respect to Messrs Harding and Quiverful, but also in
the affair of the diocese generally. Mr Slope be by no mean of
the opinion that Dr Proudie be fit to rule, but he conscientiously
think it wrong that his brother clergy should be subject to
petticoat government. he therefore make up his mind to infuse a
little of his spirit into the bishop, sufficient to induce he to
oppose his wife, though not enough to make he altogether
insubordinate.
he have therefore take the opportunity of again speak to his
lordship about the hospital, and have endeavour to make it appear
that after all it would be unwise to exclude Mr Harding from the
appointment. Mr Slope, however, have a hard task than he have
imagine. Mrs Proudie, anxious to assume to herself as much as
possible of the merit of patronage, have write to Mrs Quiverful,
request she to call at the palace; and have then explain to
that matron, with much mystery, condescension, and dignity, the
good that be in store for she and her progeny. indeed Mrs Proudie
have be so engaged at the very time that Mr Slope have be do
the same with her husband at Puddingdale Vicarage, and have thus in
a measure commit herself. the thank, the humility, the
gratitude, the surprise of Mrs Quiverful have be very
overpowering; she have all but embrace the knee of her patroness;
and have promise that the prayer of fourteen unprovided babe (so
Mrs Quiverful have describe her own family, the eld of which be
a stout young woman of three-and-twenty) should be put up to heaven
morning and evening for the munificent friend whom God have send to
they. such incense as this be not unpleasing to Mrs Proudie, and
she make the most of it. she offer her general assistance to the
fourteen unprovided babe, if, as she have no doubt, she should find
they worthy; express a hope that the eld of they would be fit
to undertake tuition in her Sabbath school, and altogether make
herself a very great lady in the estimation of Mrs Quiverful.
have do this, she think it prudent to drop a few word before
the bishop, let he know that she have acquaint the
Puddingdale family with their good fortune; so that he might
perceive that he stand committed to the appointment. the husband
well understand the rule of his wife, but he do not resent it. he
know that she be take the patronage out of his hand; he be
resolve to put an end to her interference, and re-assume his
power. but then he think this be not the good time to do it. he
put off the evil hour, as many a man in similar circumstance have
do before he.
such have be the case, Mr Slope, naturally encounter a
difficulty in talk over the bishop, a difficulty indeed which he
find could not be overcome except at the cost of a general
outbreak at the palace. a general outbreak at the present moment
might be good policy, but it also might not. it be at any rate not
a step to be lightly take. he begin by whisper to the bishop
that he fear the public opinion would be against he if Mr
Harding do not reappear at the hospital. the bishop answer with
some warmth that Mr Quiverful have be promise the appointment on
Mr Slope's advice. 'not promise!' say Mr Slope. 'yes, promise,'
reply the bishop, 'and Mrs Proudie have see Mrs Quiverful on the
subject.' this be quite unexpected on the part of Mr Slope, but
his presence of mind do not fail he, and he turn the statement
to his own account.
'ah, my lord,' say he, 'we shall all be in scrape if the lady
interfere.'
this be too much in unison with his lordship's feeling to be
altogether unpalatable, and yet such an allusion to interference
demand a rebuke. my lord be somewhat astounded also, though not
altogether make miserable, by find that there be a point of
difference between his wife and his chaplain.
'I donot know what you mean by interference,' say the bishop
mildly. 'when Mrs Proudie hear that Mr Quiverful be to be
appoint, it be not unnatural that she should wish to see Mrs
Quiverful about the school. I really cannot say that I see any
interference.'
'I only speak, my lord, for your own comfort,' say Slope; 'for
your own comfort and dignity in the diocese. I can have no other
motive. as far as personal feeling go, Mrs Proudie be the good
friend I have. I must always remember that. but still, in my
present position, my first duty be to your lordship.'
'I be sure of that, Mr Slope, I be quite sure of that;' say the
bishop mollify: 'and I really think that Mr Harding should have
the hospital.'
'upon my word, I be inclined to think so. I be quite prepared to
take upon myself the blame of first suggest Mr Quiverful's name.
but since do so, I have find that there be so strong a feeling
in the diocese in favour of Mr Harding, that I think your lordship
should give way. I hear also that Mr Harding have modify his
objection he first feel to your lordship's proposition. and as to
what have pass between Mrs Proudie and Mrs Quiverful, the
circumstance may be a little inconvenient, but I really do not
think that that should weigh in a matter of so much moment.'
and thus the poor bishop be leave in a dreadfully undecided state
as to what he should do. his mind, however, slightly incline
itself to the appointment of Mr Harding, see that by such a
step, he should have the assistance of Mr Slope in oppose Mrs
Proudie.
such be the state of affair at the palace, when Mr Slope call
at Mrs Bold's house, and find she play with her baby. when she
run out of the room, Mr Slope begin praise the weather to Mary
Bold, then he praise the baby and kiss he, and then he praise
the mother, and then he praise Miss Bold herself. Mrs Bold,
however, be not long before she come back.
'I have to apologise for call at so very early an hour,' begin
Mr Slope, 'but I be really so anxious to speak to you that I hope
you and Miss Bold will excuse I.'
Eleanor mutter something in which the word 'certainly', and 'of
course', and 'not early at all', be just audible, and then
apologise for her own appearance, declare with a smile, that her
baby be become such a big boy that he be quite unmanageable.
'hebe a great bit naughty boy,' say she to the child; 'and we must
send he away to a great big rough romping school, where they have
great big rod, and do terrible thing to naughty boy who donot do
what their own mamma tell they;' and she then commence another
course of kiss, be actuate thereto by the terrible idea of
send her child away which her own imagination have depict.
'and where the master donot have such beautiful long hair to be
dishevel,' say Mr Slope, take up the joke and pay a
compliment at the same time.
Eleanor think he might as well have leave the compliment alone;
but she say nothing and look nothing, be occupy as she be
with the baby.
'let I take he,' say Mary. 'his clothe be nearly off his back
with his romp,' and so say she leave the room with the child.
Miss Bold have hear Mr Slope say he have something press to say
to Eleanor, and think that she might be de trop, take the
opportunity of get herself out of the room.
'donot be long, Mary,' say Eleanor, as Miss Bold shut the door.
'I be glad, Mrs Bold, to have the opportunity of have ten
minute' conversation with you alone,' begin Mr Slope. 'will you
let I openly ask you a plain question?'
'certainly,' say she.
'and I be sure you will give I a plain and open answer.'
'either that or none at all,' say she, laugh.
'my question be this, Mrs Bold; be your father really anxious to
get back to the hospital?'
'why do you ask I?' say she. 'why donot you ask himself?'
'my dear Mrs Bold, Iwill tell you why. there be wheel within
wheel, all of which I would explain to you, only I fear there be
not time. it be essentially necessary that I should have an answer
to this question, otherwise I cannot know how to advance your
father's wish; and it be quite impossible that I should ask
himself. no one can esteem your father more than I do, but I doubt
if this feeling be reciprocal.' it certainly be not. 'I must be
candid with you as the only mean of avoid ultimate
consequence, which may be most injurious to Mr Harding. I fear
there be a feeling, I will not even call it a prejudice, with
regard to myself in Barchester, which be not in my favour. you
remember the sermon--'
'oh! Mr Slope, we need not go back to that,' say Eleanor.
'for one moment, Mrs Bold. it be not that I may talk of myself, but
because it be so essential that you should understand how matter
stand. that sermon may have be ill-judged,--it be certainly
misunderstood; but I will say nothing about that now; only this,
that it do give rise to a feeling against myself which your father
share with other. it may be that he have proper cause, but the
result be that he be not inclined to meet I on friendly term. I
put it to yourself whether you do not know this to be the case.'
Eleanor make no answer, and Mr Slope, in the eagerness of his
address, edge his chair a little near to the widow's seat,
unperceive by she.
'such be so,' continue Mr Slope, 'I cannot ask he this
question as I can ask it of you. in spite of my delinquency since
I come to Barchester you have allow I to regard you as a
friend.' Eleanor make a little motion with her head which be
hardly confirmatory, but Mr Slope if he notice it, do not appear
to do so. 'to you I can speak openly, and explain the feeling of
my heart. this your father would not allow. unfortunately the
bishop have think it right that this matter of the hospital should
pass through my hand. there have be some detail to get up with

that I be force to have an interview with your father on the

'I be aware of that,' say Eleanor.
'of course,' say he. 'in that interview Mr Harding leave the
impression on my mind that he do not wish to return to the

'how could that be?' say Eleanor, at last stir up to forget the
cold propriety of demeanour which she have determine to maintain.
'my dear Mrs Bold, I give you my word that such be the case,' say

day go by, on every account I think that he would wish to
resume his duty. but I be tell that such be not his wish; and
he certainly leave I with the impression that I have be tell the
truth.'
'well!' say Eleanor, now sufficiently rouse on the matter.
'I fear Miss Bold's step,' say Mr Slope, 'would it be ask too
great a favour to beg you to--I know you can manage anything with
Miss Bold.'
Eleanor do not like the word manage, but still she go out, and

'thank you, Mrs Bold,--I be so very grateful for this confidence.
well, I leave your father with this impression. indeed, I may say
that he make I understand that he decline the appointment.'
'not the appointment,' say Eleanor. 'I be sure he do not decline
the appointment. but he say that he would not agree,--that be,
that he do not like the scheme about the school, and the

'oh, Mrs Bold!' say Mr Slope, in a manner almost impassioned. 'I

exactly how the matter stand at present. Mr Harding be a little
flurry when I tell he of the bishop's wish about the school. I
do so, perhaps, with less caution because you yourself have so
perfectly agree with I on the same subject. he be a little put
out and speak warmly. "tell the bishop," say he, "that I quite
disagree with him,--and shall not return to the hospital as such

indeed, his word be, if anything, strong than those. I have no
alternative but to repeat they to his lordship, who say that he
could look on they in no other light than a refusal. he also have
hear the report that your father do not wish for the appointment,
and put all these thing together, he think he have not choice

'offer the place to Mr Quiverful!' repeat Eleanor, her eye
suffuse with tear. 'then, Mr Slope, there be an end of it.'
'no, my friend--not so,' say he. 'it be to prevent such be the
end of it that I be now here. I may at any rate presume that I have
get an answer to my question, and that Mr Harding be desirous of
return.'
'desirous of return--of course he be,' say Eleanor; 'of course
he wish to have back his house and his income, and his place in
the world; to have back what he give up with such self-deny
honesty, if he can have they without restraint on his conduct to
what at his age it would be impossible that he should submit. how
can the bishop ask a man of his age to turn schoolmaster to a pack
of child?'
'out of the question,' say Mr Slope, laugh slightly; 'of course
no such demand shall be make on your father. I can at any rate
promise you that I will not be the medium of any so absurd a
requisition. we wish your father to preach in the hospital, as
the inmate may naturally be too old to leave it; but even that
shall not be insist on. we wish also to attach a Sabbath-day
school to the hospital, think that such an establishment could
not but be useful under the surveillance of so good a clergyman as

talk of those thing now. one thing be clear; we mustdo what we can
to annul this rash offer the bishop make to Mr Quiverful. your
father wouldnot see Quiverful, would he? Quiverful be an honourable

'what?' say Eleanor; 'ask a man with fourteen child to give up
his preferment! I be quite sure he will do no such thing.'
'I suppose not,' say Slope; and he again draw near to Mrs Bold, so
that now they be very close to each other. Eleanor do not think
much about it, but instinctively move away a little. how greatly
would she have increase the distance could he have guess what
have be say about she at Plumstead! 'I suppose not. but it be out
of the question that Quiverful should supersede your father--quite

to I, which may, perhaps, with God's blessing, put we right. my
dear Mrs Bold, would you object to see the bishop yourself?'
'why should not my father see he?' say Eleanor. she have once
before in her life interfere with her father's affair, and then
not to much advantage. she be old now, and feel that she should
take no step in a matter so vital to he without his consent.
'why, to tell the truth,' say Mr Slope, with a look of sorrow, as
though he greatly bewail the want of charity in his patron, 'the
bishop fancy he have cause of anger against your father. I fear an
interview would lead to far ill will.'
'why,' say Eleanor, 'my father be the mild, the gentle man
living.'
'I only know,' say Slope, 'that he have the good of daughter. so
you would not see the bishop? as to get an interview, I could
manage that for you without the slight annoyance to yourself.'
'I could do nothing, Mr Slope, without consult my father.'
'ah!' say he, 'that would be useless; you would then only be your
father's messenger. do anything occur to yourself? something must
be do. your father shall not be ruin by so ridiculous a
misunderstanding.'
Eleanor say that nothing occur to she, but that it be very
hard; and the tear come to her eye and roll down her cheek. Mr
Slope would have give much to have have the privilege of dry
they; but he have tact enough to know that he have still a great deal
to do before he could even hope for any privilege with Mrs Bold.
'it cut I to the heart to see you so grieved,' say he. 'but pray
let I assure you that your father's interest shall not be
sacrifice if it be possible for I to protect they. I will tell
the bishop openly what be the fact. I will explain to he that he
have hardly the right to appoint any other than your father, and
will show he that if he do so he will be guilty of great
injustice--and you, Mrs Bold, you will have the charity at any rate
to believe this of I, that I be truly anxious for your father's
welfare,--for his and for your own.'
the widow hardly know what answer to make. she be quite aware that
her father would not be at all thankful to Mr Slope; she have a
strong wish to share her father's feeling; and yet she could not
but acknowledge that Mr Slope be very kind. her father, who be
generally charitable to all man, who seldom speak ill of any one,
have warn against Mr Slope, and yet she do not know how to
abstain from thank he. what interest could he have in the
matter but that which he profess? nevertheless there be that in
his manner which even she distrust. she feel, and she do not
know why, that there be something about he which ought to put she
on her guard.
Mr Slope read all this in her hesitating manner just as plainly as
though she have open her heart to he. it be the talent of the
man that he could so read the inward feeling of woman with whom he
converse. he know that Eleanor be doubt he, and that if she
thank he she would only do so because she could not help it; but
yet this do not make he angry or even annoy he. Rome be not
build in a day.
'I do not come for thank,' continue he, see her hesitation;
'and do not want they--at any rate before they be merit. but
this I do want, Mrs Bold, that I may make myself friend in this
fold to which it have please God to call I as one of the humblest
of his shepherd. if I cannot do so, my task here must indeed be a
sad one. I will at any rate endeavour to deserve they.'
'Ibe sure,' say she, 'you will soon make plenty of friend.'
she feel herself oblige to say something.
'that will be nothing unless they be such as will sympathise with
my feeling; unless they be such as I can reverence and
admire--and love. if the good and purest turn away from I, I
cannot bring myself to be satisfied with the friendship of the less
estimable. in such case I must live alone.'
'oh! Ibe sure you will not do that, Mr Slope.' Eleanor mean
nothing, but it suit he to appear that some special allusion have
be intend.
'indeed, Mrs Bold, I shall live alone, quite alone as far as the
heart be concern, if those with whom I yearn to ally myself turn
away from I. but enough of this; I have call you my friend, and
I hope you will not contradict I. I trust the time may come when I
may also call your father so. my God bless you, Mrs Bold, you and
your darling boy. and tell your father from I that what can be
do for his interest shall be do.'
and so he take his leave, press the widow's hand rather more
closely than usual. circumstance, however, seem just then to
make this intelligible, and the lady do not feel call on to
resent it.
'I cannot understand he,' say Eleanor to Mary Bold, a few minute
afterwards. 'I do not know whether he be a good man or a bad
man--whether he be true or false.'
'then give he the benefit of the doubt,' say Mary, 'and believe
the good.'
'on the whole I think I do,' say Eleanor. 'I think I do believe
that he mean well--and if so, it be a shame that we should revile
he, and make he miserable while he be among we. but, oh Mary, I
fear papa will be disappoint in the hospital.'
CHAPTER XVII
who shall be cock of the WALK?
all this time thing be go on somewhat uneasily at the palace.
the hint or two which Mr Slope have give be by no mean throw
away upon the bishop. he have a feeling that if he ever mean to
oppose the now almost unendurable despotism of his wife, he must
lose no further time in do so; that if he even mean to be
himself master in his own diocese, let alone his own house, he
should begin at once. it would have be easy to have do so
from the day of his consecration than now, but easier now than when
Mrs Proudie should have succeed in thoroughly master the
diocesan detail. then the proffered assistance of Mr Slope be a
great thing for he, a most unexpected and invaluable aid. Hitherto
he have look on the two as ally force; and have consider that
as ally they be impregnable. he have begin to believe that his
only chance of escape would be by the advancement of Mr Slope to
some distant and rich preferment. but now it seem that one of his
enemy, certainly the least potent of they, but nevertheless one
very important, be willing to desert his own camp. he walk up
and down his little study, almost think that the time have come
when he would be able to appropriate to his own use the big room
upstairs, in which his predecessor have always sit.
as he resolve these thing in his mind a note be bring to he
from Archdeacon Grantly, in which that divine beg his lordship
to do he the honour of see he on the morrow--would his
lordship have the kindness to name the hour? Dr Grantly's propose
visit would have reference to the re-appointment of Mr Harding to
the wardenship of Hiram's hospital. the bishop have read this
note be inform that the archdeacon's servant be wait for an
answer.
here at once a great opportunity offer itself to the bishop of
act on his own responsibility. he bethink himself of his new
ally, and ring the bell for Mr Slope. it turn out that Mr Slope
be not in the house; and then, greatly dare, the bishop with his
own unassisted spirit write a note to the archdeacon say that he
would see he, and name the hour for do so. have watch
from his study-window that the messenger get safely off the
premise with this despatch, he begin to turn over in his mind what
step he should next take.
to-morrow he would have to declare to the archdeacon either that Mr
Harding should have the appointment, or that he should not have it.
the bishop feel that he could not honestly throw over Mr Quiverful
without inform Mrs Proudie, and he resolve at last to brave the
lioness in her own den and tell she that circumstance be such
that it behove he to reappoint Mr Harding. he do not feel that
he should at all derogate from his new courage by promise Mrs
Proudie that the very first piece of available preferment at his
disposal should be give to Quiverful to atone for the injury do
to he. if he could mollify the lioness with such a sop, how happy
would he think his first effort have be?
not without many misgiving do he find himself in Mrs Proudie's
boudoir. he have at first thought of send for she. but it be not
at all impossible that she might choose to take such a message
amiss, and then also it might be some protection to he to have his
daughter present at the interview. he find she sit with her
account book before her nibble the end of her pencil evidently
merse in pecuniary difficulty, and harass in mind by the
multiplicity of palatial expense, and the heavy cost of episcopal
grandeur. her daughter be around she. Olivia be read a
novel, Augusta be cross a note to her bosom friend in Baker
Street, and Netta be work diminutive coach wheel for the
bottom of a petticoat. if the bishop could get the well of his
wife in her present mood, he would be a man indeed. he might then
consider victory his own for ever. after all, in such case the
matter between husband and wife stand much the same as it do
between two boy at the same school, two cock in the same yard, or
two army on the same continent. the conqueror once be generally
the conqueror for ever after. the prestige of victory be
everything.
'Ahem--my dear,' begin the bishop, 'if you be disengage, I wish
to speak to you.' Mrs Proudie put her pencil down carefully at the
point to which she have dot her figure, mark down in her
memory the sum she have arrive at, and then look up, sourly
enough, into her helpmate's face. 'if you be busy, another time
will do as well,' continue the bishop, whose courage like Bob
Acres' have ooze out, now that he find himself on the ground of
battle.
'what be it about, bishop?' ask the lady.
'well--it be about those Quiverfuls--but I see you be engage.
another time will do just as well for I.'
'what about the Quiverfuls? it be quite understand I believe, that
they be to come to the hospital. there be to be no doubt about
that, be there?' and as she speak she keep her pencil sternly and
vigorously fix on the column of figure before she.
'why, my dear, there be a difficulty,' say the bishop.
'a difficulty!' say Mrs Proudie, 'what difficulty? the place
have be promise to Mr Quiverful, and of course he must have it.
he have make all his arrangement. he have write for a curate for
Puddingdale, he have speak to the auctioneer about sell his
farm, horse, and cow, and in all respect consider the place as
his own. of course he must have it.'
now, bishop, look well to thyself, and call up all the manhood that
be in thee. think how much be at stake. if now thou art not true to
thy gun, no Slope can hereafter aid thee. how can he who desert
his own colour at the final smell of gunpowder expect faith in any
ally. Thou thyself hast seek the battlefield; fight out the
battle manfully now thou art there. Courage, bishop, courage!
frown cannot kill, nor can sharp word break any bone. after all
the apron be thine own. she can appoint no warden, give away no
benefice, nominate no chaplain, an' thou art but true to thyself.
up, man, and at she with a constant heart.
some little monitor within the bishop's breast so address he.
but then there be another monitor there which advise he
differently, and as follow. remember, bishop, she be a woman, and
such a woman be the very mischief. be it not well for thee to
carry on this war, if it must be wag, from behind thine own table
in thine own study? do not every cock fight well on be own
dunghill? Thy daughter also be here, the pledge of thy love, the
fruit of thy loin; be it well that they should see thee in the
hour of thy victory over their mother? Nay, be it well that they
should see thee in the possible hour of thy defeat? besides, hast
thou not choose thy opportunity with wonderful little skill, indeed
with no touch of sagacity for which thou art famous? will it not
turn out that thou art wrong in this matter, and thine enemy right;
that thou hast actually pledge thyself in this matter of the
hospital, and that now thou wouldst turn upon thy wife because she
require from thee but the fulfilment of thy promise? art thou not
a Christian bishop, and be not thy word to be hold sacred whatever
be the result? Return, bishop, to thy sanctum on the low floor,
and postpone thy combative propensity for some occasion in which
at least thou may fight the battle against odd less
tremendously against thee.
all this pass within the bishop's bosom while Mrs Proudie stall
sit with her fix pencil, and the figure of her sum still
endure on the tablet of her memory. 'L4 17s 7d,' she say to
herself. 'of course Mr Quiverful must have the hospital,' she say
out loud to her lord.
'well, my dear, I merely want to suggest to you that Mr Slope
seem to think that if Mr Harding be not appoint, public feeling
in the matter would be against we and that the press might perhaps
take it up.'
'Mr Slope seem to think!' say Mrs Proudie, in a tone of voice
which plainly show the bishop that he be right in look for a
breach in that quarter. 'and what have Mr Slope to do with it? I
hope, my lord, you be not go to allow yourself to be govern
by a chaplain.' and now in her eagerness the lady lose her place in
her account.
'certainly not, my dear. nothing I can assure you be less probable.
but still Mr Slope may be useful in find how the wind blow, and
I really think that if we could give something good to Mr
Quiverful--'
'nonsense,' say Mrs Proudie; 'it would be year before you could
give they anything else that could suit they half as well, and as
for the press and the public, and all that, remember there be two
way of tell a story. if Mr Harding be fool enough to tell his
tale, we can also tell ours. the place be offer to he, and he
refuse it. it have now be give to someone else, and therebe an
end of it. at least, I should think so.'
'well, my dear, I rather believe you be right;' say the bishop,
and sneak out of the room, he go down stair, trouble in his
mind as to how he should receive the archdeacon on the morrow. he
feel himself not very well just at present; and begin to consider
that he might, not improbably, be detain in his room the next
morning by an attack of bile. he be, unfortunately, very subject
to bilious annoyance.
'Mr Slope, indeed! Iwill slope he,' say the indignant matron to
she listening progeny. 'I donot know what have come to Mr Slope. I
believe he think he be to be Bishop of Barchester himself, because
I have take he by the hand, and get your father to make he his
domestic chaplain.'
'he be always full of impudence,' say Olivia; 'I tell you so once
before, mamma.' Olivia, however, have not think he too impudent
when once before he have propose to make her Mrs Slope.
'well, Olivia, I always think you like he,' say Augusta, who
at that moment have some grudge against her sister. 'I always
dislike the man because I think he thoroughly vulgar.'
'there yoube wrong,' say Mrs Proudie; 'hebe not vulgar at all;
and what be more, he be a soul-stir, eloquent preacher; but he
must be teach to know his place if he be to remain in this house.'
'he have the horrid eye I ever see in a man's head,' say Netta;
'and I tell you what, hebe terribly greedy; do you see the current
pie he eat yesterday?'
when Mr Slope get home he soon learn from the bishop, as much from
his manner as his word, that Mrs Proudie's behest in the matter
of the hospital be to be obey. Dr Proudie let fall something as
to 'this occasion only,' and 'keep all affair about patronage
exclusively in his own hand.' but he be quite decide about Mr
Harding; and as Mr Slope do not wish to have both the prelate and
the prelatess against he, he do not at present see that he could
do anything but yield.
he merely remark that he would of course carry out the bishop's
view, and that he be quite sure that if the bishop trust to his
own judgment thing in the diocese would certainly be well order.
Mr Slope know that if you hit a nail on the head often enough, it
will penetrate at last.
he be sit alone in his room on the same evening when a light
knock be make on his door, and before he could answer it the door
be open, and his patroness appear. he be all smile in a

be offer to she, and thus begin her expostulation :-
'Mr Slope, I do not at all approve your conduct the other night
with that italian woman. any one would have think that you be
her lover.'
'good gracious, my dear madam,' say Mr Slope, with a look of
horror. 'why, she be a married woman.'
'thatbe more than I know,' say Mrs Proudie; 'however she choose
to pass for such. but married or not marry, such attention as you
pay she be improper. I cannot believe that you would wish to give
offence in my drawing-room, Mr Slope; but I owe it to myself and my
daughter to tell you that I disapprove your conduct.'
Mr Slope open wide his huge protrude eye, and star out of
they with a look of well-dignified surprise. 'why, Mrs Proudie,'
say he, 'I do but fetch her something to eat when she be
hungry.'
'and you have call on she since,' continue she, look at the
culprit with the stern look of a detective policeman in the act of
declare himself.
Mr Slope turn over in his mind whether it would be well for he
to tell this termagant at once that he should call on whom he
like, and do what he like; but he remember that his footing in
Barchester be not yet sufficiently firm, and that it would be
well for he to pacify she.
'I certainly call since at Dr Stanhope's house, and certainly see
Madame Neroni.'
'yes, and you see she alone,' say the episcopal Argus.
'undoubtedly I do,' say Mr Slope, 'but that be because nobody
else happen to be in the room. surely it be no fault of mine if
the rest of the family be out.'
'perhaps not; but I assure you, Mr Slope, you will fall greatly in
my estimation if I find that you allow yourself to be catch by the
lur of that woman. I know woman well than you do, Slope, and
you may believe I that that signora, as she call herself, be not
a fitting companion for a strict evangelical, unmarried young
clergyman.'
how Mr Slope would have like to laugh at she, have he dare! but he
do not dare. so he merely say, 'I can assure you, Mrs Proudie,
the lady in question be nothing to I.'
'well, I hope not, Mr Slope. but I have consider it my duty to
give you this caution; and now there be another thing I feel myself
call upon to speak about; it be your conduct to the bishop, Mr
Slope.'
'my conduct to the bishop,' say he, now truly surprised and
ignorant what the lady allude to.
'yes, Mr Slope; your conduct to the bishop. it be by no means what
I would wish to see it.'
'has the bishop say anything, Mrs Proudie?'
'no, the bishop have say nothing. he probably think that any
remark on the matter will come well from I, who first
introduce you to his lordship's notice. the fact be, Mr Slope, you
be a little inclined to take too much upon yourself.'
an angry spot show itself upon Mr Slope's cheek, and it be with
difficulty that he control himself. but he do do so, and sit
quite silent while the lady go on.
'it be the fault of many young man in your position, and therefore
the bishop be not incline at present to resent it. you will, no
doubt, soon learn what be require from you, and what be not. if
you will take my advice, however, you will be careful not to
obtrude advice upon the bishop in any matter concern patronage.
if his lordship want advice, he know where to look for it.' and
then have add to her counsel a string of platitude as to what
be desirable and what not desirable in the conduct of a strictly
evangelical, unmarried young clergyman, Mrs Proudie retreat,
leave the chaplain to his thought.
the upshot of his thought be this, that there certainly be not
room in the diocese for the energy of both himself and Mrs
Proudie, and that it behove he quickly to ascertain whether his
energy or hers would prevail.
CHAPTER xviii
the WIDOW's persecution
early on the follow morning, Mr Slope be summon to the

'oh, Slope,' say he, take the chaplain's proffered hand.

say to Dr Grantly. he be to be tell in fact in the civil word
in which the tiding could be convey, that Mr Harding have
refuse the wardenship, the appointment have be offer to Mr

Mr Slope again point out to his patron that he think he be

the bishop's thumb, and he read it correctly. there be no need of
parchment and seal, of attestation, explanation, and

give the bishop his hand upon it. the bishop understand the little
extra squeeze, and an intelligible gleam of assent twinkle in his
eye.
'pray be civil to the archdeacon, Mr Slope,' say he out loud; 'but
make he quite understand that in this matter Mr Harding have put it
out of my power to oblige he.'
it would be calumny on Mrs Proudie to suggest that she be sit
in her bed-room with her ear at the keyhole during this interview.
she have within she a spirit of decorum which prevent she from
descend to such baseness. to put her ear to a key-hole or to
listen at a chink, be a trick for a housemaid.
Mrs Proudie know this, and therefore she do not do it; but she
station herself as near to the door as she well could, that she
might, if possible, get the advantage which the housemaid would
have have, without descend to the housemaid's artifice.
it be little, however, that she hear, and that little be only

even dream of the treacherous resolve which those two false man
have make together to upset she in the pride of her station, to dash

her power before she have taste its sweet! traitor that they
be; the husband of her bosom, and the outcast whom she have
foster and bring into the warmth of the world's bright
fireside! but neither of they have the magnanimity of this woman.
though two man have thus league themselves together against she,
even yet the battle be not lose.
Mr Slope feel pretty sure that Dr Grantly would decline the honour

when the palace door be open to he, be greet by a note. Mr
Slope present his compliment &c, &c. the bishop be ill in his

'Ill!' say the archdeacon to himself as he fling himself into his

Ill, indeed!' the archdeacon be never ill himself, and do not
therefore understand that any one else could in truth be prevent
by illness from keep an appointment. he regard all such
excuse as subterfuge, and in the present instance he be not far
wrong.
Dr Grantly desire to be drive to his father-in-law's lodging in
