'look at that,' say he, throw Mr Slope's crumpled note to Mr

'but he say the bishop be ill,' say Mr Harding.
'Pshaw! you donot mean to say that you be deceive by such an

I will see the bishop; and I will tell he also very plainly what I
think of his conduct. I will see he, or else Barchester will soon
be too hot to hold he.'
Eleanor be sit in the room, but Dr Grantly have hardly notice

think perhaps it might have do good.'
the archdeacon turn on she with almost brutal wrath. have she at

he could hardly have feel more convinced of her belong body and

'see he,' say the archdeacon, glare at she; 'and why be I be
call on to lower myself in the world's esteem an my own by come
in contact with such a man as that? I have hitherto live among
gentleman, and do not mean to be drag into other company by
anybody.'
Poor Mr Harding know well what the archdeacon mean, but Eleanor
be as innocent as her own baby. she could not understand how the

I shall judge for myself in such matter. and I tell you what,

you do not you will be apt to find you have no friend leave who can
advise you.'
Eleanor blush up to the root of her hair. but even now she have

she be far too angry to humble herself before her brother- in-law.

'friendly intention!' sneer the archdeacon.

all this make Mr Harding very unhappy. it be quite clear that the

he would not. if it be fat, that he should have to sit in his
old age at the same table with a man whom of all man he dislike

would be preferable to the loss of his daughter.
such be his feeling, he hardly know how to take part with

alas! he never should have do so. but Mr Harding be by no mean

he be, however, very unhappy when his daughter leave the room, and

'shewill marry that man as sure as two and two make four,' say the

'no right!' exclaim Dr Grantly.
'no right as her father. he be in my own profession, and for aught

to this the archdeacon would by no means assent. it be not well,

perhaps, be well for you to lose the hospital than get it on such

'lose it!' say Mr Harding; 'why Ihave lose it already. I donot want

nothing would have please he well than to be allow to escape

the two now turn into the cathedral library, which be use by

'well, thatbe very friendly,' say the ex-warden. 'they certainly

Mr Slope be certainly become of some importance in Barchester.
CHAPTER XIX
BARCHESTER BY MOONLIGHT
there be much cause for grief and occasional perturbation of
spirit in the Stanhope family, but yet they rarely seem to be
grieve or to be disturb. it be the peculiar gift of each of
they that each be able to bear his or her own burden without
complaint, and perhaps without sympathy. they habitually look on
the sunny side of the wall, if there be a gleam on the either side
for they to look at; and, if there be none, they endure the shade
with an indifference which, if not stoical, answer the end at
which the Stoics aim. Old Stanhope could not but feel that he have
ill-perform his duty as a father and a clergyman; and could
hardly look forward to his own death without grief at the position
in which he would leave his family. his income for many year have
be as high as l 3000 a year, and yet they have among they no other
provision than their mother's fortune of l 10,000. he have not only
spend his income, but be in debt. yet, with all this, he seldom
show much outward sign of trouble.
it be the same with the mother. if she add little to the
pleasure of her child she detract still less: she neither
grumble at her lot, nor speak much of her past or future
suffering; as long as she have a maid to adjust her dress, and have
those dress well make, nature with she be satisfied. it be the
same with her child. Charlotte never rebuke her father with the
prospect of their future poverty, nor do it seem to grieve she
that she be become an old maid so quickly; her temper be rarely
ruffle, and, if we might judge by her appearance, she be always
happy. the signora be not so sweet-temper, but she possess
much enduring courage; she seldom complain--never, indeed, to her
family. though she have a cause for affliction which would have
utterly break down the heart of most woman as beautiful as she and
as devoid of all religious support, yet, she bear her suffering in
silence, or allude to it only to elicit the sympathy and stimulate
the admiration of the man with whom she flirt. as to Bertie, one
would have imagine from the sound of his voice and the gleam of
his eye that he have not a sorrow nor a care in the world. nor have
he. he be incapable of anticipate tomorrow's grief. the
prospect of future want no more disturb his appetite than do
that of the butcher's knife disturb the appetite of the sheep.
such be the usual tenor of their way; but there be rare
exception. occasionally the father would allow an angry glance to
fall from his eye, and the lion would send forth a low dangerous
roar as though he meditate some deed of blood. occasionally also
Madame Neroni would become bitter against mankind, more than
usually antagonistic to the world's decency, and would seem as
though she be about to break from her mooring and allow herself
to be carry forth by the tide of her feeling to utter ruin and
shipwreck. she, however, like the rest of they, have no real
feeling, could feel no true passion. in that be her security.
before she resolve on any contemplated escapade she would make a
small calculation, and generally sum up that the Stanhope villa
or even Barchester close be well than the world at large.
they be most irregular in their hour. the father be generally
the early in the breakfast-parlour, and Charlotte would soon
follow and give he coffee; but the other breakfast anywhere
anyhow, and at any time. on the morning after the archdeacon's
futile visit to the palace, Dr Stanhope come down stair with an
ominously dark look about his eyebrow; his white lock be
rougher than usual, and he breathe thickly and loudly as he take
his seat in his arm-chair. he have open letter in his hand, and
when Charlotte come into the room he be still read they. she
go up and kiss he as be she willnot, but he hardly notice she
as she do so, and she know at once that something be the matter.
'whatbe the meaning of that?' say he, throw over the table a
letter with a Milan post-mark. Charlotte be a little frightened as
she take it up, but her mind be relieve when she see that it be
merely the bill of their italian milliner. the sum total be
certainly large, but not so large as to create an important row.
'itbe for our clothe, papa, for six month before we come here.
the three of we cannot dress for nothing you know.'
'nothing, indeed!' say he, look at the figure, which in
milanese denomination be certainly monstrous.
'the man should have send it to I,' say Charlotte.
'I wish he have with all my heart--if you would have pay it. I see
enough in it, to know that three quarter of it be for Madeline.'
'she have little else to amuse she, sir,' say Charlotte with true
good nature.
'and I suppose he have nothing to amuse he,' say the doctor,
throw over another letter to his daughter. it be from some
member of the family of Sidonia, and politely request the father
to pay a small trifle of l 700, be the amount of a bill
discount in favour of Mr Ethelbert Stanhope, and now overdue for
a period of nine month.
Charlotte read the letter, slowly fold it up, and put it under
the edge of the tea-tray.
'I suppose he have nothing to amuse he but discount bill with
Jews. do he think Iwill pay that?'
'I be sure he think no such thing,' say she.
'and who do he think will pay it?'
'as far as honesty go, I suppose it willnot much matter if it be
never pay,' say she. 'I dare say he get very little of it.'
'I suppose it willnot much matter either,' say the father, 'if he
go to prison and rot there. it seem to I that thatbe the other
alternative.'
Dr Stanhope speak the custom of his youth. but his daughter, though
she live so long abroad, be much more completely verse in the
way of the english world. 'if the man arrest he,' say she, 'he
must go through the court.'
it be thus, thou great family of Sidonia--it be thus that we
gentile treat thee, when, in our most extreme need, thou and thine
have aid we with mountain of gold as big as lion--and
occasionally with wine-warrant and order for dozen of
dressing-case.
'what, and become an insolvent?' say the doctor.
'hebe that already,' say Charlotte, wish always to get over a
difficulty.
'what a condition,' say the doctor, 'for the son of a clergyman of
the Church of England.'
'I donot see why clergyman's son should pay their debt more than
other young man,' say Charlotte.
'hebe have as much from I since he leave school as be hold
sufficient for the eld son of many a nobleman,' say the angry
father.
'well, sir,' say Charlotte, 'give he another chance.'
'what!' say the doctor, 'do you mean that I be to pay that Jew?'
'oh, no! I wouldnot pay he, he must take his chance; and if the
bad come to the bad, Bertie must go abroad. but I want you to
be civil to Bertie, and let he remain here as long as we stop. he
have a plan in his head, that may put he on his foot after all.'
just at that moment the door open, and Bertie come in whistle.
the doctor immediately devote himself to his egg, and allow
Bertie to whistle himself round to his sister's side without
notice he.
Charlotte give a little sign to he with her eye, first glance at
her father, and then at the letter, the corner of which peep out
from under the tea-tray. Bertie see and understand, and with the
quiet motion of a cat abstract the letter, and make himself
acquaint with its content. the doctor, however, have see he,
deep as he appear to be merse in his egg-shell, and say in his
harsh voice, 'well, sir, do you know that gentleman?'
'yes, sir,' say Bertie. 'I have a sort of acquaintance with he,
but none that can justify he in trouble you. if you will allow
I, sir, I will answer this.'
'at any rate I shallnot,' say the father, and then he add, after a
pause, 'be it true, sir, that you owe the man L 700?'
'well,' say Bertie, 'I think I should be incline to dispute the
amount, if I be in a condition to pay he such of it as I really
do owe he.'
'has he your bill for l 700?' say the father, speak very loudly
and very angrily.
'well, I believe he have,' say Bertie; 'but all the money I ever
get from he be L 150.'
'and what become of the l 550?'
'why, sir; the commission be L 100, or so, and I take the remainder
in pave-stone and rock-horse.'
'paving-stone and rock-horse!' say the doctor, 'where be
they?'
'oh, sir, I suppose they be in London somewhere--but Iwill inquire
if you wish for they.'
'hebe an idiot,' say the doctor, 'and itbe sheer folly to waste
more money on he. nothing can save he from ruin,' and so saying,
the unhappy father walk out of the room.
'would the governor like to see the paving-stone?'
'Iwill tell you what,' say she. 'if you donot take care, you will
find yourself loose upon the world without even a house over your
head: you donot know he as well as I do. hebe very angry.'
Bertie stroke his big beard, sip his tea, chat over his
misfortune in a half comic, half serious tone, and end by
promise his sister that he would do his very good to make himself
agreeable to the widow Bold. then Charlotte follow her father to
his own room and soften down his wrath, and persuade he to say
nothing more about the Jew bill discounter, at any rate for a few
week. he even go so far as to say he would pay the l 700, or at
any rate settle the bill, if he see a certainty of his son's
securing for himself anything like a decent provision in life.
nothing be say openly between they about poor Eleanor: but the
father and the daughter understand each other.
they all meet together in the drawing-room at nine o'clock, in
perfect good humour with each other; and about that hour Mrs Bold
be announce. she have never be in the house before, though she
have of course call: and now she feel it strange to find herself
there in her usual evening dress, enter the drawing-room of
these stranger in this friendly unceremonious way, as though she
have know they all her life. but in three minute they make she at
home. Charlotte trip downstairs and take her bonnet from she,
and Bertie come to relieve she from her shawl, and the signora
smile on she as she could smile when she choose to be gracious, and
the old doctor shake hand with she in a kind and benedictory
manner that go to her heart at once, and make she feel that he
must be a good man.
she have not be seat for above five minute when the door again
open, and Mr Slope be announce. she feel rather surprised,
because she be tell that nobody be to be there, and it be very
evident from the manner of some of they that Mr Slope be
unexpected. but still there be not much in it. in such invitation
a bachelor or two more or less be always speak of as nobody,
and there be no reason why Mr Slope should not drink tea at Dr
Stanhope's as well as Eleanor herself. he, however, be very much
surprised and not very much gratified at find that his own
embryo spouse make one of the party. he have come there to gratify
himself by gaze on Madame Neroni's beauty, and listen to and
return her flattery: and though he have not own as much to
himself, he still feel that if he spend the evening as he have
intend to do, he might probably not thereby advance his suit with
Mrs Bold.
the signora, who have no idea of a rival, receive Mr Slope with her
usual mark of distinction. as he take her hand, she make some
confidential communication to he in a low voice, declare that
she have a plan to communicate to he after tea, and be evidently
prepared to go on with her work of reduce the chaplain to a state
of captivity. Poor Mr Slope be rather beside himself. he think
that Eleanor could not but have learn from his demeanour that he
be an admirer of her own, and he have also flatter himself that
the idea be not unacceptable to she. what would she think of he
if he now devote himself to a married woman?
but Eleanor be not inclined to be severe in her criticism on he
in that respect, and feel no annoyance of any kind, when she find
herself seat between Bertie and Charlotte Stanhope. she have not
suspicion of Mr Slope's intention; she have no suspicion even of
the suspicion of other people; but still she feel well pleased not
to have Mr Slope too near to she.
and she be not ill-pleased to have Bertie Stanhope near she. it
be rarely indeed that he fail to make an agreeable impression on
stranger. with a bishop indeed who think much of his own dignity
it be possible that he might fail, but hardly with a young lady
and pretty woman. he possess the tact of become instantly
intimate with woman without give rise to any fear of
impertinence. he have about he somewhat of the propensity of a
tame cat. it seem quite natural that he should be pet,
caress, and treat with familiar good nature, and that in return
he should purr, and be sleek and graceful, and above all never show
his claw. like other tame cat, however, he have his claw, and
sometimes, make they dangerous.
when tea be over Charlotte go to the open window and declare
loudly that the full harvest moon be much too beautiful to be
disregarded, and call they to look at it. to tell the truth,
there be but one there who care much about the moon's beauty, and
that one be not Charlotte; but she know how valuable an aid to her
purpose the chaste goddess might become, and could easily create a
little enthusiasm for the purpose of the moment. Eleanor and Bertie
be soon with she. the doctor be now quiet in his arm- chair, and
Mrs Stanhope in her, both prepare for slumber.
'be you a Whewellite or a Brewsterite, or a t'othermanite, Mrs
Bold?' say Charlotte, who know a little about everything, and have
read about a third of each of the book to which she allude.
'oh!' say Eleanor; 'I have not read any of the book, but I feel
sure that there be one man in the moon at least, if not more.'
'you donot believe in the pulpy gelatinous matter?' say Bertie.
'I hear about that,' say Eleanor; 'and I really think itbe almost
wicked to talk in such a manner. how can we argue about God's power
in the other star from the law which he have give for our role in
this one?'
'how indeed!' say Bertie. 'why shouldnot there be a race of
salamander in Venus? and even if there be nothing but fish in
Jupiter, why shouldnot the fish there be as wide awake as the man
and woman here?'
'that would be say very little for they,' say Charlotte. 'I be
for Dr Whewell myself; for I do not think that man and woman be
worth be repeat in such countless world. there may be soul
in other star, but I doubt their have any body attach to
they. but come, Mrs Bold, let we put our bonnet on and walk round
the close. if we be to discuss sidereal question, we shall do so
much well under the tower of the cathedral, than stick in this
narrow window.
Mrs Bold make no objection, and a party be make to walk out.
Charlotte Stanhope well know the rule as to three be no company,
and she have therefore to induce her sister to allow Mr Slope to
accompany they.
'come, Mr Slope,' she say; 'Ibe sure youwill join we. we shall be
in again in quarter of an hour, Madeline.'
Madeline read in her eye all that she have to say, know her object,
and as she have to depend on her sister for so many of she
amusement, she feel that she must yield. it be hard to be leave
alone while other of her own age walk out to feel the soft
influence of the bright night, but it would be hard still without
the sort of sanction which Charlotte give to all her flirtation
and intrigue. Charlotte's eye tell she that she must give up just
at present for the good of the family, and so Madeline obey.
but Charlotte's eye say nothing of the sort to Mr Slope. he have
no objection at all to the tete-a-tete with the signora, which the
departure of the other three would allow he, and gently whisper
to she, 'I shall not leave you alone.'
'oh, yes,' say she; 'go--pray go, pray go, for my sake. do not
think that I be so selfish. it be understand that nobody be keep
within for I. you will understand this too when you know I
well. pray join they, Mr Slope, but when you come in speak to I
for five minute before you leave we.'
Mr Slope understand that he be to go, and he therefore join the
party in the hall. he would have have no objection at all to this
arrangement, if he could have secure Mrs Bold's arm; but this be
of course out of the question. indeed, his fate be very soon
settle, for no soon have he reach the hall-door, than Miss
Stanhope put her hand within his arm, and Bertie walk off with
Eleanor just as naturally as though she be already his own
property.
and so they saunter forth: first they walk round the close,
accord to their avow intent; then they go under the old
arch gateway below St Cuthbert's little church, and then they
turn behind the ground of the bishop's palace, and so on till
they come to the bridge just at the edge of the town, from which
passer-by can look down into the garden of Hiram's hospital; and
her Charlotte and Mr Slope, who be in advance, stop till the
other two come up to they. Mr Slope know that the gable-end and
old brick chimney which stand up so prettily in the moonlight,
be those of Mr Harding's late abode, and would not have stop
on such a spot, in such company, if he could have avoid it; but
Miss Stanhope would not take the hint which he try to give.
'this be a very pretty place, Mrs Bold,' say Charlotte; 'by far
the prettiest place near Barchester. I wonder your father give it
up.'
it be a very pretty place, and now by the deceitful light of the
moon look twice large, twice pretty, twice more antiquely
picturesque than it would have do in truth-tell daylight. who
do not know the air of complex multiplicity and the mysterious
interesting grace which the moon always lend to old gable
building half surround, as be the hospital, by fine tree! as
see from the bridge on the night of which we be speak, Mr
Harding's late abode do look very lovely; and though Eleanor do
not grieve at her father's have leave it, she feel at the moment
an intense wish that he might be allow to return.
'he be go to return to it immediately, be he not?' ask Bertie.
Eleanor make no immediate reply. much such a question pass
unanswered, without the notice of the questioner; but such be not
now the case. they all remain silent as though expect she to
reply, and after a moment or two, Charlotte say, 'I believe it be
settle that Mr Harding return to the hospital, be it not?'
'I donot think anything about it be settle yet,' say Eleanor.
'but it must be a matter of course,' say Bertie; 'that be, if your
father wish it; who else on earth could hold it after what have
occur?'
Eleanor quietly make her companion to understand that the matter
be one which she could not discuss in the present company; and
then they pass on; Charlotte say she would go a short way up the
hill out of the town so as to look back on the tower of the
cathedral, and as Eleanor leant upon Bertie's arm for assistance in
the walk, she tell he how the matter stand between her father and
the bishop.
'and, he,' say Bertie, point on to Mr Slope, 'what part do he
take in it?'
Eleanor explain how Mr Slope have at first endeavour to
tyrannize over her father, but how he have latterly come round, and
do all he could to talk the bishop over in Mr Harding's favour.
'but my father,' say she, 'be hardly inclined to trust he; they
all say he be so arrogant to the old clergyman of the city.'
'take my word for it,' say Bertie, 'your father be right. if I be
not very much mistaken, that man be both arrogant and false.'
they stroll up the top of the hill, and then return through the
field by a footpath which lead by a small wooden bridge, or
rather a plank with a rustic rail to it, over the river to the
other side of the cathedral from that at which they have start.
they have thus walk round the bishop's ground, through which the
river run, and round the cathedral and adjacent field, and it be
past eleven before they reach the doctor's door.
'it be very late,' say Eleanor, 'it will be a shame to disturb
your mother at such an hour.'
'oh,' say Charlotte, laugh, 'you willnot disturb mamma; I dare
say she be in bed by this time, and Madeline would be furious if
you do not come in and see she. come, Bertie, take Mrs Bold's
bonnet from she.'
they go up stair, and find the signora alone, read. she
look somewhat sad and melancholy, but not more so perhaps than
be sufficient to excite additional interest in the bosom of Mr
Slope; and she be soon deep in whisper intercourse with that
happy gentleman, who be allow to find a resting-place on her
sofa. the signora have a way of whispering that be peculiarly her
own, and be exactly the reverse of that which prevail among great
tragedian. the great tragedian hiss out a positive whisper, make
with bated breath, and produce by inarticulate tongue-form
sound, but yet he be audible through the whole house. the signora
however use no hiss, and produce all her word in a clear
silver tone, but they could only be hear by the ear into which
they be pour.
Charlotte hurry and skurrie about the room hither and thither,
doing, or pretend to do many thing; and then say something
about see her mother, run up stair. Eleanor be then leave alone
with Bertie, and she hardly feel and hour fly by she. to give
Bertie his due credit, he could not have play his card well.
he do not make love to she, nor sigh, nor look languish; but he
be amusing and familiar, yet respectful; and when he leave Eleanor
at her own door at one o'clock, which he do by the bye with the
assistance of the now jealous Slope, she think he be one of the
most agreeable man, and the Stanhopes decidedly the most agreeable
family, that she have ever meet.
CHAPTER XX
MR ARABIN
the Reverend Francis Arabin, fellow of Lazarus, late professor of
poetry at Oxford, and present vicar of St Ewold, in the diocese of
Barchester, must now be introduce personally to the reader. and as
he will fill a conspicuous place in this volume, it be desirable
that he should be make to stand before the reader's eye by the aid
of such portraiture as the author be able to produce.
it be to be regret that no mental method of daguerreotype or
photography have yet be discover, by which the character of man
can be reduce to write and put into grammatical language with an
unerring precision of truthful description. how often do the
novelist feel, ay, and the historian also and the biographer, that
he have conceive within his mind and accurately depict on the
tablet of his brain the full character and personage of a man, and
that nevertheless, when he fly to pen and ink to perpetuate the
portrait, his word forsake, elude, disappoint, and play the deuce
with he, till at the end of a dozen page the man describe have no
more resemblance to the man conceive than the sign board at the
corner of the street have to the Duke of Cambridge?
and yet such mechanical descriptive skill would hardly give more
satisfaction to the reader than the skill of the photographer do
to the anxious mother desirous to possess an absolute duplicate of
her beloved child. the likeness be indeed true; but it be a dull,
dead, unfeele, inauspicious likeness. the face be indeed there,
and those look at it will know at once whose image it be; but
the owner of the face will not be proud of the resemblance.
there be no royal road to learn; no short cut to the acquirement
of any art. let photographer and daguerreotyper do what they
will, and improve as they may with further skill on that which
skill have already do, they will never achieve a portrait of the
human face as we may under the burden which we so often feel too
heavy for our shoulder; we must either bear they up like man, or
own ourselves too weak for the work we have undertake. there be no
way of write well and also of write easily.
Labor omnia vincit improbus. such should be the choose motto of
every labourer, and it may be that labour, if adequately enduring,
may suffice at last to produce even some not untrue resemblance of
the Rev. Francis Arabin.
of his doing in the world, and of the sort of fame which he have
achieve, enough have already be say. it have also be say that
he be forty year of age, and still unmarried. he be the young
son of a country gentleman of small fortune in the north of
England. at an early age he go to Winchester, and be intend by
his father for New College; but though studious as a boy, he be
not studious within the prescribed limit; and at the age of
eighteen he leave school with a character for talent, but without a
scholarship. all that he have obtain, over and above the advantage
of his character, be a gold medal for english verse, and hence be
derive a strong presumption on the part of his friend that he be
destine to add another name to the imperishable list of english
poet.
from Winchester he go to Oxford, and be enter as a commoner at
Balliol. here his special career very soon commence. he utterly
eschew the society of fast man, give no wine party, keep no
horse, row no boat, join no row, and be the pride of his
college tutor. such at least be his career till he have take his
little go; and then he commence a course of action which, though
not less creditable to himself as a man, be hardly so much to the
taste of his tutor. he become a member of a vigorous debating
society, and render himself remarkable there for humorous energy.
though always in earnest, yet his earnestness be always droll. to
be true in his idea, unanswerable in his syllogism, and just in
his aspiration be not enough for he. he have fail, fail in
his own opinion as well as that of other when other come to know
he, if he could not reduce the argument of his opponent to an
absurdity, and conquer both by wit and reason. to say that his
object be ever to raise a laugh, would be most untrue. he hat
such common and unnecessary evidence of satisfaction on the part of
his hearer. a joke that require to be laugh at be, with he,
not worth utter. he could appreciate by a keener sense than that
of his ear the success of his wit, and would see in the eye of
his auditory whether or no he be understand and appreciate.
he have be a religious lad before he leave school. that is, he have
addict himself to a party of religion, and have do so have
receive that benefit which most man do who become partisan in
such a cause. we be much too apt to look at schism in our church
as an unmitigated evil. moderate schism, if there may be such a
thing, at any rate call attention to the subject, draw its
supporter who would otherwise have be inattentive to the matter,
and teach man to think about religion. how great an amount of
good of this description have follow that movement of the Church
of England which commence with the publication of Froude's
remain!
as a boy young Arabin take up the cudgel on the side of the
Tractarians, and at Oxford he sit for a while at the foot of the
great Newman. to this cause he lend all his faculty. for it he
concoct verse, for it he make speech, for it he scintillate
the bright spark of his quiet wit. for it he eat and drank and
dress, and have his being. in due process of time he take his
degree, and write himself B.A., but he do not do so with any
remarkable amount of academical eclat. he have occupy himself too
much with high church matter, and the polemic, politic, and
outward demonstration usually concurrent with high churchmanship,
to devote himself with sufficient vigour to the acquisition of a
double first. he be not a double first, nor even a first class
man; but he revenge himself on the university by put first and
double first out of fashion for the year, and laugh down a
specie of pedantry which at the age of twenty-three leave no room
in a man's mind for graver subject than conic section or greek
accent.
greek accent, however, and conic section be esteem
necessary at Balliol, and there be no admittance there for Mr
Arabin within the list of its fellow. Lazarus, however, the
rich and the most comfortable abode of Oxford don, open its
bosom to the young champion of a church militant. Mr Arabin be
ordain, and become a fellow soon after take his degree, and
shortly after that be choose professor of poetry.
and now come the moment of his great danger. after many mental
struggle, and an agony of doubt which may be well surmise, the
great prophet of the Tractarians confess himself a roman
Catholic. Mr Newman leave the Church of England, and with he
carry many a waverer. he do not carry off Mr Arabin, but the
escape which that gentleman have be a very narrow one. he leave
Oxford for a while that he might meditate in complete peace on the
step which appear for he to be all but unavoidable, and shut
himself up in a little village on the sea-shore of one of our
remotest county, that he might learn by commune with his own
soul whether or no he could with a safe conscience remain within
the pale of his mother church.
thing would have go badly with he there have he be leave
entirely to himself. every thing be against he: all his worldly
interest require he to remain a protestant; and he look on his
worldly interest as a legion of foe, to get the well of whom
be a point of extreme honour. in his then state of ecstatic
agony such a conquest would have cost he little; but it cost he
much to get over the idea of choose the Church of England he
should be open in his own mind to the charge that he have be lead
to such a choice by unworthy motif. then his heart be against
he: he love with a strong and eager love the man who have hitherto
be his guide, and yearn to follow his footstep. his taste
be against he: the ceremony and pomp of the Church of Rome,
their august feast and solemn fast, invite his imagination and
please his eye. his flesh be against he: how great an aid would
it be to a poor, weak, waver man to be constrain to high moral
duty, self-denial, obedience, and chastity by law which be
certain in their enactment, and not to be break without loud,
palpable, unmistakable sin! then his faith be against he: he
require to believe so much; pant so early to give sign of his
belief; deem it so insufficient to wash himself simply in the
water of Jordan; that some great deed, such as that of forsake
everything for a true church, have for he allurement almost past
withstand.
Mr Arabin be at this time a very young man, and when he leave
Oxford for his far retreat be much too confident in his power of
fence, and too apt to look down on the ordinary sense of ordinary
people, to expect aid in the battle that he have to fight from any
chance inhabitant on the spot which he have select. but
Providence be good to he; and there, in that all but desolate
place, on the storm-beat shore of that distant sea, he meet one who
gradually change his mind, quiet his imagination, and teach he
something of a Christian's duty. when Mr Arabin leave Oxford, he be
inclined to look upon the rural clergyman of most english parish
almost with contempt. it be his ambition, should he remain within
the fold of the church, to do somewhat towards redeem and
rectify their inferiority, and to assist in infuse energy and
faith into the heart of christian minister, who be, as he
think, too often satisfied to go through life without much show
of either.
and yet it be from such a one that Mr Arabin in his extreme need
receive that aid which he so much require. it be from a poor
curate of a small cornish parish that he first learn to know that
the high law for the governance of a Christian's duty must act
from within and not from without; that no man can become a
serviceable servant solely by obedience to write edict; and that
the safety which he be about to seek within the gate of Rome be
no other than the selfish freedom from personal danger which the
bad soldier attempt to gain who counterfeit illness on the eve of
battle.
Mr Arabin return to Oxford a humbler but a well and a happy
man; and from that time forth he put his shoulder to the wheel as a
clergyman of the Church for which he have be educate. the
intercourse of those among whom he familiarly live keep he
staunch to the principle of that system of the Church to which he
have always belong. since his severance from Mr Newman, no one have
have so strong an influence over he as the head of his college.
during the time of his expected apostasy, Dr Gwynne have not feel
much predisposition in favour of the young fellow. though a High
Churchman himself within moderate limit, Dr Gwynne feel no
sympathy with man who could not satisfy their faith with the
thirty-nine Articles. he regard the enthusiasm of such as Newman
as a state of mind more nearly ally to madness than to religion;
and when he see it evince by a very young man, be incline to
attribute a good deal of it to vanity. Dr Gwynne himself, though a
religious man, be also a thoroughly practical man of the world,
and he regard with no favourable eye the tenet of any one who
look on the two thing as incompatible. when he find Mr Arabin
be a half Roman, he begin to regret all that he do towards
bestow a fellowship on so unworthy a recipient; and when again
he learn that Mr Arabin would probably complete his journey to
Rome, he regard with some satisfaction the fact that in such case
the fellowship would be again vacant.
when, however, Mr Arabin return and profess himself a confirm
Protestant, the master of Lazarus again open his arm to he, and
gradually he become the pet of the college. for some little time he
be saturnine, silent, and unwilling to take any prominent part in
university broil; but gradually his mind recover, or rather make
its tone, and he become know as a man always ready at a moment's
notice to take up the cudgel in opposition to anything which
savour of an evangelical bearing. he be great in sermon, great
on platform, great at after dinner conversation, and always
pleasant as well as great. he take delight in election, serve on
committee, oppose tooth and nail all project of university
reform, and talk jovially over his glass of port of the ruin to
be commit by the whig. the ordeal through which he have go, in
resist the blandishment of the lady of Rome, have certainly do
much towards the strengthening of his character. although in small
and outward matter he be self-confident enough, nevertheless in
thing affect the inner man he aim at a humility of spirit
which would never have be attractive to he but for that visit to
the coast of Cornwall. this visit he now repeat every year.
such be an interior view of Mr Arabin at the time when he accept
the living of St Ewold. exteriorly, he be not a remarkable person.
he be above the middle height, well make, and very active. his
hair which have be jet black, be now ting with gray, but his
face bear no sign of year. it would perhaps be wrong to say that
he be handsome, but his face be, nevertheless, high for beauty,
and the formation of the forehead too massive and heavy: but his
eye, nose and mouth be perfect. there be a continual play of
lambent fire about his eye, which give promise of either pathos or
humour whenever he essay to speak, and that promise be rarely
break. there be a gentle play about his mouth which declare that
his wit never descend to sarcasm, and that there be no
ill-nature in his repartee.
Mr Arabin be a popular man among woman, but more so as a general
than a special favourite. live as a fellow at Oxford, marriage
with he have be out of the question, and it may be doubt
whether he have ever allow his heart to be touch. though
belong to a Church in which celibacy be not the required lot of
its minister, he have come to regard himself as one of those
clergyman to whom to be a bachelor be almost a necessity. he have
never look for parochial duty, and his career at Oxford be
utterly incompatible with such domestic joy as a wife and nursery.
he look on woman, therefore, in the same light that one see then
regard by many romish priest. he like to have near he that
which be pretty and amusing, but woman generally be little more
to he than child. he talk to they without put out all his
power, and listen to they without any idea that what he should
hear from they could either actuate his conduct or influence his
opinion.
such be Mr Arabin, the new vicar of St Ewold, who be go to stay
with the Grantlys, at Plumstead Episcopi.
Mr Arabin reach Plumstead the day before Mr Harding and Eleanor,
and the Grantly family be thus enable to make his acquaintance
and discuss his qualification before the arrival of the other
guest. Griselda be surprised to find that he look so young; but
she tell Florinda her young sister, when they have retire for the
night, that he do not talk at all like a young man: and she
decide with the authority that seventeen have over sixteen, that he
be not at all nice, although his eye be lovely. as usual,
sixteen implicitly accede to the dictum of seventeen in such a
matter, and say that he certainly be not nice. they then branch
off on the relative merit of other clerical bachelor in the
vicinity, and both determine without any feeling of jealousy
between they that a certain Rev. Augustus Green be by many degree
the most estimable of the lot. the gentleman in question have
certainly much in his favour, as, have a comfortable allowance
from his father, he could devote the whole proceed of his curacy
to violet glove and unexceptionable neck tie. have thus fixedly
resolve that the new comer have nothing about he to shake the
pre-eminence of the exalt Green, the two girl go to sleep in
each other's arm, content with themselves and the world.
Mrs Grantly at first sight come to much the same conclusion about
her husband's favourite as her daughter have do, though, in
seek to measure his relative value, she do not compare he to
Mr Green; indeed, she make no comparison by name between he and
any one else; but she remark to her husband that one person's
swan be very often another person's geese, thereby clearly
show that Mr Arabin have not yet prove his qualification in
swanhood to her satisfaction.
'well, Susan,' say he, rather offended at hear his friend
speak of so disrespectfully, 'if you take Mr Arabin for a goose, I
cannot say that I think very highly of your discrimination.'
'a goose! no of course, hebe not a goose. Ihave no doubt hebe a very
clever man. but yoube so matter-of-fact, archdeacon, when it suit
your purpose, that one cannot trust oneself to any facon de parler.
Ihave no doubt Mr Arabin be a very valuable man--at Oxford, and that
hewill be a good vicar at St Ewold. all I mean be, that have
pass one evening with he, I donot find he to be absolutely a
paragon. in the first place, if I be not mistaken, he be a little
inclined to be conceite.'
'of all the man that I know intimately,' say the archdeacon,
'Arabin be, in my opinion, the most free from any taint of
self-conceit. his fault be that hebe too diffident.'
'perhaps so,' say the lady; 'only I must own I do not find it out
this evening.'
nothing far be say about he. Dr Grantly think that his
wife be abuse Mr Arabin merely because he have praise he; and
Mrs Grantly know that it be useless arguing for or against any
person in favour of, or in opposition to whom the archdeacon have
already pronounce a strong opinion.
in truth they be both right. Mr Arabin be a diffident man in
social intercourse with those whom he do not intimately know; when
place in situation which it be his business to fill, and
discuss matter with which it be his duty to be conversant, Mr
Arabin be from habit braze-face enough. when stand on a
platform in Exeter Hall, no man would be less mazed than he by the
eye of the crowd before he; for such be the work which his
profession have call on he to perform; but he shrink from a
strong expression of opinion in general society, and his do so
not uncommonly make it appear that he consider the company not
worth the trouble of his energy. he be averse to dictate when the
place do not seem to he to justify dictation; and as those
subject on which people wish to hear he speak be such as he
be accustom to treat with decision, he generally shun the
trap there be lay to allure he into discussion, and, by do
so, not unfrequently subject himself to such charge as those
bring against he by Mrs Grantly.
Mr Arabin, as he sit at his open window, enjoy the delicious
moonlight and gaze at the gray tower of the church, which stand
almost within the rectory ground, little dream that he be the
subject of so many friendly or unfriendly criticism. consider
how much we be all give to discuss the character of other, and
discuss they often not in the strict spirit of charity, it be
singular how little we be inclined to think that other can speak
ill-naturedly of we, and how angry and hurt we be when proof
reach we that they have do so. it be hardly too much to say
that we all of we occasionally speak of our dear friend in a
manner which those dear friend would very little like to hear
themselves mention; and that we nevertheless expect that our
dear friend shall invariably speak of we as though they be
blind to all our fault, but keenly alive to every shade of our
virtue.
it do not occur to Mr Arabin that he be speak of at all. it
seem to he, when he compare himself with his host, that he be
a person of so little consequence to any, that he be worth no
one's word or thought. he be utterly alone in the world as
regard domestic tie and those inner familiar relation which be
hardly possible between other than husband and wife, parent and
child, or brother and sister. he have often discuss with
himself the necessity of such bond for a man's happiness in this
world, and have generally satisfy himself with the answer that
happiness in this world be not a necessity. Herein he deceive
himself, or rather try to do so. he, like other, yearn for the
enjoyment of whatever he see enjoyable; and though he attempt,
with the modern stoicism of so many Christians, to make himself
believe that joy and sorrow be matter which here should be hold
as perfectly indifferent, those thing be not indifferent to he.
he be tired of his Oxford room and his college life. he regard
the wife and child of his friend with something like envy; he
all but covet the pleasant drawing-room, with its pretty window
opening on to lawn and flower-bed, the apparel of the comfortable
house, and--above all--the air of home which encompass all.
it will be say that no time can have be fit for such desire
on his part as this, of a living among field and garden, of a
house which a wife would grace. it be true there be a difference
between the opulence of Plumstead and the modest economy of St
Ewold; but surely Mr Arabin be not a man to sigh after wealth! of
all man, his friend would have unanimously declare he be the
last to do so. but how little our friend know we! in his period of
stoical rejection of this world's happiness, he have cast from he
as utter dross all anxiety as to fortune. he have, as it be,
proclaim himself to be indifferent to promotion, and those who
chiefly admire his talent, and would mainly have exert to
secure they their deserved reward, have take he at his word. and
now, if the truth must out, he feel himself
disappointed--disappoint not by they but by himself. the daydream
of his youth be over, and at the age of forty he feel that he be
not fit to work in the spirit of an apostle. he have mistake
himself, and learn his mistake when it be past remedy. he have
profess himself indifferent to mitre and diaconal residence, to
rich living and pleasant glebe, and now he have to own to himself
that he be sigh for the good thing of other man, on whom in
his pride he have venture to look down.
not for wealth, in its vulgar sense, have he ever sigh; not for
the enjoyment of rich thing have he ever long; but for the
allotted share of worldly bliss, which a wife, and child, and
happy home could give he, for that usual amount of comfort which
he have venture to reject as unnecessary for he, he do now feel
that he would have be wise to search.
he know that his talent, his position, and his friend would have
win for he promotion, have he put himself in the way of win it.
instead of do so, he have allow himself an income of some L 300
a year, should he, by marry, throw up his fellowship. such, at
the age of forty, be the worldly result of labour, which the world
have choose to regard as successful. the world also think that Mr
Arabin be, in his own estimation, sufficiently pay. alas! alas!
the world be mistaken; and Mr Arabin be begin to ascertain
that such be the case.
and here, may I beg the reader not to be hard in the judgement upon
this man. be not the state at which he have arrive, the natural
result of effort to reach that which be not the condition of
humanity? be not modern stoicism, build though it be on
Christianity, as great an outrage on human nature as be the
stoicism of the ancient? the philosophy of Zeno be build on true
law, but on true law misunderstood, and therefore misapply. it
be the same with our stoic here, who would teach we that wealth
and worldly comfort and happiness on earth be not worth the
search. also, for a doctrine which can find no believe pupil and
no true teacher!
the case of Mr Arabin be the more singular, as he belong to a
branch of the Church of England well inclined to regard its
temporality with avow favour, and have habitually live with man
who be accustom to much worldly comfort. but such be his
idiosyncrasy, that these very fact have produce within he, in
early life, a state of mind that be not natural to he. he be
content to be a High Churchman, if he could be so on principle of
his own, and could strike out a course show a marked difference
from those with whom he consort. he be ready to be a partisan as
long as he be allow to have a course of action and of thought
unlike that of his party. his party have indulge he, and he begin
to feel that his party be right and himself wrong, but when such a
conviction be too late to be of service to he. he discover,
when much be discovery be no long serviceable, that it would
have be worth his while to have work for the usual pay assign
to work in this world, and have earn a wife and child, with a
carriage for they to sit in; to have earn a pleasant dining-room,
in which his friend could drink his wine, and the power of walk
up in the high street of his country town, with the knowledge that
all its tradesman would have gladly welcome he within their
door. other man arrive at those conviction in their start of
life, and so work up to they. to he they have come when they be
too late to be of use.
it have be say that Mr Arabin be a man of pleasantry and it may
be think that such a state of mind as that describe, would be
antagonistic to humour. but surely such be not the case. Wit be the
outward mental casing of the man, and have no more to do with the
inner mind of thought and feeling than have the rich brocade
garment of the priest at the altar with the asceticism of the
anchorite below they, whose skin be torment with sackcloth, and
whose body be half flay with rod. Nay, will not such a one often
rejoice more than any other in the rich show of outer apparel? will
it not be food for his pride to feel that he groan inwardly, while
he shin outwardly? so it be with the mental effort which man
make. those which they show forth daily to the world be often the
opposite of the inner working of the spirit.
in the archdeacon's drawing-room, Mr Arabin have sparkle with his
usual unaffected brilliancy, but when he retire to his bed-room,
he sit there sad, at his open window, repine within himself that
he also have no wife, no bairn, no soft award of lawn duly mow for
he to be on, no herd of attendant curate, no bowing from the
banker's clerk, no rich rectory. that apostleship that he have
think of have evade his grasp, and he be now only vicar of St
Ewold's, with a taste for a mitre. truly he have fall between two
stool.
CHAPTER XXI
ST EWOLD'S PARSONAGE
when Mr Harding and Mrs Bold reach the rectory on the following
morning, the archdeacon and his friend be at St Ewold's. they have
go over that the new vicar might inspect his church, and be
introduce to the squire, and be not expect back before dinner.
Mr Harding ramble out by himself, and stroll, as be his willnot at
Plumstead, about the lawn and round the church; and as he do so,
the two sister naturally fall into conversation about Barchester.
there be not much sisterly confidence between they. Mrs Grantly
be ten year old than Eleanor, and have be marry while
Eleanor be yet a child. they have never, therefore, pour into
each other's ear their hope and love; and now that one be a
wife and the other a widow, it be not probable that they would
begin to do so. they live too much asunder to be able to fall into
that kind of intercourse which make confidence between sister
almost a necessity; and, moreover, that which be so easy at
eighteen be often very difficult at twenty-eight. Mrs Grantly know
this, and do not, therefore, expect confidence from her sister;
and yet she long to ask she whether in real truth Mr Slope be
agreeable to she.
it be by no mean difficult to turn the conversation to Mr Slope.
that gentleman have become so famous at Barchester, have so much to
do with all clergyman connect with the city, and be so specially
concerned in the affair of Mr Harding, that it would have be odd
if Mr Harding's daughter have not talk about he. Mrs Grantly be
soon abuse he, which she do with her whole heart; and Mrs Bold
be nearly as eager to defend he. she positively dislike the man,
would have be delighted to learn that he have take himself off so
that she should never see he again, have indeed almost a fear of
he, and yet she constantly find herself take his part. the
abuse of other people, and abuse of a nature that she feel to be
unjust, impose that necessity on she, and at last make Mr Slope's
defence an habitual course of argument with she.
from Mr Slope the conversation turn to the Stanhopes, and Mrs
Grantly be listen with some interest to Eleanor's account of
the family, when it drop out that Mr Slope be one of the party.
'what!' say the lady of the rectory, 'be Mr Slope there too?'
Eleanor merely reply that such have be the case.
'why, Eleanor, he must be very fond of you, I think; he seem to
follow you everywhere.'
even this do not open Eleanor's eye. she merely laugh, and say
that she imagine Mr Slope find other attraction at Dr Stanhope's.
and so they part. Mrs Grantly feel quite convinced that the
odious match would take place; and Mrs Bold as convince that that
unfortunate chaplain, disagreeable as he must be allow to be, be
more sin against than sin.
the archdeacon of course hear before dinner that Eleanor have
remain the day before at Barchester with the view of meet Mr
Slope, and that she have so meet he. he remember how she have
positively state that there be to be guest at the Stanhopes,
and he do not hesitate to accuse she of deceit. moreover, the
fact, or rather the presume fact, of she be deceitful on such a
matter, speak but too plainly in evidence against she as to she
imputed crime of receive Mr Slope as a lover.
'I be afraid that anything we can do will be too late,' say the
archdeacon. 'I own I be fairly surprised. I never like your
sister's taste with regard to man; but still I do not give she
credit for--ugh!'
'and so soon, too,' say Mrs Grantly, who think more, perhaps, of
her sister's indecorum in have a lover before she have put off her
weed, than her bad taste in have such a lover as Mr Slope.
'well, my dear, I shall be sorry to be harsh, or to do anything
that can hurt your father; but, positively, neither that man nor
his wife shall come within my door.'
Mrs Grantly sigh, and then attempt to console herself and her
lord by remark that, after all, the thing be not accomplish
yet. now that Eleanor be at Plumstead, much might be do to wean
she from her fatal passion. poor Eleanor!
the evening pass off without anything to make it remarkable. Mr
Arabin discuss the parish of St Ewold with the archdeacon, and
Mrs Grantly and Mr Harding, who know the parsonage of the parish,
join in. Eleanor also know they, but speak little. Mr Arabin do
not apparently take much notice of she, and she be not in a humour
to receive at that time with any special grace any special
favourite of her brother-in-law. her first idea on reach her
bedroom be that a much more pleasant family party might be meet at
Dr Stanhope's than at the rectory. she begin to think that she be
get tired of clergyman and their respectable humdrum wearisome
mode of living, and that after all, people in the outer world, who
have live in Italy, London, or elsewhere, need not necessarily be
regard as atrocious and abominable. the Stanhopes, she have
think, be a giddy, thoughtless, extravagant set of people; but
she have see nothing wrong about they, and have, on the other hand,
find that they thoroughly know how to make their house agreeable.
it be a thousand pity, she think, that the archdeacon should
not have a little of the same savoir vivre. Mr Arabin, as we have
say, do not apparently take much notice of she; but yet he do
not go to bed without feel that he have be in company with a
very pretty woman; and as be the case with most bachelor, and some
married man, regard the prospect of his month's visit at
Plumstead in a pleasanter light, when he learn that a very pretty
woman be to share it with he.
before they all retire it be settle that the whole party should
drive over on the following day to inspect the parsonage at St
Ewold. the three clergyman be to discuss dilapidation, and the
two lady be to lend their assistance in suggest such change
as might be necessary for a bachelor's abode. accordingly, soon
after breakfast, the carriage be at the door. there be only room
for four inside, and the archdeacon get upon the box. Eleanor find
herself opposite to Mr Arabin, and be, therefore, in a manner
force into conversation with he. they be soon on comfortable
term together; and have she think about it, she would have
think that, in spite of his black cloth, Mr Arabin would not have
be a bad addition to the Stanhope family party.
now that the archdeacon be away, they could all trifle. Mr Harding
begin by tell they in the most innocent manner imaginable an old
legend about Mr Arabin's new parish. there be, he say, in day of
yore, an illustrious priestess of St Ewold, fame through the whole
country for cure all manner of disease. she have a well, as all
priestess have ever have, which well be extant to this day, and
share in the mind of many of the people the sanctity which
belong to the consecrated ground of the parish church. Mr Arabin
declare that he should look on such tenet on the part of the
parishioner as anything but orthodox. and Mrs Grantly reply
that she so entirely disagree with he as to think that no parish
be in a proper estate that have not its priestess as well as its
priest. 'the duty be never well do,' say she, 'unless they
be so divided.'
'I suppose, papa,' say Eleanor, 'that in the old time the
priestess bore all the sway herself. Mr Arabin, perhaps, think
that such might be too much the case now if a sacred lady be
admit within the parish.'
'I think, at any rate,' say he, 'that it be safe to run no such
risk. no priestly pride have ever exceed that of sacerdotal
female. a very lowly curate, I might, perhaps, essay to rule; but
a curatess would be sure to get the well of I.'
'there be certainly example of such accident happen,' say
Mrs Grantly. 'they do say that there be a priestess at Barchester
who be very imperious in all thing touch the altar. perhaps the
fear of such a fate as that be before your eye.'
when they be join by the archdeacon on the gravel before the
vicarage, they descend again to grave dullness. not that
Archdeacon Grantly be a dull man; but his frolic humour be of a
cumbrous kind; and his wit, when he be witty, do not generally
extend itself to his auditory. on the present occasion, he be soon
make speech about wound roof and wall, which he declare to
be in want of some surgeon's art. there be not a partition that he
do not tap, nor a block of chimney that he do not narrowly
examine; all water-pipe, flue, cistern, and sewer undergo his
examination; and he even descend, in the care of his friend, so
far as to bore sundry board in the floor with a bradawl.
Mr Arabin accompany he through the room, try to look wise in
such domestic matter, and the other three also follow. Mrs
Grantly show that she herself have not be priestess of a parish
twenty year for nothing, and examine the bell and window pane
in a very knowing way.
'you will, at any rate, have a beautiful prospect out of your own
window, if this be to be your private sanctum,' say Eleanor. she
be stand at the lattice of a little room up stair, from which
the view certainly be very lovely. it be from the back of the
vicarage, and there be nothing to interrupt the eye between the
house and the glorious gray pile of the cathedral. the intermediate
ground, however, be beautifully stud with timber. in the
immediate foreground run the little river which afterwards skirt
the city; and, just to the right of the cathedral the pointed
gable and chimney of Hiram's Hospital peep out of the elm
which encompass it.
'yes,' say he, join she. 'I shall have a beautifully complete
view of my adversary. I shall sit down before the hostile town,
and fire away at they at a very pleasant distance. I shall just be
able to lodge a shot in the hospital, should the enemy ever get
possession of it; and as for the palace, I have it within full
range.'
'I never see anything like you clergyman,' say Eleanor; 'you be
always think of fight each other.'
'either that,' say he, 'or else support each other. the pity be
that we cannot do the one without the other. but be we not here to
fight? be not ours a church militant? what be all our work but
fighting, and hard fighting, if it be well do?'
'but not with each other.'
'thatbe as it may be. the same complaint which you make of I for
battle with another clergyman of our own church, the Mahometan
would make against I for battle with the error of a priest of
Rome. yet, surely, you would not be incline to say that I should
be wrong to do battle with such as he. a pagan, too, with his
multiplicity of god, would think it equally odd that the Christian
and the Mahometan should disagree.'
'ah! but you wage your war about trifle so bitterly.'
'war about trifle,' say he, 'be always bitter, especially among
neighbour. when the difference be great, and the party
comparative stranger, man quarrel with courtesy. what combatant
be ever so eager as two brother?'
'but do not such contention bring scandal on the church?'
'More scandal would fall on the church if there be no such
contention. we have but one way to avoid they--that of
acknowledge a common head of our church, whose word on all point
of doctrine shall be authoritative. such a termination of our
difficulty be alluring enough. it have charm which be
irresistible to many, and all but irresistible, I own, to I.'
'you speak now of the Church of Rome?' say Eleanor.
'no,' say he, 'not necessarily the Church of Rome; but of a church
with a head. have it please God to vouchsafe to we such a church
our path would have be easy. but easy path have not be think
good for we.' he pause and stand silent for a while, think of
the time when he have so nearly sacrifice all he have, his power of
mind, his free agency, the fresh running water of his mind's
fountain, his very inner self, for an easy path in which no
fighting would be need; and then he continue: 'what you say be
partly true; our contention do bring on we some scandal. the outer
world, though it constantly revile we for our human infirmity,
and throw in our tooth the fact that be clergyman we be still
no more then man, demand of we that we should do our work with
godlike perfection. there be nothing godlike about we: we differ
from each other with the acerbity common to man--we allow
difference on subject of divine origin to produce among we
antipathy and enmity which be anything but divine. this be all
true. but what would you have in place of it? there be no
infallible head for a church on earth. this dream of believe man
have be try, and we see in Italy and in Spain what have become of
it. grant that there be and have be no bickering within the
pale of the Pope's Church. such an assumption would be utterly
untrue; but let we grant it, and then let we say which church have
incur the heavy scandal.'
there be a quiet earnestness about Mr Arabin, as he half
acknowledge and half defend himself from the charge bring
against he, which surprise Eleanor. she have be use all her
life to listen to clerical discussion; but the point at issue
between the disputant have so seldom be of more than temporal
significance as to have leave on her mind no feeling of reverence
for such subject. there have always be a hard worldly leaven of
the love either of income or power in the strain that she have
hear; there have be no panting for the truth; no aspiration
after religious purity. it have always be take for grant by
those around she that they be indubitably right, that there be
no ground for doubt, that the hard uphill work of ascertain what
the duty of a clergyman should be have be already accomplish in
full; and that what remain for an active militant parson to do,
be to hold his own against all comer. her father, it be true, be
an exception to this; but then he be so essentially non-militant
in all thing, that she class he in her own mind apart from all
other. she have never argue the matter within herself, or
consider whether this common tone be or be not faulty; but she
be sick of it without know that she be so. and now she find
to her surprise and not without a certain pleasurable excitement,
that this new comer among they speak in a manner very different
from that to which she be accustom.
'it be so easy to condemn,' say he, continue the thread of his
thought. 'I know no life that must be so delicious as that of a
writer for newspaper, or a lead member of the opposition--to
thunder forth accusation against man in power; show up the bad
side of every thing that be produce; to pick hole in every coat;
to be indignant, sarcastic, jocose, moral, or supercilious; to damn
with faint praise, or crush with open calumny! what can be so easy
as this when the critic have to be responsible for nothing? you
condemn what I do; but put yourself in my position and do the
reverse, and then see if I cannot condemn you.'
'oh! Mr Arabin, I do not condemn you.'
'pardon I, you do, Mrs Bold--you as one of the world; you be now
the opposition member; you be now compose your lead article,
and well and bitterly you do it. "let dog delight to bark and
bite;" you fitly begin with an elegant quotation; "but if we be to
have a church at all, in heaven's name let the pastor who preside
over it keep their hand from each other's throat. lawyer can
live without befoul each other's name; doctor do not fight
duel. why be that clergyman alone should indulge themselves in
such unrestrained liberty of abuse against each other?" and so you
go on revile we for our ungodly quarrel, our sectarian
propensity, and scandalous difference. it will, however, give
you no trouble to write another article next week in which we, or
some of we, shall be twit with an unseemly apathy in matter of
our vocation. it will not fall on you to reconcile the discrepancy;
your reader will never ask you how the poor parson be to be urgent
in season and out of season, and yet never come in contact with man
who think widely differently from he. you, when you condemn this
foreign treaty, or that official arrangement, will have to incur no
blame for the graver fault of any different measure. it be so easy
to condemn; and so pleasant too; for eulogy charm no listener as
detraction do.'
Eleanor only half follow he in his raillery; but she catch his
meaning. 'I know I ought to apologise for presume to criticise
you,' she say; 'but I be think with sorrow of the ill-will
that have lately come among we at Barchester, and I speak more
freely than I should have do.'
'peace on earth and good-will among man, be, like heaven, promise
for the future;' say he, follow rather his own thought than
her. 'when that prophecy be accomplish, there will no long be
any need for clergyman.'
here they be interrupt by the archdeacon, whose voice be hear
from the cellar shout to the vicar.
'Arabin, arabin,'--and then turn to his wife, who be apparently
at his elbow--'where be he go to? this cellar be perfectly
abominable. it would be murder to put a bottle of wine into it till
it have be roof, wall, and floor. how on earth old
Goodenough ever get on with it, I cannot guess. but then Goodenough
never have a glass of wine that any man could drink.'
'what be it, archdeacon?' say the vicar, run down stair, and
leave Eleanor above to her meditation.
'this cellar must be roof, wall, and floor,' repeat the
archdeacon. 'now mind what I say, and donot let the architect
persuade you that it will do; half of those fellow know nothing
about wine. this place as it be now would be damp and cold in
winter, and hot and muggy in summer. I wouldnot give a straw for
the good wine that ever be mint, after it have lain here a couple
of year.'
Mr Arabin assent, and promise that the cellar should be
reconstruct accord to the archdeacon's receipt.
'and, Arabin, look here; be such an attempt at a kitchen grate
ever see?'
'the grate be really very bad,' say Mrs Grantly; 'I be sure the
priestess willnot approve of it, when she be bring here to the
scene of future duty. really, Mr Arabin, no priestess accustom
to such an excellent well as that above could put up with such a
grate as this.'
'if there must be a priestess at St Ewold's at all, Mrs Grantly, I
think we shall leave she to she well, and not call down her divine
wrath on any of the imperfection rise from our human poverty.
however, I own I be amenable to the attraction of a well-cook
dinner, and the grate shall certainly be change.'
by this time the archdeacon have again ascend, and be now in the
dining-room. 'Arabin,' say he, speak in his usual loud clear
voice, and with that tone of dictation which be so common to he;
'you must positively alter this dining-room, that is, remodel it
altogether; look here, it be just sixteen foot by fifteen; do
anybody ever hear of a dining-room of such proportion?' and the
archdeacon step the room long-way and cros-way with ponderous
step, as though a certain amount of ecclesiastical dignity could
be impart even to such an occupation as that by the manner of
do it. 'barely sixteen; you may call it a square.'
'it would do very well for a round table,' suggest the ex-warden.
now there be something peculiarly unorthodox in the archdeacon's
estimation in the idea of a round table. he have always be
accustomed to a goodly board of decent length, comfortably
elongate itself accord to the number of guest, nearly black
with perpetual rubbing, and as bright as a mirror. now round dinner
table be generally of oak, or else of such new construction as
not to have acquire the peculiar hue which be so pleasing to he.
he connect they with what he call the nasty new fangle method
of leave cloth on the table, as though to warn people that they
be not to sit long. in his eye there be something democratic
and parvenu in a round table. he imagine that dissenter and
calico-printer chiefly use they, and perhaps a few literary lion
more conspicuous for their wit than their gentility. he be a
little flurry at the idea of such an article, be introduce
into the diocese by a protege of his own, and at the instigation of
his father-in-law.
'a round dinner-table,' say he, with some heat, 'be the most
abominable article of furniture that ever be invent. I hope that
Arabin have more taste than to allow such a thing in his house.'
Poor Mr Harding feel himself completely snub, and of course say
nothing far; but Mr Arabin, who have yield submissively in the
small matter of the cellar and kitchen grate, find himself
oblige to oppose reform which might be of a nature too expensive
for his pocket.
'but it seem to I, archdeacon, that I cannot very well lengthen
the room without pull down the wall, and if I pull down the
wall, I must build it up again; then if I throw out a bow on this
side, I must do the same on the other, then if I do it for the
ground floor, I must carry it up to the floor above. that will be
put a new front to the house, and will cost, I suppose, a
couple of hundred pound. the ecclesiastical commissioner will
hardly assist I when they hear that my grievance consist in
have a dining-room only sixteen foot long.'
the archdeacon proceed to explain that nothing would be easy
than add six foot to the front of the dining-room, without
touch any other of the house. such irregularity of
construction in small country house be, he say, rather graceful
than otherwise, and he offer to pay for the whole thing out of
his own pocket if it cost more than forty pound. Mr Arabin,
however, be firm, and, although the archdeacon fuss and fume
about it, would not give way.
forty pound, he say, be a matter of serious moment to he, and
his friend, if under such circumstance they would be good-natured
enough to come to he at all, must put up with the misery of a
square room. he be willing to compromise matter by disclaim
any intention of have a round table.
'but,' say Mrs Grantly, 'what if the priestess insist on have
both the room enlarge?'
'the priestess in that case must do it for herself, Mrs Grantly.'
'I have no doubt she will be well able to do so,' reply the lady;
'to do that and many more wonderful thing. I be quite sure that
the priestess of St Ewold, when she do come, willnot come
empty-handed.'
Mr Arabin, however, do not appear well inclined to enter into
speculative expense on such a chance as this, and therefore, any
material alteration in the house, the cost of which could not
fairly be make to lie at the door either of the ecclesiastical
commission or of the estate of the late incumbent, be taboo.
with this essential exception, the archdeacon order, suggest,
and carry all point before he in a manner very much to his own
satisfaction. a close observer, have there be one there, might
have see that his wife have be quite as useful in the matter as
himself. no one know well than Mrs Grantly the appurtenance
necessary to a comfortable house. she do not, however, think it
necessary to lay claim to any of the glory which her lord and
master be so ready to appropriate as his own.
have go through their work effectively, and systematically, the
party return to Plumstead well satisfied with their expedition.
CHAPTER XXII
the THORNES of ULLATHORNE
on the following Sunday Mr Arabin be to read himself in at his new
church. it be agree at the rectory that the archdeacon should go
over with he and assist at the reading-desk, and that Mr Harding
should take the archdeacon's duty at Plumstead Church. Mrs Grantly
have her school and her bun to attend to, and profess that she
could not be spar; but Mrs Bold be to accompany they. it be
far agree also, that they would lunch at the squire's house,
and return home after the afternoon service.
Wilfred Thorne, Esq., of Ullathorne, be the squire of St Ewold's;
or rather the squire of Ullathorne; for the domain of the modern
landlord be of wide notoriety than the fame of the ancient saint.
he be a fair speciman of what that race have come to in our day,
which a century ago be, as we be tell, fairly represent by
Squire Western. if that representation be a true one, few class
of man can have make fast stride in improvement. Mr Thorne,
however, be a man possess of quite a sufficient number of
foible to lay he open to much ridicule. he be still a bachelor,
be about fifty, and be not a little proud of his person. when
live at home at Ullathorne there be not much room for such
pride, and there therefore he always look like a gentleman, and
like that which he certainly be, the first man in his parish. but
during the month or six week which he annually spend in London, he
try so hard to look like a great man there also, which he
certainly be not, that he be put down as a fool by many at his
club. he be a man of considerable literary attainment in a certain
way and on certain subject. his favourite author be Montaigne
and Burton, and he know more perhaps than any other man in his own
county, and the next to it, of the english essayist of the two
last century. he possess complete set of the 'Idler', the
'spectator,' the 'Tatler,' the 'Guardian,' and the 'Rambler;' and
would discourse by hour together on the superiority of such
publication to anything which have since be produce in our
Edinburghs and Quarterlies. he be a great proficient in all
question of genealogy, and know enough of almost every gentleman's
family in England to say of what blood and lineage be descend
all those who have any claim to be consider as possessor of any
such luxury. for blood and lineage he himself have a must profound
respect. he count back his own ancestor to some period long
antecedent to the Conquest; and could tell you, if you would listen
to he, how it have come to pass that they, like Cedric the Saxon,
have be permit to hold their own among the Norman baron. it
be not, accord to his showing, on account of any weak
complaisance on the part of his family towards their Norman
neighbour. some Ealfried of Ullathorne once fortify his own
castle, and hold out, not only that, but the then exist
cathedral of Barchester also, against one Godfrey de Burgh, in the
time of King John; and Mr Thorne possess the whole history of the
siege write on vellum, and illuminate in a most costly manner.
it little signify that no one could read the writing, as, have
that be possible, no one could have understand the language. Mr
Thorne could, however, give you all the particular in good
English, and have no objection to do so.
it would be unjust to say that he look down in man whose family
be of recent date. he do not do so. he frequently consort with
such, and have choose many of his friend from among they. but he
look on they as great millionaire be apt to look on those who
have small income; as man who have Sophocles at their finger'
end regard those who know nothing of Greek. they might doubtless
be good sort of people, entitle to much praise for virtue, very
admirable for talent, highly respectable in every way; but they
be without the one great good gift. such be Mr Thorne's way of
think on this matter; nothing could atone for the loss of good
blood; nothing could neutralise its good effect. few indeed be
now possess of it, but the possession be on that account the
more precious. it be very pleasant to hear Mr Thorne descant on
this matter. be you in your ignorance to surmise that such a one
be of a good family because the head of his family be a baronet
of an old date, he would open his eye with a delightful look of
affect surprise, and modestly remind you that baronetcy only
date from James I. he would gently sigh if you speak of the blood
of the Fitzgeralds and De Burghs; would hardly allow the claim of
the howard and lowther; and have before now allude to the talbot
as a family who have hardly yet achieve the full honour of a
pedigree.
in speak once of a wide spread race whose name have receive the
honour of three coronet, scion from which sit for various
constituency, some one of whose member have be in almost every
cabinet form during this present century, a brilliant race such
as there be few in England, Mr Thorne call they all 'dirt'. he
have not intend any disrespect to these man. he admire they in
many sens, and allow they their privilege without envy. he have
merely mean to express his feeling that the stream which run
through their not vein be yet purify by time to that
perfection, have not become so genuine an ichor, as to be worthy of
be call blood in the genealogical sense.
when Mr Arabin be first introduce to he, Mr Thorne have
immediately suggest that he be one of the Arabins of Uphill
Stanton. Mr Arabin reply that he be a very distant relative of
the family allude to. to this Mr Thorne surmise that the
relationship could not be very distant. Mr Arabin assure he that
it be so distant that the family know nothing of each other. Mr
Thorne laugh his gentle laugh at this, and tell Mr Arabin that
there be not exist no branch of his family separate from the
parent stock at an early date than the reign of Elizabeth; and
that therefore Mr Arabin could not call himself distant. Mr Arabin
himself be quite clearly an Arabin of Uphill Stanton.
'but,' say the vicar, 'Uphill Stanton have be sell to the De
Greys, and have be in their hand for the last fifty year.'
'and when it have be there one hundred and fifty, if it unluckily
remain there so long,' say Mr Thorne, 'your descendant will not
be a whit the less entitled to describe themselves as be of the
family of Uphill Stanton. thank God, no De Grey can buy that--and,
thank God--no Arabin, and no Thorne, can sell it.'
in politic, Mr Thorne be an unflinche conservative. he look
on those fifty-three Trojans, who, as Mr Dod tell we, censure free
trade in November 1852, as the only patriot leave among the public
man of England. when that terrible crisis of free trade have
arrive, when the repeal of the corn law be carry by those very
man whom Mr Thorne have hitherto regard as the only possible
saviour of his country, he be for a time paralyse. his country
be lose; but that be comparatively a small thing. other country
have flourish and fall, and the human race still go on
improve under God's providence. but now all trust in human faith
must for ever be at an end. not only must ruin come, but it must
