come through the apostasy of those who have be regard as the
truest of true believer. politic in England, as a pursuit for
gentleman, must be at an end. have Mr Thorne be tread under foot
by a Whig, he could have bear it as a Tory and a martyr; but to be
so utterly throw over and deceive by those he have so earnestly
support, so thoroughly trust, be more than he could endure and
live. he therefore cease to live as a politician, and refuse to
hold any converse with the world at large on the state of the
country.
such be Mr Thorne's impression for the first two or three year
after Sir Robert Peel's apostasy; but by degree his temper, as do
that of other, cool down. he begin once more to move about, to
frequent the bench and the market, and to be see at dinner,
shoulder to shoulder with some of those who have so cruelly betray
he. it be a necessity for he to live, and that plan of his for
avoid the world do not answer. he, however, have other around
he, who still maintain the same staunch principle of
protection--man like himself, who be too true to flinch at the
cry of a mob--have their own way of console themselves. they be,
and feel themselves to be, the only true depository leave of
certain eleusinian mystery, of certain deep and wondrous service
of worship by which alone the god could be rightly approach. to
they and they only be it now give to know these thing, and to
perpetuate they, if that might still be do, by the careful and
secret education of their child.
we have read how private and peculiar form of worship have be
carry on from age to age in family, which to the outer world
have apparently adhere to the service of some ordinary church. and
so by degree it be with Mr Thorne. he learn at length to listen
calmly while protection be talk of as a thing dead, although he
know within himself that it be still quick with a mystic life. nor
be he without a certain pleasure that such knowledge though give
to he should be debar from the multitude. he become accustom
to hear, even among country gentleman, that free trade be after
all not so bad, and to bear this without dispute, although
conscious within himself that everything good in England have go
with his old palladium. he have within he something of the feeling
of Cato, who glory that he could kill himself because Romans be
no long worthy of their name. Mr Thorne have no thought of kill
himself, be a Christian, and still possess his l 4000 a year;
but the feeling be not on that account the less comfortable.
Mr Thorne be a sportsman, and have be active though not
outrageous in his sport. previous to the great downfall of
politic in his country, he have support the hunt by every mean
in his power. he have preserve game till no goose or turkey could
show a tail in the parish of St Ewold's. he have plant gorse
cover with more care than oak and larch. he have be more
anxious for the comfort of his fox than of his ewe and lamb. no
meet have be more popular than Ullathorne; no man's stable have
be more liberally open to the horse of distant man than Mr
Thorne's; no man have say more, write more, or do more to keep
the club up. the theory of protection could expand itself so
thoroughly in the practice of the country hunt! but the great ruin
come; when the noble master of the Barchester hound support the
recreant minister in the House of Lords, and basely surrender his
truth, his manhood, his friend, and his honour for the hope of a
garter, then Mr Thorne give up the hunt. he do not cut his
cover, for that would not have be the act of a gentleman. he do
not kill his fox, for that accord to his light would have be
murder. he do not say that his cover should not be draw, or his
earth stop, for that would have be illegal accord to the
by-law prevail among country gentleman. but he absent himself
from home on the occasion of every meet at Ullathorne, leave the
cover to their fate, and could not be persuade to take his pink
coat out of the press, or his hunter out of his stable. this
last for two year, and then by degree he come round. he first
appear at a neighbouring meet on a pony, dress in his shooting
coat, as though he have trot in by accident; then he walk up
one morning on foot to see his favourite gorse draw, and when his
groom bring his mare out by chance, he do not refuse to mount
she. he be next persuade, by one of the immortal fifty-three, to
bring his hunting material over to the other side of the county,
and take a fortnight with the hound there; and so gradually he
return to his old life. but in hunting as in other thing he be
only support by the inward feeling of mystic superiority to those
with whom he share the common breath of outer life.
Mr Thorne do not live in solitude at Ullathorne. he have a sister,
who be ten year old than himself, and who participate in his
prejudice and feeling so strongly, that she be a living
caricature of all his foible. she would not open a modern
quarterly, do not choose to see a magazine in her drawing-room,
and would not have pollute her finger with a shred of "the Times"
for any consideration. she speak of Addison, Swift, and Steele, as
though they be still live, regard De Foe as the well know
novelist of his country, and thought of fielding as a young but
meritorious novice in the field of romance. in poetry, she be
familiar with then name as late as Dryden, and have once be
seduce into read the "rape of the lock"; but she regard
Spenser as the pure type of her country's literature in this
line. genealogy be her favourite insanity. those thing which be
the pride of most genealogist be to her contemptible. arm and
motto set she beside herself. Ealfried of Ullathorne have want
no motto to assist he in cleave to the brisket Geoffrey De
Burgh; and Ealfried's great grandfather, the gigantic Ullafrid, have
require no other arm than those which nature give he to hurl
from the top of his own castle a cousin of the base invade
Norman. to she all modern English name be equally insignificant.
Hengist, Horsa, and such like, have for she the only true savour of
nobility. she be not content unless she could go beyond the
Saxons; and would certainly have christen her child, have she
have child, by the name of the ancient Britons. in some respect
she be not unlike Scott's Ulrica, and have she be give to
cursing, she would certainly have do so in the name of Mista,
Skogula, and Zernebock. not have submit to the embrace of any
pollute Normans, as poor Ulrica have do, and have assist no
parricide, the milk of human kindness be not curdle in her bosom.
she never curse, therefore, but bless rather. this, however, she
do in a strange uncouth Saxon manner, that would have be
unintelligible to any peasant but her own.
as a politician, Miss Thorne have be so thoroughly disgusted with
public life by base deed long antecedent to the Corn Law question,
that that have but little move she. in her estimation her brother
have be a fast young man, hurry away by a too ardent temperament
into democratic tendency. now happily he be bring to sounder
view by see the iniquity of the world. she have not yet
reconcile herself to the Reform Bill, and still groan in spirit
over the defalcation of the Duke as touch the Catholic
Emancipation. if ask whom she think the Queen should take as
her counsellor, she would probably have name Lord Eldon; and when
remind that that venerable man be no long present in the flesh
to assist we, she would probably have answer with a sigh that
none now could help we but the dead.
in religion, Miss Thorne be a pure druidess. we would not have it
understand by that, that she do actually in these latter day
assist at any human sacrifice, or that she be in fact hostile to
the Church of Christ. she have adopt the christian religion as a
milder form of the worship of her ancestor, and always appeal to
she do so as evidence that she have no prejudice against reform,
when it could be show that reform be salutary. this reform be
the most modern of any to which she have as yet accede, it be
presume that british lady have give up their paint and take to
some sort of petticoat before the day of St Augustine. that
further feminine step in advance which combine paint and
petticoat together, have not find votary in Miss Thorne.
but she be a Druidess in this, that she regret she know not
what in the usage and practice of her Church. she sometimes
talk and constantly think of good thing go by, though she
have but the faint idea of what those good thing have be. she
imagine that a purity have exist which be now go; that a piety
have adorn our pastor and a simple docility our people, for which
it may be fear history give she but little true warrant. she be
accustomed to speak of Cranmer as though he have be the firm
and most simple-minded of martyr, and of Elizabeth as though the
pure protestant faith of her people have be the one anxiety of her
life. it would have be cruel to undeceive she, have it be
possible; but it would have be impossible to make she believe
that the one be a time-serve priest, willing to go any length to
keep his place, and that the other be in heart a papist, with this
sole proviso, that she should be her own pope.
and so Miss Thorne go on sigh and regretting, look back to
the divine right of king as the rule axiom of a golden age, and
cherishing, low down in the bottom of her heart of heart, a dear
unmentione wish for the restoration of some exile Stuart. who
would deny she the luxury of her sigh, or the sweetness of her
soft regret!
in her person and her dress she be perfect, and well she know she
own perfection. she be a small elegantly make old woman, with a
face from which the glow of her youth have not depart without
leave some streak of a roseate hue. she be proud of her colour,
proud of her grey hair which she wear in short crisp curl peer
out all around her face from the dainty white cap. to think of all
the money that she spend in lace use to break the heart of poor
Mrs Quiverful with her seven daughter. she be proud of her tooth,
which be still white and numerous, proud of her bright cheery
eye, proud of her short jaunty step, and very proud of the neat,
precise, small foot with which those step be take. she be
proud also, ay, very proud, of the rich brocade silk in which it
be her custom to ruffle through her drawing-room.
we know what be the custom of the lady of branksome--
"nine and twenty knight of fame
Hung their shield in Branksome Hall."
the lady of Ullathorne be not so martial in her habit, but hardly
less costly. she might have boast that nine-and-twenty silken
shirt might have be produce in her chamber, each fit to stand
alone. the nine-and-twenty shield of the scottish hero be less
independent, and hardly more potent to withstand any attack that
might be make on they. Miss Thorne when fully dress might be say
to have be armed cap-a-pie, and she be always fully dress, as
far as be ever know to mortal man.
for all this rich attire Miss Thorne be not indebted to the
generosity of her brother. she have a very comfortable independence
of her own, which she divide among juvenile relative, the
milliner, and the poor, give much the large share to the
latter. it may be imagine, therefore, that with all her little
folly she be not unpopular. all her folly have, we believe,
be tell. her virtue be too numerous to describe, and not
sufficiently interesting to deserve description.
while we be on the subject of the Thornes, one word must be say
of the house they live in. it be not a large house, nor a fine
house, nor perhaps to modern idea a very commodious house; but by
those who love the peculiar colour and peculiar ornament of
genuine Tudor architecture it be consider a perfect gem. we beg
to own ourselves among the number, and therefore take this
opportunity to express our surprise that so little be know by
english man and woman of the beauty of english architecture. the
ruin of the Colosseum, the Campanile at Florence, St Mark's,
Cologne, the Bourse and Notre Dame, be with our tourist as
familiar as household word; but they know nothing of the glory
of Wiltshire, dorsetshire, and Somersetshire. Nay, we much question
whether many noted traveller, many who have pitch their tent
perhaps under Mount Sinai, be not still ignorant that there be
glory in Wiltshire, Dorsetshire and Somersetshire. we beg that
they will go and see.
Mr Thorne's house be call Ullathorne Court, and be properly so
call; for the house itself form two side of a quadrangle,
which be complete in the other two side by a wall about twenty
foot high. this be build of cut stone, rudely cut indeed, and now
much worn, but of a beautiful rich tawny yellow colour, the effect
of that stonecrop of minute growth, which it have take three
century to produce. the top of this wall be ornament by huge
round stone ball of the same colour as the wall itself. entrance
into the court be have through a pair of iron gate, so massive
that no one could comfortably open or close they, consequently they
be rarely disturb. from the gateway two path lead obliquely
across the court; that to the left reach the hall-door, which
be in the corner make by the angle of the house, and that to the
right lead to the back entrance, which be at the further end of
the long portion of the building.
with those who be now adept at contrive house accommodation, it
will militate much against Ullathorne Court, that no carriage could
be bring to the hall-door. if you enter Ullathorne at all, you
must do so, fair reader, on foot, or at least in a bath-chair. no
vehicle draw by horse ever come within that iron gate. but this
be nothing to the next horror that will encounter you. on enter
the front door, which you do by no very grand portal, you find
yourself immediately in the dining-room. what--no hall? exclaim my
luxurious friend, accustom to all the comfortable appurtenance
of modern life. yes, kind sir; a noble hall, if you will but
observe it; a true old english hall of excellent dimension for a
country gentleman's family; but, if you please, no dining-parlour.
but Mr and Miss Thorne be proud of this peculiarity of their
dwelling, though the brother be once all but tempt by his
friend to alter it. they delight in the knowledge that they,
like Cedric, positively din in their true hall, even though they
so din tete-a-tete. but though they have never own, they have
feel and endeavour to remedy the discomfort of such an
arrangement. a huge screen partition off the front door and a
portion of the hall, and from the angle so screen off a second
door lead into a passage, which run along the large side of the
house next to the courtyard. either my reader or I must be a bad
hand at topography, if it be not clear that the great hall form
the ground-floor of the small portion of the mansion, that which
be to your left as you enter the iron gate, and that it occupy
the whole of this wing of the building. it must be equally clear
that it look out on a trim mow lawn, through three quadrangular
window with stone mullion, each window divide into a large
portion at the bottom, and a small portion at the top, and each
portion again divide into five by perpendicular stone supporter.
there may be window which give a well light than such as those,
and it may be, as my utilitarian friend observe, that the giving
of light be the desire object of a window. I will not argue the
point with he. indeed I cannot. but I shall not the less die in
the assured conviction that no sort of description of window be
capable of impart half as much happiness to mankind as that
which have be adopt at Ullathorne Court. what--not an oriel?
say Miss Diana de Midellage. no, Miss Diana; not even an oriel,
beautiful as be an oriel window. it have not about it so perfect a
feeling of quiet English homely comfort. let oriel window grace a
college, or the half public mansion of a potent peer; but for the
sitting room of quiet country lady, of ordinary homely folk,
nothing can equal the square mullione window of the Tudor
architect.
the hall be hang round with family female insipidity by lely,
and unprepossesse male Thornes in red coat by Kneller; each
Thorne have be let into a panel in the wainscoting in the
proper manner. at the further end of the room be a huge
fire-place, which afford much ground of difference between the
brother and sister. an antiquated grate that would hold about a
hundred weight of coal, have be stick on the hearth, by Mr
Thorne's father. this hearth have of course be intend for the
consumption of wood fagot, and the iron dog for the purpose be
still stand, though half bury in the masonry of the grate.
Miss Thorne be very anxious to revert to the dog. the dear good
old creature be always to revert to anything, and have she be
systematically indulge, would doubtless in time have reflect
that finger be make before fork, and have revert accordingly.
but in the affair of the fire-place, Mr Thorne would not revert.
country gentleman around he, all have comfortable grate in their
dining-room. he be not exactly the man to have suggest a modern
usage; but he be not so far prejudiced as to banish those which
his father have prepare for his use. Mr Thorne have, indeed, once
suggest that with very little contrivance the front door might
have be so altered, as to open at least into the passage; but on
hear this, his sister Monica, such be Miss Thorne's name, have
be take ill, and have remain so for a week. before she come
down stair she receive a pledge from her brother that the
entrance should never be change in her lifetime.
at the end of the hall opposite to the fire-place a door lead into
the drawing-room, which be of equal size, and light with
precisely similar window. but yet the aspect of the room be very
different. it be paper, and the ceiling, which in the hall
show the old rafter, be whiten and finish with a modern
cornice. Miss Thorne's drawing-room, or, as she always call it,
withdrawing-room, be a beautiful apartment. the window open on
to the full extent of the lovely trim garden; immediately before
the window be plot of flower in stiff, stately, stubborn
little bed, each bed surround by a stone cop of its own;
beyond, there be a low parapet wall, on which stand urn and
image, fawn, nymph, satyr, and a whole tribe of Pan's
follower; and then again, beyond that, a beautiful lawn slop
away to a sunk fence, which divide the garden from the park. Mr
Thorne's study be at the end of the drawing-room, and beyond that
be the kitchen and the office. door open into both Miss
Thorne's withdrawing-room and Mr Thorne's sanctum from the passage
above allude to; which, as it come to the latter room, widen
itself so as to make space for the huge black oak stair, which lead
to the upper region.
such be the interior of Ullathorne Court. but have thus
describe it, perhaps somewhat too tediously, we beg to say that it
be not the interior to which we wish to call the english tourist's
attention, though we advise he to lose no legitimate opportunity
of become acquaint with it in a friendly manner. it be the
outside of Ullathorne that be so lovely. let the tourist get
admission at least into the garden, and fle himself on that soft
award just opposite to the exterior angle of the house. he will
there get the double frontage, and enjoy that which be so
lovely--the expanse of architectural beauty without the formal
dullness of one long line.
it be the colour of Ullathorne that be so remarkable. it be all of
that delicious tawny hue which no stone can give, unless it have on
it the vegetable richness of century. strike the wall with your
hand, and you will think that the stone have on it no covering, but
rub it carefully, and you will find that the colour come off upon
your finger. no colourist that ever yet work from a palette have
be able to come up to this rich colouring of year crowd
themselves on year.
Ullathorne be a high building for a country house, for it possess
three story; and in each storey, the window be of the same sort
as that describe, though vary in size, and vary also in
their line athwart the house. those of the ground floor be all
uniform in size and position. but those above be irregular both in
size and place, and this irregularity give a bizarre and not
unpicturesque appearance to the building. along the top, on every
side, run a low parapet, which nearly hide the roof, and at the
corner be more figure of fawn and satyr.
such be Ullathorne House. but we must say one word of the approach
to it, which shall include all the description which we mean to
give of the church also. the picturesque old church of St Ewold's
stand immediately opposite to the iron gate which open into the
court, and be all but surround by the branch of lime tree,
which form the avenue lead up to the house from both side.
this avenue be magnificent, but it would lose much of its value in
the eye of many proprietor, by the fact that the road through it
be not private property. it be a public lane between hedgerow,
with a broad grass margin on each side of the road, from which the
lime tree spring. Ullathorne Court, therefore, do not stand
absolutely surround by its own ground, though Mr Thorne be owner
of all the adjacent land. this, however, be the source of very
little annoyance to he. man, when they be acquire property,
think much of such thing, but they who live where their ancestor
have live for year, do not feel the misfortune. it never occur
to either Mr or Miss Thorne that they be not sufficiently
private, because the world at large might, if it so wish, walk or
drive by their iron gate. that part of the world which avail
itself of the privilege be however very small.
such a year or two since be the Thornes of Ullathorne. such, we
believe, be the inhabitant of many an english country home. May
it be long before their number diminish.
CHAPTER XXIII
MR ARABIN READS himself in AT ST EWOLD'S
on the Sunday morning the archdeacon with his sister-in-law and Mr
Arabin drive over to Ullathorne, as have be arrange. on their way
thither the new vicar declare himself to be considerably disturb
in his mind at the idea of thus face his parishioner for the
first time. he have, he say, be always subject to mauvaise honte
and an annoying degree of bashfulness, which often unfit he for
any work of a novel description; and now he feel this so strongly
that he fear he should acquit himself badly in St Ewold's
reading-desk. he know, he say, that those sharp little eye of
Miss Thorne would be on to he, and that they would not approve.
all this the archdeacon greatly ridicule. he himself know not, and
have never know, what it be to be shy. he could not conceive that
Miss Thorne, surround as she would be by the peasant of
Ullathorne, and a few of the poor inhabitant of the suburb of
Barchester, could in any way affect the composure of a man well
accustom to address the learn congregation of St Mary's at
Oxford, and he laugh accordingly at the idea of Mr Arabin's
modesty.
Thereupon Mr Arabin commence to subtilise. the change, he say,
from St Mary's to St Ewold's be quite as powerful on the spirit
as would be that from St Ewold's to St Mary's. would not a peer
who, by chance of fortune, might suddenly be drive to herd among
the navvy be as afraid of the jeer of his companion, as would
any navvy suddenly exalt to a seat among the peer? Whereupon the
archdeacon declare with a loud laugh that he would tell Miss
Thorne that her new minister have liken she to a navvy. Eleanor,
however, pronounce such a conclusion unfair; a comparison might be
very just in its proportion which do not at all assimilate the
thing compare. but Mr Arabin go in subtilise, regard
neither the archdeacon's raillery nor Eleanor's defence. a young
lady, he say, would execute with most perfect self-possession a
difficult piece of music in a room crowd with stranger, who
would not be able to express herself in any intelligible language,
even on any ordinary subject and among her most intimate friend,
if she be require to do so stand on a box somewhat elevated
among they. it be all an affair of education, and he at forty
find it difficult to educate himself now.
Eleanor dissent on the matter of the box; and aver she could
speak very well about dress, or baby, or leg of mutton from
any box, provide it be big enough for she to stand upon without
fear, even though all her friend be listen to she. the
archdeacon be sure she would not be able to say a word; but this
prove nothing in favour of Mr Arabin. Mr Arabin say that he would
try the question out with Mrs Bold, and get she on a box some day
when the rectory might be full of visitor. to this Eleanor
assent, make condition that the visitor should be of their own
set, and the archdeacon cogitate in his mind, whether by such a
condition it be intend that Mr Slope should be include,
resolve also that, if so, the trial should certainly never take
place in the rectory drawing-room at Plumstead.
and so arguing, they drive up to the iron gate of Ullathorne
Court.
Mr and Miss Thorne be stand ready dress for church in the
hall, and greet their clerical visitor with cordiality. the
archdeacon be an old favourite. he be a clergyman of the old
school, and this recommend he to the lady. he have always be an
opponent of free trade as long as free trade be an open question;
and now that it be no long so, he, be a clergyman, have not
be oblige, like most of his lay Tory companion, to read his
recantation. he could therefore be regard as a supporter of the
immaculate fifty-three, and be on this account a favourite with Mr
Thorne. the little bell be tinkle, and the rural population be
stand about the lane, lean on the church stile, and against
the wall of the old court, anxious to get a look at their new
minister as he pass from the house to the rectory. the
archdeacon's servant have already precede they thither with the
vestment.
they all go together; and when the lady pass into the church
the three gentleman tarry a moment in the lane, that Mr Thorne
might name to the vicar with some kind of one-sided introduction,
the most leading among his parishioner.
'here be our churchwarden, Mr Arabin; Farmer Greenacre and Mr
Stiles. Mr Stiles have the mill as you go into Barchester; and very
good churchwarden they be.'
'not very severe, I hope,' say Mr Arabin: the two ecclesiastical
officer touch their hat, and each make a leg in the approved
rural fashion, assure the vicar that they be glad to have the
honour of see he, and add that the weather be very good for
the harvest. Mr Stiles be a man somewhat verse in town life,
have an impression of his own dignity, and do not quite like leave
his pastor under the erroneous idea that he be a churchwarden
keep the child in order during church time. 'twas thus he
understand Mr Arabin's allusion to his severity, and hasten to
put matter right by observe that 'Sexton Clodheave look to the
youngun, and perhaps sometimes there maybe a thought too much
stick go on during sermon.' Mr Arabin's bright eye twinkle as
he catch that of the archdeacon; and he smile to himself as he
observe how ignorant his officer be of the nature of their
authority, and of the surveillance which it be their duty to keep
even over himself.
Mr Arabin read the lesson and preach. it be enough to put a man
a little out, let he have be ever so used to pulpit reading, to
see the knowing way in which the farmer cock their ear, and set
about a mental criticism as to whether their new minister do or
do not fall short of the excellence of he who have lately depart
from they. a mental and silent criticism it be for the exist
moment, but soon to be make public among the elder of St Ewold's
over the green graf of their child and forefather. the
excellence, however, of poor old Mr Goodenough have not be
wonderful, and there be few there who do not deem that Mr Arabin
do his work sufficiently well, in spite of the slightly nervous
affection which at first impede he, and which nearly drive the
archdeacon beside himself.
but the sermon be the thing to try the man. it often surprise we
that very young man can muster courage to preach for the first time
to a strange congregation. man who be as yet little more than
boy, who have but just leave, what indeed we may not call a school,
but a seminary intend for their tuition as scholar, whose
thought have be mostly of boat, cricketing, and wine party,
ascend a rostrum high above the head of the submissive crowd, not
that they may read God's word to those below, but that they may
preach their own word for the edification of their hearer. it
seem strange to we that they be not stricken dumb by the new and
awful solemnity of their position. how be I, just turn
twenty-three, who have never yet pass then thoughtful day since
the power of thought first come to I, how be I to instruct these
grey beard, who with the weary thinking of so many year have
approach so near the grave? can I teach they their duty? can I
explain to they that which I so imperfectly understand, that which
year of study may have make so plain to they? have my newly acquire
privilege, as one of God's minister, impart to I as yet any
fitness for the wonderful work of a preacher?
it must be suppose that such idea do occur to young clergyman,
and yet they overcome, apparently with ease, this difficulty which
to we appear to be all but insurmountable. we have never be
subject in the way of ordination to the power of a bishop's
hand. it may be that there be in they something that sustain the
spirit and banish the natural modesty of youth. but for ourselves
we must own that the deep affection which Dominie Sampson feel for
his young pupil have not more endear he to we than the bashful
spirit which send he mute and inglorious from the pulpit when he
rise there with the futile attempt to preach God's gospel.
there be a rule in our church which forbid the young order of
our clergyman to perform a certain portion of the service. the
absolution must be read by a minister in priest's order. if there
be no such minister present, the congregation can have the benefit
of no absolution but that which each may succeed in administer
to himself. the rule may be a good one, though the necessity for it
hardly come home to the general understanding. but this
forbearance on the part of youth would be much more appreciated if
it be extend likewise to sermon. the only danger would be that
the congregation would be too anxious to prevent their young
clergyman from advance themselves to the rank of the ministry.
Clergymen who could not preach would be such blessing that they
would be bribe to adhere to their incompetence.
Mr Arabin, however, have not the modesty of youth to impede he, and
he succeed with his sermon even well than with the lesson. he
take for his text two verse out of the second epistle of St John:
'Whosoever trangresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ,
hath not God. he that abideth in the doctrine of Christ he hath
both the Father and Son. if there come any unto you and bring you
not this doctrine, receive he not into your house, neither bid he
God speed.' he tell they that the house of theirs to which he
allude be this their church in which he now address they for
the first time; that their most welcome and proper manner of
bid he God speed would be their patient obedience to this
teaching of the gospel; but that he could put forward no claim to
such conduct on their part unless he teach they the great
christian doctrine of work and faith combine. on this he
enlarge, but not very amply, and after twenty minute succeed in
send his new friend home to their baked mutton and pudding well
pleased with their new minister.
then come the lunch at Ullathorne. as soon as they be in the hall
Miss Thorne take Mr Arabin's hand, and assure he that she
receive he into her house, into the temple, she say, in which
she worship, and bid he God speed with all her heart. Mr
Arabin be touch, and squeeze the spinster's hand without
utter a word in reply. then Mr Thorne express a hope that Mr
Arabin find the church easy to fill, and Mr Arabin have reply
that he have no doubt he should do so as soon as he have learn to
pitch his voice to the building, they all sit down to the good
thing before they.
Miss Thorne take special care of Mrs Bold. Eleanor still wear her
widow's weed, and therefore have about she that air of grave and
sad maternity which be the lot of recent widow. this open the
soft heart of Miss Thorne, and make she look on her young guest as
though too much could not be do for she. she heap chicken and
ham upon her plate, and pour out for she a full bumper of port
wine. when Eleanor, who be not sorry to get it, have drink a little
of it, Miss Thorne at once essay to fill it again. to this
Eleanor object, but in vain. Miss Thorne wink and nod and
whisper, say that it be the proper thing and must be do,
and that she know all about it; and so she desire Mrs Bold to
drink it up, and mind any body.
'it be your duty, you know, to support yourself,' she say into the
ear of the young mother; 'therebe more than yourself depend on
it;' and thus she coshere up Eleanor with cold fowl and port wine.
how it be that poor man's wife, who have no cold fowl and port
wine on which to be coshere up, nurse their child without
difficulty, whereas the wife of rich man, who eat and drink
everything that be good, cannot do so, we will for the present
leave to the doctor and mother to settle between they.
and then Miss Thorne be great about tooth. little Johnny Bold have
be trouble for the last few day with his first incipient
masticator, and with that freemasonry which exist between lady,
Miss Thorne become aware of the fact before Eleanor have half
finish her wing. the old lady prescribe at once a receipt which
have be much in vogue in the young day of her grandmother, and
warn Eleanor with solemn voice against the fallacy of modern
medicine.
'take his coral, my dear,' say she, 'and rub it well with
carrot-juice; rub it till the juice dry on it, and then give it
to he to play with--'
'but he havenot get a coral,' say Eleanor.
'not get a coral!' say Miss Thorne, with almost angry vehemence.
'not get a coral!--how can you expect that he should cut his tooth?
have you get Daffy's elixir?'
Eleanor explain that she have not. it have not be order by Mr
Rerechild, the Barchester doctor whom she employ; and then the
young mother mention some shockingly modern succedaneum, which Mr
Rerechild's new light have teach he to recommend.
Miss Thorne look awfully severe. 'take care, my dear,' say she,
'that the man know what he be about; take care he donot destroy
your little boy. 'but'--and her voice soften into sorrow as she
say it, and speak more in pity than in anger--'but I donot know
who there be in Barchester now that you can trust. poor dear old Dr
Bumpwell, indeed--'
'why, Miss Thorne, he die when I be a little girl.'
'yes, my dear, he do, and an unfortunate day it be for
Barchester. as to those young man that have come up since' (Mr
Rerechild, by the by, be quite as old as Miss Thorne herself),
'one donot know where they come from or who they be, or whether
they know anything about their business or not.'
'I think there be very clever man in Barchester,' say Eleanor.
'perhaps there may be; only I donot know they; and itbe admit on
all side that medical man benot now what they use to be. they
use to be talente, observe, educate man. but now any
whipper-snapper out of an apothecary's shop can call himself a
doctor. I believe no kind of education be now think necessary.'
Eleanor be herself the widow of a medical man, and feel a little
inclined to resent all these hard saying. but Miss Thorne be so
essentially good-natured that it be impossible to resent anything
she say. she therefore sip her wine and finish her chicken.
'at any rate, my dear, donot forget the carrot-juice, and by all
mean get he a coral at once. my grandmother Thorne have the good
tooth in the county, and carry they to the grave with she at
eighty. I have hear she say it be all the carrot-juice. she
couldnot bear the Barchester doctor. even poor Dr Bumpwell donot
please she.' it clearly never occur to Miss Thorne that some
fifty year ago Dr Bumpwell be only a rise man, and therefore as
much in need of character in the eye of the then lady of
Ullathorne, as the present doctor be in her own.
the archdeacon make a very good lunch, and talk to his host about
turnip-driller and new machine for reap; while the host,
think it only polite to attend to a stranger, and fear that
perhaps he might not care about turnip crop on a Sunday, moot
all manner of ecclesiastical subject.
'I never see a heavy lot of wheat, Thorne, than youhave get there
in the field beyond the copse. I suppose thatbe guano,' say the
archdeacon.
'yes, guano. I get it from Bristol myself. youwill find you often
have a tolerable congregation of Barchester people out here, Mr
Arabin. they be very fond of St Ewold's, particularly of an
afternoon, when the weather be not too hot for a walk.'
'I be under an obligation to they for stay away today, at any
rate,' say the vicar. 'the congregation can never be too small for
a maiden sermon.'
'I get a ton and a half at Bradley's in High Street,' say the
archdeacon, 'and it be a complete take in. I donot believe there
be five hundred-weight of guano in it.'
'that Bradley never have anything good,' say Miss Tborne, who have
just catch the name during her whispering with Eleanor. 'and such
a nice shop as there use to be in that very house before he come.
Wilfred, donot you remember what good thing old Ambleoff use to
have?'
'there have be three man since Ambleoff's time,' say the
archdeacon, 'and each as bad as the other. but who get it for you
at Bristol, Thorne?'
'I run up myself this year and buy it out of the ship. I be
afraid as the evening get short, Mr Arabin, youwill find the
reading desk too dark. I must send a fellow with an axe and make
he lop off some of those branch.'
Mr Arabin declare that the morning light at any rate be perfect,
and deprecate any interference with the lime tree. and then they
take a stroll out among the trim parterre, and Mr Arabin explain
to Mrs Bold the difference between a naiad and a dryad, and dilate
on vas and the shape of urn. Miss Thorne busy herself among
the pansy; and her brother, find it quite impracticable to
give anything of a peculiarly Sunday tone to the conversation,
abandon the attempt, and have it out with the archdeacon about the
Bristol guano.
at three o'clock they again go into church; and now Mr Arabin
read the service and the archdeacon preach. nearly the same
congregation be present, with some adventurous pedestrian from
the city, who have not think the heat of the mid-day August sun
too great to deter they. the archdeacon take his text from the
Epistle of Philemon. 'I beseech thee for my son Onesimus, whom I
have beget in my bond.' from such a text it may be imagine the
kind of sermon which Dr Grantly preach, and on the whole it be
neither dull, nor bad, nor out of place.
he tell they it have become his duty to look about for a pastor for
they; to supply the place of one who have be long among they; and
that in this manner he regard as a son he whom he have select,
as St Paul have regard the young disciple whom he send forth. then
he take a little merit to himself for have studiously provide
the good man he could without reference to patronage or favour; but
he do not say that the good man accord to his view be he who
be well able to subdue Mr Slope, and make that gentleman's
situation in Barchester too hot to be comfortable. as to the bond,
they have consist in the exceed struggle which he have make to
get a good clergyman for they. he deprecate any comparison between
himself and St Paul, but say that he be entitle to beseech they
for their good will towards Mr Arabin, in the same manner that the
apostle have beseech Philemon and his household with regard to
Onesimus.
the archdeacon's sermon, text, blessing and all, be conclude
within the half hour. then they shake hand with their Ullathorne
friend, and return to Plumstead. 'twas thus that Mr Arabin read
himself in at St Ewold's.
CHAPTER XXIV
MR SLOPE'S MANAGES matter very CLEVERLY AT PUDDINGDALE
the next two week pass pleasantly enough at Plumstead. the whole
party there assemble seem to get on well together. Eleanor make
the house agreeable, and the archdeacon and Mrs Grantly seem to
have forget her injury as regard Mr Slope. Mr Harding have his
violoncello, and play to they while his daughter accompany
he. Johnny Bold, by the help either of Mr Rerechild or else by
that of his coral and carrot-juice, get through his teethe
trouble. there have be gaiety too of all sort. they have din
at Ullathorne, and the Thornes have din at the rectory. eleanor
have be duly put to stand on her box, and in that position have
find herself quite unable to express her opinion on the merit of
flounce, such have be the subject give to try her elocution.
Mr Arabin have of course be much in his own parish, look to the
doing at his vicarage, call on his parishioner, and take on
himself the duty of his new calling. but still he have be every
evening at Plumstead, and Mrs Grantly be partly willing to agree
with her husband that he be a pleasant inmate in a house.
they have also be at a dinner party at Dr Stanhope's, of which Mr
Arabin have make one. he also, moth-like, burn his wing in the
flame of the signora's candle. Mrs Bold, too, have be there, and
have feel somewhat displeased with the taste, want of taste she
call it, show by Mr Arabin in pay so much attention to Madame
Neroni. it be as infallible that Madeline should displease and
irritate the woman, as that she should charm and captivate the man.
the one result follow naturally on the other. it be quite true
that Mr Arabin have be charm. he think she a very clever and a
very handsome woman; he think also that her peculiar affliction
entitle she to the sympathy of all. he have never, he say, meet so
much suffering join to such perfect beauty and so clear a mind.
'twas thus he speak of the signora come home in the archdeacon's
carriage; and Eleanor by no mean like to hear the praise. it be,
however, exceedingly unjust of she to be angry with Mr Arabin, as
she have herself spend a very pleasant evening with Bertie Stanhope,
who have take she down to dinner, and have not leave her side for one
moment after the gentleman come out of the dining-room. it be
unfair that she should amuse herself with Bertie and yet begrudge
her new friend his licence of amuse himself with Bertie's sister.
and yet she do so. she be half angry with he in the carriage,
and say something about meretricious manner. Mr Arabin do not
understand the way of woman very well, or else he might have
flatter himself that Eleanor be in love with he.
but Eleanor be not in love with he. how many shade there be
between love and indifference, and how little the graduated scale
be understand! she have now be nearly three week in the same
house with Mr Arabin, and have receive much of his attention, and
listen daily to his conversation. he have usually devote at least
some portion of his evening to she exclusively. at Dr Stanhope's he
have devote himself exclusively to another. it do not require
that a woman should be in love to be irritated at this; it do not
require that she should even acknowledge to herself that it be
unpleasant to she. Eleanor have no such self-knowledge. she think
in her own heart it be only on Mr Arabin's account that she
regret that he could condescend to be amuse by the signora. 'I
think he have more mind,' she say to herself, as she sit watch
her baby's cradle on her return from the party. 'after all, I
believe Mr Stanhope be the pleasanter man of the two.' Alas for the
memory of poor John Bold! Eleanor be not in love with Bertie
Stanhope, nor be she in love with Mr Arabin. but her devotion to
her late husband be fast fade, when she could revolve in her
mind, over the cradle of his infant, the fault and failing of
other aspirant to her favour.
will any one blame my heroine for this? let he or her rather thank
God for all his goodness,--for his mercy endureth for ever.
Eleanor, in truth, be not in love; neither be Mr Arabin. neither
indeed be Bertie Stanhope, though he have already find occasion to
say nearly as much as that he be. the widow's cap have prevent
he from make a positive declaration, when otherwise he would
have consider himself entitle to do so on a third or fourth
interview. it be, after all, but a small cap now, and have but
little of the weeping-willow leave in its construction. it be
singular how these emblem of grief fade away by unseen gradation.
each pretend to be the counterpart of the forerunner, and yet the
last little bit of crimp white crape that sit so jauntily on the
back of the head, be as dissimilar to the first huge mountain of
woe which disfigure the face of the weeper, as the state of the
Hindoo be to the jointure of the English dowager.
but let it be clearly understand that Eleanor be in love with no
one, and that no one be in love with Eleanor. under these
circumstance her anger against Mr Arabin do not last long, and
before two day be over they be both as good friend as ever.
she could not but like he, for every hour spend in his company be
spend pleasantly. and yet she could not quite like he, for there
be always apparent in his conversation a certain feeling on his
part that he hardly think it worth his while to be in earnest. it
be almost as though he be play with a child. she know well
enough that he be in truth a sober thoughtful man, who in some
matter and on some occasion could endure an agony of earnestness.
and yet to she he be always gently playful. could she have see
his brow once cloud she might have learn to love he.
so thing go on at Plumstead, and on the whole not unpleasantly,
till a huge storm darken the horizon, and come down upon the
inhabitant of the rectory with all the fury of a water-spout. it
be astonish how in a few minute the whole face of the heaven
be change. the party break up from breakfast in perfect harmony;
but fierce passion have arise before the evening, which do not
admit of their sitting at the same board for dinner. to explain
this, it will be necessary to go back a little.
it will be remember that the bishop express to Mr Slope in his
dressing-room, his determination that Mr Quiverful should be
confirm in his appointment to the hospital, and that his lordship
request Mr Slope to communicate this decision to the archdeacon.
it will also be remember that the archdeacon have indignantly
decline see Mr Slope, and have, instead, write a strong letter
to the bishop, in which he all but demand the situation of warden
for Mr Harding. to this letter the archdeacon receive an immediate
formal reply from Mr Slope, in which it be state, that the bishop
have receive and would give his good consideration to the
archdeacon's letter.
the archdeacon feel himself somewhat checkmate by this reply. what
could he do with a man who would neither see he, nor argue with
he by letter, and who have undoubtedly the power of appoint any
clergyman he please? he have consult with Mr Arabin, who have
suggest the propriety of call in the aid of the master of
Lazarus. 'if,' say he, 'you and Dr Gwynne formally declare your
intention of wait upon the bishop, the bishop will not dare to
refuse to see you; and if two such man as you see he together, you
will probably not leave he without carry your point.'
the archdeacon do not quite like admit the necessity of his
be back by the master of Lazarus before he could obtain
admission into the episcopal palace of Barchester; but still he
feel that the advice be good, and he resolve to take it. he write
again to the bishop, express a hope that nothing far would
be do in the matter of the hospital, till the consideration
promise by his lordship have be give, and then send off a warm
appeal to his friend the master, implore he to come to Plumstead
and assist in drive the bishop into compliance. the master have
rejoin, raise some difficulty, but not decline; and the
archdeacon again press his point, insist on the necessity for
immediate action. Dr Gwynne unfortunately have the gout, and could
therefore name no immediate day, but still agree to come, if it
should be finally find necessary. so the matter stand, as regard
the party at Plumstead.
but Mr Harding have another friend fight the battle for he,
quite as powerful as the master of Lazarus, and this be Mr Slope.
though the bishop have so pertinaciously insist on give way to
his wife in the matter of the hospital, Mr Slope do not think it
necessary to abandon the object. he have, he think, daily more and
more reason to imagine that the widow would receive his overture
favourably, and he could not but feel that Mr Harding at the
hospital, and place there by his mean would be more likely to
receive he as a son-in-law, than Mr Harding growl in opposition
and disappointment under the archdeacon's wing at Plumstead.
moreover, to give Mr Slope due credit, he be actuate by great
motif even than these. he want a wife, and he want money, but
he want power more than either. he have fully realise the fact
that he must come to blow with Mrs Proudie. he have no desire to
remain in Barchester as her chaplain. soon than do so, he would
risk the loss of his whole connection with the diocese. what! be
he to feel within he the possession of no ordinary talent; be he
to know himself to be courageous, firm, and, in matter where his
conscience do not interfere, unscrupulous; and yet be content to
be the work factotum of a woman-prelate? Mr Slope have high
idea of his own destiny. either he or Mrs Proudie must go to the
wall; and now have come the time when he would try which it would
be.
the bishop have declare that Mr Quiverful should be the new warden.
as Mr Slope go down stair prepared to see the archdeacon if
necessary, but fully satisfied that no such necessity would arise,
he declare to himself that Mr Harding should be warden. with the
object of carry this point he ride over to Puddingdale, and have
a further interview with the worthy expectant of clerical good
thing. Mr Quiverful be on the whole a worthy man. the impossible
task of bring up as lady and gentleman fourteen child on an
income which be insufficient to give they with decency the common
necessity of life, have have an effect upon he not beneficial
either to his spirit, or his keen sense of honour. who can boast
that he would have support such a burden with a different result?
Mr Quiverful be an honest, pain- staking, drudge man; anxious,
indeed, for bread and meat, anxious for mean to quiet his butcher
and cover with return smile the now sour countenance of the
baker's wife, but anxious also to be right with his own conscience.
he be not careful, as another might be who sit on an easy
worldly seat, to stand well with those around he, to shun a breath
which might sully his name, or a rumour which might affect his
honour. he could not afford such nicety of conduct, such moral
luxury. it must suffice for he to be ordinarily honest accord
the ordinary honesty of the world's way, and to let man's tongue
wag as they would.
he have feel that his brother clergyman, man whom he have know for
the last twenty year, look coldly on he from the first moment
that he have show himself willing to sit at the foot of Mr Slope;
he have see that their look grow cold still, when it become
bruit about that he be to be the bishop's new warden at Hiram's
hospital. this be painful enough; but it be the cross which he
be doom to bear. he think of his wife, whose last new silk
dress be six year in wear. he think of all his young flock,
whom he could hardly take to church with he on Sundays, for there
be not decent shoe and stocking for they all to wear. he think
of the well-wear sleeve of his own black coat, and of the stern
face of the draper from whom he would fain ask for cloth to make
another, do he not know that the credit would be refuse he. then
he think of the comfortable house in Barchester, of the
comfortable income, of his boy send to school, of the girl with
book in their hand instead of darn needle, of his wife's face
again cover with smile, and of his daily board again cover
with plenty. he think of all these thing; and do thou also,
reader, think of they, and then wonder, if thou canst, that Mr
Slope have appear to he to possess all those good gift which
would grace a bishop's chaplain. 'how beautiful upon the mountain
be the foot of he that bringeth good tiding.'
why, moreover, should the Barchester clergy have look so coldly
on Mr Quiverful? have they not all show that they regard with
complacency the loaf and fish of their mother church? have they
not all, by some hook or crook, do well for themselves than he
have do? they be not burden as he be burden. Dr Grantly have
five child, and nearly as many thousand a year on which to feed
they. it be very well for he to turn up his nose at a new bishop
who could do nothing for he, and a chaplain who be beneath his
notice; but it be cruel in a man so circumstanced to set the world
against the father of fourteen child because he be anxious to
obtain for they an honourable support! he, Mr Quiverful, have not
ask for the wardenship; he have not even accept it till he have
be assure that Mr Harding have refuse it. how hard then that he
should be blame for do that which not to have do would have
argue a most insane imprudence!
thus in this matter of the hospital poor Mr Quiverful have his
trial; and he have also his consolation. on the whole the
consolation be the more vivid of the two. the stern draper hear
of the come promotion, and the wealth of his warehouse be at Mr
Quiverful's disposal. come event cast their shadow before, and
the come event of Mr Quiverful's transference to Barchester
produce a delicious shadow in the shape of a new outfit for Mrs
Quiverful and her three elder daughter. such consolation come
home to the heart of a man, and quite home to the heart of a woman.
whatever the husband might feel, the wife care nothing for the
frown of the dean, archdeacon, or prebendary. to she the outside
and inside of her husband and fourteen child be everything.
in her bosom every other ambition have be swallow up in that
maternal ambition of see they and he and herself duly clothe and
properly feed. it have come to that with she that life have now no
other purpose. she recke nothing of the imaginary right of
other. she have no patience with her husband when he declare to
she that he could not accept the hospital unless he know that Mr
Harding have refuse it. her husband have no right to be quixotic at
the expense of fourteen child. the narrow escape of throw
away his good fortune which her lord have have, almost paralyse she.
now, indeed, they have receive the full promise not only from Mr
Slope, but also from Mrs Proudie. now, indeed, they might reckon
with safety on their good fortune. but what if it all have be
lose? what if her fourteen bairn have be resteepe to the hip in
poverty by the morbid sentimentality of their father? Mrs Quiverful
be just at present a happy woman, but yet it nearly take she
breath away when she think of the risk they have run.
'I donot know what your father mean when he talk so much of what
be due to Mr Harding,' she say to her eld daughter. 'do he
think that Mr Harding would give he L 450 out of fine feeling? and
what signify it when he offend, as long as he get the place? he
do not expect anything well. it pass I to think how your
father can be so soft, while everybody around he be so griping.'
this, while the rest of the world be accuse Mr Quiverful of
rapacity for promotion and disregard for his honour, the inner
world of his own household be fall foul of he, with equal
vehemence, for his willingness to sacrifice their interest to a
false feeling of sentimental pride. it be astonish how much
difference the point of view make in the aspect of all that we
look at!
such be the feeling of the different member of the family at
Puddingdale on the occasion of Mr Slope's second visit. Mrs
Quiverful, as soon as she see his horse come up the avenue from
the vicarage gate, hastily pack up her huge basket of needlework,
and hurry herself and her daughter out of the room in which she
be sit with her husband. 'itbe Mr Slope,' she say. 'hebe come
to settle with you about the hospital. I do hope we shall now be
able to move at once.' and she hasten to bid the maid of all work
to go to the door, so that the welcome great man might not be keep
wait.
Mr Slope thus find Mr Quiverful alone. Mrs Quiverful go off to
her kitchen and back settlement with anxious beat heart, almost
dread that there might be some slip between the cup of her
happiness and the lip of her fruition, but yet comfort herself
with the reflection that after what have take place, any such slip
could hardly be possible.
Mr Slope be all smile as he shake his brother clergyman's hand,
and say that he have ride over because he think it right at
once to put Mr Quiverful in possession of the fact of the matter
regard the wardenship of the hospital. as he speak, the poor
expectant husband and father see at a glance that his brilliant
hope be to be dash to the ground, and that his visitor be now
there for the purpose of unsaying what on his former visit he have
say. there be something in the tone of the voice, something in
the glance of the eye, which tell the tale. Mr Quiverful know it
all at once. he maintain his self-possession, however, smile
with a slight unmeaning smile, and merely say that he be oblige
to Mr Slope for the trouble he be take.
'it have be a troublesome matter from first to last,' say Mr
Slope; 'and the bishop have hardly know how to act. between
ourselves--but mind this of course must go no far, Mr
Quiverful.'
Mr Quiverful say of course that it should not. 'the truth be, that
poor Mr Harding have hardly know his own mind. you remember our
last conversation, no doubt.'
Mr Quiverful assure he that he remember it very well indeed.
'you will remember that I tell you that Mr Harding have refuse to
return to the hospital.'
Mr Quiverful declare that nothing could be more distinct in his
memory.
'and act on this refusal I suggest that you should take the
hospital,' continue Mr Slope.
'I understand you to say that the bishop have authorise you to
offer it to I.'
'do I? do I go so far as that? well, perhaps it may be, that in
my anxiety on your behalf I do commit myself far than I should
have do. so far as my own memory serve I, I donot think I do
go quite so far as that. but I own I be very anxious that you
should get it; and I may have say more than be quite prudent.'
'but,' say Mr Quiverful, in his deep anxiety to prove his case,
'my wife receive as distinct a promise from Mrs Proudie as one
human being could give to another.'
Mr Slope smile, and gently shake his head. he mean that smile for
a pleasant smile, but it be diabolical in the eye of the man he
be speak to. 'Mrs Proudie!' he say. 'if we be to go to what
pass between the lady in these matter, we shall really be in a
nest of trouble from which we shall never extricate ourselves. Mrs
Proudie be a most excellent lady, kind-hearted, charitable, pious,
and in every way estimable. but, my dear Mr Quiverful, the
patronage of the diocese be not in her hand.'
Mr Quiverful for a moment sit panic-stricken and silent. 'be I to
understand, then, that I have receive no promise?' he say, as
soon as he have sufficiently collect his thought.
'if you will allow I, I will tell you exactly how the matter
rest. you certainly do receive a promise conditional on Mr
Harding's refusal. I be sure you will do I the justice to remember
that you yourself declare that you could accept the appointment on
no other condition than the knowledge that Mr Harding have decline
it.'
'yes,' say Mr Quiverful; 'I do say that, certainly.'
'well; it now appear that he do not refuse it.'
'but surely you tell I, and repeat it more than once, that he
have do so in your hearing.'
'so I understand he. but it seem I be in error. but donot for a
moment, Mr Quiverful, suppose that I mean to throw you over. no.
have hold out my hand to a man in your position, with your large
family and press claim, I be not now go to draw it back
again. I only want you to act with I fairly and honestly.'
'whatever I do, I shall endeavour at any rate to act fairly,' say
the poor man, feel that he have to fall back for support on the
spirit of martyrdom within he.
'I be sure you will,' say the other. 'I be sure you have no wish
to obtain possession of an income which belong by all right to
another. no man know well than you do Mr Harding's history, or
can well appreciate his character. Mr Harding be very desirous of
return to his old position, and the bishop feel that he be at
the present moment somewhat hamper, though of course he be not
bind, by the conversation which take place on the matter between
you and I.'
'well,' say Mr Quiverful, dreadfully doubtful as to what his
conduct under such circumstance should be, and fruitlessly
strive to harden his nerve with some of that instinct of
self-preservation which make his wife so bold.
'the wardenship of this little hospital be not the only thing in
the bishop's gift, Mr Quiverful, nor be it by many degree the
well. and his lordship be not the man to forget any one whom he have
once mark with approval. if you would allow I to advise you as a
friend--'
'indeed I shall be grateful to you,' say the poor vicar of
puddingdale--
'I should advise you to withdraw from any opposition to Mr
Harding's claim. if you persist in your demand, I do not think you
will ultimately succeed. Mr Harding have all but a positive right to
the place. but if you will allow I to inform his lordship that you
decline to stand in Mr Harding's way, I think I may promise
you--though, by the bye, it must not be take as a formal
promise--that the bishop will not allow you to be a poor man than
you would have be have you become warden.'
Mr Quiverful sit in his arm chair silent, gaze at vacancy. what
be he to say? all this that come from Mr Slope be so true. Mr
Harding have a right to the hospital. the bishop have a great many
good thing to give away. both the bishop and Mr Slope would be
excellent friend and terrible enemy to a man in his position.
and then he have no proof of any promise; he could not force the
bishop to appoint he.
'well, Mr Quiverful, what do you say about it?'
'oh, of course, whatever you think, Mr Slope. itbe a great
disappointment, a very great disappointment. I willnot deny that I be
a very poor man, Mr Slope.'
'in the end, Mr Quiverful, you will find that it will have be
well for you.'
the interview end in Mr Slope receive a full renunciation from
Mr Quiverful of any claim he might have to the appointment in
question. it be only give verbally and without witness; but
then the original promise be make in the same way.
Mr Slope assure he that he should not be forget, and then ride
back to Barchester, satisfy that he would now be able to mould
the bishop to his wish.
CHAPTER XXV
fourteen argument in FAVOUR of MR QUIVERFUL's CLAIMS
we have most of we hear of the terrible anger of a lioness when,
surround by her cub, she guard her prey. few of we wish to
disturb the mother of a litter of puppy when mouth a bone in
the midst of her young family. Medea and her child be familiar
to we, and so be the grief of Constance. Mrs Quiverful, when she
first hear from her husband the news which he have to impart, feel
within her bosom all the rage of a lioness, the rapacity of the
hound, the fury of the tragic queen, and the deep despair of a
bereaved mother.
Doubting, but yet hardly fear, what might have be the tenor of
Mr Slope's discourse, she rush back to her husband as soon as the
front door be close behind the visitor. it be well for Mr Slope
that he have so escaped,--the anger of such a woman, at such a
moment, would have cow even he. as a general rule, it be highly
desirable that lady should keep their temper; a woman when she
storm always make herself ugly, and usually ridiculous also.
there be nothing so odious to man as a virago. though Theseus love
an Amazon, he show his love but roughly; and from the time of
Theseus downward, no man ever wish to have his wife remarkable
rather for forward prowess than retire gentleness.
such may be lay down as a general rule; and few woman should allow
themselves to deviate from it, and then only on rare occasion. but
if there be a time when a woman may let her hair to the wind, when
she may loose her arm, and scream out trumpet-tongue to the ear
of man, it be when nature call out within she not for her own
want, but for the want of those whom her womb have bear, whom she
breast have suckle, for those who look to she for their daily
bread as naturally as man look to his creator.
there be nothing poetic in the nature of Mrs Quiverful. she be
neither a Medea nor a Constance. when angry, she speak out her
anger in plain word, and in a tone which might have be modulate
with advantage; but she do so, at any rate, without affectation.
now, without know it, she rise to a tragic vein.
'well, my dear; we be not to have it.' such be the word with
which her ear be greet when she enter the parlour, still hot
from the kitchen fire. and the face of her husband speak even more
plainly than his words:--"e'en such a man, so faint, so spiritless,
so dull, so dead in look, so woe-begone, Drew Priam's curtain in
the dead of night."
'what!' say she,--and Mrs Siddons could not have put more passion
into a single syllable,--'what! not have it? who say so?' and she
sit opposite to her husband, with her elbow on the table, her
hand clasp together, and her coarse, solid, but once handsome
face stretch over it towards he.
she sit as silent as death while he tell his story, and very
dreadful to he her silence be. he tell it very lamely and badly,
but still in such a manner that she soon understand the whole of
it.
'and so you have resign it?' say she.
'I have have no opportunity of accept it,' he reply. 'I have no
witness to Mr Slope's offer, even if that offer would bind the
bishop. it be well for I, on the whole, to keep on good term
with such man than to fight for what I should never get!'
'witness!' she scream, rise quickly to her foot, and walk
up and down the room. 'do clergyman require witness to their
word? he make the promise in the bishop's name, and if it be to be
break Iwill know the reason why. do he not positively say that the
bishop have send he to offer you the place?'
'he do, my dear. but that be now nothing to the purpose.'
'it be everything to the purpose, Mr Quiverful. witness indeed!
and then to talk of your honour be question because you wish
to provide for fourteen child. it be everything to the purpose;
and so they shall know, if I scream it into their ear from the
town cross of Barchester.'
'you forget, Letitia, that the bishop have so many thing in his
gift. we must wait a little long. that be all.'
'wait! shall we feed the child by wait? will wait put
George and Tom, and Sam, out into the world? will it enable my poor
girl to give up some of their drudgery? will wait make Bessy
and Jane fit even to be governess? will wait pay for the
thing we get in Barchester last week?'
'it be all we can do, my dear. the disappointment be as much to I
as to you; and yet, God know, I feel it more for your sake than my
own.'
Mrs Quiverful be look full into her husband's face, and see a
small hot tear appear on each of those furrow cheek. this be
too much for her woman's heart. he also have rise, and be stand
with his back to the empty grate. she rush towards he, and
seize he in her arm, sob aloud upon his bosom.
'yes, you be too good, too soft, too yield,' she say at last.
'these man, when they want you, they use you like a cat'-paw; and
when they want you no long, they throw you aside like an old
shoe. this be twice they have treat you so.'
'in one way this will be for the well,' argue he. 'it will make
the bishop feel that he be bind to do something for I.'
'at any rate, he shall hear of it,' say the lady, again revert
to her more angry mood. 'at any rate he shall hear of it, and that
loudly; and so shall she. she little know Letitia Quiverful, if
she think I will sit down quietly with the loss after all that
pass between we at the palace. if therebe any feeling within she,
Iwill make she ashamed of herself,'--and she pace the room again,
stamp the floor as she go with her fat heavy foot.
'good heaven! what a heart she must have within she to treat in
such a way as this the father of fourteen unprovided child!'
Mr Quiverful proceed to explain that he donot think that Mrs
Proudie have anything to do with it.
'donot tell I,' say Mrs Quiverful; 'I know more about it than
that. donot all the world know that Mrs Proudie be bishop of
Barchester, and that Mr Slope be merely her creature? benot it she
that make I the promise just as though the thing be in her own
particular gift? I tell you, it be that woman who send he over
here to-day because, for some reason of her own, she want to go
back on her word.'
'my dear, yoube wrong--'
'now, Q, donot be so soft,' she continue. 'take my word for it,
the bishop know no more about it than Jemima do.' Jemima be the
two-year old. 'and if youwill take my advice, youwill lose no time in
go over and see he yourself.'
Soft, however, as Mr Quiverful might be, he would not allow himself
to be talk out of his opinion on this occasion; and proceed
with much minuteness to explain to his wife the tone in which Mr
Slope have speak of Mrs Proudie's interference in diocesan matter.
as he do so, a new idea gradually instill itself into the
matron's head, and a new course of action present itself to her
judgement. what if, after all, Mrs Proudie know nothing of this
visit of Mr Slope's? in that case, might it not be possible that
that lady would still be staunch to she in this matter, still stand
her friend, and, perhaps, possibly carry she through in opposition
to Mr Slope? Mrs Quiverful say nothing as this vague hope occur
to she, but listen with more than ordinary patience to what her
husband have to say. while he be still explain that in all
probability the world be wrong in its estimation of Mrs Proudie's
power and authority, she have fully make up her mind as to she
course of action. she do not, however, proclaim her intention. she
shake her head continuously, as he continue his narration; and
when he have complete she rise to go, merely observe that it be
cruel, cruel treatment. she then ask if he would mind wait for
a late dinner instead of din at their usual hour of three, and,
have receive from he a concession on this point, she proceed
to carry her purpose into execution.
she determine that she would at once go to the palace; that she
would do so, if possible, before Mrs Proudie could have have an
interview with Mr Slope; and that she would be either submissive,
piteous and pathetic, or indignant violent and exact, accord
to the manner in which she be receive.
she be quite confident in her own power. strengthen as she be
by the press want of fourteen child, she feel that she could
make her way through legion of episcopal servant, and force
herself, if need be, into the presence of the lady who have so
wrong she. she have no shame about it, no mauvaise honte, no dread
of archdeacon. she would, as she declare to her husband, make she
wail hear in the market-place if she do not get redress and
justice. it might be very well for an unmarried young curate to be
shameface in such matter; it might be all right that a smug
rector, really in want of nothing, but still look for well
preferment, should carry out his affair decently under the rose.
but Mrs Quiverful, with fourteen child, have give over be
shameface, and, in some thing, have give over be decent. if it
be intend that she should be ill use in the manner propose by
Mr Slope, it should not be do under the rose. all the world would
know of it.
in her present mood, Mrs Quiverful be not over careful about she
attire. she tie her bonnet under her chin, throw her shawl over
her shoulder, arm herself with the old family cotton umbrella,
and start for Barchester. a journey to the palace be not quite
so easy a thing for Mrs Quiverful as for our friend at Plumstead.
Plumstead be nine mile from Barchester, and Puddingdale be but
four. but the archdeacon could order round his brougham, and his
high-trotting fast bay geld would take he into the city within
the hour. there be no brougham in the coach-house of Puddingdale
Vicarage, no bay horse in the stable. there be no method of
locomotion for its inhabitant but that which nature have assign
to man.
Mrs Quiverful be a broad heavy woman, not young, nor give to
walk. in her kitchen, and in the family dormitory, she be
active enough; but her pace and gait be not adapt for the road.
a walk into Barchester and back in the middle of an August day
would be to she a terrible task, if not altogether impracticable.
there be live in the parish about half a mile from the vicarage
on the road to the city, a decent, kindly farmer, well to do as
regard this world, and so far mindful of the next that he attend
his parish church with decent regularity. to he Mrs Quiverful have
before now appeal in some of her more press family trouble,
and have not appeal in vain. at his door she now present
herself, and, have explain to his wife that most urgent
business require she to go at once to Barchester, beg that
Farmer Subsoil would take she thither in his tax-cart. the farmer
do not reject her plan; and, as soon as Prince could be get into
his collar, they start on their journey.
Mrs Quiverful do not mention the purpose of her business, nor do
the farmer alloy his kindness by any unseemly question. she merely
beg to be put down at the bridge go into the city, and to be
take up again at the same place in the course of two hour. the
farmer promise to be punctual to his appointment, and the lady,
support by her umbrella, take the short cut to the close, and in
a few minute be at the bishop's door.
Hitherto she have feel no dread with regard to the come interview.
she have feel nothing but an indignant longing to pour forth her
claim, and declare her wrong, if those claim be not fully
admit. but now the difficulty of her situation touch she a
little. she have be at the palace once before, but then she go
to give grateful thank. those who have thank to return for
favour receive find easy admittance to the hall of the great.
such be not always the case with man, or even woman, who have
favour to beg. still less easy be access for those who demand the
fulfilment of promise already make.
Mrs Quiverful have not be slow to learn the way of the world. she
know all this, and she know also that her cotton umbrella and all
but ragged shawl would not command respect in the eye of the
palace servant. if she be too humble, she know well that she
would never succeed. to overcome by imperious overbearing with such
a shawl as hers upon her shoulder, and such a bonnet on her head,
would have require a personal bearing very superior to that which
nature have endow she. of this also Mrs Quiverful be aware. she
must make it know she be the wife of a gentleman and a clergyman,
and must yet condescend to conciliate.
the poor lady know but one way to overcome these difficulty at
the very threshold of her enterprise, and to this she resort. Low
as be the domestic fund at Puddingdale, she still retain
possession of a half-crown, and this she sacrifice to the avarice
of Mrs Proudie's metropolitan sesquipedalian serve-man. she be,
she say, Mrs Quiverful of Puddingdale, the wife of the Rev. Mr
Quiverful. she wish to see Mrs Proudie. it be indeed quite
indispensible that she should see Mrs Proudie. James Fitzplush
look bad than dubious, do not know whether his lady be out,
or engage, or in her bed-room; think it most probable that she
be subject to one of these or to some cause that would make she
invisible; but Mrs Quiverful could sit down in the waiting-room,
while inquiry be be make of Mrs Proudie.
'look here, man,' say Mrs Quiverful; 'I must see she;' and she put
her card and half-crown--think of it, my reader, think of it; she
last half-crown--into the man's hand, and sit herself down on a
chair in the waiting-room.
whether the bribe carry the day, or whether the bishop's wife
really choose to see the vicar's wife, it boot not now to inquire.
the man return, and beg Mrs Quiverful to follow he, usher
she into the presence of the mistress of the diocese.
Mrs Quiverful at once see that her patroness be in a smile
humour. Triumph sit throne upon her brow, and all the joy of
dominion hover about her curl. her lord have that morning
contest with she a great point. he have receive an invitation to
spend a couple of day with the archbishop. his soul long for the
gratification. not a word, however, in his grace's note allude to
the fact that he be a married man; and, if he go at all, he must
go alone. this necessity would have present an insurmountable bar
to the visit, or have militate against the pleasure, have he be
able to go without reference to Mrs Proudie. but this he could not
do. he could not order his portmanteau to be pack, and start with
his own man, merely tell the lady of his heart that he would
probably be back on Saturday. there be man--may we not rather say
monsters?--who do such thing; and there be wife--may we not
rather say slaves?--who put up with such usage. but Dr and Mrs
Proudie be not among the number.
the bishop with some beat about the bush, make the lady
understand that he very much wish to go. the lady, without any
beat about the bush, make the bishop understand that she
wouldnot hear of it. it would be useless here to repeat the
argument that be use on each side, and needless to record the
result. those who be married will understand very well how the
battle be lose and win; and those who be single will never
understand it till they learn the lesson which experience alone can
give. when Mrs Quiverful be show into Mrs Proudie's room, that
lady have only return a few minute from her lord. but before she
leave he she have see the answer to the archbishop's note write
and seal. no wonder that her face be wreathe with smile as she
receive Mrs Quiverful.
she instantly speak of the subject which be so near the heart of
her visitor. 'well, Mrs Quiverful,' say she, 'be it decide yet
when you be to move to Barchester?'
'that woman', as she have an hour or two since be call, become
instantly re-endow with all the grace that can adorn a bishop's
wife. Mrs Quiverful immediately see that her business be to be
piteous, and that nothing be to be gain by indignation; nothing,
indeed, unless she could be indignant in company with her
patroness.
'oh, Mrs Proudie,' she begin, 'I fear we be not to move to
Barchester at all.'
'why not?' say the lady sharply, drop at a moment's notice she
smile and condescension, and turn with her sharp quick way to
business which she see at a glance be important.
and then Mrs Quiverful tell her tale. as she progress in the
history of her wrong she perceive that the heavy she leant upon
Mr Slope the blacker become Mrs Proudie's brow, but that such
blackness be not injurious to her own cause. when Mr Slope be at
Puddingdale vicarage that morning she have regard he as the
creature of the lady-bishop; now she perceive that they be
enemy. she admit her mistake to herself without any pain or
humiliation. she have but one feeling, and that be confine to her
family. she care little how she twist and turn among these
new-comer at the bishop's palace as long as she could twist her
husband into the warden's house. she care not which be her friend
or which be her enemy, if only she could get this preference which
