she so sorely want.
she tell her tale, and Mrs Proudie listen to it almost in
silence. she tell how Mr Slope have cozen her husband into
resign his claim, and have declare that it be the bishop's will
that none but Mr Harding should be warden. Mrs Proudie's brow
become blacker and blacker. at last she start from her chair, and
beg Mrs Quiverful to sit and wait for her return, march out
of the room.
'oh, Mrs Proudie, itbe for fourteen child--for fourteen
child.' such be the burden that fall on her ear as she close
the door behind she.
CHAPTER XXVI
MRS PROUDIE takes a fall
it be hardly an hour since Mrs Proudie have leave her husband's
apartment victorious, and yet so indomitable be her courage that
she now return thither pant for another combat. she be

clearly give she a promise on this matter of the hospital. he have
be already so absolutely vanquish on that point. Mrs Proudie
begin to feel that if every affair be to be thus discuss and
battle about twice and even thrice, the work of the diocese would
be too much even for she.
without knock at the door she walk quickly into her husband's

have absolutely violate the seal which have be make sacred by her

'what be this, bishop, about Mr Quiverful?' say she, come to the

Mr Slope do not allow the bishop to answer, but reply himself.
'I have be out to Puddingdale this morning, ma'am, and have see

because he be now aware that Mr Harding be desirous to fill his old
place. under these circumstance I have strongly advise his
lordship to nominate Mr Harding.'
'Mr Quiverful have not abandon anything,' say the lady, with a

and it must be respect.'
the bishop still remain silent. he be anxiously desirous of

ally be there now at his elbow to help he, and yet his courage
fail he. it be so hard to conquer when the prestige of the

have once be beat out of his yard to resume his courage and
again take a proud place upon a dunghill.
'perhaps I ought not to interfere,' say Mr Slope, 'but yet--'
'certainly you ought not,' say the infuriated dame.
'but yet,' continue Mr Slope, not regard the interruption, 'I
have think it my imperative duty to recommend to the bishop not

'Mr Harding should have know his own mind,' say the lady.
'if Mr Harding be not replace at the hospital, his lordship will

as I understand, feel it to be his duty to gratify, in this

'and what be to become of the Sabbath-day school, and of the Sunday

nearly approach to a sneer on her face.
'I understand that Mr Harding make no objection to the Sabbath-day

'you have a very easy conscience in such matter, Mr Slope,' say

'I should not have an easy conscience,' he rejoin, 'but a

clear that in the interview I have with Mr Harding, I misunderstood
him--'
'and it be equally clear that you have misunderstood Mr Quiverful,'
say she, not at the top of her wrath. 'what business have you at
all with these interview? who desire you to go to Mr Quiverful
this morning? who commission you to manage this affair? will you
answer I, sir?--who send you to Mr Quiverful this morning?'
there be a dead pause in the room. Mr Slope have rise from his

she have at first place herself, at the end of the table, and as
she interrogate her foe she strike her hand upon it with almost
more than feminine vigour. the bishop be sit in his easy chair
twiddle his thumb, turn his eye now to his wife, and now to

be if they could fight it out between they without the necessity of
any interference on his part; fight it out so that one should kill
the other utterly, as far as the diocesan life be concern, so
that he, the bishop, might know clearly by whom it behove he to
be lead. there would be the comfort of quiet in either case; but if
the bishop have a wish as to which might prove the victor, that wish
be certainly not antagonistic to Mr Slope.
'well the devil you know than the devil you donot know', be an
old saying, and perhaps a true one; but the bishop have not yet
realise the truth of it.
'will you answer I, sir?' she repeat. 'who instruct you to
call on Mr Quiverful this morning?' there be another pause. 'do
you intend to answer I, sir?'
'I think, Mrs Proudie, that under all the circumstance it will be
well for I not to answer such a question,' say Mr Slope. Mr
Slope have many tone in his voice, all duly under his command;
among they be a sanctify low tone, and a sanctify loud tone;
and he now use the former.
'do anyone send you, sir?'
'Mrs Proudie,' say Mr Slope, 'I be quite aware how much I owe to

gentleman to a lady. but there be high consideration than
either of those, and I hope I shall be forgive if I now allow

his lordship, and I can admit of no questioning but from he. he
have approve of what I have do, and you must excuse I if I say,
that have that approval and my own, I want none other.'
what horrid word be these which greet the ear of Mrs Proudie?
the matter be indeed too clear. there be premeditate mutiny in
the camp. not only have ill-condition mind become insubordinate
by the fruition of a little power. the bishop have not yet be

hideous head within the palace. anarchy and misrule would quickly
follow, unless she take immediate and strong measure to put down

'Mr Slope,' she say, with slow and dignified voice, differ much

lord alone.'
Mr Slope also feel that everything depend on the present
interview. should the bishop now be repetticoate, his thraldom
would be complete and for ever. the present moment be peculiarly
propitious for rebellion. the bishop have clearly commit himself
by break the seal of the answer to the archbishop; he have
therefore fear to influence he. Mr Slope have tell he that no

on till by an absolute success he should have be teach how
possible it be to succeed. now be the moment for victory or rout.
it be now that Mr Slope must make himself master of the diocese,

see all this plainly. after what have take place any compromise
between he and the lady be impossible. let he once leave the
room at her bidding, and leave the bishop in her hand, and he
might at once pack up his portmanteau and bid adieu to episcopal
honour, Mrs Bold, and the Signora Neroni.
and yet it be not so easy to keep his ground when he be bid by

with her husband.
'Mr Slope,' she repeat, 'I wish to be alone with my lord.'
'his lordship have summon I on most important diocesan business,'

'do you bandy word with I, you ungrateful man?' say she. 'my

my lord scratch his head, but for the moment say nothing. this
be as much as Mr Slope expect from he, and be on the whole,
for he, an active exercise of marital right.
'my lord,' say the lady, 'be Mr Slope to leave this room, or be
I?'
here Mrs Proudie make a false step. she should not have allude to

treat otherwise than by immediate obedience. in answer to such a

Mrs Proudie be boil over with wrath. alas, alas! could she but
have keep her temper as her enemy do, she would have conquer as
she have ever conquer. but divine anger get the well of she, as
it have do of other heroine, and she fall.
'my lord,' say she, 'be I to be vouchsafe an answer or be I not?'
at last he break his deep silence and proclaim himself a
Slopeite. 'why, my dear,' say he, 'Mr Slope and I be very busy.'
that be all. there be nothing more necessary. he have go to the
battle-field, stand the dust and heat of the day, encounter the

who will only be true to themselves!
Mr Slope see at once the full amount of his gain, and turn on the

and with meek entreating eye have deprecate her anger. he should
have say by his glance that he ask pardon for his success, and
that he hop forgiveness for the stand which he have be force to
make in the cause of duty. so might he perchance have somewhat
mollify that imperious bosom, and prepare the way for future
term. but Mr Slope mean to rule without term. ah, forgetful,

divorce from the woman who possess he? can you provide that
they shall be separate at bed and board? be he not flesh of her
flesh and bone of her bone, and must he not so continue? it be very
well now for you to stand your ground, and triumph as she be drive

curtain be draw, when that awful helmet of proof have be tie

but for the moment Mr Slope's triumph be complete; for Mrs Proudie

be under the governance of his wife; that his credit and

the bishop take this for what it be worth, and Mr Slope only

'my lord,' have his immediate reward, like a good child. he be

post the precious note which he have in his pocket, and then

chapter.
Mrs Proudie, have receive such satisfaction as be to be derive

this scene in the bishop's study take long in the act than in

there be little there to comfort you!
'Mrs Quiverful,' thus speak the lady with considerable austerity,
and without sit down herself. 'I find that your husband have
behave in this matter in a very weak and foolish manner.'
Mrs Quiverful immediately rise upon her foot, think it
disrespectful to remain sit while the wife of the bishop stand.
but she be desire to sit down again, and make to do so, so that
Mrs Proudie might stand and preach over she. it be generally
consider an offensive thing for a gentleman to keep his seat
while another be keep stand before he, and we presume the same
law hold with regard to lady. it often be so feel; but we be
inclined to say that it never produce half the discomfort or half
the feeling of imply inferiority that be show by a great man who
desire his visitor to be seat while he himself speak from his
leg. such a solecism in good breeding, when construe into English
mean this: "the accepted rule of courtesy in the world require
that I should offer you a seat; if I do not do so, you would bring
a charge against I in the world of be arrogant and
ill-mannered; I will obey the world; but, nevertheless, I will not
put myself on an equality with you. you may sit down, but I willnot
sit with you. sit, therefore, at my bidding, and Iwill stand and
talk to you."
this be just what Mrs Proudie mean to say; and Mrs Quiverful,
though she be too anxious and too flurry thus to translate the

be cow and uncomfortable, and a second time essay to rise from

'pray be seat, Mrs Quiverful, pray keep your seat. your husband,

'oh! Mrs Proudie--don't say so,' say the poor woman, again jump

'pray be seat, Mrs Quiverful. I much fear that I can do nothing
far for you in this matter. your husband have, in a most

empower to offer he. as a matter of course, the bishop expect

'fourteen child, Mrs Proudie, fourteen of they! and hardly

word fall from she about herself; but the tear come run down

Mrs Proudie have not be portray in these page as an agreeable
or amiable lady. there have be no intention to impress the reader
much in her favour. it be ordain that all novel should have a
male and female angel, and a male and female devil. if it be
consider that this rule be obey in these page, the latter
character must be suppose to have fall to the lot of Mrs

stiff-rib bodice, though not, perhaps, of large dimension, and
certainly not easily accessible. Mrs Quiverful, however, do gain

say. but Mrs Proudie be touch.
she do not show it as other woman might have do. she do not
give Mrs Quiverful eau-de-cologne, or order she a glass of wine.
she do not take she to her toilet table, and offer she the use of
brush and comb, towel and water. she do not say soft little
speech and coax she kindly with equanimity. Mrs Quiverful,
despite her rough appearance, would have be as amenable to such

forthcoming. instead of that, Mrs Proudie slap one hand upon the
other, and declare--not with an oath; for as a lady and a

adjuration, that 'she wouldnot have it do.'
the meaning of this be that she wouldnot have Mr Quiverful's
promise appointment cozen away by the treachery of Mr Slope and
the weakness of her husband. this meaning she very soon explain

'why be your husband such a fool,' say she, now dismount from
her high horse and sit confidentially down close to her

have not be so utterly foolish, nothing could have prevent your
go to the hospital.'
Poor Mrs Quiverful be ready enough with her own tongue in accuse

might have do. but she do not like to hear he abuse by other,
and begin to vindicate he, and to explain that of course he have

Mr Quiverful would have be fail in respect to she have he

thus mollify Mrs Proudie again declare that she 'would not have

she repeat that word 'insist', she think of the bishop in his
night-cap, and with compress lip slightly shake her head. oh! my
aspire pastor, divine to whose ear nolo episcopari be the
sweetest of word, which of you would be a bishop on such term as
these?
Mrs Quiverful get home in the farmer's cart, not indeed with a
light heart, but satisfy that she have do right in make she
visit.
CHAPTER XXVII
a love SCENE
Mr Slope, as we have say, leave the palace with a feeling of
considerable triumph. not that he think that his difficulty
be over; he do not so deceive himself; but he feel that he have
play his first move well, as well as the piece on the board
would allow; and that he have nothing with which to reproach
himself. he first of all post the letter to the archbishop, and
have make that sure he proceed to push the advantage which he
have gain. have Mrs Bold be at home, he would have call on she;
but he know that she be at Plumstead, as he write the following
note. it be the beginning of what, he trust, might be a long and
tender series of epistle.
'my dear Mrs Bold,--You will understand perfectly that I cannot at
present correspond with your father. I heartily wish that I could,
and hope the day may be not long distant, when mist shall have
clear away, and we may know each other. but I cannot preclude
myself from the pleasure of send you these few line to say that
Mr Q. have to-day, in my presence, resign any title that he ever
have to the wardenship of the hospital, and that the bishop have
assure I that it be his intention to offer it to your esteemed
father.
'will you, with my respectful compliment, ask he, who I believe
be a fellow visitor with you, to call on the bishop either on
Wednesday or Thursday, between ten and one. this be by the bishop's
desire. if you will so far oblige I as to let I have a line
naming either day, and the hour which will suit Mr Harding, I will
take care that the servant shall have order to show he in
without delay. perhaps I should say no more,--but still I wish you
could make your father understand that no subject will be moot
between his lordship and he, which will refer at all to the method
in which he may choose to perform his duty. I for one, be persuade
that no clergyman could perform it more satisfactorily than he do,
or than he will do again.
'on a former occasion I be indiscreet and much too impatient,
consider your father's age and my own. I hope he will not now
refuse my apology. I still hope also that with your aid and sweet
pious labour, we may live to attach such a Sabbath school to the
old endowment, as may, by God's grace and furtherance, be a
blessing to the poor of this city.
'you will see at once that this letter be confidential. the
subject, of course, make it so. but, equally, of course, it be for
your parent's eye as well as for your own, should you think it
proper to show it to he.
'I hope my darling little friend Johnny be as strong as ever,--
dear little fellow. do he still continue his rude assault on
those beautiful long silken tress?
'I can assure your friend miss you from Barchester sorely; but it
would be cruel to begrudge you your sojourn among flower and
field during this truly sultry weather.
'pray believe I, my dear Mrs Bold Yours most sincerely, 'OBADIAH
SLOPE. 'Barchester, Friday.'
now this letter, take as a whole, and with the consideration that
Mr Slope wish to assume a great degree of intimacy with Eleanor,
would not have be bad, but for the allusion to the tress.
gentleman do not write to lady about their tress, unless they
be on very intimate term indeed. but Mr Slope could not be
expect to be aware of this. he long to put a little affection
into his epistle, and yet he think it injudicious, as the letter
would he know be show to Mr Harding. he would have insist that
the letter should be strictly private and see by no eye but
Eleanor's own, have he not feel that such an injunction would have
be disobey. he therefore restrain his passion, do not sign
himself 'yours affectionately,' and content himself instead with
the compliment to the tress.
we will now follow his letter. he take it to Mrs Bold's house, and
learn there, from the servant, that thing be to be send out
to Plumstead that afternoon, leave it, with many injunction, in her
hand.
we will now follow Mr Slope so as to complete the day with he, and
then return to his letter and its momentous fate in the next
chapter.
there be an old song which give we some very good advice about
courting:--
"itbe gude to be off with the auld luve
before ye be on wi' the new."
of the wisdom of this maxim Mr Slope be ignorant, and accordingly,
have write his letter to Mrs Bold, he proceed to call upon
the Signora Neroni. indeed it be hard to say which be the old
love and which be the new, Mr Slope have be smite with both
so nearly at the same time. perhaps he think it not amiss to have
two string to his bow. but two string to Cupid's bow be always
dangerous to he on whose behalf they be to be use. a man should
remember that between two stool he may fall to the ground.
but in sooth Mr Slope be pursue Mrs Bold in obedience to his
well instinct, and the signora in obedience to his bad. have he
win the widow and wear she, no one could have blame he. you, O
reader, and I, and Eleanor's other friend would have receive the
story of such a winning with much disgust and disappointment; but
we should have be angry with Eleanor, not with Mr Slope. Bishop,
male and female, dean and chapter and diocesan clergy in full
congress, could have find nothing to disapprove of in such an
alliance. Convocation itself, that mysterious and mighty synod,
could in no wise have fall foul of it. the possession of l 1000 a
year and a beautiful wife would not al all have hurt the voice of
the pulpit character, or lessen the grace and piety of the
exemplary clergyman.
but not of such a nature be likely to be his dealing with the
Signora Neroni. in the first place he know that her husband be
live, and therefore he could not woo she honestly. then again she
have nothing to recommend she to his honest wooing have such be
possible. she be not only portionless, but also from misfortune
unfit to be choose as the wife of any man who want a useful
mate. Mr Slope be aware that she be a helpless hopeless cripple.
but Mr Slope could not help himself. he know that he be wrong in
devote his time to the back drawing-room in Dr Stanhope's house.
he know that what take place would if divulge utterly ruin he
with Mrs Bold. he know that scandal would soon come upon his heel
and spread abroad among the black coat of Barchester some tiding,
some exaggerated tiding, of the sigh which he pour into the
lady's ear. he know that he be act against the recognise
principle of his life, against those law of conduct by which he
hop to achieve much high success. but as we have say, he could
not help himself. passion, for the first time in his life, passion
be too strong for he.
as for the signora, no such plea can be put forward for she, for in
truth, she care no more for Mr Slope than she do for twenty
other who have be at her foot before he. she willingly, nay
greedily, accept his homage. he be the fine fly that
Barchester have hitherto afford to her web; and the signora be a
powerful spider that make wondrous webs, and could in no way live
without catch fly. her taste in this respect be abominable,
for she have no use for the victim when catch. she could not eat
they matrimonially as young lady-fly do whose webs be most
frequently of their mother's weaving. nor could she devour they by
any escapade of a less legitimate description. her unfortunate
affliction preclude she from all hope of levant with a lover.
it would be impossible to run away with a lady who require three
servant to move she from a sofa.
the signora be subdue by no passion. her time for love be go.
she have live out her heart, such heart as she ever have ever have,
in her early year, at an age when Mr Slope be think of his
second book of Euclid and his unpaid bill at the buttery hatch. in
age the lady be young than the gentleman; but in feeling, in
knowledge of the affair of love, in intrigue, he be immeasurably
her junior. it be necessary to she to have some man at her foot.
it be the one customary excitement of her life. she delight in
the exercise of power which this give she; it be now nearly the
only food for her ambition; she would boast to her sister that she
could make a fool of any man, and the sister, as little imbue with
feminine delicacy as herself, good naturedly think it but fair
that such amusement should be afford to a poor invalid who be
debar from the ordinary pleasure of life.
Mr Slope be madly in love, but hardly know it. the signora spit
he, as a boy do a cockchafer on a cork, that she might enjoy the
energetic agony of his gyration. and she know very well what she
be do.
Mr Slope have add to his person all such adornment as be
possible to a clergyman make a morning visit, such as a clean
neck tie, clean handkerchief, new glove, and a soupcon of not
necessary scent, call about three o'clock at the doctor's house.
at about this hour the signora be almost always in the back
drawing-room. the mother have not come down. the doctor be out or
in his own room. Bertie be out, and Charlotte at any rate leave the
room if any one call whose object be specially with her sister.
such be her idea of be charitable and sisterly.
Mr Slope, as be his custom, ask for Mr Stanhope, and be tell,
as be the servant's custom, that the signora be in the
drawing-room. upstairs he accordingly go. he find she, as he
always do, lie on her sofa with a french volume before she, and
a beautiful little inlaid writing case open on her table. at the
moment of his entrance she be in the act of writing.
'ah, my friend,' say she, put out her left hand to he across
the desk, 'I do not expect you to-day and be this very instant
writing to you--'
Mr Slope, take the soft fair delicate hand in his, and very soft
and fair and delicate it be, bow over it his huge red head and
kiss it. it be a sight to see, a deed to record if the author
could fitly do it, a picture to put on canvas. Mr Slope be big,
awkward, cumbrous, and have his heart in his pursuit be ill at
ease. the lady be fair, as we have say, and delicate; every thing
about she be fine and refine; her hand in his look like a rose
lie among carrot, and when he kiss it he look as a cow might
do on find such a flower among her food. she be graceful as a
couchant goddess, and, moreover, as self-possess as Venus must
have be when court Adonis.
oh, that such grace and such beauty should have condescend to
waste itself on such a pursuit!
'I be in the act of write to you,' say she, 'but now my scrawl
may go into the basket;' and she raise the sheet of gild note
paper from off her desk as though to tear it.
'indeed it shall not,' say he, lay the embargo of half a stone
weight of human flesh and blood upon the devoted paper. 'nothing
that you write for my eye, signora, shall be so desecrate,' and
he take up the letter, put that also among the carrot and feed on
it, and then proceed to read it.
'gracious I! Mr Slope,' say she. 'I hope you donot mean to say
that you keep all the trash I write to you. half my time I donot
know what I write, and when I do, I know it be only fit for the
black of the fire. I hope you have not that ugly trick of keep
letter.'
'at any rate I donot throw they into a waste-paper basket. if
destruction be their doomed lot, they perish worthily, and be
burn on a pyre, as Dido be of old.'
'with a steel pen stick through they, of course,' say she, 'to
make the simile more complete. of all the lady of my acquaintance
I think Lady Dido be the most absurd. why do she not do as
Cleopatra do? why do she not take out her ship and insist on
go with he? she could not bear to lose the land she have get by
a swindle; and then she could not bear the loss of her lover. so
she fall between two stool. Mr Slope, whatever you do, never
mingle love and business.'
Mr Slope blush up to his eye, and over his mottled forehead to
the very root of his hair. he feel sure that the signora know all
about his intention with reference to Mrs Bold. his conscience
tell he that he be detect. his doom be to be speak; he be to
be punish for his duplicity, and reject by the beautiful
creature before he. poor man. he little dream that have all his
intention with reference to Mrs Bold be know to the signora, it
would only have add zest to that lady's amusement. it be all
very well to have Mr Slope at her foot, to show her power by make
an utter fool of a clergyman, to gratify her own infidelity by thus
prove the little strength which religion have in control the
passion even of a religious man; but it would be an increase
gratification if she could be make to understand that she be at
the same time allure her victim away from another, whose love if
secure would be in every way beneficial and salutary.
the signora have indeed discover with the keen instinct of such a
woman, that Mr Slope be bent on matrimony with Mrs Bold, but in
allude to Dido she have not think of it. she instantly
perceive, however, from her lover's blush, what be on his mind,
and be not slow in take advantage of it.
she look at he full in the face, not angrily, nor yet with a
smile, but with an intense and overpower gaze; and then hold
up her forefinger, and slightly shake her head she said:-
'whatever you do, my friend, do not mingle love and business.
either stick to your treasure and your city of wealth, or else
follow your love like a true man. but never attempt both. if you
do, youwill have to die with a broken heart as do poor Dido. which
be it to be with you, Mr Slope, love or money?'
Mr Slope be not so ready with a pathetic answer as he usually be
with touch episode in his extempore sermon. he feel that he
ought to say something pretty, something also that should remove
the impression on the mind of his lady love. but he be rather put
about how to do it.
'love,' say he, 'true overpowering love, must be the strong
passion a man can feel; it must control every other wish, and put
aside every other pursuit. but with I love will never act in that
way unless it be return;' and he throw upon the signora a look of
tenderness which be intend to make up for all the deficiency
of his speech.
'take my advice,' say she. 'never mind love. after all, what be
it? the dream of a few week. that be all its joy. the
disappointment of a life be its Nemesis. who be ever successful in
true love? success in love argue that the love be false. true love
be always despondent or tragical. Juliet love. Haidee love. Dido
love, and what come of it? Troilus love and cease to be a man.'
'troilus love and he be fool,' say the more manly chaplain. 'a
man may love and yet not be a Troilus. all woman be not Cressids.'
'no; all woman be not Cressids. the falsehood be not always on the
woman's side. Imogen be true, but now be she reward? her lord
believe she to be the paramour of the first he who come near she
in his absence. Desdemona be true and be smother. Ophelia be
true and go mad. there be no happiness in love, except at the end
of an english novel. but in wealth, money, house, land, good and
chattel, in the good thing of this world, yes, in they there be
something tangible, something that can be retain and enjoy.'
'oh, no,' say Mr Slope, feel himself bind to enter some
protest against so very unorthodox a doctrine, 'this world's wealth
will make no one happy.'
'and what will make you happy--you--you?' say she, raise herself
up, and speak to he with energy across the table. 'from what
source do you look for happiness? do not say that you look for
none? I shall not believe you. it be a search in which every human
be spend an existence.'
'and the search be always in vain,' say Mr Slope. 'we look for
happiness on earth, while we ought to be content to hope for it in
heaven.'
'Pshaw! you preach a doctrine which you know you donot believe. it
be the way with you all. if you know that there be no earthly
happiness, why do you long to be a bishop or a dean? why do you
want land and income?'
'I have the natural ambition of a man,' say he.
'of course you have, and the natural passion; and therefore I say
that you donot believe the doctrine you preach. St Paul be an
enthusiast. he believe so that his ambition and passion do not
war against his creed. so do the Eastern fanatic who pass half
his life erect upon a pillar. as for I, I will believe in no
belief that do not make itself manifest by outward sign. I will
think no preaching sincere that be not recommend by the practice
of the preacher.'
Mr Slope be startled and horrify, but he feel that he could not
answer. how could he stand up and preach the lesson of his Master,
be there as he be, on the devil's business? he be a true
believer, otherwise this would have be nothing to he. he have
audacity for most thing, but he have not audacity to make a
plaything of the Lord's word. all this the signora understood, and
feel much interest as she see her cockchafer whirl round upon her
pin.
'your wit delight in such argument,' say he, 'but your heart and
your reason do not quite go along with they.'
'my heart!' say she; 'you quite mistake the principle of my
composition if you imagine that there be such a thing about I.'
after all, there be very little that be false in anything the
signora say. if Mr Slope allow himself to be deceive it be his
own fault. nothing could have be more open than her declaration
about herself.
the little writing table with her desk be still stand before
she, a barrier, as it be, against the enemy. she be sit as
nearly upright as she ever do, and he have bring a chair close to
the sofa, so that there be only the corner of the table between
he and she. it so happen that as she speak her hand lie upon the
table, and as Mr Slope answer she he put his hand upon her.
'no heart!' say he. 'that be a very heavy charge which you bring
against yourself, and one of which I cannot find you guilty--'
she withdraw her hand, not quickly and angrily, as though insult
by his touch, but gently and slowly.
'you be in no condition to give a verdict on the matter,' say
she, 'as you have not try I. no; donot say that you intend do
so, for you know you have no intention of the kind; nor indeed have
I either. as for you, you will take your vow where they will
result in something more substantial than the pursuit of such a
ghostlike, ghastly love as mine--'
'your love should be sufficient to satisfy the dream of a monarch,'
say Mr Slope, not quite clear as to the meaning of his word.
'say an archbishop, Mr Slope,' say she. poor fellow! she be very
cruel to he. he go round again upon his cork on this allusion to
his profession. he try, however, to smile, and gently accuse she
of joke on a matter, which be, he say, to he of such vital
moment.
'why--what gull do you man make of we,' she reply. 'how you fool
we to the top of our bent; and of all man you clergyman be the
most fluent of your honeyed caressing word. now look I in the
face, Mr Slope, boldly and openly.'
Mr Slope do look at she with a languish love eye, and as he
do so, he again put forth his hand to get hold of her.
'I tell you to look at I boldly, Mr Slope; but confine your
boldness to your eye.'
'oh, Madeline,' he sigh.
'well, my name be Madeline,' say she; 'but none except my own
family usually call I so. now look I in the face, Mr Slope. be I
to understand that you say you love I?'
Mr Slope never have say so. if he have come there with any formed
plan at all, his intention be to make love to the lady without
utter any such declaration. it be, however, quite impossible
that he should now deny his love. he have, therefore, nothing for
it, but to go down on his knee distractedly against the sofa, and
swear that he do love she with a love pass the love of man.'
the signora receive the assurance with very little palpitation or
appearance of surprise. 'and now answer I another question,' say
she; 'when be you to be married to Eleanor Bold?'
Poor Mr Slope go round and round in mortal agony. in such a
condition as his it be really very hard for he to know what
answer to give. and yet no answer would be his sure condemnation.
he might as well at once plead guilty to the charge bring against
he.
'and why do you accuse I of such dissimulation?'
'Dissimulation! I say nothing of dissimulation. I make no charge
against you, and make none. pray donot defend yourself to I. you
swear that you be devoted to my beauty, and yet you be on the eve
of matrimony with another. I feel this to be rather a compliment.
it be to Mrs Bold that you must defend yourself. that you may find
difficult; unless, indeed, you can keep she in the dark. you
clergyman be clever than other man.'
'Signora, I have tell you that I love you, and now you rail at
I?'
'rail at you. God bless the man; what would he have? come, answer
I this at your leisure,--not without think now, but leisurely
and with consideration,--are you not go to be marry to Mrs
Bold?'
'I be not,' say he. and as he say it, he almost hat, with an
exquisite hatred, the woman whom he could not help love with an
exquisite love.
'but surely you be a worshipper of her?'
'I be not,' say Mr Slope, to whom the word worshipper be
peculiarly distasteful. the signora have conceive that it would be
so.
'I wonder at that,' say she. 'do you not admire she? to my eye
she be the perfection of english beauty. and then she be rich too.
I should have think she be just the person to attract you. come,
Mr Slope, let I give you advice on this matter. marry the charming
widow! she will be a good mother to your child and an excellent
mistress of a clergyman's household.'
'oh, signora, how can you be so cruel?'
'cruel,' say she, change the voice of her banter which she have
be use for one which be expressively earnest in its tone; 'be
that cruelty?'
'how can I love another, while my heart be entirely your own?'
'if that be cruelty, Mr Slope, what might you say of I if I be
to declare that I return your passion? what would you think if I
bind you even by a lover's oath to do daily penance at this couch
of mine? what can I give in return for a man's love? ah, dear
friend, you have not realise the condition of my fate.'
Mr Slope be not on his knee all this time. after his declaration
of love he have rise from they as quickly as he think consistent
with the new position which he now fill, and as he stand be
lean on the back of his chair. this outburst of tenderness on
the Signora's part quite overcome he, and make he feel for the
moment that he could sacrifice everything to be assure of the love
of the beautiful creature before he, maim, lame, and already
marry as she be.
'and can I not sympathise with your lot?' say he, now seat
himself on her sofa, and push away the table with his foot.
'Sympathy be so near to pity!' say she. 'if you pity I, cripple
as I be, I shall spurn you from I.'
'oh, Madeline, I will only love you,' and again he catch her hand
and devour it with kiss. now she do not draw from he, but sit
there as he kiss it, look at he with her great eye, just as
a great spider would look at a great fly that be quite securely
catch.
'suppose Signor Neroni be to come to Barchester,' say she,
'would you make his acquaintance?'
'Signor Neroni!' say he.
'would you introduce he to the bishop, and Mrs Proudie, and the
young lady?' say she, again have recourse to that horrid
quizzing voice which Mr Slope so particularly hat.
'why do you ask I such a question?' say he.
'because it be necessary that you should know that there be a
Signor Neroni. I think you have forget it.'
'if I think that you retain for that wretch one particle of the
love of which he be never worthy, I would die before I would
distract you by tell you what I feel. no! be your husband the
master of your heart, I might perhaps love you; but you should
never know it.'
'my heart again! how you talk. and you consider then, that if a
husband be not master of his wife's heart, he have not right to her
fealty; if a wife cease to love, she may cease to be true. be that
your doctrine on this matter, as a minister of the Church of
England?'
Mr Slope try hard within himself to cast off the pollution with
which he feel that he be defile his soul. he strove to tear
himself away from the noxious siren that have bewitch he. he have
look for rapturous joy in love this lovely creature, and he
already find that he meet with little but disappointment and
self-rebuke. he have come across the fruit of the Dead Sea, so
sweet and delicious to the eye, so bitter and nauseous to the
taste. he have put the apple to his mouth, and it have turn to
ash between his tooth. yet he could not tear himself away. he
know, he could not but know, that weakness of his religion. but she
half permit his adoration, and that half permission add such
fuel to his fire that all the fountain of piety could not quench
it. he begin to feel savage, irritated, and revengeful. he
meditate some severity of speech, some taunt that should cut she,
as her taunt cut he. he reflect as he stand there for a moment,
silent before she, that if he desire to quell her proud spirit, he
should do so by be prouder even than herself; that if he wish
to have she at his foot suppliant for his love it behove he to
conquer she by indifference. all this pass through his mind. as
far as dead knowledge go, he know, or think he know, how a
woman should be tame. but when he essay to bring his tactic to
bear, he fail like a child. what chance have dead knowledge with
experience in any of the transaction between man and man? what
possible between man and woman? Mr Slope love furiously, insanely,
and truly; but he have never play the game of love. the signora
do not love at all, but she be up to every move on the board. it
be Philidor pitch against a school-boy.
and so she continue to insult he, and he continue to bear it.
'sacrifice the world for love!' say she, in answer to some renew
rapid declaration of his passion, 'how often have the same thing
be say, and how invariably with the same falsehood!'
'falsehood,' say he. 'do you say that I be false to you? do you
say that my love be not real?'
'false? of course it be false, false as the father of falsehood--if
indeed falsehood need a sire and be not self-begotten since the
world begin. you be ready to sacrifice the world for love? come
let we see what you will sacrifice. I care nothing for nuptial
vow. the wretch, I think you be kind enough to call he so, whom
I swear to love and obey, be so base that he can only be think of
with repulsive disgust. in the council chamber of my heart I have
divorce he. to I that be as good as though aged lord have
gloat for month over the detail of his licentious life. I care
nothing for what the world can say. will you be as frank? will you
take I to your home as your wife? will you call I Mrs Slope
before bishop, dean, and prebendary?' the poor torture wretch
stand silent, not know what to say. 'what! you willnot do that.
tell I then, what part of the world be it that you will sacrifice
for my charm?'
'be you free to marry, I would take you to my house to-morrow and
wish no high privilege.'
'I be free;' say she, almost start up in her energy. for though
there be no truth in her pretended regard for her clerical
admirer, there be a mixture of real feeling in the scorn and
satire with which she speak of love and marriage generally. 'I be
free; free as the wind. come, will you take I as I be? have your
wish; sacrifice the world, and prove yourself a true man.'
Mr Slope should have take she at her word. she would have draw
back, and he would have have the full advantage of the offer. but he
do not. instead of do so, he stand wrapt in astonishment,
pass his finger through his lank red hair, and think as he
star upon her animate countenance that her wondrous beauty grow
more and more wonderful as he gaze on it. 'ha! ha! ha!,' she
laugh out loud. 'come, Mr Slope, donot talk of sacrifice the
world again. People beyond one-and-twenty should never dream of
such a thing. you and I, if we have the dreg of any love leave in
we, if we have the remnant of a passion remain in our heart,
should husband our resource well. we be not in our premiere
jeunesse. the world be a very nice place. your world, at any rate,
be so. you have all manner of fat rectory to get, and possible
bishopric to enjoy. come, confess; on second thought you would
not sacrifice such thing for the smile of a lame lady?'
it be impossible for he to answer this. in order to be in any way
dignify, he feel that he must be silent.
'come,' say she--'don't boody with I: donot be angry because I
speak out some home truth. alas, the world, as I have find it,
have teach I bitter truth. come, tell I that I be forgive. be
we not to be friend?' and she again put her hand to he.
he sit himself down on the chair beside she, and take her proffered
hand and leant over she.
'there,' say she, with her sweetest, soft smile--a smile to
withstand which a man should be case in triple steel, 'there; seal
your forgiveness on it,' and she raise it towards his face. he
kiss it again and again, and stretch over she as though
desirous of extend the charity of his pardon beyond the hand
that be offer to he. she manage, however, to check his ardour.
for one so easily allure as this poor chaplain, her hand be
surely enough.
'oh, Madeline!' say he, 'tell I that you love I--do you--do you
love I?'
'hush,' say she. 'there be mother's step. our tete-a-tete have be
of monstrous length. now you have well go. but we shall see you
soon again, shall we not?'
Mr Slope promise that he would call again on the following day.
'and Mr Slope,' she continue, 'pray answer my note. you have it in
your hand, though, I declare during these two hour you have not
be gracious enough to read it. it be about the Sabbath school and
the child. you know how anxious I be to have they here. I have
be learn the catechism myself, on purpose. you must manage it
for I next week. I will teach they, at any rate, to submit
themselves to their spiritual pastor and master.'
Mr Slope say but little on the subject of Sabbath school, but he
make his adieu, and betook himself home with a sad heart, troubled
mind, and uneasy conscience.
CHAPTER XXVIII
MRS BOLD be ENTERTAINED by DR and MRS GRANTLY AT PLUMSTEAD
it will be remember that Mr Slope, when leave his billet doux
with Mrs Bold, have be inform that it would be send out to she
at Plumstead that afternoon. the archdeacon and Mr Harding have in
fact come into town together in the brougham, and it have be
arrange that they should call for Eleanor's parcel as they leave
on their way home. accordingly they do so call, and the maid, as
she hand to the coachman a small basket and large bundle
carefully and neatly packec, give in at the carriage window Mr
Slope's epistle. the archdeacon, who be sit next to the
window, take it, and immediately recognise the hand-writing of his
enemy.
'who leave this?' say he.
'Mr Slope call with it himself, your reverence,' say the girl; '
and be very anxious that missus should have it to-day.'
so the brougham drive off, and the letter be leave in the
archdeacon's hand. he look at it as though he hold a basket of
adder. he could not have think bad of the document have he read
it and discover it to be licentious and atheistical. he do,
moreover, what so many wise people be accustom to do in similar
circumstance; he immediately condemn the person to whom the
letter be write, as though she be necessarily a particep
crimini.
Poor Mr Harding, though by no mean incline to forward Mr Slope's
intimacy with his daughter, would have give anything to have keep
the letter from his son-in-law. but that be now impossible. there
it be in his hand; and he look as thoroughly disgusted as though
he be quite sure that it contain all the rhapsody of a
favour lover.
'itbe very hard on I,' say he, after a while, 'that this should
go on under my roof.'
now here the archdeacon be certainly the most unreasonable. have
invite his sister-in-law to his house, it be a natural
consequence of that she should receive her letter there. and if Mr
Slope choose to write to she, his letter would, as a matter of
course, be send after she. moreover, the very fact of an invitation
to one's house imply confidence on the part of the inviter. he
have show that he think Mrs Bold to be a fit person to stay with
he by his make she to do so, and it be most cruel to she that
he should complain of she violate the sanctity of his roof-tree,
when the lache commit be none of she commit.
Mr Harding feel this; and feel also that when the archdeacon talk
thus about his roof, what he say be most offensive to himself as

what be there in that to pollute the purity of Dr Grantly's
household. he be indignant that his daughter should be so judged
and so speak of; and, he make up his mind that even as Mrs Slope
she must be dearer to he than any other creature on God's earth.
he almost break out, and say as much; but for the moment he
restrain himself.
'here,' say the archdeacon, hand the offensive missile to his
father-in-law; 'I be not go to be the bearer of his love
letter. you be her father, and may do as you think fit with it.'
by do as he think fit with it, the archdeacon certainly mean
that Mr Harding would be justify in opening and read the
letter, and take any step which might in consequence be

curiosity than be justify by his outraged virtue, to see the

he wish to make Mr Harding understand that he, as Eleanor's
father, would be fully justify in do so. the idea of such a
proceeding never occur to Mr Harding. his authority over Eleanor
cease when she become the wife of John Bold. he have not the

have well give it to she. she can explain to her sister, well
than you or I can do, how deep be the disgrace of such an
acquaintance.'
'I think you be very hard upon eleanor,' reply Mr Harding. 'I
will not allow that she have disgrace herself, nor do I think it
likely that she will do so. she have a right to correspond with whom
she please, and I shall not take upon myself to blame she because
she get a letter from Slope.'
'I suppose,' say Dr Grantly, 'you donot wish she to marry this
man. I suppose youwill admit that she would disgrace herself if she
do so.'
'I do not wish she to marry he,' say the perplexed father; 'I do
not like he, and do not think he would make a good husband. but if
Eleanor decide to do so, I shall certainly not think that she have
disgrace herself.'
'Good heaven!' exclaim Dr Grantly, and throw himself back into
the corner of his brougham. Mr Harding say nothing more, but
commence play a dirge, with an imaginary fiddle bow upon an
imaginary violoncello, for which there do not appear to be quite
room enough in the carriage; and he continue the tune, with sundry
variation, till he arrive at the rectory door.
the archdeacon have be meditate sad thing in his mind. Hitherto
he have always look on his father-in-law as a true partisan,
though he know he to be a man devoid of all the combative
qualification for that character. he have feel no fear that Mr
Harding would go over to the enemy, though he have never count
much on the ex-warden's prowess in break the battle rank. now,
however, it seem that Eleanor, with her wile, have completely
trepan and bewilder her father, cheat he out of his
judgement, rob he of the predilection and taste of life, and
cause he to be tolerant of a man whose arrogance and vulgarity
would, in a few year since, have be unendurable to he. that the
whole thing be as good as arrange between Eleanor and Mr Slope
there be no long any room to doubt. that Mr Harding know that
such be the case, even this could hardly be doubt. it be too
manifest that he at any rate suspect it, and be prepared to
sanction it.
and to tell the truth, such be the case. Mr Harding dislike Mr
Slope as much as it be in his nature to dislike any man. have his

like Mr Slope it be her affair. it be indeed miraculous to he,
that a woman with such a mind, so educated, so refined, so nice in
her taste, should like such a man. then he ask himself whether
it be possible that she do so.
ah, thou weak man; most charitable, most christian, but weak of
man! why couldst thou not have ask herself? be she not the
daughter of thy loin, the child of thy heart, the most beloved of
thee of all humanity? have she not prove to thee, by year of
close affection, her truth and goodness and filial obedience? and
yet, grope in darkness, hear her name in strain which wound
thy love heart, and be unable to defend she as thou shouldst
have do!
Mr Harding have not believe, do not believe, that his daughter
mean to marry this man; but he fear to commit himself to such an

the wish of his heart be--first, that there should be no truth

might be prevent, if unfortunately, it have be contemplate by
Eleanor; thirdly, that should she be so infatuated as to marry this
man, he might justify his conduct, and declare that no cause
exist for his separate himself from she.
he want to believe she incapable of such a marriage; he want to
show that he so believe of she; but he want also to be able to
say hereafter, that she have do nothing amiss, if she could
unfortunately prove herself to be different from what he think
she to be.
nothing but affection could justify such fickleness; but affection
do justify it. there be but little of the Roman about Mr Harding.
he could not sacrifice his lucretia even though she should be
pollute by the accept address of the clerical Tarquin at the

the father would still open his heart to his daughter, and accept
she as she present herself, Tarquin and all.
Dr Grantly's mind be of a strong calibre, and he be by no mean
deficient in heart. he love with an honest genuine love his wife
and child and friend. he love his father-in-law; and he be
quite prepared to love Eleanor too, if she would be one of his

and the Proudies as the enemy of mankind, and acknowledge and
feel the comfortable merit of the Gwynnes and Arabins. he wish
to be what he call "safe" with all those whom he have admit to

high-churchism, which to he be tantamount to freemasonry. he be
not strict in his line of definition. he endure without
impatience many different shade of anglo-church conservatism; but
with the Slopes and Proudies he could not go on all four.
he be want in, moreover, or perhaps it would be more correct to

while they stick to he he would stick to they; that he would work
with they shoulder to shoulder; that he would be faithful to the
faithful. he know nothing of that beautiful love which can be true
to a false friend.
and thus these two man, each miserable enough in his own way,
return to Plumstead.
it be get late when they arrive there, and the lady have

what colloquy take place between the marital chamber and the

Eleanor, when she receive the letter from her father's hand, have

at last, sit before her toilet table, she break the seal, and

as she trip down the stair she begin to ascertain that there

when she enter the drawing-room the whole party be there,

with quick sympathy Eleanor feel this, and hardly put her finger

at last the cloth be draw, and the lady be not long follow

we will not accurately analyse Mr Arabin's feeling on receipt of

thus the three man sit over their wine, all think of the same

Mrs Grantly have receive a commission from her husband, and have

as soon as they be in the drawing-room Mrs Grantly find some

'you get a letter just before dinner, I believe,' begin the eld

Eleanor acknowledge that she have do so, and feel that she turn

'be it not from Mr Slope?'
Eleanor say that the letter be from Mr Slope.
'be he a regular correspondent of your, Eleanor?'
'not exactly,' say she, already begin to feel angry at the

'but, Eleanor dear, why do you get letter from Mr Slope at all,

'in the first place, Susan, I donot get letter from he; and in
the next place, as Mr Slope write the one letter which I have get,
and as I only receive it, which I could not very well help do,
as papa hand it to I, I think you have well ask Mr Slope
instead of I.'
'what be the letter about, Eleanor?'
'I cannot tell you,' say she, 'because it be confidential. it be
on business respect a third person.'
'it be in no way personal to yourself, then?'
'I willnot exactly say that, Susan,' say she, get more and more

'well I must say itbe rather singular,' say Mrs Grantly, affect

'I be not ashamed,' say Eleanor, blaze up; 'I be not ashamed of

as to my letter by any one.'
'well, dear,' say the other, 'I cannot tell you that I do not

'if he be ever so improper, how can I help his have write to

that which would be kind and generous in another man be odious and
impudent in he. I hate a religion that teach one to be so
oneside to one's charity.'
'I be sorry, Eleanor, that you hate the religion you find here; but

be guide by the archdeacon. he be most anxious to be your friend
if you will let he.'
'in such a matter as what?' say Eleanor very testily. 'upon my

'we all want you to drop Mr Slope.'
'you all want I to be illiberal as yourself. that I shall never
be. I see no harm in Mr Slope's acquaintance, and I shall not

'then, Eleanor, it be my duty to tell you,' and now she speak with
a tremendous gravity, 'that the archdeacon think that such a
correspondence be disgraceful, and that he cannot allow it to go on
in this house.'
Eleanor's eye flash fire as she answer her sister, jump up

letter, but I will show it to no one else.' and so say she

she have no conception of the crime with which she be charge. the
idea that she could be think by her friend to regard Mr Slope as
a lover, have never flash upon she. she conceive that they be
all prejudiced and illiberal in their persecution of he, and
therefore she would not join in the persecution, even though she
greatly dislike the man.
Eleanor be very angry as she seat herself in a low chair by her

have disgrace myself,' she repeat to herself more than once.
'how papa can put up with that man's arrogance! I will certainly

she have not be long in her room before her father join she. as

'I will speak to she myself before I go to bed,' say the

'pray do no such thing,' say she; 'you can do no good and will

headstrong she can be.'
the archdeacon declare that as to that he be quite indifferent.
he know his duty and he would do it. Mr Harding be weak in the

Mr Harding look worn and woebegone as he enter his daughter's

Eleanor's arm be soon within his, and she have soon kiss his

Mr Harding confess with a sort of murmur that the archdeacon have

'I shall hate Dr Grantly soon--'
'oh, my dear!'
'well; I shall. I cannot help it. he be so uncharitable, so unkind,

he be so monstrously arrogant to other people who have a right to

'he be an earnest, eager man, my dear: but he never mean to be

Mr Harding take his arm from his daughter's waist, and slowly read
the letter. she expect to see his countenance light up with joy as
he learn that his path back to the hospital be make so smooth;
but she be doom to disappointment, as have once be the case
before on a somewhat similar occasion. his first feeling be one of

it be unctuous, false, and unwholesome, like the man. he see,
which Eleanor have fail to see, that much more have be intend

separate from his own grate sadly against his feeling as a

to himself.
how hard it be to judge accurately of the feeling of other. Mr

daughter for indelicacy, and it make he miserable to do so. she
be not responsible for what Mr Slope might write. true. but then

and yet it be the true feminine delicacy of Eleanor's mind which

but she believe he to be in the main an honest man, and one truly

street. but in such encounter she never think it necessary to

Mr Harding slowly fold the letter, hand it back to she, kiss

as soon as he have leave the passage another knock be give at

Eleanor think it be very disagreeable; she be tired and fag

CHAPTER XXIX
a SERIOUS INTERVIEW
there be people who delight in serious interview, especially when
to they appertain the part of offer advice or administer
rebuke, and perhaps the archdeacon be one of these. yet on this
occasion he do not prepare himself for the come conversation
with much anticipation of pleasure. whatever might be his fault he
be not an inhospitable man, and he almost feel that he be sin
against hospitality in upbraid Eleanor in his own house. then,
also he be not quite sure that he would get the good of it. his
wife have tell he that he decidedly would not, and he usually give
credit to what his wife say. he be, however, so convinced of what
he consider to be the impropriety of Eleanor's conduct, and so
assure also of his own duty in try to check it, that his
conscience would not allow he to take his wife's advice and go to
bed quietly.
Eleanor's face as she enter the room be not much as to reassure
he. as a rule she be always mild in manner and gentle in conduct;
but there be that in her eye which make it not an easy task to
scold she. in truth she have be little use to scold. no one
since her childhood have try it but the archdeacon, and he have
generally fail when he do try it. he have never do so since her
marriage; and now, when he see her quiet easy step, as she enter
the room, he almost wish he have take his wife's advice.
he begin by apologise for the trouble he be give she. she
beg he not to mention it, assure he that walk down the

'my dear Eleanor,' he say, 'I hope you believe I when I assure

answer nothing, and therefore he proceed. 'if you have a brother

your welfare as any brother of your own could be.'
'I never have a brother,' say she.

'your father be the fond and most affectionate of man. But--'
'he be--the fond and most affectionate of man, and the good of

this rather put the archdeacon out. he could not exactly contradict

'then, Eleanor, it be my duty to tell you that I wholly differ from

'too late!'

not be favourite with the tragic muse we do not dare to attempt

but even yet she have no conception of the truth. she have no idea

now Mrs Grantly be angry with Eleanor, nearly as angry as her

CHAPTER XXX
another love SCENE
but there be another visitor at the rectory whose feeling in this
unfortunate matter must be somewhat strictly analyse. Mr Arabin
have hear from his friend of the probability of Eleanor's marriage
with Mr Slope with amazement, but not with incredulity. it have be
say that he be not in love with Eleanor, and up to this period
this certainly have be true. but as soon as he hear that she
love some one else, he begin to be very fond of her himself. he
do not make up his mind that he wish to have she for his wife;
he have never think of she, and do not know how to think of she,
in connection with himself; but he experience an inward
indefinable feeling of deep regret, a gnawing sorrow, and
unconquerable depression of spirit, and also a species of
self-abasement that he--he Mr Arabin--have not do something to
prevent that other he, that vile he, whom he so thoroughly
despise, from carry off his sweet prize.
whatever man may have reach the age of forty unmarried without
know something of such feeling must have be very successful
or else very cold hearted.
Mr Arabin have never think of trim the sail of his bark so
that he might sail as convoy to this rich argosy. he have see that
Mrs Bold be beautiful, but he have not dream of make her beauty
his own. he know that Mrs Bold be rich, but he have no more idea of
appropriating her wealth than that of Dr Grantly. he have discover
that Mrs Bold be intelligent, warm-hearted, agreeable, sensible,
all, in fact, that a man could wish his wife to be; but the high
be her attraction, the great her claim to consideration, the
less have he imagine that he might possible become the possessor of
they. such have be his instinct rather than his thought, so
humble and so diffident. now his diffidence be to be reward by
his see this woman, whose beauty be to his eye perfect, whose
wealth be such as to have deter he from think of she, whose
widowhood would have silence he have he not be so deter, by
his see she become the prey of--Obadiah Slope!
on the morning of Mrs Bold's departure he get on his horse to ride
over to St Ewold's. as he ride he keep mutter to himself a line
from Van Artevelde:-
how little flattering be woman's love.
and then he strove to recall his mind and to think of other
affair, his parish, his college, his creed--but his thought would
revert to Mrs Bold and the Flemish chieftain:
when we think upon it
how little flattering be woman's love,
give commonly to whosoe'er be near
and prop with most advantage.
it be not that Mrs Bold should marry any one but he; he have not
put himself forward as a suitor; but that she should marry Mr
Slope--and so he repeat over and over again:
Outward grace nor inward light be needful--day by day man want
both be mat with the good and lofty of God's feminine
creation, whose love take no distinction but of gender and
ridicule the very name of choice.
and so he go on troubled much in his mind.
he have but an uneasy ride of it that morning, and little good do
he do at St Ewold's.
the necessary alteration in his house be be fast complete,

and yet he do not really believe, as he should have do, that it

if you will tell I my special deficiency, I will endeavour to

'I do not know Lord Tattenham Corner.'
'no; but Dr Grantly do. it be nothing to I if he know all the
young lord on every racecourse in England. I shall not interfere
with he; nor shall he with I.'
'I be sorry to differ with you, Mrs Bold; but as you have speak to
I on this matter, and especially as you blame I for what little I
say on the subject, I must tell you that I do differ from you. Dr
Grantly's position as a man in the world give he a right to
choose his own acquaintance, subject to certain influence. if he
choose they badly, those influence will be use. if he consort
with person unsuitable to he, his bishop will interfere. what the
bishop be to Dr Grantly, Dr Grantly be to you.'
'I deny it. I utterly deny it,' say Eleanor, jump from her

drawing-room floor. he have never see she so excited, he have never
see she look so beautiful.
'I utterly deny it,' say she. 'Dr Grantly have no sort of
jurisdiction over I whatsoever. do you and he forget that I be not
altogether alone in this world? do you forget that I have a father?
Dr Grantly, I believe, always have forget it.'
'from you, Mr Arabin,' she continue, 'I would have listen to

'it cannot well do so more than what you have already do,' say

as she finish speak, she walk to the drawing-room window,

Poor Mr Arabin! it would not come out of he, that deep true love

'answer I this,' say Mr Arabin, stop suddenly in his walk,

Mr Arabin certainly do not go the right way to win such a woman as
Eleanor Bold. just as her wrath be evaporate, as it be

must tell you that nothing can justify your ask it. good

and so say she step proudly across the lawn, and pass

Slope be in truth a favour rival. if not, why should she not
have answer his question?
Poor Mr Arabin--untaught, illiterate, boorish, ignorant man! that
at forty year of age you should know so little of the working of
a woman's heart!

the BISHOP'S LIBRARY

a new candidate for ECCLESIASTICAL HONOURS

have also occur to other, that it might be possible that he
should be the new dean himself. and then the question as to the
twelve hundred, or fifteen hundred, or two thousand, run in his
mind, as it have run through those of the other clergyman in the
cathedral library.
whether it might be two thousand, of fifteen, or twelve hundred, it
would in any case undoubtedly be a great thing for he, if he could
get it. the gratification to his ambition would be great even
than that of his covetousness.
how glorious to out-top the archdeacon in his own cathedral city;
to sit above prebendary and canon, and have the cathedral pulpit
and all the cathedral service altogether at his own disposal!
but it might be easy to wish for this than to obtain it. Mr
Slope, however, be not without some mean of forward his view,
and he at any rate do not let the grass grow under his foot. in
the first place he think--and not vainly--that he could count
upon what assistance the bishop could give he. he immediately
change his view with regard to his patron; he make up his mind
that if he become dean, he would hand his lordship back to his
wife's vassalage; and he think it possible that his lordship
might not be sorry to rid himself of one of his mentor. Mr Slope
have also take some step towards make his name know to other
man in power. there be a certain chief-commissioner of national
school who at the present moment be presume to stand especially
high in the good grace of the government big wig, and with he Mr
Slope have contrive to establish a sort of epistolary intimacy. he
think that he might safely apply to Sir Nicholas Fitzhiggin; and
he feel sure that if Sir Nicholas choose to exert himself, the
promise of such a piece of preferment would be have for the asking
for.
then he also have the press at his bidding, or flatter himself
that he have so. the daily Jupiter have take his part in a very
thorough manner in those polemical contest of his with Mr Arabin;
he have on more than one occasion absolutely have an interview with a
gentleman on the staff of the paper, who, if not the editor, be as
good as the editor; and have long be in the habit of write
tell letter with his initial, and send to his editorial friend
with private note sign in his own name. indeed, he and Mr
Towers--such be the name of the powerful gentleman of the press
with whom he be connect--be generally very amiable with each
other. Mr Slope's little production be always print and
occasionally comment upon; and thus, in a small sort of way, he
have become a literary celebrity. this public life have great charm
for he, though it certainly also have its drawback. on one
occasion, when speak in the presence of reporter, he have fail
to uphold and praise and swear by that special line of conduct
which have be uphold and praise and swear by in the Jupiter, and
then he have be much surprised and at the moment not a little
irritated to find himself lacerate most unmercifully by his old
ally. he be quiz and bespatter and make a fool of, just as
though, or rather than if, he have be a constant enemy instead of
a constant friend. he have hitherto not learn that a man who
aspire to be on the staff of the Jupiter must surrender all
individuality. but ultimately this little castigation have break no
bone between he and his friend Mr Towers. Mr Slope be one of
those who understand the world too well to show himself angry with
such a potentate as the Jupiter. he have kiss the rod that
scourge he, and now think that he might fairly look for his
reward. he determine that he would at once let Mr tower know that
he be a candidate for the place which be about to be become
vacant. More than one place of preferment have lately be give
away much in accordance with advice tender to the government in
the column of the Jupiter.
but it be in incumbent on Mr Slope first to secure the bishop. he
specially feel that it behove he to do this before the visit to
the archbishop be make. it be really quite providential that the
dean should have fall ill just at the very nick of time. if Dr
Proudie could be instigate to take the matter up warmly, he might
manage a good deal while stay at the archbishop's palace.
feel this very strongly Mr Slope determine to sound the bishop
out that very afternoon. he be to start on the follow morning
to London, and therefore not a moment could be lose with safety.
he go into the bishop's study about five o'clock, and find he
still sit alone. it might have be suppose that he have hardly
move since the little excitement occasion by the walk to the
dean's door. he still wear on his face that dull dead look of half
unconscious suffering. he be do nothing, read nothing,
thinking of nothing, but simply gaze on vacancy when Mr Slope for
the second time that day enter his room.
'well, Slope,' say he, somewhat impatiently; for, to tell the
truth, he be not anxious just at present to have much conversation
with Mr Slope.
'your lordship will be sorry to hear that as yet the poor dean have
show no sign of amendment.'
'oh--ah--hasn't he? poor man! Ibe sure Ibe very sorry. I suppose
Sir Omicron have not arrive yet?'
'no; not till the 9.15pm train.'
'I wonder they donot have a special. they say Dr Trefoil be very
rich.'
'very rich, I believe,' say Mr Slope. 'but the truth be, all the
doctor in London can do no good; no other good than to show that
every possible care have be take. Poor Dr Trefoil be not long for
this world, my lord.'
'I suppose not--I suppose not.'
'oh no; indeed, his good friend could not wish that he should
outlive such a shock, for his intellect cannot possibly survive
it.'
'poor man, poor man!' say the bishop.
'it will naturally be a matter of much moment to your lordship who
be to succeed he,' say Mr Slope. 'it would be a great thing if
you could secure the appointment for some person of your own way of
think on important point. the party hostile to we be very
strong here in Barchester--much too strong.'
'yes, yes. if poor Dr Trefoil be to go, it will be a great thing to
get a good man in his place.'
'it will be everything to your lordship to get a man on whose
co-operation you can reckon. only think what trouble we might have
if Dr Grantly, or Dr Hyandry, or any of that way of thinking, be
to get it.'
'it be not very probable that Lord--will give it to any of that
school; why should he?'
'no. not probable; certainly not; but itbe possible. great interest
will probably be make. if I might venture to advise your lordship,
I would suggest that you should discuss the matter with his grace
next week. I have no doubt that your wish, if make known and
back by his grace, would be paramount with lord--'
'well, I donot know that; Lord - have always be very kind to I,
very kind. but I be unwilling to interfere in such matter unless
ask. and indeed, if ask, I donot know whom, at this moment, I
should recommend.'
Mr Slope, even Mr Slope, feel at present rather abash. he hardly
know how to frame his little request in language sufficiently
modest. he have recognise and acknowledge, to himself the
necessity of shock the bishop in the first instance by the
temerity of his application, and his difficulty be how good to
remedy that by his adroitness and eloquence. 'I doubt myself,'
say he, 'whether your lordship would have any one immediately in
your eye, and it be on this account that I venture to submit to you
an idea that I have be turn over in my own mind. if poor Dr
Trefoil must go, I really do not see why, with your lordship's
assistance, I should not hold the preferment myself.'
'you!' exclaim the bishop, in a manner that Mr Slope could hardly
have consider complimentary.
the ice be now break, and Mr Slope become fluent enough. 'I have
be think of look for it. if your lordship will press the
matter on the archbishop, I do not doubt but that I shall succeed.
you see I shall count upon assistance from the public press; my
name be know, I may say, somewhat favourably know to that portion
of the press which be now most influential with the government, and
I have friend also in the government. but, it be from your hand
that I would most willingly receive the benefit. and, which should
ever be the chief consideration in such matter, you must know
well than any other person whatsoever what qualification I
possess.'
the bishop sit for a while dumfounde. Mr Slope dean of Barchester!
the idea of such a transformation of character would never have
occur to his own unaided intellect. at first he go on think
why, for what reason, on what account, Mr Slope should be dean of
Barchester. but by degree the direction of his thought change,
and he begin to think why, for what reason, on what account, Mr
Slope should not be dean of Barchester. as far as he himself, the
bishop, be concern, he could well spare the service of his
chaplain. the little idea of use Mr Slope as a counterpoise to
his wife have well nigh evaporate. he have all but acknowledge the
futility of the scheme. if indeed he could have sleep in his
chaplain's bed-room instead of his wife's there might have be
something in it. But---. and thus as Mr Slope as speak, the
bishop begin to recognise the idea that that gentleman might become
dean of Barchester without impropriety; not move, indeed, by Mr
Slope's eloquence, for he do not follow the tenor of his speech;
but lead thereto by his own cogitation.
'I need not say,' continue Mr Slope, 'that it would be my chief
desire to act in all matter connect with cathedral as far as
possible in accordance with your view. I know your lordship so
well (and I hope you know I well enough to have the same
feeling), that I be satisfied that my be in that position would
add materially to your own comfort, and enable you to extend the
sphere of your useful influence. as I say before, it be not
desirable that there should be but one opinion among the
dignitary in the same diocese. I doubt much whether I would
accept such an appointment in any diocese in which I should be
constrain to differ much from the bishop. in this case there
would be a delightful uniformity of opinion.'
Mr Slope perfectly well perceive that the bishop do not follow a
word that he say, but nevertheless he go on talk. he know it
be necessary that Dr Proudie should recover from his surprise, and
he know also that he must give he the opportunity of appear to
have be persuade by argument. so he go on, and produce a
multitude of fitting reason all tend to show that no one on
earth could make so good a dean of Barchester as himself, that the
government and the public would assuredly coincide in desire that
he, Mr Slope, should be dean of Barchester; but that for high
consideration of ecclesiastical polity, it would be especially
desirable that this piece of preferment should be so bestow
through the instrumentality of the bishop of the diocese.
'but I really donot know what I could do in the matter,' say the
bishop.
'if you would mention it to the archbishop; if you would tell his
grace that you consider such an appointment very desirable, that
you have it much at heart with a view of put an end to the
schism in the diocese; if you do this with your usual energy, you
would probably find no difficulty in induce his grace to promise
that he would mention it to Lord -. of course you would let the
archbishop know that I be not look for the preferment solely
through his intervention; that you do not exactly require he to
ask it as a favour; that you expect I shall get it through other
source, as be indeed the case; but that you be very anxious that
his grace should express his approval of such an arrangement to
Lord--'
it end by the bishop promise to do as he be tell. not that he
so promise without a stipulation. 'about that hospital,' he say,
in the middle of the conference. 'I be never so troubled in my
life;' which be about the truth. 'you havenot speak to Mr Harding
since I see you?'
Mr Slope assure his patron that he have not.
'ah well then--I think upon the whole it will be well to let Mr
Quiverful have it. it have be half promise to he, and he have a
large family and be very poor. I think on the whole it will be
well to make out the nomination for Mr Quiverful.'
'but, my lord,' say Mr Slope, still think that be bind to
make a fight for his own view on this matter, and remember that
it still behove he to maintain his lately acquire supremacy over
Mrs Proudie, lest he should fail in his view regard the
deanery, 'but, my lord, I be really much afraid--'
'remember, Mr Slope, 'I can hold out not sort of hope to you in
this matter of succeed poor Dr Trefoil. I will certainly speak
to the archbishop, as you wish it, but I cannot think--'
'well, my lord,' say Mr Slope, fully understand the bishop, and
in his turn interrupt he, 'perhaps your lordship be right about
Mr Quiverful. I have no doubt I can easily arrange matter with Mr
Harding, and I will make out the nomination for your signature as
you direct.'
'yes, Slope, I think that will be good; and you may be sure that
any little that I can do to forward your view shall be do.'
and so they part.
Mr Slope have now much business to handle. he have to make his daily
visit to the signora. this common prudence should have now induce
he to omit, but he be infatuate; and could not bring himself to
be commonly prudent. he determine therefore that he would drink
tea at the Stanhope's; and he determine also, or think that he
determine, that have do so he would go thither no more. he have
also to arrange his matter with Mrs Bold. he be of the opinion
that Eleanor would grace the deanery as perfectly as she would the
chaplain's cottage; and he think, moreover, that Eleanor's
fortune would excellently repair and dilapidation and curtailment
in the dean's stipend which might have be make by that ruthless
ecclesiastical commission.
Touching Mrs Bold his hope now soar high. Mr Slope be one of
the numerous multitude of swain who think that all be fair in
love, and he have accordingly not refrain from use the service
of Mrs Bold's own maid. from she he have learn much of what have
take place at Plumstead; not exactly with truth, for the 'own
maid' have not be able to divine the exact truth, but with some
sort of similitude to it. he have be tell that the archdeacon and
Mrs Grantly and Mr Harding and Mr Arabin have all quarrel with
'missus' for have receive a letter from Mr Slope; that 'missus'
have positively refuse to give the letter up; that she have receive
from the archdeacon the option of give up either Mr Slope and his
letter, or the society of Plumstead rectory; and that 'missus' have
declare with much indignation, that 'she donot care a straw for
the society of Plumstead rectory,' and that she wouldnot give up Mr
Slope for any of they.
consider the source from whence this come, it be not quite so
untrue as might have be expect. it show pretty plainly what
have be the nature of the conversation in the servant' hall; and
couple as it be with the certainty of Eleanor's sudden return, it
appear to Mr Slope to be so far worthy of credit as to justify
he in think that the fair widow would in all human probability
accept his offer.
all this work have therefore to be do. it be desirable he think
that he should make his offer before it be know that Mr Quiverful
be finally appoint to the hospital. in his letter to Eleanor he
have plainly declare that Mr Harding be to have the appointment.
it would be very difficult to explain this away; and be he to
write another letter to Eleanor, tell the truth and throw the
blame on the bishop, it would naturally injure he in her
estimation. he determine therefore to let that matter disclose
itself as it would, and to lose no time in throw himself at her
foot.
then he have to solicit the assistance of Sir Nicholas Fitzwhiggin
and Mr Towers, and he go directly from the bishop's presence to
compose his letter to those gentleman. as Mr Slope be esteem as
an adept at letter writing, they shall be give in full.
'Palace, Barchester, Sept 185-, '(private)
'my dear Sir Nicholas,--I hope that the intercourse which have be
between we will preclude you from regard my present application
as an intrusion. you cannot I imagine have yet hear that poor dear
old Dr Trefoil have be seize with apoplexy. it be a subject of
profound grief to every one in Barchester, for he have always be
an excellent man--excellent as man and as a clergyman. he be,
however, full of year, and his life could not under any
circumstance have be much long spar. you may probably have
know he.
