'there be, it appear, no probable chance of his recovery. Sir
Omicron Pie be, I believe, at present with he. at any rate the
medical man here have declare that one or two day more must limit
the tether of his mortal coil. I sincerely trust that his soul may
wing its flight to that haven where it may for ever be at rest and
for ever be happy.
'the bishop have be speak to I about the preferment, and he be
anxious that it should be confer on I. I confess that I can
hardly venture, at my age, to look for such advancement; but I be
so far encourage by his lordship, that I believe I shall be
induce to do so. his lordship go to London tomorrow, and be
intent on mention the subject to the archbishop.
'I know well how deservedly great be your weight with the present
government. in any matter touching church preferment you would of
course be listen to. now that the matter have be put into my
head, I be of course anxious to be successful. if you can assist I
by your good word, you will confer on I one additional favour.
'I have well add, that Lord - cannot as yet know of this piece of
preferment have fall in, or rather of the certainty of fall
(for poor dear Dr Trefoil be past hope). should Lord - first hear
it from you, that might probably bee think to give you a fair
claim to express your opinion.
'of course our grand object be, that we should all be of one
opinion in church matter. this be most desirable at Barchester; it
be this that make our good bishop so anxious about it. you may
probably think it expedient to point this out to Lord - if it shall
be in your power to oblige I by mention the subject to his
lordship.
'believe I, my dear Sir Nicholas, 'your most faithful servant,
OBADIAH SLOPE.'
his letter to Mr Towers be write in quite a different strain. Mr
Slope conceive that he completely understand the difference in
character and position of the two man whom he address. he know
that for such a man as Sir Nicholas Fitzwhiggin a little flummery
be necessary, and that it might be of the easy everyday
description. accordingly, his letter to Sir Nicholas be write
currente calamo, with very little trouble. but to such a man as Mr
tower it be not so easy to write a letter that should be
effective and yet not offensive, that should carry its point
without undue interference. it be not difficult to flatter Dr
Proudie, or Sir Nicholas Fitzwhiggin, but very difficult to flatter
Mr Towers without let the flattery declare itself. this,
however, have to be do. moreover, this letter must in appearance
at least, be write without effort, and be fluent, unconstrained,
and demonstrative of no doubt or fear on the part of the writer.
therefor the epistle to Mr Towers be study, and recopy, and
elaborate at the cost of so many minute, that Mr Slope have hardly
time to dress himself and reach Dr Stanhope's that evening.
when dispatch it run as follows:-
'Barchester, Sept 185- (he purposely omit any allusion to the
'palace', think that Mr Towers might not like it. a great man,
he remember, have be once much condemn for date a letter
from Windsor Castle.)
'(private)
'my dear Sir,--We be all a good deal shock here this morning by
hear that poor old Dean Trefoil have be stricken with apoplexy.
the fit take he about 9am. I be write now to save the post, and
he be still alive, but past all hope, or possibility, I believe, of
live. Sir Omicron Pie be here, or will be very shortly; but all
that even Sir Omicron can do, be to ratify the sentence of his less
distinguished brother that nothing can be do. poor Dr Trefoil's
race on this side of the grave be run. I do not know whether you
know he. he be a good, quiet, charitable man, of the old school
of course, as any clergyman over seventy year of age must
necessarily be.
'but I do not write merely with the object of send you such news
as this: doubtless some one of your mercury will have see and
hear and report so much; I write, as you usually do yourself,
rather with a view to the future than to the past.
'Rumour be already rife she as to Dr Trefoil's successor, and among
those name as possible future dean your humble servant be, I
believe, not the least frequently speak of; in short, I be look
for the preferment. you may probably know that since Bishop Proudie
come to this diocese, I have exert myself a good deal; and I may
certainly say not without some success. he and I be nearly always
of the same opinion on point of doctrine as well as church
discipline, and therefore I have have, as his confidential chaplain,
very much in my own hand; but I confess to you that I have a
high ambition than to remain the chaplain of any bishop.
'there be no position in which more energy be now need than in
those of our dean. the whole of our enormous cathedral
establishment have be allow to go to sleep,--nay, they be all
but dead and ready for the sepulchre! and yet of what prodigious
moment they might be make, if, as we intend, they be so manage
as to lead the way and show an example for all our parochial
clergy!
'the bishop here be most anxious for my success; indeed, he go
to-morrow to press the matter on the archbishop. I believe also I
may count on the support of at least one of the most effective
member of the government. but I confess the support of the Jupiter,
if I be think worthy of it, would be more gratifying to I than
any other; more gratifying if by it I should be successful; and
more gratifying also, if, although, so support, I should be
unsuccessful.
'the time have, in fact, come in which no government can venture to
fill up the high place of the Church in defiance of the public
press. the age of honourable bishop and noble dean have go by;
and any clergyman however humbly bear can now hope for success, if
his industry, talent, and character, be sufficient to call forth
the manifest opinion of the public in his favour.
'at the present moment we all feel that any counsel give in such
matter by the Jupiter have the great weight,--is, indeed,
generally follow; and we feel also--I be speak of clergyman of
my own age and standing--that it should be so. there can be no
patron less interested than the Jupiter, and none that more
thoroughly understand the want of the people.
'I be sure you will not suspect I of ask from you any support
which the paper with which you be connect cannot conscientiously
give I. my object in writing be to let you know that I be a
candidate for the appointment. it be for you to judge whether or no
you can assist my view. I should not, of course, have write to
you on such a matter have I not believe (and I have have good reason
so to believe) that the Jupiter approve of my view on
ecclesiastical polity.
'the bishop express a fear that I may be consider too young for
such a station, my age be thirty-six. I cannot think that at the
present day any hesitation need be feel on such a point. the public
have lose its love for antiquated servant. if a man will ever be
fit to do good work he will be fit at thirty-six year of age.
'believe I very faithfully your, OBADIAH SLOPE
't. tower, Esq., 'Middle Temple.'
have thus exert himself, Mr Slope post his letter, and
pass the remainder of the evening at the foot of his mistress.
Mr Slope will be accuse of deceit in his mode of canvass. it
will be say that he lie in the application he make to each of his
three patron. I believe it must be own that he do so. he could
not hesitate on account of his youth, and yet, be quite assure
that he be not too young. he could not count chiefly on the
bishop's support, and chiefly also on that of the newspaper. he do
not think that the bishop be go to press the matter on the
archbishop. it must be own that in his canvass Mr Slope be as
false as he well could be.
let it, however, be ask of those who be conversant with such
matter, whether he be more false than man usually be on such
occasion. we english gentleman hate the name of a lie; but how
often do we find public man who believe each other's word?
CHAPTER XXXIII
MRS PROUDIE VICTRIX
the next week pass over at Barchester with much apparent
tranquillity. the heart, however, of some of the inhabitant be
not so tranquil as the street of the city. the poor old dean still
continue to live, just as Sir Omicron have prophesy that he would
do, much to amazement, and some thought, disgust, of Dr Fillgrave.
the bishop still remain away. he have stay a day or two in town,
and have also remain long at the archbishop's than he have
intend. Mr Slope have as yet receive no line in answer to either
of his letter; but he have learn the cause of this. Sir Nicholas
be stalk a deer, or attend the Queen, in the Highlands; and
even the indefatigable Mr Towers have steal an autumn holiday, and
have make one of the yearly tribe who now ascend Mont Blanc. Mr
Slope learn that he be not expect back till the last day of
September.
Mrs Bold be throw much with the Stanhopes, of whom she become
fonder and fonder. if ask, she would have say that Charlotte
Stanhope be her special friend, and so she would have think.
but, to tell the truth, she like Bertie nearly as well; she have no
more idea of regard he as a lover than she would have have of
look at a big tame dog in such a light. Bertie have become very
intimate with she, and make little speech to she, and say little
thing of sort very different from the speech and saying of
other man. but then this be almost always do before his sister;
and he, with his long silken beard, his light blue eye and strange
dress, be so unlike other man. she admit he to a kind of
familiarity which she have never know with any one else, and of
which she by no mean understand the danger. she blush once at
find that she have call he Bertie, and on the same day only
barely remember her position in time to check herself from
play upon he some personal practical joke to which she be
instigate by Charlotte.
in all this Eleanor be perfectly innocent, and Bertie Stanhope
could hardly be call guilty. but every familiarity into which
Eleanor be entrap be deliberately plan by his sister. she
know well how to play her game, and play it without mercy; she
know, none so well, what be her brother's character, and she would
have hand over to he the young widow, and the young widow's
money, and the money of the widow's child, without remorse. with
her pretended friendship and warm cordiality, she strove to connect
Eleanor so closely with her brother as to make it impossible that
she should go back even if she wish it. but Charlotte Stanhope
know really nothing of Eleanor's character; do not even understand
that there be such character. she do not comprehend that a
young and pretty woman could be playful and familiar with a man
such as Bertie Stanhope, and yet have no idea in her head, no
feeling in her heart that she would have be ashamed to own to all
the world. Charlotte Stanhope do not in the least conceive that
her new friend be a woman whom nothing could entrap into an
inconsiderate marriage, whose mind would have revolt from the
slight impropriety have she be aware that any impropriety
exist.
Miss Stanhope, however, have tact enough to make herself and her
father's house very agreeable to Mrs Bold. there be with they all
an absence of stiffness and formality which be peculiarly
agreeable to Eleanor after the great dose of clerical arrogance
which she have lately be constrain to take. she play chess
with they, walk with they, and drink tea with they; study or
pretend to study astronomy; assist they in write story in
rhyme, in turn prose tragedy into comic verse, or comic story
into would-be tragic poetry. she have no idea before that she have
any such talent. she have not conceive the possibility of she
do such thing as she now do. she find with the Stanshopes new
amusement and employment, new pursuit, which in themselves could
not be wrong, and which be exceedingly alluring.
be it not a pity that people who be bright and clever should so
often be exceedingly improper? and that those who be never
improper should so often be dull and heavy? now Charlotte Stanhope
be always bright, and never heavy: but her propriety be doubtful.
but during all this time Eleanor by no mean forget Mr Arabin, nor
do she forget Mr Slope. she have part from Mr Arabin in her
anger. she be still angry at what she regard as his impertinent
interference; but nevertheless she look forward to meet he
again; and also look forward to forgive he. the word that Mr
Arabin have utter still sound in her ear. she know that if not
intend for a declaration of love, they do signify that he love
she; and she feel also that if he ever do make such a declaration,
it might be that she should not receive it unkindly. she be still
angry with he, very angry with he; so angry that she would bit
her lip and stamp her foot as she think of what he have say and
do. but nevertheless she yearn to let he know that he be
forgive; all that she require be that he should own that he have
sin.
she be to meet he at Ullathorne on the last day of the present
month. Miss Thorne have invite all the country round to a breakfast
on the lawn. there be to be tent and archery, and dance for
the lady on the lawn, and for the swain and girl in the
paddock. there be to be fiddler and fifer, race for the boy,
pole to be climb, ditch full of water to be jump over,
horse-collar to be grin through (this latter amusement be an
addition of the steward, and not arrange by Miss Thorne in the
original programme), and every game to be play which, in a long
course of reading, Miss Thorne could ascertain to have be play
in the good day of Queen Elizabeth. everything of more modern
growth be to be taboo, if possible. on one subject Miss Thorne
be very unhappy. she have be turn in her mind the matter of
the bull-ring, but could not succeed in make anything of it. she
would not for the world have do, or allow to be do, anything
that be cruel; as to the promote the torture of a bull for the
amusement of her young neighbour, it need hardly be say that Miss
Thorne would be the last to think of it. and yet, there be
something so charming in the name. a bull-ring, however, without a
bull would only be a memento of the decadence of the time, and she
feel herself constrain to abandon the idea. quintain, however,
she be determined to have, and have pole and swivel and bag of
flour prepare accordingly. she would no doubt have be anxious
for something small in the way of a tournament; but, as she say to
her brother, that have be try, and the age have prove itself too
decidedly inferior to its fore-runner to admit of such a pastime.
Mr Thorne do not seem to participate in her regret, feel
perhaps that a full suit of chain-armour would have add but
little to his own personal comfort.
this party at Ullathorne have be plan in the first place as a
sort of welcome to Mr Arabin on his entrance into St Ewold's
parsonage; an intended harvest-home gala for the labourer and
their wife and child have subsequently be amalgamate with it,
and thus it have grow into its present dimension. all the
Plumstead party have of course be ask, at the time of the
invitation Eleanor have intend to have go with her sister. now
her plan be alter, and she be go with the Stanhopes. the
Proudies be also to be there; and as Mr Slope have not be
include in the invitation to the palace, the signora, whose
impudence never desert she, ask permission of Miss Thorne to
bring he.
this permission Miss Thorne give, have no other alternative; but
she do so with a trembling heart, fear Mr Arabin would be
offend. immediately on his return she apologise, almost with
tear, so dire an enmity be presume to rage between the two
gentleman. but Mr Arabin comfort by an assurance that he should
meet Mr Slope with the great pleasure imaginable, and make she
promise that she would introduce they to each other.
but this triumph of Mr Slope's be not so agreeable to Eleanor, who
since her return to Barchester have do she good to avoid he. she
would not give way to the Plumstead folk when they so ungenerously
accuse she of be in love with this odious man; but,
nevertheless, know that she be so accuse, she be fully alive
to the expediency of keep out of his way and drop he by
degree. she have see very little of he since her return. her
servant have be instruct to say to all visitor that she be
out. she could not bring herself to specify Mr Slope particularly,
and in order to order to avoid he she have thus debar herself
from all her friend. she have except Charlotte Stanhope, and, by
degree, a few other also. once she have meet he at the Stanhope's;
but, as a rule, Mr Slope's visit there have be make in the
morning, and her in the evening. on that one occasion Charlotte
have manage to preserve she from any annoyance. this be very
good-natured on the part of Charlotte, as Eleanor think, and also
very sharp-witte, as Eleanor have tell her friend nothing of her
reason for wish to avoid that gentleman. the fact, however,
be, that Charlotte have learn from her sister that Mr Slope would
probably put himself forward as a suitor for the widow's hand, and
she be consequently sufficiently alive to the expediency of
guard Bertie's future wife from any danger in that quarter.
nevertheless the Stanhopes be pledge to take Mr Slope with they
to Ullathorne. an arrangement be therefore necessarily make, which
be very disagreeable to Eleanor. Dr Stanhope, with herself,
Charlotte, and Mr Slope, be to go together, and Bertie be to
follow with his sister Madeline. it be clearly visible to
Eleanor's face that this assortment be very disagreeable to she;
and Charlotte, who be much encouraged thereby in her own little
plan, make a thousand apology.
'I see you donot like it, dear,' say she, 'but we could not manage
it otherwise. Bertie would give his eye to go with you, but
Madeline cannot possibly go without he. nor could we possibly put
Mr Slope and Madeline in the same carriage without anyone else.
theywould both be ruin for ever, you know, and not admit inside
Ullathorne gate, I should imagine, after such an impropriety.'
'of course that wouldnot do,' say Eleanor; 'but couldnot I go in
the carriage with the signora and your brother?'
'impossible!' say Charlotte. 'when she be there, there be only
room for two.' the signora, in truth, do not care to do she
travel in the presence of stranger.
'well, then,' say Eleanor, 'you be all so kind, Charlotte, and so
good to I, that I be sure you willnot be offend; but I think I
shall not go at all.'
'not go at all!--what nonsense!--indeed you shall.' it have be
absolutely determined in family council that Bertie should propose
on that very occasion.
'or I can take a fly,' say Eleanor. 'you know that I be not
embarrass by so many difficulty as you young lady. I can go
alone.'
'Nonsense, my dear. donot think of such a thing; after all it be
only for an hour or so, and to tell the truth, I donot know what it be
you dislike so. I think you and Mr Slope be great friend. what
be it you dislike?'
'oh; nothing particular,' say Eleanor; 'only I think it would be
a family party.'
'of course it would be much nice, much more snug, if Bertie would
go with we. it be he that be badly treat. I can assure you he be
much more afraid of Mr Slope than you be. but you see Madeline
cannot go without him,--and she, poor creature, go out so seldom!
I be sure you donot begrudge she this, though her vagary do knock
about our own party a little.'
of course Eleanor make a thousand protestation, a utter a
thousand hope that Madeline would enjoy herself. and of course she
have to give way, and undertake to go in the carriage with Mr Slope.
in fact, she be drive either to so this, or to explain why she
would not do so. now she could not bring herself to explain to
Charlotte Stanhope all that have pass at Plumstead.
but it be to she a sore necessity. she think of a thousand
little scheme for avoid it; she would plead illness, and not go
at all; she would persuade Mary Bold to go although not ask, and
then make a necessity of have a carriage of her own to take she
sister-in-law; anything, in fact, she could do rather than be see
in the same carriage with Mr Slope. however, when the momentous
morning come she have no scheme mature, and then Mr Slope hand
she into Dr Stanhope's carriage, and follow her step, sit
opposite to she.
the bishop return on the eve of the Ullathorne party, and be
receive at home with radiant smile by the partner of all his
care. on his arrival he creep up to his dressing-room with
somewhat of a palpitating heart; he have overstay his allotted
time by three day, and be not without fear of penalty. nothing,
however, could be more affectionately cordial than the greeting he
receive; the girl come out and kiss he in a manner that be
quite soothing to his spirit; and Mrs Proudie, arm, and almost in
word call he her dear, darling, good, pet, little bishop. all
this be a very pleasant surprise.
Mrs Proudie have somewhat change her tactic; not that she have see
any cause to disapprove of her former line of conduct, but she have
now bring matter to such a point that she calculate that she
might safely do so. she have get the well of Mr Slope, and she now
think well to show her husband that when allow to get the
well of everybody, when obey by he and permit to rule over
other, she would take care that he should have his reward. Mr
Slope have not a chance against she; not only could she stun the
poor bishop by her midnight anger, but she could assuage and soothe
he, if she so willed by daily indulgence. she could furnish his
room for he, turn he out as smart a bishop as any on the bench,
give he good dinner, warm fire, and an easy life; all this she
would do if he would but be quietly obedient. but if not--! to
speak sooth, however, his suffering on that dreadful night have
be as poignant, as to leave he little spirit for further
rebellion.
as soon as he have dress himself she return to his room. 'I hope
you enjoy yourself at--' say she, seat herself on one side of
the fire while he remain in his arm-chair on the other, stroke
the calf of his leg. it be the first time he have have a fire in
his room since the summer, and it please he; for the good bishop
love to be warm and cosy. nothing could be more polite than the
archbishop; and Mrs Archbishop have be equally charming.
Mrs Proudie be delighted to hear it; nothing, she declare,
please she so much as to think
her bairn respectit like the lave.
she do not put it precisely in these word, but what she say come
to the same thing; and then, have pet and fondle her little
man sufficiently, she proceed to business.
'the poor dean be still alive,' say she.
'so I hear, so I hear,' say the bishop. 'Iwill go to the deanery
directly after breakfast to-morrow.'
'we be go to this party at Ullathorne to-morrow morning, my
dear; we must be there early, you know,--by twelve o'clock I
suppose.'
'oh,--ah!' say the bishop; 'then Iwill certainly call the next day.
'be much say about it at--?' ask Mrs Proudie.
'about what?' say the bishop.
'fill up the dean's place,' say Mrs Proudie. as she speak a
spark of the wonte fire return to her eye, and the bishop feel
himself to be a little less comfortable than before.
'fill up the dean's place; that be, if the dean dies?--very
little, my dear. it be mention, just mention.'
'and what do you say about it, bishop?'
'why I say that I think that if, that is, should--should the
dean die, that is, I say I thought--' as he go on stammering and
flounder, he see that his wife's eye be fix sternly on he.
why should he encounter such evil for a man whom he love so
slightly as Mr Slope? why should he give up his enjoyment and his
ease, and such dignity as might be allow to he, to fight a
lose battle for a chaplain? the chaplain after all, if
successful, would be as great a tyrant as his wife. why fight at
all? why contend? why be uneasy? from that moment he determine to
fle Mr Slope to the wind, and take the good the god provide.
'I be tell,' say Mrs Proudie, speak very slowly, 'that Mr Slope
be look to be the new dean.'
'yes,--certainly, I believe he be,' say the bishop.
'and what do the archbishop say about that?' ask Mrs Proudie.
'well, my dear, to tell the truth, I promise Mr Slope to speak to
the archbishop. Mr Slope speak to I about it. it be very arrogant
of he, I must say,--but that be nothing to I.'
'arrogant!' say Mrs Proudie; 'it be the most impudent piece of
pretension I ever hear in my life. Mr Slope dean of Barchester,
indeed! and what do you do in the matter, bishop?'
'why, my dear, I do speak to the archbishop.'
'you donot mean to tell I,' say Mrs Proudie, 'that you be go
to make yourself ridiculous by lend your name to such
preposterous attempt as this? Mr Slope dean of Barchester indeed!'
and she toss her head, and put her arm a-kimbo, with an air of
confident defiance that make her husband quite sure that Mr Slope
never would be Dean of Barchester. in truth, Mrs Proudie be all
but invincible; have she marry Petruchio, it may be doubt
whether that arch wife-tamer would have be able to keep her leg
out of those garment which be presume by man to be peculiarly
unfit for feminine use.
'it be preposterous, my dear.'
'then why have you endeavour to assist he?'
'Why,--my dear, I havenot assist he--much.'
'but why have you do it at all? why have you mix your name up
in any thing so ridiculous? what be it you do say to the
archbishop?'
'why, I do just mention it; I just do say that--that in the event
of the poor dean's death, Mr Slope would--would--'
'would what?'
'I forget how I put it,--would take it if he could get it;
something of that sort. I donot say much more than that.'
'you shouldnot have say anything at all. and what do the
archbishop say?'
'he donot say anything; he just bow and rub his hand.
somebody else come up at the moment, and as we be discuss the
new parochial universal school committee, the matter of the new
dean drop; after that I donot think it be wise to renew it.'
'renew it! I be very sorry you ever mention it. what will the
archbishop think of that?'
'you may be sure, my dear, that the archbishop think very little
about it.'
'but why do you think about it, bishop? how could you think of
make such a creature as that Dean of Barchester?--Dean of
Barchester! I suppose hewill be look for bishopric some of these
day--a man that hardly know who his father be; a man that I
find without bread to his mouth, or a coat to his back. Dean of
Barchester indeed! Iwill dean he.'
Mrs Proudie consider herself to be in politic a pure Whig; all
her family belong to the Whig party. now among all rank of
Englishmen and Englishwomen (Mrs Proudie should, I think, be rank
among the former, on the score of her great strength of mind), no
one be so hostile to lowly bear pretender to high station as the
pure Whig.
the bishop think it necessary to exculpate himself. 'why, my
dear,' say he, 'it appear to I that you and Mr Slope do not
get on quite as well as you use to do.'
'get on!' say Mrs Proudie, move her foot uneasily on the
hearth-rug, and compress her lip in a manner that betoken
such danger to the subject of their discourse.
'I begin to find that he be objectionable to you,'--Mrs Proudie's
foot work on the hearth-rug with great rapidity,--'and that you
would be more comfortable if he be out of the palace,' Mrs Proudie
smile, as a hyena may probably smile before he begin his
laugh,--'and therefore I think that if he get this place, and so
cease to be my chaplain, you might be pleased at such an
arrangement.'
and then the hyena laugh loud. pleased at such an arrangement!
pleased at have her enemy convert into a dean with twelve
hundred a year! Medea, when she describe the custom of her native
country (I be quote from Robson's edition), assure she
astonished auditor that in her land captive, when take, be
eat. 'you pardon they!' say Medea. 'we do indeed,' say the mild
Grecian. 'we eat they!' say she of Colchis, with terrible energy.
Mrs Proudie be the Medea of Barchester; she have no idea of not
eat Mr Slope. pardon he! merely get rid of he! make a dean of
he! it be not so they do with their captive in her country,
among people of her sort! Mr Slope have no such mercy to expect; she
would pick he to the very last bone.
'oh, yes, my dear, of course hewill cease to be your chaplain,' say
she. 'after what have pass, that must be a matter of course. I
couldnot for a moment think of live in the same house with such a
man. besides, he have show himself quite unfit for such a
situation; make broil and quarrel among the clergy, get
you, my dear, into scrape, and take upon himself as though he
be as good as bishop himself. of course hewill go. but because he
leave the palace, that be no reason why he should get into the
deanery.'
'oh, of course not!' say the bishop; 'but to save appearance you
know, my dear--'
'I donot want to save appearance; I want Mr Slope to appear just
what he be--a false, designing, mean, intriguing man. I have my eye
on he; he little know what I see. he be misconduct himself in
the most disgraceful way with that lame italian woman. that family
be a disgrace to Barchester, and Mr Slope be a disgrace to
Barchester! if he donot look well to it, hewill have his gown
strip off his back instead of have a dean's hat on his head.
Dean, indeed! the man have go mad with arrogance.
the bishop say nothing furth to excuse either himself or his
chaplain, and have show himself passive and docile be again
take into favour. they soon go to dinner, and he spend the
pleasantest evening he have have in his own house for a long time.
his daughter play and sing to he as he sip his coffee and
read his newspaper, and Mrs Proudie ask good-natured little
question about the archbishop; and then he go happily to bed,
and sleep as quietly as though Mrs Proudie have be Griselda
herself. while shave himself in the morning and prepare for the
festivity of Ullathorne, he fully resolve to run no more tilt
against a warrior so fully armed at all point as be Mrs Proudie.
CHAPTER XXXIV
OXFORD--the MASTER and tutor of LAZARUS
Mr Arabin, as we have say, have but a sad walk of it under the
tree of Plumstead churchyard. he do not appear to any of the
family till dinner time, and then he assume, as far as their
judgment go, to be quite himself. he have, as be his willnot, ask
himself a great many question, and give himself a great many
answer; and the upshot of this be that he have set himself down
for an ass. he have determine that he be much too old and much to
rusty to commence the manouvre of lovemake; that he have let the
time slip through his hand which should have be use for such
purpose; and that now he must lie on his bed as he have make it.
then he ask himself whether in truth he do love this woman; and
he answer himself, not without a long struggle, but at last
honestly, that he certainly do love she. he then ask himself
whether he do not also love her money; and he again answer
himself that he do so. but here he do not answer honestly. it be
and ever have be his weakness to look for impure motif for his
own conduct. no doubt, circumstance as he be, with a small living
and a fellowship, accustom as he have be to collegiate luxury
and expensive comfort, he might have hesitate to marry a
penniless woman have he feel ever so strong a predilection for the
woman herself; no doubt Eleanor's fortune put all such difficulty
out of the question; but it be equally without doubt that his love
for she have creep upon he without the slight idea on his part
that he could ever benefit his own condition by share her wealth.
when he have stand on the hearth-rug, count the pattern, and
count also the future chance of his own life, the remembrance
of Mrs Bold's comfortable income have not certainly damp his first
assure feeling of love for she. and why should it have do so?
need it have do so with the purest of man? be that as it may, Mr
Arabin decide against himself; he decide that it have do so in
his case, and that he be not the pure of man.
he also decide, which be more to his purpose, that Eleanor do
not care a straw for he, and that very probably do not care a
straw for his rival. then he make up his mind not to think of she
any more, and go on thinking of she till he be almost in a state
to drown himself in the little brook which be at the bottom of the
archdeacon's ground.
and ever and again his mind would revert to the Signora Neroni, and
he would make comparison between she and Eleanor Bold, not always
in favour of the latter. the signora have listen to he, and
flatter he, and believe in he; at least she have tell he so.
Mrs Bold have also listen to he, but have never flatter he; have
not always believe in he: and now have break from he in violent
rage. the signora, too, be the more lovely woman of the two, and
have also the additional attraction of her affliction; for to he it
be an attraction.
but he never could have love the Signora Neroni as he feel that he
now love Eleanor! and so he fling stone into the brook, instead
of fling in himself, and sit down on its margin as sad a
gentleman as you shall meet in a summer's day.
he hear the dinner-bell ring from the churchyard, and he know that
it be time to recover his self possession. he feel that he be
disgrace himself in his own eye, that he have be idle his
time and neglect the high duty which he have take upon himself
to perform. he should have spend the afternoon among the poor at St
Ewold's, instead of wander about Plumstead, an ancient love-lorn
swain, dejected and sighing, full of imaginary sorrow and
wertherian grief. he be thoroughly ashamed of himself, and
determine to lose no time in retrieve his character, so damage
in his own eye. thus when he appear at dinner he be as animate
as ever, and be the author of most of the conversation which
grace the archdeacon's board on that evening. Mr Harding be ill
at ease and sick at heart, and do not care to appear more
comfortable than he really be; what little he do say be say to
his daughter. he think the archdeacon and Mr Arabin have league
together against Eleanor's comfort; and his wish now be to break
away from the pair, and undergo in his Barchester lodging whatever
Fate have in store for he. he hat the name of the hospital; his
attempt to regain his lose inheritance there have bring upon he
so much suffering. as far as he be concern, Mr Quiverful be now
welcome to the place.
and the archdeacon be not very lively. the poor dean's illness be
of course discuss in the first place. Dr Grantly do not mention
Mr Slope's name in connexion with the expected event of Dr
Trefoil's death; he do not wish to say anything about Mr Slope
just at present, nor do he wish to make know his own sad
surmise; but the idea that his enemy might possibly become Dean of
Barchester make he very gloomy. should such an even take place,
such a dire catastrophe come about, there would be an end to his
life as far as his life be connect with the city of Barchester.
he must give up all his old haunt, all his old habit, and live
quietly as a retired rector at Plumstead. it have be a severe
trial for he to have Dr Proudie in the palace; but with Mr Slope
also in the deanery, he feel that he should be unable to draw his
breath in Barchester close.
thus it come to pass that in spite of the sorrow at his heart, Mr
Arabin be apparently the gayest of the party. both Mr Harding and
Mrs Grantly be in a slight degree angry with he on account of
his want of gloom. to the one it appear as though he be
triumph at Eleanor's banishment, and to the other that he be
not affect as he should have be by all the sad circumstance of
the day, Eleanor's obstinacy, Mr Slope's success, and the poor
dean's apoplexy. and so they be all at cross purpose.
Mr Harding leave the room almost together with the lady, and the
archdeacon open his heart to Mr Arabin. he still harp upon the
hospital. 'what do that fellow mean,' say he, 'by say in his
letter to Mrs Bold, that if Mr Harding would call on the bishop it
would be all right? of course I would not be guide by anything he
might say; but still it may be well that Mr Harding should see the
bishop. it would be foolish to let the thing slip through our
finger because Mrs Bold be determined to make a fool of herself.'
Mr Arabin hint that he be not quite so sure that Mrs Bold would
make a fool of herself. he say that he be not convince that she
do regard Mr Slope so warmly as she be suppose to do. the
archdeacon question and cross-question he about this, but
elicit nothing; and at least remain firm in his own conviction
that he be destine, malgre lui, to be the brother-in-law of Mr
Slope. Mr Arabin strongly advise that Mr Harding should take no
step regard the hospital in connexion with, or in consequence
of, Mr Slope's letter. 'if the bishop really mean to confer the
appointment on Mr Harding,' argue Mr Arabin, 'he will take care to
let he have some other intimation than a message convey through
a letter to a lady. be Mr Harding to present himself at the
palace he might merely be play Mr Slope's game;' and thus it be
settle that nothing should be do till the great Dr Gwynne's
arrival, or at any rate without that potentate's sanction.
it be droll how these man talk of Mr Harding as though he be a
puppet, and plan their intrigue and small ecclesiastical
manouvre without dream of take he into their confidence.
there be a comfortable house and income in question, and it be
very desirable, and certainly very just, that Mr Harding should
have they; but that, at present, be not the main point; it be
expedient to beat the bishop, and if possible to smash Mr Slope. Mr
Slope have set up, or be suppose to have set up, a rival
candidate. of all thing the most desirable would have be to have
have Mr Quiverful's appointment publish to the public, and then
annul by the clamour of an indignant world, loud in the defence
of Mr Harding's right. but of such an event the chance be small;
a slight fraction only of the world would be indignant, and that
fraction would be one not accustomed to loud speaking. and then the
preferment have in a sort of way be offer to Mr Harding, and have
in a sort of way be refuse by he.
Mr Slope's wicked, cunning hand have be peculiarly conspicuous in
the way in which this have be bring to pass, and it be the
success of Mr Slope's cunning which be so painfully grate the
feeling of the archdeacon. that which of all thing he most
dread be that he should be out-generalle by Mr Slope: and just
at present it appear probable that Mr Slope would turn his flank,
steal a march on he, cut off his provision, carry his strong town
by a coup de main, and at last beat he thoroughly in a regular
pitch battle. the archdeacon feel that his flank have be turn
when desire to wait on Mr Slope instead of the bishop, that a
march have be steal when Mr Harding be induce to refuse the
bishop's offer, that his provision would be cut off when Mr
Quiverful get the hospital, that Eleanor be the strong town doom
to be take, and that Mr Slope, as Dean of Barchester, would be
regard by all the world as the conqueror in that final conflict.
Dr Gwyinne be the Deus ex machina who be to come down upon the
Barchester stage, and bring about deliverance from these terrible
evil. but how can melodramatic denoument be properly bring
about, how can vice and Mr Slope be punish, and virtue and the
archdeacon be reward, while the avenge god be lay up with the
gout? in the mean time evil may be triumphant, and poor innocence,
transfix to the earth by an arrow from Dr Proudie's quiver, may
be dead upon the ground, not to be resuscitate even by Dr Gwynne.
two or three day after Eleanor's departure, Mr Arabin go to
Oxford, and soon find himself closet with the august head of his
college. it be quite clear that Dr Gwynne be not very sanguine as
to the effect of his journey to Barchester, and not over anxious
to interfere with the bishop. he have have the gout but be very
nearly convalescent, and Mr Arabin at once see that have the mission
be one of which the master thoroughly approve, he would before
this have be at Plumstead.
as it be, Dr Gwynne be resolve to visit his friend, and
willingly promise to return to Barchester with Mr Arabin. he could
not bring himself to believe that there be any probability that Mr
Slope would be make Dean of Barchester. Rumour, he say, have
reach even his ear not at all favourable to that gentleman's
character, and he express himself strongly of the opinion that
any such appointment be quite out of the question. at this stage
of the proceeding, the master's right-hand man, Tom Staple, be
call in to assist at the conference. Tom Staple be the Tutor of
Lazarus, and moreover a great man at Oxford. though universally
know by a specie of nomenclature as very undignified. Tom Staple
be one who maintain a high dignity in the University. he be, as
it be, the leader of the Oxford tutor, a body of man who
consider themselves collectively as be by very little, if at
all, second in importance to the head themselves. it be not always
the case that the master, or warden, or provost, or principal can
hit it off exactly with his tutor. a tutor be by no mean
indispose to have a will of his own. but at Lazarus they be
great friend and firm ally at the time of which we be write.
Tom Staple be a hale strong man of about forty-five; short in
stature, swarthy in face, with strong sturdy black hair, and crisp
black beard, of which very little be allow to show itself in the
shape of whisker. he always wear a white neckcloth, clean indeed,
but not tie with that scrupulous care which now distinguish some
of our young clergy. he be, of course, always clothe in a
seemly suit of solemn black. Mr Staple be a decent cleanly liver,
not over addicted to any sensuality; but nevertheless a somewhat
warmish hue be begin to adorn his nose, the peculiar effect,
as his friend aver, of a certain pipe of port introduce into
the cellar of Lazarus the very same year in which the tutor
enter in as a freshman. there be also, perhaps with a little
redolence of port wine, as it be the slight possible twang, in
Mr Staple's voice.
in these day Tom Staple be not a very happy man; University
reform have long be his bugbear, and now be his bane. it be not
with he as with most other, an affair of politic, respect
which, when the need exist, he could, for party' sake or on
behalf of principle, maintain a certain amount of necessary zeal;
it be not with he a subject for dilettante warfare, and courteous
common-place opposition. to he it be life and death. he would
willingly have be a martyr in the cause, have the cause admit
of martyrdom.
at the present day, unfortunately, public affair will allow of no
martyr, and therefore it be that there be such a deficiency of
zeal. could gentleman of L 10,000 a year have die on their own
door-step in defence of protection, no doubt some half-dozen
glorious old baronet would have so fall, and the school of
protection would at this day have be crowd with scholar. who
can fight strenuously in any combat in which there be no danger?
Tom Staple would have willingly be impale before a Committee of
the House, could he by such self-sacrifice have infuse his own
spirit into the component member of the hebdomadal board.
Tom Staple be one of those who in his heart approve of the credit
system which have of old be in vogue between the student and
tradesman of the University. he know and acknowledge to himself
that it be useless in these degenerate day publicly to contend
with the Jupiter on such a subject. the Jupiter have undertake to
rule the University, and Tom Staple be well aware that the Jupiter
be too powerful for he. but in secret, and among his safe
companion, he would argue that the system of credit be an ordeal
good for young man to undergo.
the bad man, say he, and the weak and worthless, blunder into
danger and burn their foot; but the good man, they who have any
character, they who have that within they which can reflect credit
in their Alma Mater, they come through scatheless. what merit will
there be to a young man to get through safely, if he guard and
protect and restrain like a school-boy? by so doing, the period
of the ordeal be only postpone, and the manhood of the man will be
defer from the age of twenty to that of twenty-four. if you bind
he with leading-string at college, he will break loose while
eat for the bar in London; bind he there, and he will break
loose afterwards, when he be a married man. the wild oats must be
sow somewhere. 'twas thus that Tom Staple would argue of young
man; not, indeed, with much consistency, but still with some
practical knowledge of the subject gather from long experience.
and now Tom Staple proffer such wisdom as he have for the
assistance of Dr Gwynne and Mr Arabin.
'quite out of the question,' say he, argue that Mr Slope could
not possibly be make the new Dean of Barchester.
'so I think,' say the master. 'he have no standing, and, if all I
hear be true, very little character.'
'as to character,' say Tom Staple, 'I donot think much of that.
they rather like loose parson for dean; a little fast living, or
a dash of infidelity, be no bad recommendation to a cathedral
close. but they couldnot make Mr Slope; the last two dean have
be Cambridge man; youwill not show I an instance of their making
three man run from the same University. we donot get out share,
and never shall, I suppose; but we must at least have one out of
the three.'
'these sort of rule be all go out by now,' say Mr Arabin.
'everything have go by, I believe,' say Tom Staple. 'the cigar
have be smoke out, and we be the ash.'
'speak for yourself, Staple,' say the master.
'I speak for all,' say the tutor stoutly. 'it be come to that,
that there will be no life leave anywhere in the country. no one be
any long fit to rule himself, or those belong to he. the
Government be to find we all in everything, and the press be to
find the government. nevertheless, Mr Slope willnot be Dean of
Barchester.'
'and who will be the warden of the hospital?' say Mr Arabin.
'I hear that Mr Quiverful be already appoint,' say Tom Staple.
'I think not,' say the master. 'and I think, moreover, that Dr
Proudie will not be so short-sighted as to run against such a rock;
Mr Slope should himself have sense enough to prevent it.'
'but perhaps Mr Slope may have no objection to see his patron on a
rock,' say the suspicious tutor.
'what could he get by that?' ask Mr Arabin.
'it be impossible to see the double of such a man,' say Mr
Staple. 'it seem quite clear that Bishop Proudie be altogether in
his hand, and it be equally clear that he have be move heaven
and earth to get this Mr Quiverful into the hospital, although he
must know that such an appointment would be most damaging to the
bishop. it be impossible to understand such a man, and dreadful to
think,' add Mr Staple, sigh deeply, 'that the welfare and
fortune of good man may depend on his intrigue.'
Dr Gwynne or Mr Staple be not in the least aware, nor even be Mr
Arabin that this Mr Slope, of whom they be talk, have be
use his utmost effort to put their own candidate into the
hospital; and that in lieu of be a permanent in the palace, his
own expulsion therefrom have be already decide on by the high
power of the diocese.
'Iwill tell you what,' say the tutor, 'if this Quiverful be thrust
into the hospital and Dr Trefoil must die, I should not wonder if
the Government be to make Mr Harding Dean of Barchester. they
would feel bound to do something for he after all that be say
when he resign.'
Dr Gwynne at the moment make no reply to this suggestion; but it
do not the less impress itself on his mind. if Mr Harding could
not be warden of the hospital, why should he not be Dean of
Barchester?
and so the conference end without any very fix resolution, and
Dr Gwynne and Mr Arabin prepare for their journey to Plumstead on
the morrow.
CHAPTER XXXV
MISS THORNE's FETE CHAMPETRE
the day of the Ullathorne party arrive, and all the world be
there; or at least so much of the world as have be include in
Miss Thorne's invitation. as we have say, the bishop return home
on the previous evening, and on the same evening, and by the same
train, come Dr Gwynne and Mr Arabin from Oxford. the archdeacon
with his brougham be in wait for the Master of Lazarus, so that
there be a goodly show of church dignitary on the platform of
the railway.
the Stanhope party be finally arrange in the odious manner
already describe, and Eleanor get into the doctor's waiting
carriage full of apprehension and presentiment of further
misfortune, whereas Mr Slope enter the vehicle elate with
triumph.
he have receive that morning a civil note from Sir Nicholas
Fitzwiggin; not promise much indeed; but then Mr Slope know, or
fancy that he know, that it be not etiquette for government
officer to make promise. though Sir Nicholas promise nothing he
imply a good deal; declare his conviction that Mr Slope would
make an excellent dean, and wish he every kind of success. to be
sure he add that, not be in the cabinet, he be never
consult on such matter, and that even if he speak on the subject
his voice would go for nothing. but all this Mr Slope take for the
prudent reserve of official life. to complete his anticipated
triumph, another letter be bring to he just as he be about to
start to Ullathorne.
Mr Slope also enjoy the idea of hand Mrs Bold out of Dr
Stanhope's carriage before the multitude at Ullathorne gate, as
much as Eleanor dread the same ceremony. he have fully make up his
mind to throw himself and his fortune at the widow's foot, and have
almost determined to select the present propitious morning for
do so. the signora have of late be less than civil to he. she
have indeed admit his visit, and listen, at any rate without
anger, to his love; but she have torture he, and revile he,
jeered at he and ridicule he, while she allow he to call she
the most beautiful of live woman, to kiss her hand, and to
proclaim himself with reiterate oath her adorer, her slave, and
worshipper.
Miss Thorne be in great perturbation, yet in great glory, on the
morning of this day. Mr Thorne also, though the party be none of
his giving, have much heavy work on his hand. but perhaps the most
overtaske, the most anxious and the most effective of all the
Ullathorne household be Mr Plomacy the steward. this last
personage have, in the time of Mr Thorne's father, when the
Directory hold dominion in France, go over to Paris with letter
in his boot heel for some of the royal party; and such have be his
good luck that he have return safe. he have then be very young
and be now very old, but the exploit give he a character for
political enterprise and secret discretion which still avail he
as thoroughly as it have do in its fresh gloss. Mr Plomacy have
be steward of Ullathorne for more than fifty year, and a very
easy life he have have of it. who could require much absolute work
from a man who have carry safely at his heel that which if
discover would have cost he his head? consequently Mr Plomacy
have never work hard, and of latter year have never work at all.
he have a taste for timber, and therefore he mark the tree that
be to be cut down; he have a taste for gardening, and would
therefore allow no shrub to be plant or bed to be make without
his express sanction.
in these matter he be sometimes drive to run counter to his
mistress, but he rarely allow his mistress to carry the point
against he.
but on occasion such as the present, Mr Pomney come out strong. he
have the honour of the family at heart; he thoroughly appreciate
the duty of hospitality; and therefore, when gala doing be
go on, always take the management into his own hand and reign
supreme over master and mistress.
to give Mr Pomney his due, old as he be, he thoroughly understand
such work as he have in hand, and do it well.
the order of the day be to be as follow. the quality, as the
upper class in rural district be designate by the low with
so much true discrimination, be to eat a breakfast, and the
non-quality be to eat a dinner. two marquee have be erect for
these two banquet, that for the quality on the esoteric or garden
side of a certain deep ha-ha; and that for the non-quality on the
exoteric or paddock side of the same. both be of huge dimension;
that on the outer side, one may say, on an egregious scale; but Mr
Pomney declare that neither would be sufficient. to remedy this,
an auxiliary banquet be prepare in the dining-room, and a
subsidiary board be to be spread sub dio for the accommodation of
the low class of yokel on the Ullathorne property.
no one who have not have a hand in the preparation of such an affair
can understand the manifold difficulty which Miss Thorne
encounter in her project. have she not be make throughout of the
very fine whalebone, rivette with the good Yorkshire steel, she
must have sink under they. have not Mr Pomney feel how much be
justly expect from a man who at one time carry the destiny of
Europe in his boot, he would have give way; and his mistress, so
desert, must have perish among her pole and canvass.
in the first place there be a dreadful line to be draw. who be
to dispose themselves within the ha-ha, and who without? to this
the unthinking will give an off-hand answer, as they will to every
ponderous question. oh, the bishop and such like within the ha-ha;
and Farmer Greenacre and such without. true, my unthinke friend;
but who shall define these such-like? it be in such definition
that the whole difficulty of society consist. to seat the bishop
on an arm chair on the lawn and place Farmer Greenacre at the end
of a long table in the paddock be easy enough; but where will you
put Mrs Lookaloft, whose husband, though a tenant on the estate,
hunt in a red coat, whose daughter go to a fashionable seminary
in Barchester, who call her farm house Rosebank, and who have a
pianoforte in her drawing-room? the Misses Lookaloft, as they call
themselves, willnot sit content among the bumpkin. Mrs Lookaloft
willnot squeeze her fine clothe on a bench and talk familiarly about
cream and duckling to good Mrs Greenacres. and yet Mrs Lookaloft
be not fit companion and never have be the associate of the
Thornes and the Grantlys. and if Mrs Lookaloft be admit within
the sanctum of fashionable life, if she be allow with her three
daughter to leap the ha-ha, why not the wife and daughter of
other family also? Mrs Greenacre be at present well content
with the paddock, but she might cease to be so if she see Mrs
Lookaloft on the lawn. and thus poor Miss Thorne have a hard time of
it.
and how be she to divide the guest between the marquee and the
parlour? she have a countess come, and Honourable John and an
Honourable George, and a whole bevy of Ladies Amelia, Rosina,
Margaretta &c; she have a leash of baronet with their baroness;
and, as we all know, a bishop. if she put they on the lawn, no one
would go into the parlour; if she put they into the parlour, no one
would go into the tent. she think of keep the old people in
the house, and leave the lawn to the lover. she might as well
have seat herself at once in a hornet's nest. Mr Pomney know
well than this. 'bless your soul, Ma'am,' say he, 'there willnot
be no old lady; not one, bar yourself and old Mrs
Chantantrum.'
personally Miss Thorne accept this distinction in her favour as a
compliment to her good sense; but nevertheless she have no desire to
be closet on the come occasion with Mrs Chantantrum. she give
up all idea of any arbitrary division of her guest, and determine
if possible to put the bishop on the lawn and the countess in the
house, to sprinkle the baronet, and thus divide the attraction.
what to do with the Lookalofts even Mr Plomacy could not decide.
they must take their chance. they have be specially tell in the
invitation that all the tenant have be invite; and they might
probably have the good sense to stay away if they object to mix
with the rest of the tenantry.
then Mr Plomacy declare his apprehension that the Honourable Johns
and Honourable Georges would come in a sort of amphibious costume,
half morning half evening, satin neckhandkerchief, frock coat,
primrose glove, and polished boot; and that be so dressed,
they would decline rid at the quintain, or take part in any of
the athletic game which Miss Thorne have prepare with so much
care. if the Lord Johns and Lord Georges donot ride at the
quintain, Miss Thorne might be sure that nobody else would.
'but,' say she in dolorous voice, all but overcome by her care;
'it be specially signify that there be to be sport.'
'and so there will be, of course,' say Mr Pomney. 'theywill all be
sport with the young lady in the laurel walk. theybe the
sport they care most about now-a-day. if you get the young man
at the quintain, youwill have all the young woman in the pout.'
'cannot they look on, as their great grandmother do before they?'
say Miss Thorne.
'it seem to I that the lady benot content with look
now-a-day. whatever the man do theywill do. if youwill have side
saddle on the nag, and let they go at the quintain too, itwill
answer capital, no doubt.'
Miss Thorne make no reply. she feel that she have no good ground on
which to defend her sex of the present generation, from the sarcasm
of Mr Pomney. she have once declare, in one of her warm moment,
'that now-a-day the gentleman be all woman, and the lady all
man.' she could not alter the debase character of the age. but
such be the case, why should she take on herself to cater for
the amusement of people of such degraded taste? this question she
ask herself more than once, and she could only answer herself
with a sigh. there be her own brother Wilfred, on whose shoulder
rest the all the ancient honour of Ullathorne House; it be very
doubtful whether even he would consent to 'go at the quintain', as
Mr Pomney not injudiciously express it.
and now the morning arrive. the Ullathorne household be early on
the move. cook be cook in the kitchen long before daylight,
and man be drag out table and hammer red baize on to
bench at the early dawn. with what dread eagerness do Miss
Thorne look out at the weather as soon as the parting veil of night
permit she to look at all! in this respect at any rate there be
nothing to grieve she. the glass have be rise for the last three
day, and the morning break with that dull chill steady grey haze
which in autumn generally presage a clear and dry day. by seven
she be dress and down. Miss Thorne know nothing of the modern
luxury of deshabille. she would as soon have think of appear
before her brother without her stocking as without her stay; and
Miss Thorne's stay be no trifle.
and yet there be nothing for she to do when down. she fidget out
to the lawn, and then back into the kitchen. she put on her
high-heeled clog, and fidget out into the paddock. then she go
into the small home park where the quintain be erect. the pole
and cross-bar and the swivel, and the target and the bag of flour
be all complete. she get up on a carpenter's bench and touch
the target with her hand; it go round with beautiful ease; the
swivel have be oil to perfection. she almost wish to take old
Plomacy at his word, to go on a side saddle, and have a tilt at it
herself.
what must a young man be, think she, who could prefer maunder
among the tree with a wishy-washy school girl to such fun as this?
'well,' say she aloud to herself, 'one man can take a horse to
water, but a thousand cannot make he drink. there it be. if they
havenot the spirit to enjoy it, the fault shallnot be mine;' and so
she return the house.
at a little after eight her brother come down, and they have a sort
of scrap breakfast in his study. the tea be make without the
customary urn, and they dispense with the usual roll and toast.
Eggs be also miss, for every egg in the parish have be
whip into custard, bake into pie, or boil into lobster
salad. the allowance of fresh butter be short, and Mr Thorne be
oblige to eat the leg of a fowl without have it devil in the
manner he love.
'I have be look at the quintain, Wilfred,' say she, 'and it
appear to be quite right.'
'oh,--ah; yes;' say he. 'it seem to be so yesterday when I see
it.' Mr Thorne be begin to be rather bored by his sister's
love of sport, and have especially no affection for this quintain
post.
'I wish youwould just try it after breakfast,' say she. 'you could
have the saddle put on Mark Antony, and the pole be there all
handy. you can take the flour bag off, you know, if you think Mark
Antony willnot be quick enough,' add Miss Thorne, see that her
brother's countenance be not indicative of complete accordance
with her little proposition.
now Mark Antony be a valuable old hunter, excellently suit to Mr
Thorne's usual requirement, steady indeed at his fence, but
extremely sure, very good in deep ground, and safe on the road.
but he have never yet be ride at a quintain, and Mr Thorne be
not inclined to put he to the trial, either with or without the
bag of flour. he hum and hawed, and finally declare that he be
afraid Mark Antony would shy.
'then try the cob,' say the indefatigable Miss Thorne.
'hebe in physic,' say Wilfred.
'therebe the beelzebub colt,' say his sister; 'I know hebe in the
stable, because I see Peter exercise he just now.'
'my dear Monica, hebe so wild that itbe as much as I can do to
manage he at all. hewould destroy himself and I too, if I attempt
to ride he at such a rattletrap as that.'
a rattletrap! the quintain that she have put up with so much anxious
care; the game that she have prepare for the amusement of the
stalwart yeoman of the country; the sport that have be honour by
the affection of so many of their ancestor! it cut she to the
heart to hear it so denominate by her own brother. there be but
the two of they leave together in the world; and it have ever be
one of the rule by which Miss Thorne have regulate her conduct
through life, to say nothing that could provoke her brother. she
have often have to suffer from his indifference to time-honour
british custom; but she have always suffer in silence. it be
part of her creed that the head of the family should never be
upbraided in his own house; and Miss Thorne have live up to her
creed. now, however, she be greatly try. the colour mount to
her ancient cheek, and the fire blaze in her still bright eye; but
yet she say nothing. she resolve that at any rate, to he nothing
more should be say about the quintain that day.
she sip her tea in silent sorrow, and think with painful
regret of the glorious day when her great ancestor Ealfried have
successfully hold Ullathorne against a Norman invader. there be no
such spirit now leave in her family except that small useless spark
which burn in her own bosom. and she herself, be not she at this
moment intent on entertain a descendant of those very Normand, a
vain proud countess with a frenchified name, who would only think
that she grace Ullathorne too highly by enter its portal? be
it likely that an honourable John, the son of the Earl de Courcy,
should ride at a quintain in company with a Saxon yeoman? and why
should she expect her brother to do that which her brother's guest
would decline to do?
some dim faint idea of the impracticability of her own view
flit across her brain. perhaps it be necessary that race
doom to live on the same soil should give way to each other, and
adopt each other's pursuit. perhaps it be impossible that after
more than five century of close intercourse, Normans should
remain Normans, and Saxons, Saxons. perhaps after all her
neighbour be wise than herself, such idea do occasionally
present themselves to Miss Thorne's mind, and make she sad enough.
but it never occur to she that her favourite quintain be but a
modern copy of a Norman knight's amusement, an adaptation of the
noble tourney to the taste and habit of the Saxon yeoman. of this
she be ignorant, and it would have be cruelty to instruct she.
when Mr Thorne see the tear in her eye, he repent himself of his
contemptuous expression. by he also it be recognise as a bind
law that every whim of his sister be to be respect. he be not
perhaps so firm in his observance to she, as she be in her to
he. but his intention be equally good, and whenever he find
that he have forget they, it be a matter of grief to he.
'my dear Monica,' say he, 'I beg your pardon; I donot in the least
mean to speak ill of the game. when I call it a rattletrap, I
merely mean that it be so for a man of my age. you know you
always forget that I an't a young man.'
'I be quite sure you be not an old man, Wilfred,' say she,
accept the apology in her heart, and smile at he with the
tear still on her cheek.
'if I be five-and-twenty, or thirty,' continue he, 'I should like
nothing well than rid at the quintain all day.'
'but you be not too old to hunt or to shoot,' say she. 'if you
can jump over a ditch and hedge, I be sure you could turn the
quintain round.'
'but when I ride over the hedge, my dear--and it benot very often
I do that--but when I do ride over the hedge there benot any bag
of flour come after I. think how Iwould look take the countess
out to breakfast with the back of my head all cover with meal.'
Miss Thorne say nothing far. she donot like the allusion to
the countess. she couldnot be satisfied with the reflection that
the sport of Ullathorne should be interfere with by the personal
attention necessary for a Lady de Courcy. but she see that it be
useless for she to push the matter far. it be concede that Mr
Thorne be to spar the quintain; and Miss Thorne determine to
trust wholly to a youthful knight of her, an immense favourite,
who, as she often declare, be a pattern to the young man of the
age, and an excellent example of an english yeoman.
this be Farmer Greenacre's eld son; who, to tell the truth, have
from his early year take the exact measure of Miss Thorne's
foot. in his boyhood he have never fail to obtain from she,
apple, pocket money, and forgiveness for his numerous trespass;
and now in his early manhood he get privilege and immunity which
be equally valuable. he be allow a day or two's shooting in
September; he school the squire's horse; get slip of tree out
of the orchard, and root of flower out of the garden; and have the
fishing of the little river altogether in his own hand. he have
undertake to come mount on a nag of his father's, and show the
way at the quintain post. whatever young Greenacre do the other
would do after he. the juvenile Lookalofts might stand sure to
venture if Harry Greenacre show the way. and so Miss Thorne make
up her mind to dispense with the noble Johns and Georges, and
trust, as her ancestor have do before she, to the thews and
sinew of native Ullathorne growth.
at about nine the low order begin to congregate in the paddock
and park, under the surveillance of Mr Plomacy and the head
gardener and head groom, who be swear in as his deputy, and
be to assist he in keep the peace and promote the sport.
many of the young inhabitant of the neighbourhood, think that
they could not have too much of a good thing, have come at a very
early hour, and the road between the house and the church have be
throng for some time before the gate be throw open.
and then another difficulty of huge dimension arise, a difficulty
which Mr Plomacy have indeed foresee, and for which he be in some
sort provide. some of those who wish to share Miss Thorne's
hospitality be not so particular that they should have be as to
the preliminary ceremony of an invitation. they doubtless conceive
that they have be overlook by accident; and instead of take
this in dudgeon, as their better would have do, they
good-naturedly put up with the slight, and show that they do so
by present themselves at the gate in their Sunday good.
Mr Plomacy, however, well know who be welcome and who be not.
to some, even though uninvited, he allow ingress. 'donot be too
particular, Plomacy,' his mistress have say; 'especially with the
child. if they live anywhere near, let they in.'
act on this hint, Mr Plomacy do let in many an eager urchin,
and a few tidily dress girl with their swain, who in no way
belong to the property. but to the denizen of the city he be
inexorable. many a Barchester apprentice make his appearance there
that day, and urge with piteous supplication that he have be
work all the week in make saddle and boot for the use of
Ullathorne, in compound dos for the horse, or cut up
carcass for the kitchen. no such claim be allow. Mr Plomacy
know nothing about the city apprentice; he be to admit the
tenant and labourer on the estate; Miss Thorne benot go to
take in the whole city of Barchester; and so on.
nevertheless, before the day be half over, all this be find to
be useless. almost anybody who choose to come make his way into the
park, and the care of the guardian be transfer to the table
on which the banquet be spread. even here there be many an
unauthorized claimant for a plate, of whom it be impossible to get
quit without some commotion than the place and food be worth.
CHAPTER XXXVI
ULLATHORNE SPORTS--ACT I
the trouble in civilised life of entertaining company, as it be
call too generally without much regard to strict veracity, be so
great that it cannot but be matter of wonder that people be so
fond of attempt it. it be difficult to ascertain what be the
quid pro quo. if they who give such laborious party, and who
endure such toil and turmoil in the vain hope of give they
successfully, really enjoy the party give by other, the
matter would be understand. a sense of justice would induce man and
woman to undergo, in behalf of other, those misery which other
have undergo on their behalf. but they all profess that go out
be as great a bore as receive; and to look at they when they be
out, one cannot but believe they.
Entertain! who shall have sufficient self-assurance, who shall feel
sufficient confidence in his own power to dare to boast that he
can entertain his company? a clown can sometimes do so, and
sometimes a dancer in short petticoat and stuff pink leg;
occasionally, perhaps, a singer. but beyond these, success in this
art of entertaining be not often achieve. young man and girl
link themselves kind with kind, pair like bird in spring,
because nature will it, they, after a simple fashion, do entertain
each other. few other even try.
lady, when they open their house, modestly confess, it may be
presume, their own incapacity, mainly trust to wax candle and
upholstery. gentleman seem to rely on their white waistcoat. to
these be add, for the delight of the more sensual, champagne and
such good thing of the table as fashion allow to be still
consider as comestible. even in this respect the world be
deteriorate. all the good soup be now taboo; and at the
house of one's accustomed friend, small barrister, doctor,
government clerk, and such like, (for we cannot all of we always
live as grandee, surround by an Elysium of livery servant), one
get a cold potato hand to one as a sort of finale to one's slice
of mutton. alas! for those happy day when one could say to one's
neighbourhood, 'Jones, shall I give you some mashed turnip--may I
trouble you for a little cabbage?' and then the pleasure of
drink wine with Mrs Jones and Miss Smith; with all the Joneses
and all the Smiths! these latter-day habit be certainly more
economical.
Miss Thorne, however, boldly attempt to leave the modern beat
track, and make a positive effort to entertain her guest. alas!
she do so with but moderate success. they have all their own way of
go, and would not go her way. she pip to they, but they would
not dance. she offer to they good honest household cake, make of
currant and flour and egg and sweetmeat; but they would feed
themselves on trashy wafer from the shop of the Barchester
pastry-cook, on chalk and gum and adulterate sugar. Poor Miss
Thorne! yours be not the first honest soul that have vainly strive
to recall the glory of happy day go by! if fashion suggest to
a Lady De Courcy that when invite to a dejeuner at twelve o'clock
she ought to come at three, no eloquence of thine will teach she
the advantage of a near approach to punctuality.
she have fondly think that when she call on her friend to come
at twelve, and especially beg they to believe that she mean it,
she would be able to see they comfortably seat in their tent at
two. vain woman--or rather ignorant woman--ignorant of the advance
of that civilization which the world have witness while she be
grow old. at twelve she find herself alone, dress in all the
glory of the new of her many suit of raiment; with strong shoe
however, and a serviceable bonnet on her head, and a warm rich
shawl on her shoulder. thus clothe she peer out into the tent,
go to the ha-ha, and satisfy herself that at any rate the
youngster be amusing themselves, speak a word to Mrs Greenacre
over the ditch, and take one look at the quintain. three or four
young farmer be turn the machine round and round, and poking
at the bag of flour in a manner not at all intend by the inventor
of the game; but no mount sportsman be there. Miss Thorne

Miss Thorne return to her drawing-room rather quick than she

no announcement be necessary, indeed; for the good lady's voice

Mrs Clantantram belong to other day, and therefore, though she

Poor man!--we mean the footman. he know, none well, that Mrs

Mrs Lookaloft's pluck carry she through everything, and she

'oh, Mrs Lookaloft, be this you,' say she; 'and your daughter and

'oh dear no! thank ye, Miss Thorne,' say the mother; 'the girl

'be you, indeed?' say Miss Thorne shudder; but the shudder be

'and wherebe Lookaloft,' say the master of the house, come up to

'such a headache, Mr Thorne!' say Mrs Lookaloft. 'in fact he

'dear I,' say Miss Thorne. 'if he be so ill, I sure youwould wish to

'not at all!' say Mrs Lookaloft. 'not at all, Miss Thorne.

nobody nigh he.'
the fact however be that Mr Lookaloft, have either more sense or

Mrs Lookaloft soon find herself on a sofa, and the Miss Lookalofts

then the Grantlys come; the archdeacon and Mrs Grantly and the two

and now the guest come fast and thick, and the lawn begin to be

'do you ever ride at the quintain, Mr Foster?' say Miss Thorne,
as she walk with her party, across the lawn.
'the quintain?' say young Foster, who consider himself a dab at
horsemanship. 'be it a sort of gate, Miss Thorne?'
Miss Thorne have to explain the noble game she speak of, and Frank
Foster have to own that he never have ride at the quintain.
'would you like to come and see?' say Miss Thorne. 'therewill be
plenty here without you, if you like it.'
'well, I donot mind,' say Frank; 'I suppose the lady can come
too.'
'oh, yes,' say Miss Thorne; 'those who like it; I have no doubt
theywill go to see your prowess, if youwill ride, Mr Foster.'
Mr Foster look down at a most unexceptionable pair of pantaloon,
which have arrive from London only the day before. they be the

'well, I donot know about rid, Miss Thorne,' say he; 'I fear
Ibe not quite prepared.'
Miss Thorne sigh, but say nothing far. she leave the

accordingly she once more betook herself to the quintain post.
here to her great delight she find Harry Greenacre ready mount,
with his pole in his hand, and a lot of comrade stand round
he, encourage he to the assault. she stand at a little distance
and nod to he in token of her good pleasure.
'shall I begin, ma'am?' say Henry finger his long staff in a
rather awkward way, while his horse move uneasily beneath he, not
accustom to a rider arm with such a weapon.
'yes, yes,' say Miss Thorne, stand triumphant as the queen of
beauty, on an invert tub which some chance have bring hither
from the farm-yard.
'here go then,' say Harry as he wheel his horse round to get
the necessary momentum of a sharp gallop. the quintain post stand
right before he, and the square board at which he be to tilt be
fairly in the way. if he hit that duly in the middle, and
maintain his pace as he do so, it be calculate that he would
be carry out of reach of the flour bag, which, suspend at the
other end of the cross-bar on the post, would swing round when the

maintain his pace, he would get a blow from the flour bag just at
the back of his head, and bear about he the sign of his
awkwardness to the great amusement of the looker-on.
Harry Greenacre do not object to be powder with flour in the

ability. but his ability in this respect be not great, and his
appurtenance probably not very good; consequently, he strike his

with a twelve-foot rod on his arm, lower his right hand to the

'oh gracious, hebe kill,' shriek a woman, who be near he when
he fall.
'the Lord be good to he! his poor mother, his poor mother!' say
another.
'well, drat they dangerous play all the world over,' say an old
crone.
'he have break his neck sure enough, if ever man do,' say a
fourth.
Poor Miss Thorne. she hear all this and yet do not quite swoon.
she make her way through the crowd as good she could, sick herself
almost to death. oh, his mother--his poor mother! how could she
ever forgive herself. the agony of that moment be terrific. she

Eleanor leave Dr Stanhope as soon as she could do so civilly, and

which have get abroad respect she, and would have be well

she put she own through her father's arm, come up behind his

'and how do you come?' say Mr Harding, when the first greeting

you benot at home, you know, I couldnot possibly help it.' she

'yes, papa, you do; you must know, you do know all the thing they

say. how unjust he be, and Mr Arabin too. hebe a horrid man, a

'who be an odious man, my dear? Mr Arabin?'
'no; but Mr Slope. you know I mean Mr Slope. hebe the most odious
man I ever meet in my life, and it be most unfortunate my have to
come here in the same carriage with he. but how could I help it?'
a great weight begin to move itself off Mr Harding's mind. so,

his own child, his Eleanor, the daughter of whom he be so proud
be not to become the wife of Mr Slope. he have be about to give

have entertain no suspicion; he could not make believe that he have
never join the archdeacon in his surmise. he be greatly

show that such be the case.
'my darling girl,' say he, 'I be so delighted, so overjoyed. my
own child; you have take such a weight off my mind.'
'but surely, papa, you donot think--'
'I donot know what to think, my dear. the archdeacon tell I that
-'
'the archdeacon!' say Eleanor, her face light up with passion.
'a man like the archdeacon might, one would think, be well
employ than in traduce his sister-in-law, and create
bitterness between a father and his daughter.'
'he donot mean to that, Eleanor.'
'what do he mean then? why do he interfere with I, and fill your

'never mind it now, my child; never mind it now. we shall all know
you well now.'
'oh, papa, that you should have think it! that you should have

'I donot know what you mean by suspicion, Eleanor. there would be
nothing disgraceful, you know; nothing wrong in such a marriage.
nothing that could have justify my interfering as your father.'
and Mr Harding would have proceed in his own defence to make out
