he be so indifferent as to the loss of the hospital, so thankful
for the recovery of his daughter, that, strong as be the ground
for Eleanor's anger, she could not find it in her heart to be long
angry with he.
'dear papa,' she say, hang closely to his arm, 'never suspect
I again: promise I that you never will. whatever I do, you may be
sure I shall tell you first; you may be sure I shall consult you.'
and Mr Harding do promise, and own his sin, and promise again.
and so, while he promise amendment and she utter forgiveness,
they return together to the drawing-room window.
and what have Eleanor mean when she declare that whatever she do,
she would tell her father first? what be she think of do?
so end the first act of the melodrama which Eleanor be call on
to perform this day at Ullathorne.
CHAPTER XXXVII
the signora NERONI, the COUNTESS DE COURCY, and MRS PROUDIE meet
each other AT ULLATHORNE
and now there be new arrival. just as Eleanor reach the
drawing-room the signora be be wheel into it. she have be
bring out of the carriage into the dining-room and there place
on a sofa, and be now in the act of enter the other room, by
the joint aid of her brother and sister, Mr Arabin, and two
servant in livery. she be all in her glory, and look so
pathetically happy, so full of affliction and grace, be so
beautiful, so pitiable, and so charming, that it be almost
impossible not to be glad she be there.
Miss Thorne be unaffectedly glad to welcome she. in fact, the
signora be a sort of lion; and though there be no drop of the
Leohunter blood in Miss Thorne's vein, she nevertheless do like
to see attractive people at her house.
the signora be attractive, and on her first settlement in the
dining-room she have whisper two or three soft feminine word into
Miss Thorne's ear, which, at the moment, have quite touch that
lady's heart.
'oh, Miss Thorne; where be Miss Thorne?' she say, as soon as she
attendant have place she in her position just before one of the
window, from whence she could see all that be go on upon the
lawn; 'how be I to thank you for permit a creature like I to
be here? but if you know the pleasure you give I, I be sure you
would excuse the trouble I bring with I.' and as she speak she
squeeze the spinster's little hand between her own.
'we be delighted to see you here,' say Miss Thorne; 'you give we
no trouble at all, and we think it a great favour confer by you
to come and see we; donot we, Wilfred?'
'a very great favour indeed,' say Mr Thorne, with a gallant bow,
but of somewhat less cordial welcome than that concede by his
sister. Mr Thorne have learn perhaps more of the antecedent of
his guest than his sister have do, and not as yet undergo the
power of the signora's charm.
but while the mother of the last of the Neros be thus in he full
splendour, with crowd of people gaze at she and the elite of the
company stand round her couch, her glory be pal by the
arrival of the Countess De Courcy. Miss Thorne have now be wait
three hour for the countess, and could not therefore but show very
evident gratification when the arrival at last take place. she and
her brother of course go off to welcome the title grandee, and
with they, alas, go many of the signora's admirer.
'oh, Mr Thorne,' say the countess, while the act of be disrobe
of her fur cloak, and re-rob in her gauze shawl, 'what dreadful
road you have; perfectly frightful.'
it happen that Mr Thorne be way-warden for the district, and not
like the attack, begin to excuse his road.
'oh yes, indeed they be,' say the countess, not mind he in
the least, 'perfectly dreadful; be they not, Margaretta? why, dear
Miss Thorne, we leave Courcy Castle just at eleven; it be only just
past eleven, be it not, John? and--'
'just past one, I think you mean,' say the Honourable John,
turn from the group and eye the signora through his glass.
the signora give he back his own, as the saying be, and more with
it; so that the young nobleman be force to avert his glance, and
drop his glass.
'I say, Thorne,' whisper he, 'who the deuce be that on the sofa?'
'Dr Stanhope's daughter,' whisper back Mr Thorne. 'Signora Neroni
she call herself.'
'Whew-ew-ew!' whistle the Honourable John. 'the devil she be! I
have hear no end of story about that filly. you must positively
introduce I, Thorne; you positively must.'
Mr Thorne who be respectability itself, do not quite like have
a guest about whom the Honourable John De Courcy have hear no end
of story; but he couldnot help himself. he merely resolve that
before he go to bed he would let his sister know somewhat of the
history of the lady she be so willing to welcome. the innocence of
Miss Thorne, at her time of life, be perfectly charming; but even
innocence may be dangerous.
'John may say what he like,' continue the countess, urge she
excuse on Miss Thorne; 'I be sure we be past the castle gate
before twelve, benot we, Margaretta?'
'upon my word, I donot know,' say the Lady Margaretta, 'for I be
half asleep. but I do know that I be call sometime in the middle
of the night, and be dress myself before daylight.'
wise people, when they be in the wrong, always put themselves
right by find fault with the people against whom they have
sin. Lady De Courcy be a wise woman; and therefore, have
treat Miss Thorne very badly by stay away till three o'clock,
she assume the offensive and attack Mr Thorne's road. her
daughter, not less wise, attack Miss Thorne's early hour. the
art of do this be among the most precious of those usually
cultivate by person who know how to live. there be no
withstand it. who can go systematically to work, and have do
battle with the primary accusation and settle that, then bring
forward a counter-charge and support that also? life be not long
enough for such labour. a man in the right rely easily on his
rectitude, and therefore go about unarmed. his very strength be
his weakness; his very weakness be his strength. the one be never
prepare for combat, the other be always ready. therefore it be
that in this world the man that be in the wrong almost invariably
conquer the man that be in the right, and invariably despise he.
a man must be an idiot or else an angel, who, after the age of
forty shall attempt to be just to his neighbour. many like the
Lady Margaretta have learn their lesson at a much early age. but
this of course depend on the school in which they have be
teach.
Poor Miss Thorne be altogether overcome. she know very well that
she have be ill-treat, and yet she find herself make
apology to Lady De Courcy. to do her ladyship justice, she
receive they very graciously, and allow herself with her train
of daughter to be lead towards the lawn.
there be two window in the drawing-room wide open for the
countess to pass through; but she see that there be a woman on the
sofa, at the third window, and that that woman have, as it be, a
following attach to she. her ladyship therefore determine to
investigate the woman. the De Courcys be hereditarily short
sight, and have be so for thirty century at least. so Lady De
Courcy, who, when she enter the family have adopt the family
habit, do as her son have do before she, and take her glass to
investigate the Signora Neroni, press in among the gentleman who
surround the couch, and bow slightly to those whom she choose to
honour by her acquaintance.
in order to get to the window she have to pass close to the front of
the couch, and as she do so she star hard at the occupant. the
occupant in return star hard at the countess. the countess who
since her countess-ship commence have be accustom to see all
eye, not royal, ducal, or marquesal, fall down before her own,
pause as she go on, raise her eyebrow, and star even hard
than before. but she have now to do with one who care little for
countess. it be, one may say, impossible for mortal man or woman
to abash Madeline Neroni. she open her large bright lustrous eye
wider and wide, till she seem to be all eye.
she gaze up into the lady's face, not as though she do it with an
effort, but as if she delight in do it. she use no glass to
assist her effrontery, and need none. the faint possible smile
of derision play round her mouth, and her nostril be slightly
dilate, as if in sure anticipation of her triumph. and it be
sure. the Countess De Courcy, in spite of her thirty century and
De Courcy castle, and the fact that Lord De Courcy be grand master
of the pony to the Prince of Wales, have not a chance with she.
at first the little circlet of gold waver in the countess's hand,
then the hand shake, then the circlet fall, the countess's head
toss itself into the air, and the countess's foot shamble out to
the lawn. she do not however go so fast but what she hear the
signora's voice, asking--
'who on earth be that woman, Mr Slope?'
'that be Lady De Courcy.'
'oh, ah. I might have suppose so. ha, ha, ha. well, thatbe as good
as a play.'
it be as good as a play to any there who have eye to observe it,
and wit to comment on what they observe.
but the Lady De Courcy soon find a congenial spirit on the lawn.
there she encounter Mrs Proudie, and as Mrs Proudie be not only
the wife of a bishop, but be also the cousin of an earl, Lady De
Courcy consider she to be the fit companion she be likely to
meet in that assemblage. they be accordingly delighted to see
each other. Mrs Proudie by no mean despise a countess, and as
this countess live in the county and within a sort of extensive
visit distance of Barchester, she be glad to have this
opportunity of ingratiate herself.
'my dear Lady De Courcy, I be so delighted,' say she, look as
little grim as it be in her nature to do so. 'I hardly expect to
see you here. it be such a distance, and then you know, such a
crowd.'
'and such road, Mrs Proudie! I really wonder how the people ever
get about. but I donot suppose they ever do.'
'well, I really donot know; but I suppose not. the Thorne donot, I

know. she always make I think of the Esquimaux and the Indians.
benot her dress quite delightful?'
'delightful,' say Mrs Proudie; 'I wonder now whether she paint.
do you ever see such colour?'
'oh, of course,' say Lady De Courcy; 'that be, I have no doubt she
do. but, Mrs Proudie, who be that woman on the sofa by the

countess lead she to a spot where she could plainly see the
signora's well-remember face and figure.
she do not however do so without be equally well see by the
signora. 'look, look,' say that lady to Mr Slope, who be still

but the enemy know well. they could gain nothing be contact with

'shebe that horrid italian woman, Lady De Courcy; you must have

'oh--h--h--h!' exclaim the countess.
'I be sure you have hear of she,' continue Mrs Proudie. 'I donot

Neroni be still alive. I believe she do marry such a man abroad,
but I do not at all know who or what he be.'
'ah--h--h--h!' say the countess, shake her head with much

about she. George hear about she in Rome.'
'shebe an abominable woman at any rate,' say Mrs Proudie.
'insufferable,' say the countess.

'be it?' say the delighted countess.
'insufferable,' say the prelatess.

she be the most insolent creature I have ever put my eye on.'
'indeed she be,' say Lady De Courcy.

'dear I!' say the countess, become again excited, happy, and

'yes, yes.'
'she have absolutely ruin that man. the bishop, or I should rather

'but you say she have only get one leg!'

'no,' say she, as at her instance he get up to leave she, and

at home that I really can live and enjoy myself. my day of go
out, day such as these, be rare indeed. come and see I at home,
Mr Thorne, and then I will not bid you to leave I.'
it be, we believe, common with young man of five and twenty to look
on their senior--on man of, say, double their own age--as so many
stock and stone--stock and stone, that is, in regard to
feminine beauty. there never be a great mistake. woman, indeed,
generally know well; but on this subject man of one age be
thoroughly ignorant of what be the very nature of mankind of other
age. no experience of what go on in the world, no reading of
history, no observation of life, have any effect in teach the
truth. man of fifty donot dance mazurkas, be generally too fat
and wheezy; nor do they sit for the hour together on river bank at
their mistress' foot, be somewhat afraid of rheumatism. but
for real true love, love at first sight, love to devotion, love
that rob a man of his sleep, love that 'will gaze an eagle blind,'
love that 'will hear the low sound when the suspicious tread of
theft be stop,' love that be 'like a Hercules still climb
tree in the Hesperides,'--we believe this good age be from
forty-five to seventy; up to that, man be generally give to mere
flirting.
at the present moment Mr Thorne, aetat. fifty, be over head and

nata Stanhope.
nevertheless he be sufficiently master of himself to offer his arm

such have be Miss Thorne's order, as she have succeed in

and now the eating and drinking begin in earnest. Dr Grantly, to

Dr Gwynne get a baronet's wife, and Mrs Grantly fall to the lot of

CHAPTER XXXVIII
the BISHOP sit down to BREAKFAST, and the DEAN die

''twas merry in the hall when the beard wag all;' and the

but there be one sad man among they that day. Mr Arabin's beard

he have pass the previous night alone at his new parsonage, and it

if--and then Mr Arabin poke his fire most unnecessarily, speak

but have know in what light he regard she. why have she not

he be in this state of confused uncertainty, hope, and doubt, when

'I wonder,' say Dr Grantly, 'if it be true that Mr Slope and Mrs

Mr Arabin have nothing for it but to bear his testimony to the

it be impossible to say how the knowledge have be acquire, but

such a sense can work with such acuteness. the organ by which woman

sens the signora be aware that Mr Arabin love Eleanor Bold; and

the work be half do before she come to Ullathorne, and when

bagging for family us.
but Mr Arabin be game of another sort. the signora be herself
possess of quite sufficient intelligence to know that Mr Arabin

'why benot you shoot with bow and arrow, Mr Arabin?' say

talk with young lady in shady bower, or turn your talent
to account in some way? what be a bachelor like you ask here

you, I should be ashamed to be so idle.'
Mr Arabin murmur some sort of answer. though he wish to be

it be not difficult for so acute an observer as Madeline Neroni to

Mr Arabin wince visibly before her attack, and she know at once
that he be jealous of Mr Slope.

passive. he work, you think. he like woman, you despise they. he

different reason. he love to be praise, you very foolishly abhor

a comfortable income, and a reputation for sanctimony. you will
also gain your.'
'well, and what will they be?' say Mr Arabin, who know that he be

'the heart of some woman whom you will be too austere to own that

'rich reward,' say he; 'but of little worth if they be to be so
treat.'
'oh, you be not to look for such success as await Mr Slope. he be
bear to be a successful man. he suggest to himself an object, and
then start for it with eager intention. nothing will deter he
from his pursuit. he will have no scruple, no fear, no
hesitation. his desire be to be a bishop with a rise family, the
wife will come first, and in due time the apron. you will see all

'well, and what then?'
'then you will begin to wish that you have do the same.'
Mr Arabin look placidly out at the lawn, and rest his shoulder

cord with their fine edge, while those not so highly temper
sever the every-day gordian knot of the world's struggle, and win

not do for this world's work, Mr Arabin.'
who be this woman that thus read the secret of his heart, and

the gentleman blush crimson, but the lady do not blush at all. a

a charming woman. your look seem to say so; and by her look I
should say that she be jealous of I. come, Mr Arabin, confide in
I, and if it be so, Iwill do all in my power to make up the match.'
it be needless to say that the signora be not very sincere in her
offer. she be never sincere on such subject. she never expect
other to be so, nor do she expect other to think she so. such
matter be her plaything, her billiard table, her hound and
hunter, her waltz and polkas, her picnic and summer-day
excursion. she have little else to amuse she, and therefore play
at love-making in all its form. she be now play at it with Mr
Arabin, and do not at all expect the earnestness and truth of his
answer.
'all in your power would be nothing,' say he; 'for Mrs Bold be, I
imagine, already engage to another.'
'then you own the impeachment yourself.'
'you cross-question I rather unfairly,' he reply, 'and I do not
know why I answer you at all. Mrs Bold be a very beautiful woman,
and as intelligent as beautiful. it be impossible to know she
without admire she.'
'so you think the widow a very beautiful woman?'
'indeed I do.'
'and one that would grace the parsonage at St Ewold's.'
'one that would grace any man's house.'
'and you really have the effrontery to tell I this,' say she; 'to
tell I, who, as you very well know, set up to be a beauty myself,
and who be at this very moment take such an interest in your
affair, you really have the effrontery to tell I that Mrs Bold be
the most beautiful woman you know.'
'I do not say so,' say Mr Arabin; 'you be more beautiful--'
'ah, come now, that be something like. I think you would not be
so unfeeling.'
'you be more beautiful, perhaps more clever.'
'thank you, thank you, Mr Arabin. I know that you and I should be
friend.'
'but--'
'not a word far. I will not hear a word far. if you talk
till midnight, you cannot improve what you have say.'
'but Madame Neroni, Mrs bold--'
'I will not hear a word about Mrs Bold. dread thought of
strychnine do pass across my brain, but she be welcome to the
second place.'
'her place--'
'I willnot hear anything about she or her place. I be satisfied and
that be enough. but, Mr Arabin, I be die with hunger; beautiful
and clever as I be, you know I cannot go to my food, and yet you do
not bring it to I.'
this at any rate be so true as to make it unnecessary that Mr
Arabin should not act upon it, and he accordingly go into the
dining-room and supply the signora's want.
'and yourself,' say she.
'oh,' say he, 'I be not hungry; I never eat at this hour.'
'come, come, Mr Arabin, donot let love interfere with your
appetite. it never do with mine. give I half a glass more
champagne, and then go to the table. Mrs Bold will do I an injury
if you stay talk to I any long.'
Mr Arabin do as he be bid. he take her plate and glass from she,
and go into the dining-room, help himself to a sandwich from
the crowded table and begin munch it in a corner.
as he be do so, Miss Thorne, who have hardly sit down for a
moment, come into the room, and see he stand, be greatly
distressed.
'oh, my dear Mr Arabin,' say she, 'have you never sit down yet? I
be so distressed. you of all man too.'
Mr Arabin assure she that he have only just come into the room.
'that be the very reason why you should lose no more time. come
Iwill make room for you. thank'ee my dear,' she say, see that
Mrs Bold be make an attempt to move from her chair, 'but I would
not for the world see you stir, for all the lady would think it
necessary to follow. but, perhaps, if Mr Stanhope have do--just
for a minute, Mr Stanhope--till I can get another chair.'
and so Bertie have to rise to make way for his rival. this he do,
as he do everything, with an air of good-humour pleasantry,
which make it impossible for Mr Arabin to refuse the proffer
seat.
'his bishopric let another take,' say Bertie; the quotation be
certainly not very appropriate, either for the occasion, or the
person speak to. 'I have eat and be satisfied; Mr Arabin, pray
take my chair. I wish for your sake, it really be a bishop's
seat.'
Mr Arabin do sit down, and as he do so, Mrs Bold get up as though
to follow her neighbour.
'pray, pray donot move,' say Miss Thorne, almost force eleanor
back into her chair. 'Mr Stanhope be not go to leave we. he will
stand behind you like a true knight as he be. and now I think of
it, Mr Arabin, let I introduce you to Mr Slope. Mr Slope, Mr
Arabin.' and the two gentleman bow stiffly to each other across
the lady they both intend to marry, while the other gentleman who
also intend to marry she stand behind, watch they.
the two have never meet each other before, and the present be
certainly not a good opportunity for much cordial conversation,
even if cordial conversation between they have be possible. as it
be, the whole four who form the party seem as though their
tongue be tie. Mr Slope, who be wide awake to what he hop
be his come opportunity, be not much concerned in the interest
of the moment. his wish be to see Eleanor move, that he might
pursue she. Bertie be not exactly in the same frame of mind; the
evil of the day be near enough; there be no reason why he should
precipitate it. he have make up his mind to marry Eleanor Bold if he
could, and be resolve to-day to take the first preliminary step
towards do so. but there be time enough before he. he be not
go to make an offer of marriage over the table-cloth. have
thus good-naturedly make way for Mr Arabin, he be willing also to
let he talk to the future Mrs Stanhope as long as they remain in
their present position.
Mr Arabin bow to Mr Slope, begin eat his food, without say
a word far. he be full of thought, and though he eat he do
so unconsciously.
but poor Eleanor be the most to be pity. the only friend on whom
she think she could rely, be Bertie Stanhope, and he, it seem,
be determined to desert she. Mr Arabin do not attempt to address
she. she say a few word in reply to some remark from Mr Slope,
and then feel the situation too much for she, start from her
chair in spite of Miss Thorne, and hurry from the room. Mr Slope
follow she, and young Stanhope lose the occasion.
Madame Neroni, when she be leave alone, could not help ponder
much on the singular interview she have have with this singular man.
not a word that she have speak to he have be intend by she to
be receive as true, and yet he have answer she in the very spirit
of truth. he have do so, and she have be aware that he have do
so. she have worm from he his secret; and he, debar as it
would seem from man's usual privilege of lying, have innocently lay
bare his whole soul to she. he love Eleanor Bold, but Eleanor be
not in his eye so beautiful as herself. he would fain have eleanor
for his wife, but yet he have acknowledge that she be the less
gifted of the two. the man have literally be unable to falsify his
thought when question, and have be compel to be true malgre
lui, even when truth must have be disagreeable to he.
this teacher of man, this Oxford pundit, this double-distil
quintessence of university perfection, this writer of religious
treatise, this speaker of ecclesiastical speech, have be like a
little child in her hand; she have turn he inside out, and read
his very heart as she might have do that of a young girl. she
could not but despise he for his facile openness, and yet she
like he too. it be a novelty to she, a new trait in a man's
character. she feel also that she could never so completely make a
fool of he as she do of the Slopes and the Thornes. she feel that
she could never induce Mr Arabin to make protestation to she that
be not true, or to listen to nonsense that be mere nonsense.
it be quite clear that Mr Arabin be heartily in love with Mrs
Bold, and the signora, with very unwonted good nature, begin to
turn it over in her mind whether she could not do he a good turn.
of course Bertie be to have the first chance. it be an understood
family arrangement that her brother be, if possible, to marry the
widow Bold. Madeline know too well the necessity and what be due
to her sister to interfere with so excellent a plan, as long as it
might be feasible. but she have strong suspicion that it be not
feasible. she do not think it likely that Mrs Bold would accept a
man in her brother's position, and she have frequently say so to
Charlotte. she be inclined to believe that Mr Slope have more
chance of success; and with she it would be a labour of love to rob
Mr Slope of his wife.
and so the signora resolve, should Bertie fail, to do a
good-natured act for once in her life, and give up Mr Arabin to the
woman whom he love.
CHAPTER XXXIX
THE LOOKALOFTS and the GREENACRES
on the whole, Miss Thorne's provision for the amusement and feeding
of the outer class in the exoteric paddock be not unsuccessful.
two little drawback to the general happiness do take place, but
they be of a temporary nature, and apparent rather than real. the
first be the downfall of young Harry Greenacre, and the other be
the uprise of Mrs Lookaloft and her family.
as to the quintain, it become more popular among the boy on foot,
than it would ever have be among the man on horseback, even have
young Greenacre be more successful. it be twirl round and
round till it be nearly twist out of the ground; and the bag of
flour be use with great gusto in powder the back and head of
all who could be coax within the vicinity.
of course it be report all throughout the assemblage that Harry
be dead, and there be a pathetic scene between he and his mother
when it be find that he have escape scatheless from the fall. a
good deal of beer be drunk on the occasion, and the quintain be
'dratte' and 'bother', and very generally anathematise by all
the mother who have young son likely to be place in similar
jeopardy. but the affair of Mrs Lookaloft be of a more serious
nature.
'I do tell 'ee plainly,--face to face--she be there in madam's
drawing-room; herself and Gussy, and they two walloping gal,
dress up to their very eyes.' this be say by a very positive,
very indignant, and very fat farmer's wife, who be sit on the
end of a bench lean on the handle of a huge cotton umbrella.
'but you donot zee she, Dame Guffern?' say Mrs Greenacres, whom
this information, join to the recent peril undergone by her son,
almost overpowered. Mr Greenacres hold just as much land as Mr
Lookaloft, pay his rent quite as punctually, and his opinion in
the vestry-room be reckon to be every whit as good. Mrs
Lookaloft's rise in the world have be wormwood to Mrs Greenacre.
she have not taste herself for the sort of finery which convert
Barleystubb farm into Rosebank, and which have occasionally grace
Mr Lookaloft's letter with the dignity of esquirehood. she have no
wish to convert her own homeland into Violet Villa, or to see she
goodman go about with a new-fangled handle to his name. but it be
a mortal injury to she that Mrs Lookaloft should be successful in
her hunt after such honour. she have abuse and ridicule Mrs
Lookaloft to the extent of her little power. she have push against
she go out of church, and have excuse herself with all the
easiness of equality. 'ah, dame, I axis pardon; but you be grow so
mortal stout these time.' she have inquire with apparent cordiality
of Mr Lookaloft after 'the woman that own he,' and have, as she
think, be on the whole able to hold her own pretty well against
her aspire neighbour. now, however, she find herself distinctly
put into a separate and inferior class. Mrs Lookaloft be ask
into the Ullathorne drawing-room, merely because she call she
house Rosebank, and have talk over her husband into buy piano
and silk dress instead of put his money by to stock farm for
his son.
Mrs Greenacre, much as she reverence Miss Thorne, and highly as
she respect her husband's landlord, could not but look on this as
an act of injustice do to she and her. Hitherto the Lookalofts
have never be recognise as be of a different class from the
greenacre. their pretension be all self-pretension, their
finery be all pay for by themselves and not grant to they by
other. the local sovereign of the vicinity, the district
fountain of honour, have hitherto confer on they the stamp of no
rank. Hitherto their crinoline petticoat, late hour, and mincing
gait have be a fair subject of Mrs Greenacre's raillery, and this
raillery have be a safety valve for her envy. now, however, and
from henceforward, the case would be very different. now the
Lookalofts would boast that their aspiration have be sanction
by the gentry of the country; now they would declare with some show
of truth that their claim to peculiar consideration have be
recognise. they have sit as equal guest in the presence of bishop
and baronet; they have be curtsey to by Miss Thorne on her own
drawing-room carpet; they be about to sit down to table in
company with a live countess! Bab Lookaloft, as she have always be
call by the young Greenacres in the day of their juvenile
equality, might possibly sit next to the Honourable George, and
that wretche Gussy might be permit to hand a custard to the
Lady Margaretta De Courcy.
the fruition of these honour, or such of they as fall to the lot
of the envy family, be not such as should have cause much envy.
the attention pay to the Lookalofts by the De Courcys be very
limited, and the amount of society be hardly in itself a
recompense for the dull monotony of their day. but of what they
endure Mrs Greenacre take no account; she think only of what she
consider they must enjoy, and of the dreadfully exalt tone of
living which would be manifest by the Rosebank family, as the
consequence of their present distinction.
'but do 'ee zee them there, dame, do 'ee zee them then with your
own eye?' ask poor Mrs Greenacre, still hop that there might
be some ground for doubt.
'and how could I do that, unless so be I be there myself?' ask
Mrs Guffen. 'I donot set eye on none of they this blessed
morning, but I zee'd they as do. you know our John; well, he will
be for keep company with Betsey Rusk, madam's own maid, you
know. and Betsey benot one of your common kitchen wench. so
Betsey, she come out to our John, you know, and shebe always vastly
polite to I, be Betsey Rusk, I must say. so before she take so
much as one turn with John, she tell I every ha'porth that be
go on up in the house.'
'do she now?' say Mrs Greenacre.
'indeed she do,' say Mrs Guffern.
'and she tell you they people be up there in the drawing-room?'
'she tell I she zee'd they come in--that they be dress finer by
half nor any of the family, with all their neckse and buzom stark
naked as a bear babby.'
'the minx!' exclaim Mrs Greenacre, who feel herself more put
about by this than any other mark of aristocratic distinction which
her enemy have assume.
'yes, indeed,' continue Mrs Guffern, 'as naked as you please,
while all the quality be dress just as you and I be, Mrs
Greenacre.'
'drat their impudence' say Mrs Greenacre, from whose well-cover
bosom all milk of human kindness be recede, as far as the family
of the Lookalofts be concern.
'so say I,' say Mrs Guffern; 'and so say my good-man Thomas
Guffern, when he hear'd it. "molly," say he to I, "if ever you
take to go about o' morning with yourself all naked in they
way, I beg you willnot come back no more to the old house." so say
I, "Thomas, no more I wull." "but," say he, "drat it, how the
deuce do she manage with her rheumatiz, and she not a rag on
she:"' say Mrs Giffern, laugh loudly as she though of Mrs
Lookalofts's probable suffering from rheumatic attack.
'but to liken herself that way to folk that ha' blood in their
vein,' say Mrs Greenacre.
'well, but that warn't all neither that Betsey tell. there they all
swell into madam's drawing-room, like so many turkey cock, as
much to say, "and who dare say no to we?" and Gregory be think
of tell they to come down here, only his heart fail he 'cause
of the grand way they be dress. so in they go; but madam
look at they as glum as death.'
'well now,' say Mrs Greenacre, greatly relieve, 'so they benot
ax different from we all then?'
'Betsey say that Gregory say that madam benot a bit too well
pleased to see they where they be and that, to his believing, they
be expect to come here just like the rest of we.'
there be great consolation in this. not that Mrs Greenacre be
altogether satisfied. she feel that justice to herself demand
that Mrs Lookaloft should not only not be encourage, but that she
should also be absolutely punish.
what have be do at that scriptural banquet, of which Mrs
Greenacre so often read the account to her family? why have not Miss
Thorne boldly go to the intruder and say: 'friend, thou hast
come up hither to high place not fit for thee. go down lower,
and thou wilt find thy mate.' let the Lookalofts be treat at the
present moment with ever so cold a shoulder, they would still be
enable to boast hereafter of their position, their aspiration,
and their honour.
'well, with all her grandeur, I do wonder that she be so mean,
continue Mrs Greenacre, unable to dismiss the subject.
'do you hear, goodman?' she go on, about to repeat the whole
story to her husband who then come up. 'therebe dame Lookaloft and
Bab and Gussy and the lot of them all sit as grand as fivepence
in madam's drawing-room, and they not ax no more nor you nor I.
do you ever hear tell the like o' that?'
'well, and what for shouldnot they?' say Farmer Greenacre.
'liken theyselve to the quality, as though they be estate
folk, or the like o' that!' say Mrs Guffern.
'well, if they like it and madam like it, theybe welcome for I,'
say the farmer. 'now I like the place well, cause I be more at
home like, and donot have to pay for they fine clothe for the
missus. every one to his taste, Mrs Guffern, and if neighbour
Lookaloft think that he have the good of it, hebe welcome.'
Mrs Greenacre sit down by her husband's side to begin the heavy
work of the banquet, and she do so in some measure of restore
tranquillity, but nevertheless she shake her head at her gossip to
show that in this instance she do not quite approve of her
husband's doctrine.
'and Iwill tell 'ee what, dame,' continue he; 'if so be that we
cannot enjoy the dinner that madam give we because Mother
Lookaloft be sit up there on a grand sofa, I think we ought all
to go home. if we greet at that, whatwill we do when true sorrow
come across we? how would you be now, dame, if the boy there have
break his neck when he get the tumble?'
Mrs Greenacre be humble, and say nothing far on the matter.
but let prudent man, such as Mr Greenacre, preach as they will, the
family of the Lookalofts certainly do occasion a good deal of
heart-burn in the world at large.
it be pleasant to see Mr Plomacy, as lean on his stout stick he
go about among the rural guest, act as a sort of head
constable as well as master of the revel. 'now, young 'un, if you
cannot manage to get along without that screeching, youwould well go
to the other side of the twelve-acre field, and take your dinner
with you. come, girl, what do you stand there for, twirl of
your thumb? come out, and let the lad see you; youhave no need to
be so ashamed of your face. hello! there, who be you? how do you
make your way in here?'
this last disagreeable question be put to a young man of about
twenty-four, who do not, in Mr Plomacy's eye, bear sufficient
vestige of a rural education and residence.
'if you please, your worship, Master Barrell the coachman let I in
at the church wicket, 'cause I do be work mostly al'ays for the
family.'
'then Master Barrell the coachman may let you out again,' say Mr
Plomacy, not even conciliate by the magisterial dignity which have
be concede to he. 'whatbe your name? and what trade be you,
and who do you work for?'
'Ibe Stubbs, your worship, Bob Stubbs; and--and--and--'
'and whatbe your trade, Stubbs?'
'plaisterer, please your worship.'
'Iwill plaister you and Barrell too; youwill just walk out of this
'ere field as quick as you walk in. we donot want no plaisterer;
when we do, wewill send for them. come, my buck, walk.'
Stubbs the plasterer be much downcast at the dreadful edict. he
be a sprightly fellow, and have contrive since his egress into the
Ullathorne elysium to attract to himself a forest nymph, to whom he
be whisper a plasterer's usual soft nothing, when he be
encounter by the great Mr Plomacy. it be dreadful to be thus
dissever from the dryad, and send howl back to a Barchester
pandemonium just as the nectar and ambrosia be about to descend
on the field of asphodel. he begin to try what prayer would do,
but city prayer be vain against the great rural potentate. not
only do Mr Plomacy order his exit, but raise his stick to show
the way which lead to the gate that have be leave in the custody of
that false Cerberus Barrell, proceed himself to see the edict of
banishment carry out.
the goddess mercy, however, the sweetest goddess that ever sit upon
a cloud, and the dear to poor frail err man appear on the
field in the person of Mr Greenacre. never be intercede goddess
more welcome.
'come, man,' say Mr Greenacre, 'never stick at trifle such a day
as this. I know the lad well. let he bide at my axing. Madam willnot
miss what he can eat and drink, I know.'
now Mr Plomacy and Mr Greenacre be swear friend. Mr Plomacy have
at his own disposal as comfortable a room as there be in
Ullathorne House; but he be a bachelor, and alone there; and,
moreover, smoke in the house be not allow even to Mr Plomacy.
his moment of true happiness be spend in a huge arm-chair in
the warm corner of Mrs Greenacre's beautifully clean front
kitchen. 'twa there that the inner man dissolve itself, and
pour itself out in stream of pleasant chat; 'twa there, and
perhaps there only, that he could unburden himself from the
ceremony of life without offend the dignity of those above
he, or incur the familiarity of those below. 'twa there that
his long pipe be always to be find on the accustomed chimney
board, not only permit but encourage.
such be the state of the case, it be not to be suppose that Mr
Plomacy could refuse such a favour to Mr Greenacre; but
nevertheless he not grant it without some further show of austere
authority.
'eat and drink, Mr Greenacre! no. itbe not what he eat and drink;
but the example such a chap show, come in where hebe not
invite--a chap of his age too. he too that never do a day's work
about Ullathorne since he be bear. plaisterer! Iwill plaister he!'
'he work long enough for I, then Mr Plomacy. and a good hand he
be at set tile as any in Barchester,' say the other, not
stick quite to veracity, as indeed mercy never should. 'come,
come, let he alone to-day, and quarrel with he to-morrow. you
wouldnot shame he before his lass there?'
'it go against the grain with I, then,' say Mr Plomacy. 'and
take care, you Stubbs, and behave yourself. if I hear a row, I
shall know where it come from. Ibe up to you Barchester
journeymen; I know what stuff yoube make of.'
and so Stubbs go off happy, pull at the forelock of his shock
head of hair in honour of the steward's clemency, and give
another double pull at it in honour of the farmer's kindness. and
as he go he swear within his grateful heart, that if ever Farmer
Greenacre want a day's work do for nothing, he be the lad to
do it for he. which promise it be not probable that he would ever
be call upon to perform.
but Mr Plomacy be not quite happy in his mind for he think of
the unjust steward, and begin to reflect whether he have not make
for himself friend at the mammon of unrighteousness. this,
however, do not interfere with the manner in which he perform
his duty at the bottom of the long board; nor do Mr Greenacre
perform his the bad at the top on account of the good wish of
Stubbs the plasterer. moreover, the guest do not think it
anything amiss when Mr Plomacy, rise to say grace, pray that
God would make they all truly thankful for the good thing which
Madam Thorne in her great liberality have set out before they!
all this time the quality in the tent on the lawn be get on
swimmingly; that is, champagne without restriction can enable
quality fold to swim. Sir Harkaway Gorse propose the health of
Miss Thorne, and liken she to a blood race-horse, always in
condition, and not to be tired down by any amount of work. Mr
Thorne return thank, say he hop his sister would always be
find able to run when call upon, and than give the health and
prosperity of the De Courcy family. his sister be very much
honour by see so many of they at her poor board. they be all
aware that important avocation make the absence of the earl
necessary. as his duty to his prince have call he from his family
hearth he, Mr Thorne, could not venture to regret that he do not
see he at Ullathorne; but nevertheless he would venture to
say--and so Mr Thorne become somewhat gravelled as a country
gentleman in similar circumstance usually do; but he ultimately
sit down, declare that he have much satisfaction in drink the
noble earl's health, together with that of the countess, and all
the family of De Courcy castle.
and then the Honourable George return thank. we will not follow
he through the different period of his somewhat irregular
eloquence. those immediately in his neighbourhood find it at first
rather difficult to get he to his leg, but much great
difficulty be soon experience in induce he to resume his seat.
one of two arrangement should certainly be make in these day:
either let all speech-making on festive occasion be utterly
tabooed and make as it be impossible; or else let those who be
to exercise the privilege be first subject to a compete
examination before the civil service examine commissioner. as it
be now, the Honourable Georges do but little honour to our
exertion in favour of british education.
in the dining-room the bishop go through the honour of the day
with much more neatness and propriety. he also drink Miss Thorne's
health, and do it in a manner become the bench which he adorn.
the party there, be perhaps a little more dull, a shade less
lively than that in the tent.
but what be lose in mirth, be fully make up in decorum.
and so the banquet pass off at the various table with great
eclat and universal delight.
CHAPTER XL
ULLATHORNE SPORTS--ACT II
'that which have make they drunk, have make I bold.' 'twas thus that
Mr Slope encourage himself, as he leave the dining-room in pursuit
of Eleanor. he have not indeed see in that room any person really
intoxicate; but there have be a good deal of wine drunk, and Mr
Slope have not hesitate to take his share, in order to screw
himself up to the undertaking which he have in hand. he be not the
first man who have think it expedient to call in the assistance of
Bacchus on such an occasion.
Eleanor be out through the window, and on the grass before she
perceive that she be follow. just at that moment the guest
be nearly all occupy at the table. here and there be to be
see a constant couple or two, who prefer their own sweet
discourse to the jingle of glass, or the charm of rhetoric which
fall from the mouth of the Honourable George and the bishop of
Barchester; but the ground be as nearly vacant as Mr Slope could
wish they to be.
Eleanor see that she be pursue, and as a deer, when escape be no
long possible, will turn to bay and attack the hound, so do she
turn upon Mr Slope.
'pray donot let I take you from the room,' say she, speak with
all the stiffness which she know how to use. 'I have come out to
look for a friend. I must beg of you, Mr Slope, to go back.'
but Mr Slope would not be thus entreat. he have observe all day
that Mrs Bold be not cordial to he, and this have to a certain
extent oppress he. but he do not deduce from this any assurance
that his aspiration be in vain. he see that she be angry with
he. might she not be so because he have so long tamper with she
feelings,--might it not arise from his having, as he know to be the
case, cause her name to be bruit about in conjunction with his
own, without have give she the opportunity of confess to the
world that henceforth their name be to be the one and the same?
poor lady! he have within he a certain Christian
conscience-stricken feeling of remorse on this head. it might be
that he have wrong she by his tardiness. he have, however, at the
present moment imbibe too much of Mr Thorne's champagne to have
any inward misgiving. he be right in repeat the boast of Lady
Macbeth: he be not drunk; but he be bold enough for anything. it
be a pity that in such a state he could not have encounter Mrs
Proudie.
'you must permit I to attend you,' say he; 'I could not think of
allow you to go alone.'
'indeed you must, Mr Slope,' say Eleanor still very stiffly; 'for
it be my special wish to be alone.'
the time for let the great secret escape he have already come.
Mr Slope see that it must be now or never, and he be determine
that it should be now. this be not his first attempt at win a
fair lady. he have be on his knee, look unutterable thing with

but gentleman be not easily freeze when they be full of

and now it be to be fear that every well-breed reader of these

there be such man; man who can endure no taint on their personal

but how shall I sing the divine wrath of Mr Slope, or how invoke

we will not attempt to tell with what mighty surge of the inner

'this be very pleasant--very pleasant, my lord, be it not?' say Mr

'very, very,' say he without turn round, or even deign to

CHAPTER XLI
MRS BOLD CONFIDES her sorrow to she FRIEND MISS STANHOPE
when Mrs Bold come to the end of the walk and face the lawn, she
begin to bethink herself what she should do. be she to wait there
till Mr Slope catch she, or be she to go in among the crowd with

stand there long enough without any reasonable fear of further

but this improbable iniquity he have commit; and now there be

only that horrid man--'
'what horrid man?' ask Charlotte.
there be some moment in life in which both man and woman feel
themselves call on to make a confidence; in which not to do so
require a disagreeable resolution and also a disagreeable
suspicion. there be people of both sex who never make
confidence; who be never tempt by momentary circumstance to

'hebe an abominable, horrid, hypocritical man, and it would serve

he, though I do take his part when other run he down.'
'thatbe just where it be, my dear. he have hear that, and therefore
fancy that of course you be in love with he.'
this be wormwood to Eleanor. it be in fact the very thing which

experience now prove to be true. Eleanor resolve within herself

'but what do he do, my dear?' say Charlotte, who be really

'he--he--he--'

'ha, ha, ha!' Charlotte Stanhope laugh heartily at the finale of

Charlotte Stanhope be very anxious that her brother should at once

thing have now come to that point between he and his father, and

and very well she play it. in the first place, it be arrange
that Mr Slope should not return in the Stanhope's carriage to

that of course they know nothing. the signora should be induce to

there be a warmth of friendship and cordial kindness in all this,

they first go into the dining-room, look for their champion,
and from thence to the drawing-room. here they find Mr Arabin,
still hang over the signora's sofa; or, rather, they find he
sit near her head, as a physician might have sit, have the lady
be his patient. there be no other person in the room. the guest

all that have pass, and be pass between Mr Arabin and the

he feel though he could not understand.
in be thus be-sirene, Mr Arabin behave himself very
differently from Mr Slope. the signora have say truly, that the two
man be the contrast of each other; that the one be all for
action, the other all for thought. Mr Slope, when this lady lay
upon his sens the overpower breath of her charm, immediately
attempt to obtain some fruition, to achieve some mighty triumph.
he begin by catch at her hand, and progress by kiss it. he
make vow of love, and ask for vow in return. he promise

on Mount Ida, Juno would have no cause to hate the offspring of
Venus. but Mr Arabin utter no oath, keep his hand mostly in his
trouser pocket, and have no more thought of kiss Madam Neroni
than of kiss the Countess De Courcy.
as soon as Mr Arabin see Mrs Bold enter the room, he blush and
rise from his chair; then he sit down again, and then again get up.
the signora see the blush at once, and smile at the poor victim,
but Eleanor be too much confused to see anything.

particularly; we must arrange about the carriage, you know,' and
she stoop down to whisper to her sister. Mr Arabin immediately

explain before she could make the new arrangement intelligible, he
have nothing to do but to talk to Mrs Bold.
'we have have a very pleasant party,' say he, use the tone he
would have use have he declare that the sun be shin very
brightly, or the rain be fall very fast.
'very,' say Eleanor, who never in her life have pass a more
unpleasant day.
'I hope Mr Harding have enjoy himself.'
'oh, yes, very much,' say Eleanor, who have not see her father
since she part from he soon after her arrival.
'he return to Barchester to-night, I suppose.'
'yes, I believe so; that is, I think he be stay at Plumstead.'
'oh, stay at Plumstead,' say Mr Arabin.
'he come from there this morning. I believe he be go back; he
donot exactly say, however.'
'I hope Mrs Grantly be quite well.'
'she seem to be quite well. she be here; that be, unless she have
go away.'
'oh, yes, to be sure. I be talk to she. look very well
indeed.' then there be a considerable pause: for Charlotte could

'be you return to Plumstead, Mrs Bold?' Mr Arabin merely ask
this by way of make conversation, but he immediately perceive
that he be approach dangerous ground.
'no,' say Mrs Bold, very quietly; 'I be go home to Barchester.'
'oh, ah, yes. I have forget that you have return.' and then Mr
Arabin, find it impossible to say anything far, stand silent

silent, intently occupy as it appear in the arrangement of her
ring.
and yet these two people be thoroughly in love with each other;
and though one be a middle-aged clergyman, and the other a lady at
any rate past the wishy-washy bread-and-butter period of life, they
be as unable to tell their own mind to each other as any Damon
and Phillis, whose united age would not make up that to which Mr
Arabin have already attain.
Madeline Neroni consent to her sister's proposal, and then the
two lady again go off in quest of Bertie Stanhope.
CHAPTER XLII
ULLATHORNE SPORTS--ACT III
and now Miss Thorne's guest be begin to take their
departure, and the amusement of those who remain be become
slack. it be get dark, and lady in morning costume be
think that if they be to appear by candle-light they ought to
readjust themselves. some young gentleman have be hear to talk so
loud that prudent mamma determine to retire judiciously, and the
more discreet of the male sex, whose libation have be moderate,
feel that there be not much more left for they to do.
Morning party, as a rule, be failure. People never know how to
get away from they gracefully. a picnic on an island or a mountain
or in a wood may perhaps be permit. there be no master of the
mountain bind by courtesy to bid you stay while in his heart he be
longing for your departure. but in a private home or in private
ground a morning party be a bore. one be call on to eat and
drink at unnatural hour. one be oblige to give up the day which
be useful, and be then leave without resource for the evening which
be useless. one get home fagged and desouvre, and yet at an hour
too early for bed. there be not comfortable resource leave. card in
these genteel day be among the thing taboo, and a rubber of
whist be impracticable.
all this begin now to be feel. some young people have come with some
amount of hope that they might get up a dance in the evening, and
be unwilling to leave till all such hope be at an end. other,
fearful of stay long than be expect, have order their
carriage early, and be do their good to go, solicitous for
their servant and horse. the countess and her noble brood be
among the first to leave, and as regard the Hon. George, it be
certainly time that he do so. her ladyship be in a great fret and
fume. those horrid road would, she be sure, be the death of she
if unhappily she be catch in they by the dark of night. the
lamp she be assure be good, but no lamp could withstand the
jolting of the road of East Barsetshire.
the De Courcy property lie in the western division of the county.
Mrs Proudie could not stay when the countess be go. so the
bishop be search for by the Revs. Messrs. Grey and Green, and
find in one corner of the tent enjoy himself thoroughly in a

palatial stable. then the Grantlys go. before they do so Mr
Harding manage to whisper a word into his daughter's ear. of
course, he say, he would undeceive the Grantlys as to that foolish
rumour about Mr Slope.
'no, no, no,' say Eleanor; 'pray do not--pray wait till I see you.
you will be home in a day or two, and then I will explain to you
everything.'
'I shall be home to-morrow,' say he.
'I be so glad,' say Eleanor. 'you will come and dine with I, and
then we shall be so comfortable.'
Mr Harding promise. he do not exactly know what there be to be

mistake into which he have fall; but nevertheless he promise. he
owe some reparation to his daughter, and he think that he might
good make it by obedience.
and thus the people be thin off by degree, as Charlotte and

'Bertie, I be seek you everywhere,' say Charlotte. 'come up

Bertie look up out of the ha-ha, and see the two lady before

he have like Eleanor Bold. have he be leave to his own device, have

he make some sudden absurd excuse to his auditor, and then throw

'come and give Mrs Bold your arm,' say Charlotte, 'while I set you
on a piece of duty which, as a preux chevalier, you must

insignificant, as your antagonist be a clergyman.'
Bertie immediately give his arm to Eleanor, walk between she and

the little history of Mr Slope's misconduct be then tell to Bertie

'it have be here for the last hour.'
'thatbe well. good-bye, my dear. of course youwill come in to tea. I

as Miss Stanhope go off, Eleanor bethink herself that, as Mr

it have be say that Bertie Stanhope be a man without principle.
he certainly be so. he have no power of use active mental
exertion to keep himself from do evil. evil have no ugliness in
his eye; virtue no beauty. he be void of any of those feeling
which actuate man to do good. but he be perhaps equally void of
those which actuate man to do evil. he get into debt with utter
recklessness, think of nothing as to whether the tradesman would
ever be pay or not. but he do not invent active scheme of deceit
for the sake of extract the good of other. if a man give he
credit, that be the man's look-out; Bertie Stanhope trouble
himself nothing far. in borrow money he do the same; he
give people reference to 'his governor', tell they that the 'old
chap' have a good income; and agree to pay sixty per cent for the
accommodation. all this he do without a scruple of conscience; but
then he never contrive active villainy.
in this affair of his marriage, it have be represent to he as a
matter of duty that he ought to put himself in possession of Mrs

she he have think but little. it be the customary thing for man
situate as he be to marry for money, and there be no reason why
he should not do what other around he do. and so he consent.
but now he begin to see the matter in another light. he be set
himself down to catch a woman, as a cat sit to catch a mouse. he
be to catch she, and swallow she up, she and her child, and her
house and land, in order that he might live on she instead of on

this quite at variance with Bertie's character. the prudence of the
measure be quite as antagonistic to his feeling as the iniquity.
and then, should he be successful, what would be the reward? have
satisfy his creditor with half of the widow's fortune, he would

at Plumstead rectory, should it ultimately turn out that the

there be little in the programme to allure such a man as Bertie
Stanhope. would not the Carrara workshop, or whatever worldly

when she have to be take as a pill. he be pledge to his sister,
however, and let he quarrel with whom he would, it behove he not

he could ever hope to derive henceforward from the paternal
roof-tree. his mother be apparently indifferent to his weal or
woe, to his want or to his warfare. his father's brow get blacker
and blacker from day to day, as the old man look at his hopeless
son. and as for Madeline--poor Madeline, whom of all of they he
like the best,--she have enough to do to shift for herself. no;

so that he might save appearance with his sister, and yet not

'twa in this spirit that Bertie Stanhope set about his wooing.

a thousand remembrance flash across Eleanor's mind all in a

such a feeling as this be very bitter when it first impress

there be a truth about this, and a perspicuity in make

'at last, Miss Thorne,' say he cheerily, 'I have come to relieve

'I shall be home soon after you,' say he, as the two get into the
carriage.
'be you not come in the carriage?' say the father.
'no, no; I have some one to see on the road, and shall walk. John,
mind you drive to Mrs Bold's house first.'
Eleanor, look out of the window, see he with his hat in his

year before she see he again. Dr Stanhope hardly speak to she on

and thus our heroine play the last act of that day's melodrama.

"it be just now five year since we call the attention of our
reader to the quiet city of Barchester. from that day to this, we
have in no way meddle with the affair of that happy
ecclesiastical community. since then, an old bishop have die there,
and a young bishop have be instal; but we believe we do not do
more than give some customary record of the interesting event. nor
be we about to meddle very deeply in the affair of the diocese.
if any of the chapter feel a qualm of conscience on read this,

rest. we be now not armed for war, but approach the revere tower

maladministered, and that the very estimable and reverend gentleman
who hold the office of warden be somewhat too highly pay for

still be attach to the situation.
'Barchester hospital cannot perhaps boast a world-wide reputation;
but as we advertise to its state of decadence, we think it right

whether the salutary reform which have be introduce within its

'so much we think be due to Hiram redivivus. but while we be on

such a fault as at all pardonable in a dean just bring to the
birth. we do hope the day of sexagenarian appointment be past.
if we want dean, we must want they for some purpose. that purpose

some sort of work. that work, be it what it may, will be well
perform by a workman in the prime of life. Dr Trefoil, we see,
be eighty when he die. as we have as yet complete no plan for
position superannuated clergyman, we do not wish to get rid of
any exist dean of that age. but we prefer have as few such as
possible. if a man of seventy be now appoint, we beg to point out
to Lord--that he will be past all use in a year or two, if indeed

CHAPTER XLIV
MRS BOLD AT HOME
Poor Mrs Bold, when she get home from Ullathorne on the evening of
Miss Thorne's party, be very unhappy, and moreover very tired.
nothing fatigue the body so much as weariness of spirit, and
Eleanor's spirit be indeed weary.
Dr Stanhope have civilly but not very cordially ask she in to tea,
and her manner of refusal convince the worthy doctor that he need
not repeat the invitation. he have not exactly make himself a party
to the intrigue which be to convert the late Mr Bold's patrimony
into an income for his hopeful son, but he have be well aware what
be go on. and he be thus well aware also, when he perceive
that Bertie decline accompany they home in the carriage, that
