Eleanor be very much afraid that Charlotte would have dart out
upon she, as the prebendary get out at his own door, but Bertie
thoughtfully save she from this, by cause the carriage to go
round by her house. this also Dr Stanhope understand, and allow
to pass by without remark.
when she get home, she find Mary Bold in the drawing-room with the
child in her lap. she rush forward, and, throw herself on her
knee, kiss the little fellow till she almost frighten he.
'oh, Mary, I be so glad you do not go. it be an odious party.'
now the question of Mary's going have be one greatly moot
between they. Mrs Bold, when invite, have be the guest of the
Grantlys, and Miss Thorne, who have chiefly know Eleanor at the
hospital or at Plumstead rectory, have forget all about Mary
Bold. her sister-in-law have implore she to go under her wing, and
have offer to write to Miss Thorne, or to call on she. but Miss
Bold have decline. in fact, Mr Bold have not be very popular with
such people as the Thornes, and his sister would not go among they
unless she be specially ask to do so.
'well then,' say Mary cheerfully, 'I have the less to regret.'
'you have nothing to regret; but oh! Mary, I have--so much--so
much;'--and then she begin kiss her boy, whom her caress have
arouse from his slumber. when she raise her head, Mary see that
the tear be run down her cheek.
'Good heaven, Eleanor, what be the matter? what have happen to
you?--Eleanor, dear Eleanor--what be the matter?' and Mary get
up with the boy still in her arm.
'give he to I--give he to I,' say the young mother. 'give he
to I, Mary,'and she almost tear the child out of her sister's
arm. the poor little fellow murmur somewhat at the disturbance,
but nevertheless nestle himself close into his mother's bosom.
'here, Mary, take the cloak from I. my own, own darling, darling,
darling jewel. you be not false to I. everybody else be false;
everybody else be cruel. Mamma will care for nobody, nobody,
nobody, but her own, own, own, little man;' and she again kiss
and press the baby, and cry till the tear run down over the
child's face.
'who have be cruel to you, Eleanor?' say Mary. 'I hope I have
not.'
now, in this matter, Eleanor have great cause for uneasiness.
she could not certainly accuse her love sister-in-law of cruelty;
but she have to do that which be more galling; she have to accuse
herself of an imprudence against which her sister-in-law have warn
she. Miss Bold have never encourage Eleanor's acquaintance with Mr
Slope, and she have positively discourage the friendship of the
Stanhopes as far as her usual gentle mode of speak have
permit. Eleanor have only laugh at she, however, when she say
that she disapprove of married woman who live apart from their
husband, and suggest that Charlotte Stanhope never go to
church. now, however, Eleanor must either hold her tongue, which
be quite impossible, or confess herself to have be utterly
wrong, which be nearly equally so. so she stave off the evil day
by more tear, and console herself by induce little Johnny to
rouse himself sufficiently to return her caress.
'he be a darling--as true as gold. what would mamma do without he?
Mamma would lie down and die if she have not her own Johnny Bold to
give her comfort.' this and much more she say of the same kind,
and for a time make no other answer to Mary's inquiry.
this kind of consolation from the world's deceit be very common.
mother obtain it from their child, and man from their dog.
some man even do so from their walking-stick, which be just as
rational. how be it that we can take joy to ourselves in that we
be not deceive by those who have not attain the art to deceive
we? in a true man, if such can be find, or a true woman, much
consolation may indeed be take.
in the caress of her child, however, Eleanor do receive
consolation; and may ill befall the man who would begrudge it to
she. the evil day, however, be only postpone. she have to tell she
disagreeable tale to Mary, and she have also to tell it to her
father. must it not, indeed, be tell to the whole circle of her
acquaintance before she could be make to stand all right with they?
at the present moment there be no one to whom she could turn for
comfort. she hat Mr Slope; that be a matter of course, in that
feeling she revel. she hat and despise the Stanhopes; but
that feel distressed she greatly. she have, as it be, separate
herself from her old friend to throw herself into the arm of this
family; and then how have they intend to use she? she could hardly
reconcile herself to her own father, who have believe ill of she.
Mary Bold have turn Mentor. that she could have forgive have the
Mentor turn out to be in the wrong; but mentor in the right be
not to be pardon. she could not but hate the archdeacon; and now
she hat he even bad than ever, for she must in some sort
humble herself before he. she hat her sister, for she be part
and parcel of the archdeacon. and she would have hat Mr Arabin if
she could. he have pretend to regard she, and yet before her face
he have hang over that italian woman as though there have be no
beauty in the world but her--no other woman worth a moment's
attention. and Mr Arabin would have to learn all this about Mr
Slope! she tell herself she hat he, and she know that she be
lie to herself as she do so. she have no consolation but her
baby, and of that she make the most. Mary, though she could not
surmise what it be that have so violently affect her
sister-in-law, see at once her grief be too great to be keep under
control, and wait patiently till the child should be in his
cradle.
'youwill have some tea, Eleanor,' she say.
'oh, I donot care,' say she; though in fact she must have be
very hungry, for she have eat nothing at Ullathorne.
Mary quietly make the tea, and butter the bread, lay aside the
cloak, and make thing look comfortable.
'hebe fast asleep,' say she, 'yoube very tired; let I take he
up to bed.'
but Eleanor would not let her sister touch he. she look
wistfully at her baby's eye, see that they be lose in the
deep slumber, and then make a sort of couch for he on the sofa.
she be determined that nothing should prevail upon she to let he
out of her sight that night.
'come, nelly,' say Mary, 'donot be cross with I. I at least have
do nothing to offend you.'
'I an't cross,' say Eleanor.
'be you angry then? surely you cannot be angry with I.'
'no, I an't angry; at least not with you.'
'if you be not, drink the tea I have make for you. I be sure you
must want it.'
Eleanor do drink it, and allow herself to be persuade. she eat
and drank, and as the inner woman be recruit she feel a little
more charitable towards the world at large. at last she find word
to begin her story, and before she go to bed, she have make a
clean breast of it and tell everything--everything, that is, as to
the lover she have reject: of Mr Arabin she say not a word.
'I know I be wrong,' say she, speak of the blow she have give
to Mr Slope; 'but I donot know what he might do, and I have to
protect myself.'
'he richly deserve it,' say Mary.
'deserve it!' say Eleanor, whose mind as regard Mr Slope be
almost bloodthirsty. 'have I stab he with a dagger, he would
have deserve it. but what will they say about it at Plumstead?'
'I donot think I should tell they,' say Mary. Eleanor begin to
think that she would not.
there could have be no kind comforter than Mary Bold. there be
not the slight dash of triumph about she when she hear of the
Stanhope scheme, nor do she allude to her former opinion when
Eleanor call she late friend Charlotte a base, design woman.
she re-echo all the abuse that be heap on Mr Slope's head, and
never hint that she have say as much before. 'I tell you so! I
tell you so!' be the croak of a true Job's comforter. but Mary,
when she find her friend lie in her sorrow and scrap herself
with potsherd, forbore to argue and to exult. Eleanor acknowledge
the merit of the forbearance, and at length allow herself to be
tranquillise.
on the next day she do not go out of the house. Barchester she
think would be crowd with Stanhopes and Slopes; perhaps also
with Arabins and Grantlys. indeed there be hardly any one among
her friend whom she could have meet, without some cause of
uneasiness.
in the course of the afternoon she hear that the dean be dead;
and she also hear that Mr Quiverful have be finally appoint to
the hospital.
in the evening her father come to she, and then the story, or as
much of it as she could bring herself to tell he, have to be
repeat. he be not in truth much surprised at Mr Slope's
effrontery; but he be oblige to act as though he have be, to
save his daughter's feeling. he be, however, anything but skilful
in his deceit, and she see through it.
'I see,' say she, 'that you think it only the common course of
thing that Mr Slope should have treat I in this way.'
she have say nothing to he about the embrace, nor yet of the way
in which it have be meet.
'I do not think it at all strange,' say he, 'that any one should
admire my Eleanor.'
'it be strange to I,' say she, 'that any man should have so much
audacity, without ever have receive the slight
encouragement.'
to this Mr Harding answer nothing. with the archdeacon it would
have be the text for a rejoinder, which would not have disgrace
Bildad the Shuhite.
'but youwill tell the archdeacon,' ask Mr Harding.
'tell he what?' say she sharply.
'or Susan?' continue Mr Harding. 'youwill tell Susan; youwill let
they know that they wrong you in suppose that this man's
address would be agreeable to you.'
'they may find out their own way,' say she; 'I shall not ever
willingly mention Mr Slope's name to either of they.'
'but I may.'
'I have no right to hinder you from do anything that may be
necessary to your own comfort, but pray do not do it for my sake.
Dr Grantly never think well of I, and never will. I donot know
now that I an even anxious that he should do so.'
and then they go to the affair of the hospital. 'but be it true,
papa?'
'what, my dear,' say he. 'about the dean? yes, I fear quite true.
indeed, I know there be no doubt about it.'
'Poor Miss Trefoil. I be so sorry for she. but I do not mean
that,' say Eleanor. 'but about the hospital, papa?
'yes, my dear. I believe it be true that Mr Quiverful be to have
it.'
'oh, what a shame!'
'no, my dear, not at all, not at all a shame: I be sure I hope it
will suit he.'
'but, papa, you know it be a shame. after all your hope, all your
expectation to get back your old house, to see it give away in
this way to a perfect stranger!'
'my dear, the bishop have a right to give it to whom he please.'
'I deny that, papa. he have no such right. it be not as though you
be a candidate for a new piece of preferment. if the bishop have a
grain of justice--'
'the bishop offer it to I on his term, and as I do not like
the term, I refuse it. after that, I cannot complain.'
'term! he have not right to make term.'
'I donot know about that; but it seem he have the power. but to
tell you the truth, Nelly, I be as well satisfied as it be. when
the affair become the subject of angry discussion, I thoroughly
wish to be rid of it altogether.'
'but you do want to go back to the old house, papa. you tell I so
yourself.'
'yes, my child, I do. for a short time I do wish it. and I be
foolish in do so. I be get old now; and my chief worldly
wish be for peace and rest. have I go back to the hospital, I
should have have the endless contention with the bishop,
contention with his chaplain, and contention with the archdeacon.
I be not up to this now, I be not able to meet such trouble; and
therefore I be not ill-pleased to find myself leave to the little
church of St Cuthbert's. I shall never starve,' add he, laugh
'as long as you be here.'
'but if you will come and live with I, papa?' she say earnestly,
take he by both his hand. 'if you will do that, if you will
promise that, I will own that you be right.'
'I will dine with you to-day, at any rate.'
'no, but live here altogether. give up that close, odious little
room in High Street.'
'my dear, itbe a very nice little room; and you be really quite
uncivil.'
'oh, papa, donot joke. itbe not a nice place for you. you say you
be grow old, though I be sure you be not.'
'be I not, my dear?'
'no, papa, not old--not to say old. but you be quite old enough to
feel the want of a decent room to sit in. you know how lonely Mary
and I be here. you know nobody ever sleep in the big front
bed-room. it be really unkind of you to remain there alone, when
you be so much want here.'
'thank you, Nelly--thank you. but, my dear--'
'if you have be live here, papa, with we, as I really think you
ought to have do, consider how lonely we be, there would have
be none of all this dreadful affair about Mr Slope.'
Mr Harding, however, do not allow himself to be talk over into
give up his own and only little pie a terre in the High Street.
he promise to come and dine with his daughter, and stay with she,
and visit she, and do everything but absolutely live with she. it
do not suit the peculiar feeling of the man to tell his daughter
that though she have reject Mr Slope, and be ready to reject Mr
Stanhope, some other more favour suitor would probably soon
appear; and that on the appearance of such a suitor the big front
bed-room might perhaps be more frequently in requisition than at
present. but doubtless such an idea cross his mind, and add its
weight to the other reason which make he decide on still keep
the close, odious little room in High Street.
the evening pass over quietly and in comfort. Eleanor be always
happy with her father than with any one else. he have not,
perhaps, any natural taste for baby-worship, but he be always
ready to sacrifice himself, and therefore make an excellent third
in a trio with his daughter and Mary Bold in sing the praise of
the wonderful child.
they be stand together over their music in the evening, the
baby have again be put to bed upon the sofa, when the servant
bring in a very small note in a beautiful pink envelope. it quite
fill the room with perfume as it lie upon the small salver. Mary
Bold and Mrs Bold be both at the piano, and Mr Harding be
sit close to they, with the violoncello between his leg; so
that the elegance of the epistle be visible to they all.
'please, ma'am, Dr Stanhope's coachman say he be to wait for an
answer,' say the servant.
Eleanor get very red in the face as she take the note in her hand.
she have never see the writing before. Charlotte's epistle, to
which she be well accustom, be of a very different style and
kind. she generally write on large note-paper; she twist up her
letter into the shape and sometimes into the size of cocked hat;
she address they in a sprawling manly hand, and not unusually
add a blot or a smudge, as though such be her own peculiar
sign-manual. the address of this note be write in a beautiful
female hand, and the gum wafer bore on it an impress of a gilt
coronet. Though Eleanor have never see such a one before, she
guess that it come from the signora. such epistle be very
numerously send out from any house in which the signora might
happen to be dwell, but they be rarely address to lady.
when the coachman be tell by the lady's maid to take the letter to
Mrs Bold, he openly express his opinion that there be some
mistake about it. whereupon the lady's maid box the coachman's
ear. have Mr Slope see in how meek a spirit the coachman take the
rebuke, he might have learn a useful lesson, both in philosophy
and religion.
the note be as follow. it may be take as a faithful promise that
no further letter whatever shall be transcribe at length in these
page.
'my dear Mrs Bold--may I ask you, as a great favour, to call on I
to-morrow? you can say what hour will well suit you; but quite
early, if you can. I need hardly say that if I could call upon you
I should not take this liberty with you.
'I partly know what occur the other day, and I promise you that
you shall meet with no annoyance if you will come to I. my brother
leave we for London to-day; from thence he go to Italy.
'it will probably occur to you that I should not thus intrude on
you, unless I have that to say to you which may be of considerable
moment. pray therefore excuse I, even if you do not grant my
request, and believe I, 'very sincerely your, m.vesey NERONI
the three of they sit in consultation on this epistle for some ten
or fifteen minute, and then decide that Eleanor should write a
line say that she would see the signora the next morning, at
twelve o'clock.
CHAPTER XLV
the STANHOPES AT HOME
we must now return to the Stanhopes, and see how they behave
themselves on their return from Ullathorne.
Charlotte, who come back in the first homeward journey with her
sister, wait in palpitating expectation till the carriage drive
up to the door a second time. she do not run down or stand at the
window, or show in any outward manner that she look for anything
wonderful to occur; but, when she hear the carriage-wheel, she
stand up with erect ear, listen for Eleanor's footfall on the
pavement or the cheery sound of Bertie's voice welcome she in.
have she hear either, she would have feel that all be right; but
neither sound be there for she to hear. she hear only her
father's slow step, as he ponderously let himself down from the
carriage, and slowly walk along the hall, till he get into his
own private room on the ground floor. 'send Miss Stanhope to I,'
he say to the servant.
'therebe something wrong now,' say Madeline, who be lie on her
sofa in the back drawing-room.
'itbe all up with Bertie,' reply Charlotte. 'I know, I know,' she
say to the servant, as he bring up the message. 'tell my father
I will be with he immediately.'
'Bertie's wooing go astray,' say Madeline. 'I know it would.'
'it have be his own fault then. she be ready enough. I be quite
sure,' say Charlotte, with that sort of ill-nature which be not
uncommon when one woman speak of another.
'what will you say to he now?' by 'he' the signora mean their
father.
'that will be as I find he. he be ready to pay two hundred pound
for Bertie, to stave off the bad of his creditor, if this
marriage have go on. Bertie must now have the money instead, and
go and take his chance.'
'where be he now?'
'Heaven know! smoke at the bottom of Mr Thorne's ha-ha, or
philander with some of those Miss Chadwicks. nothing will ever
make an impression on he. but hewill be furious if I donot go
down.'
'no; nothing ever will. but donot be long, Charlotte, for I want my
tea.'
and so Charlotte go down to her father. there be a very black
cloud on the old man's brow; blacker than his daughter could ever
remember to have see there. he be sit in his own arm-chair,
not comfortably over the fire, but in the middle of the room,
wait till she should come and listen to he.
'what have become of your brother?' he say, as soon as the door be
shut.
'I should rather ask you,' say Charlotte. 'I leave you both at
Ullathorne, when I come away. what have you do with Mrs Bold?'
'Mrs Bold! nonsense. the woman have go home as she ought to do.
and heartily glad I be that she should not be sacrifice to so
heartless a reprobate.'
'oh, papa!'
'a heartless reprobate! tell I now where he be, and what he be
go to do. I have allow myself to be fool between you.
marriage indeed! who on earth that have money, or credit, or respect
in the world to lose, would marry he?'
'it be no use your scold I, papa. I have do the good I could
for he and you.'
'and Madeline be nearly as bad,' say the prebendary, who be in
truth, very, very angry.
'oh, I suppose we be all bad,' reply Charlotte.
the old man emit a huge leonine sigh. if they be all bad, who
have make they so? if they be unprincipled, selfish, and
disreputable, who be to be blame for the education which have have
so injurious an effect.
'I know youwill ruin I among you,' say he.
'why, papa, what nonsense that be. you be live within your
income this minute, and if there be any new debt, I donot know of
they. I be sure there ought to be none, for we be dull enough
here.'
'be those bill of Madeline's pay?'
'no, they be not. who be to pay they?'
'her husband may pay they.'
'her husband! would you wish I to tell she you say so? do you wish
to turn she out of your home?'
'I wish she would know how to behave herself.'
'why, what on earth have she do now? poor madeline! to-day be only
the second time she have go out since we come to this vile town.'
he then sit silent for a time, think in what shape he would
declare his resolve. 'well, papa,' say Charlotte, 'shall I stay
here, or may I go up-stair and give mamma her tea?'
'you be in your brother's confidence. tell I what he be go to
do?'
'nothing, that I be aware of.'
'nothing--nothing! nothing but eat and drink, and spend every
shilling of my money he can lay his hand upon. I have make up my
mind, Charlotte. he shall eat and drink no more in this house.'
'very well. then I suppose he must go back to Italy.'
'he may go where he please.'
'thatbe easily say, papa; but what do it mean? you cannot let he
live--'
'it mean this,' say the doctor, speak more loudly than be his
willnot, and with wrath flash from his eye; 'that as sure as God
rule in heaven, I will not maintain he any long in idleness.'
'oh, rule in heaven!' say Charlotte. 'it be no use talk about
that. you must rule he here on earth; and the question be, how you
can do it. you cannot turn he out of the house penniless, to beg
about the street.'
'he may beg where he like.'
'he must go back to Carrara. that be the cheap place he can live
at, and nobody there will give he credit for above two or three
hundred paul. but you must let he have the mean of go.'
'as sure as--'
'oh papa, donot swear. you know you must do it. you be ready to
pay two hundred pound for he if the marriage come off. half that
will start he to Carrara.'
'what? give he a hundred pound!'
'you know we be all in the dark, papa,' say she, think it
expedient to change the conversation. 'for anything we know, he may
be at this moment engage to Mrs Bold.'
'Fiddlestick,' say the father, who have see the way in which Mrs
Bold have get into the carriage, while his son stand apart without
even offer she his hand.
'well, then, he must go to Carrara.' say Charlotte.
just at this moment the lock of the front door be hear, and
Charlotte's quick care detect her brother's cat-like step in the
hall. she say nothing, feel that for the present Bertie have
well keep out of her father's way. but Dr Stanhope also hear the
sound of the lock.
'whobe that?' he demand. Charlotte make no reply, and he ask
again. 'who be that that have just come in? open the door. who be
it?'
'I suppose it be Bertie.'
'bid he to come here,' say the father. but Bertie, who be close
to the door and hear the call, require no further bidding, but
walk in with a perfectly unconcerned and cheerful air. it be
this peculiar insouciance which anger Dr Stanhope, even more than
his son's extravagance.
'well, sir,' say the doctor.
'and how do you get home, sir, with your fair companion?' say
Bertie. 'I suppose she be not up-stair, Charlotte?'
'Bertie,' say Charlotte, 'papa be in no humour for joke. he be
very angry with you.'
'angry!' say Bertie, raise his eyebrow, as though he have never
yet give his parent cause for a single moment's uneasiness.
'sit down, if you please, sir,' say Dr Stanhope very sternly, but
not now very loudly. 'and Iwill trouble you to sit down, too,
Charlotte. your mother can wait for her tea a few minute.'
Charlotte sit down on the chair nearest the door, in somewhat of a
perverse sort of manner; as much as though she would say--well,
here I be; you shallnot say I donot do as I be bid; but Iwill be
whip if I give way to you. and she be determine not to give
way. she too be angry with Bertie; but she be not the less ready
on that account to defend he from his father. Bertie also sit
down. he draw his chair close to the library table, upon which he
put his elbow, and then rest his face comfortably on one hand,
he begin draw little picture on a sheet of paper with the
other. before the scene be over have have complete admirable
figure of Miss Thorne, Mrs Proudie, and Lady De Courcy, and begin
a family piece to comprise the whole set of Lookalofts.
'would it suit you, sir,' say the father, 'to give I some idea as
to what your present intention are?--what way of live you
propose to yourself?'
'Iwill do anything you suggest, sir,' say Bertie.
'no, I shall suggest nothing far. my time for suggest have
go by. I have only one order to give, and that be, that you leave
my house.'
'to-night?' say Bertie; and the simple tone of the question leave
the doctor without any adequately dignified method of reply.
'Papa do not quite mean to-night,' say Charlotte, 'at least I
suppose not.'
'to-morrow perhaps,' suggest Bertie.
'yes sir, to-morrow,' say the doctor. 'you shall leave this to-morrow.'
'very well, sir. will the 4.30 P.M. train be soon enough?' say
Bertie, as he ask, put the finishing touch to Miss Thorne's
high-heeled boot.
'you may go how and when and where you please, so that you leave my
house to-morrow. you have disgrace I, sir; you have disgrace
yourself, and I, and your sister.'
'I be glad at least sir, that I have not disgrace my mother,' say
Bertie.
Charlotte could hardly keep her countenance; but the doctor's brow
grow still black than ever. Bertie be execute his chef d'ouvre
in the delineation of Mrs Proudie's nose and mouth.
'you be a heartless reprobate, sir; a heartless, thankless,
good-for-nothing reprobate. I have do with you. you be my
son--that I cannot help; but you shall have no more part or parcel
in I as my child, nor I in you as your father.'
'oh, papa, papa! you must not, shall not say so,' say Charlotte.
'I will say so, and do say so,' say the father, rise from his
chair. 'and now leave the room, sir.'
'stop, stop,' say Charlotte; 'why donot you speak, Bertie? why
donot you look up and speak? it be your manner that make he so
angry.'
'he be perfectly indifferent to all decency, to all propriety,'
say the doctor; and then he shout out, 'leave the room, sir! do
you hear what I say?'
'Papa, papa, I will not let you part so. I know you will be sorry
for it.' and then she add, get up and whisper into his
ear. 'be he only to blame? think of that. we have make our own bed,
and, such as it be, we must lie on it. it be no use for we to
quarrel among ourselves,' and as she finish her whisper, Bertie
finish off the countess's bustle, which be so well do that it
absolutely seem to be sway to and fro on the paper with its
usual lateral motion.
'my father be angry at the present time,' say Bertie, look up
for a moment from his sketch, 'because I be not go to marry
Mrs Bold. what can I say on the matter? it be true that I be not
go to marry she. in the first place--'
'that be not true, sir,' say Dr Stanhope; 'but I will not argue
with you.'
'you be angry just this moment because I would not speak,' say
Bertie, go on with a young Lookaloft.
'give over drawing,' say Charlotte, go up to he and take the
paper from under his hand. the caricature, however, she preserve,
and show they afterwards to the friend of the Thornes, the
Proudies, and De Courcys. Bertie, deprive of his occupation, throw
himself back in his chair and wait further order.
'I think it will certainly be for the good that Bertie should leave
this at once, perhaps to-morrow,' say Charlotte; 'but pray, papa,
let we arrange some scheme together.'
'if he will leave to-morrow, I will give he L 10, and he shall be
pay L 5 a month by the banker at Carrara as long as he stay
permanently in that place.'
'well, sir! it willnot be long,' say Bertie; 'for I shall be starve
to death in about three month.'
'he must have marble to work with,' say Charlotte.
'I have plenty there in the studio to last I three month,' say
Bertie. 'it will be no use attempt anything large in so limited
a time; unless I do my own tombstone.'
term, however, be ultimately come to, somewhat more liberal than
those propose, and the doctor be induce to shake hand with his
son, and bid he good-night. Dr Stanhope would not go up to tea,
but have it bring to he in his study by his daughter.
but Bertie go up-stair and spend a pleasant evening. he finish
the Lookalofts, greatly to the delight of his sister, though the
manner of portray their decollete dress be not the most
refine. find how matter be go, he by degree allow it
to escape from he that he have not press his suit upon the widow
in a very urgent way.
'I suppose, in point of fact, you never propose at all?' say
Charlotte.
'oh, she understand that she might have I if she wish,' say he.
'and she donot wish,' say the signora.
'you have throw I over in the most shameful manner,' say
Charlotte. 'I suppose you tell she all about my little plan?'
'well, it come out somehow; at least the most of it.'
'therebe an end of that alliance,' say Charlotte; 'but it donot
matter much. I suppose we shall all be back in Como soon.'
'I be sure I hope so,' say the signora; 'Ibe sick of the sight of
black coat. if that Mr Slope come here any more, hewill be the
death of I.'
'youhave be the ruin of he, I think,' say Charlotte.
'and as for a second black-coat lover of mine, I be go to make
a present to he of another lady with most singular
disinterestedness.'
the next day, true to his promise, Bertie pack up and go of by
the 4.30 P.M. train, with L 20 in his pocket, bind for the marble
quarry of Carrara. and so he disappear from our scene.
at twelve o'clock on the day follow that on which Bertie go,
Mrs Bold, true also to her word, knock at Dr Stanhope's door with
a timid hand and palpitate heart. she be at once show up to the
back drawing-room, the fold door of which be closed, so that
in visit the signora, Eleanor be not necessarily throw into
any communication with those in the front room. as she go up the
stair, she none of the family, and be so far save much of the
annoyance which she have dread.
'this be very kind of you, Mrs Bold; very kind, after what have
happen,' say the lady on the sofa with her sweetest smile.
'you write in such a strain that I could not but come to you.'
'I do, I do; I want to force you to see I.'
'well, signora; I be here.'
'how cold you be to I. but I suppose I must put up with that. I
know you think you have reason to be displeased with we all. poor
Bertie! if you know all, you would not be angry with he.'
'I be not angry with your brother--not in the least. but I hope you
do not send for I to talk about he.'
'if you be angry with Charlotte, that be bad; for you have no
warm friend in all Barchester. but I do not send for you to talk
about this--pray bring your chair nearer, Mrs Bold, so that I may
look at you. it be so unnatural to see you keep so far off from
I.'
Eleanor do as she be bid, and bring her chair close to the
sofa.
'and now, Mrs Bold, I be go to tell you something which you may
think indelicate; but yet I know that I be right in do so.'
Hereupon Mrs Bold say nothing, but feel inclined to shake in her
chair. the signora, she know, be not very particular, and that
which to she appear to be indelicate might to Mrs Bold appear to
be extremely indecent.
'I believe you know Mr Arabin?'
Mrs Bold would have give the world not to blush, but her blood be
not at her own command. she do blush up to her forehead, and the
signora, who have make she sit in a special light in order that she
might watch she, see that she do so.
'yes--I be acquaint with he. that be, slightly. he be an
intimate friend of Dr Grantly, and Dr Grantly be my
brother-in-law.'
'well; if you know Mr Arabin, I be sure you must like he. I know
and like he much. everybody that know he must like he.'
Mrs Bold feel it quite impossible to say anything in reply to this.
her blood be rush about her body she know not how or why. she
feel as though she be swing in her chair; and she know that
she be not only red in the face, but also almost suffocated with
heat. however, she sit still and say nothing.
'how stiff you be with I, Mrs Bold,' say the signora; 'and I the
while be do for you all that one woman can do to serve another.'
a kind of thought come over the widow's mind that perhaps the
signora's friendship be real; and that at any rate it could not
hurt she; and another kind of thought, a glimmering of a thought,
come to she also,--that Mr Arabin be to precious to be lose. she
despise the signora; but might she not stoop to conquer? it should
be but the small fraction of a stoop!
'I donot want to be stiff,' she say, 'but your question be so
very singular.'
'well, then, I will ask you one more singular still,' say Madeline
Neroni, raise herself on her elbow and turn her own face full
upon her companion's. 'do you love he, love he with all your
heart and soul, with all the love your bosom can feel? for I can
tell you that he love you, worship you, think of you and nothing
else, be now think of you as he attempt to write his sermon for
next Sunday's preaching. what would I not give to be love in such
a way by such a man, that be, if I be an object for any man to
love!'
Mrs Bold get up from her seat and stand speechless before the woman
who be now address she in this impassioned way. when the
signora thus allude to herself, the widow's heart be soften,
and she put her own hand, as though caressingly, on that of her
companion which be rest on the table. the signora grasp it
and go on speak.
'what I tell you be God's own truth; and it be for you to use it as
may be good for your own happiness. but you must not betray I. he
know nothing of this. he know nothing of my know his inmost
heart. he be simple as a child in these matter. he tell I his
secret in a thousand way because he could not dissemble; but he
do not dream that have tell it. you know it now, and I advise you
to use it.'
Eleanor return the pressure of the other's hand with an
infinitesimal soupcon of a squeeze.
'and remember,' say the signora, 'he be not like other man.
you must not expect he to come to you with vow and oath and
pretty present, to kneel at your foot, and kiss your shoe-string.
if you want that, there be plenty to do it; but he willnot be one of
they.' Eleanor's bosom nearly burst with a sigh; but Madeline, not
heed she, go on. 'with he, yea will stand for yea, and nay
for nay. though his heart should break for it, the woman who shall
reject he once, will have reject he once and for all. remember
that. and now, Mrs Bold, I will not keep you, for you be
flattered. I partly guess what use you will make of what I have
say to you. if ever you be a happy wife in that man's house, we
shall be far away; but I shall expect you to write I one line to
say that you have forgive the sin of the family.'
Eleanor half whisper that she would, and then without utter
another word, creep out of the room, and down the stair, open
the front door for herself without hear or see any one, and
find herself in the close.
it would be difficult to analyse Eleanor's feeling as she walk
home. she be nearly stupefy by the thing that have be say to
she. she feel sore that her heart should have be so search and
riddle by a comparative stranger, by a woman whom she have never
like and never could like. she be mortify that the man whom she
own to herself that she love should have conceal his love from
she and show it to another. there be much to vex her proud
spirit. but there be, nevertheless, an under-stratum of joy in all
this which buoy she up wondrously. she try if she could
disbelieve what Madame Neroni have say to she; but she find that
she could not. it be true; it must be true. she could not, would
not, do not doubt it.
on one point she fully resolve to follow the advice give she. if
it should ever please Mr Arabin to put such a question to she as
suggest, her 'yea' should be 'yea'. would not all her misery be
at an end, if she could talk of they to he openly, with her hand
rest on his shoulder?
CHAPTER XLVI
MR SLOPE'S parting interview with the signora
on the following day the signora be in her pride. she be dress
in her bright of morning dress, and have quite a levee round
her couch. it be a beautifully bright October afternoon; all the
gentleman of the neighbourhood be in Barchester, and those who
have the entry of Dr Stanhope's house be in the signora's back
drawing-room. Charlotte and Mrs Stanhope be in the front room,
and such of the lady's squire as could not for the moment get near
the centre of attraction have to waste their fragrance on the mother
and sister.
the first who come and the last to leave be Mr Arabin. this be
the second visit he have pay to Madame Neroni since he have meet she
at Ullathorne. he come he know not why, to talk about he know not
what. but, in truth, the feeling which now trouble he be new
to he, and he could not analyse they. it may seem strange that he
should thus come dangle about Madame Neroni because he be in
love with Mrs Bold; but it be nevertheless the fact; and though he
could not understand why he do so, Madame Neroni understand it
well enough.
she have be gentle and kind to he, and have encourage his
staying. therefore he stay on. she press his hand when he first
greet she; and whisper to he little nothing. and then she
eye, brilliant and bright, now mirthful, now melancholy, and
invincible in either way! what man with warm feeling, blood
unchilled, and a heat not guard by a triple steel of experience
could have withstand those eye! the lady, it be true, intend to
do no mortal injury; she merely choose to inhale a slight breath of
incense before she hand the casket over to another. whether Mrs
Bold would willingly have spar even so much be another question.
and then come Mr Slope. all the world now know that Mr Slope be a
candidate for the deanery, and that he be generally consider to
be the favourite. Mr Slope, therefore, walk rather largely upon
the earth. he give to himself a portly air, such as might become a
dean, speak but little to other clergyman, and shun the bishop
as much as possible. how the meagre little prebendary, and the
burly chancellor, and all the minor canon and vicar choral, ay,
and all the chorister too, cower and shake and walk about with
long face when they read or hear of that article of the Jupiter.
now be come the day when nothing would avail to keep the
impure spirit from the cathedral pulpit. that pulpit would indeed
be his own. precentor, vicar, and chorister might hang up their
harp on the willow. ichabod! ichabod! the glory of their house
be depart from they.
Mr Slope, great as he be with embryo grandeur, still come to see
the signora. indeed, he could not keep himself away. he dream of
that soft hand which have kiss so often, and of the imperial brow
which his lip have once press, and he then dream also of
far favour.
and Mr Thorne be there also. it be the first visit he have ever
pay to the signora, and he make it not without due preparation. Mr
Thorne be a gentleman usually precise in his dress, and prone to
make the most of himself in an unpretending way. the grey hair in
his whisker be eliminate perhaps once a month; those on his
head be soften by a mixture which we will not call a dye; it
be only a wash. his tailor live in St James's Street, and his
bootmaker at the corner of that street and Piccadilly. he be
particular in the article of glove, and the getting up of his
shirt be a matter not lightly think of in the Ullathorne
laundry. on the occasion of the present visit he have rather
overdone his usual effort, and cause some little uneasiness to
his sister, who have not hitherto receive very cordially the
proposition for a lengthened visit from the signora at Ullathorne.
there be other also there--young man about the city who have not
much to do, and who be induce by the lady's charm to neglect
that little; but all give way to Mr Thorne, who be somewhat of a
grand signor, as a country gentleman always be in a provincial
city.
'oh, Mr Thorne, this be so kind of you!' say the signora. 'you
promise to come; but I really do not expect it. I think you
country gentleman never keep your pledge.'
'oh, yea, sometimes,' say Mr Thorne, look rather sheepish, and
make salutation a little too much in the style of the last
century.
'you deceive none but your consti-stit-stit; what do you call the
people that carry you about in chair and pelt you with egg and
apple when they make you a member of parliament?'
'one another also, sometimes, signora,' say Mr Slope, with a
deanish sort of smirk on his face. 'country gentleman do deceive
one another sometimes, donot they, Mr Thorne?'
Mr Thorne give he a look which undress he completely for the
moment; but he soon remember his high hope, and recover
himself quickly, sustain his probable come dignity by a laugh
at Mr Thorne's expense.
'I never deceive a lady, at any rate,' say Mr Thorne; 'especially
when the gratification of my own wish be so strong an inducement
to keep I true, as it now be.'
Mr Thorne go on thus awhile, with antediluvian grimace and
compliment which he have pick up from Sir Charles Grandison, and
the signora at every grimace and at every bow smile a little smile
and bow a little bow. Mr Thorne, however, be keep stand at
the foot of the couch, for the new dean sit in the seat of honour
near the table. Mr Arabin the while be stand with his back to
the fire, his coat tail under his arm, gaze at she with all his
eye--not quite in vain, for every now and again a glance come up
at he, bright as a meteor out of heaven.
'oh, Mr Thorne, you promise to let I introduce my little girl to
you. can you spare a moment?--will you see she now?'
Mr Thorne assure she that he could, and would see the young lady
with the great pleasure in life. 'Mr Slope, might I trouble you
to ring the bell?' say she; and when Mr Slope get up she look at
Mr Thorne and point to the chair. Mr Thorne, however, be much
too slow to understand she, and Mr Slope would have recover his
seat have not the signora, who never choose to be unsuccessful,
somewhat summarily order he out of it.
'oh, Mr Slope, I must ask you to let Mr Thorne sit here just for a
moment or two. I be sure you will pardon I. we can take a liberty
with you this week. next week, you know, when you move into the
dean's house, we shall all be afraid of you.'
Mr Slope, with an air of much indifference, rise from his seat,
and, walk into the next room, become greatly interested in Mrs
Stanhope's worst work.
and then the child be bring in. she be a little girl, about
eight year of age, like her mother, only that her enormous eye
be black, and her hair quite jet. her complexion too be very
dark, and bespoke her foreign blood. she be dress in the most
outlandish and extravagant way in which clothe could be put on a
childbe back. she have great bracelet on her naked little arm, a
crimson fillet braid with gold round her head, and scarlet shoe
with high heel. her dress be all flounce, and stick out from
she as though the object be to make it lie off horizontally from
her little hip. it do not nearly cover her knee; but this be
atone for by a loose pair of drawer which seem make throughout
of lace; then she have on pink silk stocking. it be thus that the
last of the Neros be habitually dress at the hour when visitor
be willnot to call.
'Julia, my love,' say the mother,--Julia be ever a favourite name
with the lady of the family, 'Julia, my love, come here. I be
tell you about the beautiful party poor mamma go to. this be
Mr Thorne; will you give he a kiss, dearest?'
Julia put up her face to be kiss, as she do to all her mother's
visitor; and then Mr Thorne find that he have get she, and, which
be much more terrible to he, all her finery, into his arm. the
lace and starch crumple against his waistcoat and trouser, the
greasy black curl hang upon his cheek, and one of the bracelet
clasp scratch his ear. he do not at all know how to hold she.
however, he have on other occasion be compel to fondle little
niece and nephew, and now set about the task in the mode he always
use.
'Diddle, diddle, diddle, diddle,' say he, put the child on one
knee, and work away with it as though he be turn a
knife-grinder's wheel with his foot.
'Mamma, mamma,' say Julia, crossly. 'I donot want to be diddle
diddle. let I go, you naughty old man, you.'
Poor Mr Thorne put the child down quietly on the ground, and draw
back his chair; Mr Slope, who have return to the pole star that
attract he, laugh aloud; Mr Arabin wince and shut his eye;
and the signora pretend not to hear her daughter.
'go to Aunt Charlotte, lovey,' say the mamma, 'and ask she it if
be not time for you to go out.'
but little Julia, though she have not exactly like the nature of Mr
Thorne's attention, be accustom to be play with by gentleman,
and do not relish the idea of be send so soon to her aunt.
'Julia, go when I tell you, my dear.' but Julia still go pout
about the room. 'Charlotte, do come and take she,' say the
signora. 'she must go out; and the day get so short now.' and thus
end the much-talk of interview between Mr Thorne and the last
of the Neros.
Mr Thorne recover from the child's crossness soon than from Mr
Slope's laughter. he could put up with be call an old man by
an infant, but he do not like to be laugh at by the bishop's
chaplain, even though that chaplain be about to become a dean. he
say nothing, but he show plainly enough that he be angry.
the signora be ready enough to avenge he. 'Mr Slope,' say she,
'I hear that you be triumph on all side.'
'how so,' say he smile. he do not dislike be talk to about
the deanery, though, of course, he strongly deny the imputation.
'you carry the day both in love and war.' Mr Slope hereupon do not
look quite so satisfied as he have do.
'Mr Arabin,' continue the signora, 'donot you think Mr Slope be a
very lucky man?'
'not more than he deserve, I be sure,' say Mr Arabin.
'only think, Mr Thorne, he be to be our new dean; of course we all
know that.'
'indeed, signora,' say Mr Slope, 'we all know nothing about it. I
can assure you I myself--'
'he be to be the new dean--there be no manner of doubt of it, Mr
Thorne.'
'Hum,' say Mr Thorne.
'pass over the head of old man like my father and Archdeacon
Grantly--'
'oh--oh!' say Mr Slope.
'the archdeacon would not accept it,' say Mr Arabin; whereupon Mr
Slope smile abominably, and say, as plainly as a look could
speak, that the grape be sour.
'go over all our head,' continue the signora; 'for, of course,
I consider myself one of the chapter.'
'if I be ever dean,' say Mr slope--'that be, be I ever to become
so, I should glory in such a canoness.'
'oh, Mr Slope, stop; I havenot half do. there be another canoness
for you to glory in. Mr Slope be not only to have the deanery, but
a wife to put in it.'
Mr Slope again look disconcert.
'a wife with a large fortune, too. it never rain but it pour,
do it Mr Thorne?'
'no, never,' say Mr Thorne, who do not quite relish talk about
Mr Slope and his affair.
'when will it be, Mr Slope?'
'when will what be?' say he.
'oh! we know when the affair of the dean will be: a week will
settle that. the new hat, I have no doubt, have already be
order. but when will the marriage come off?'
'do you mean mine or Mr Arabin's,' say he, strive to be
facetious.
'well, just then I mean your, though perhaps, after all, Mr
Arabin's may be first. but we know nothing of he. he be too close
for any of we. now all be open and above board with you; which, by
the bye, Mr Arabin, I beg to tell you I like much the good. he who
run can read that Mr Slope be a favoured lover. come, Mr Slope,
when be the widow to be make Mrs Dean?'
to Mr Arabin this badinage be peculiarly painful; and yet he could
not tear himself away and leave it. he believe, still believe
with that sort of belief which the fear of a thing engender, that
Mrs Bold would probably become the wife of Mr Slope. of Mr Slope's
little adventure in the garden he know nothing. for aught he know,
Mr Slope might have have an adventure of quite a different
character. he might have throw himself at the widow's foot, be
accept, and then return to town a jolly, thrive wooer. the
signora's joke be bitter enough to Mr Slope, but they be quite
as bitter to Mr Arabin. he still stand lean against the
fire-place, fumble with his hand in his trouser's pocket.
'come, come, Mr Slope, donot be so bashful,' continue the signora.
'we all know that you propose to the lady the other day at
Ullathorne. tell we with what word she accept you. be it with a
simple "yes", or with two "no, no's", which make an affirmative?
or do silence give consent: or do she speak out with that spirit
which so well become a widow, and say openly, "by my troth, sir,
you shall make I Mrs Slope as soon as it be your pleasure to do
so"?'
Mr Slope have seldom in his life feel himself less at his case.
there sit Mr Thorne, laugh silently. there stand his old
antagonist, Mr Arabin, gaze at he with all his eye. there round
the door between the two room be cluster a little group of
people, include Miss Stanhope and the Rev. Messrs. Gray and
Green, all listen to his discomfiture. he know that it depend
solely on his own wit whether or no he could throw the joke back
upon the lady. he know that it stand he to do so if he possibly
could; but he say not a word. ''tis conscience that make coward
of we all.' he feel on his cheek the sharp point of Eleanor's
finger, and do not know who might have see the blow, who might
have tell the tale to this pestilent woman who take such delight in
jeer he. he stand there, therefore, red as a carbuncle and mute
as a fish; grin just sufficiently to show his tooth; an object
of pity.
but the signora have no pity; she know nothing of mercy. her present
object be to put Mr Slope down, and she be determined to do it
thoroughly, now that she have he in her power.
'what, Mr Slope, no answer? why it cannot possibly be that this
woman have be fool enough to refuse you? she surely cannot be
look out after a bishop. but I see how it be, Mr Slope. widow
be proverbially cautious. you should have let she alone till the
new hat be on your head; till you could show she the key of the
deanery.'
'Signora,' say he at last, try to speak in a tone of dignified
reproach, 'you really permit yourself to talk on such solemn
subject in a very improper way.'
'solemn subject--what solemn subject? surely a dean's hat be not
such a solemn subject.'
'I have no aspiration such as those you impute to I. perhaps you
will drop the subject.'
'oh, certainly, Mr Slope; but one word first. go to she again with
the prime minister's letter in your pocket. Iwill wager my shawl to
your shovel she do not refuse you then.'
'I must say, signora, that I think you be speak of the lady in
a very unjustifiable manner.'
'and one other piece of advice, Mr Slope; Iwill only offer you one
other;' and then she commence singing--
'itbe gude to be merry and wise, Mr Slope, itbe gude to be honest
and true; itbe gude to be off with the old love, Mr Slope, before
you be on with the new--
'ha, ha, ha!'
and the signora, throw herself back on her sofa, laugh
merrily. she little recke how those who hear she would, in their
own imagination, fill up the little history of Mr Slope's first
love. she little care that some among they might attribute to she
the honour of his early admiration. she be tired of Mr Slope and
want to get rid of he; she have grind for anger with he, and
she choose to be revenge.
how Mr Slope get out of that room he never himself know. he do
succeed ultimately, and probably with some assistance, in get
he his have and escape into the air. at last his love for the
signora be cure. whenever he again think of she in his dream,
it be not as of an angel with azure wing. he connect she rather
with fire and brimstone, and though he could still believe she to
be a spirit, he banish she entirely out of heaven, and find a
place for she among the infernal god. when he weigh in the
balance, as he not seldom do, the two woman to whom he have
attach himself in Barchester, the pre-eminent place in his soul's
hatred be usually allot to the signora.
CHAPTER XLVII
the DEAN ELECT
during the entire next week Barchester be ignorant who be to be
its new dean on Sunday morning. Mr Slope be decidedly the
favourite; but he do not show himself in the cathedral, and then
he sink a point or two in the betting. on Monday, he get a scolding
from the bishop in the hearing of the servant, and down he go
till nobody would have he at any price; but on Tuesday he receive
a letter, in an official cover, mark private, by which he fully
recover his place in the public favour. on Wednesday, he be say
to be ill, and that do not look well; but on Thursday morning he
go down to the railway station, with a very jaunty air; and when
it be ascertain that he have take a first-class ticket for
London, there be no long any room for doubt on the matter.
while matter be in this state of ferment at Barchester, there
be not much mental comfort at Plumstead. our friend the archdeacon
have many ground for inward grief. he be much displeased at the
result of Dr Gwynne's diplomatic mission to the palace, and do not
even scruple to say to his wife that have he go himself he would
have manage the affair much well. his wife do not agree with
he, but that do not mend the matter.
Mr Quiverful's appointment to the hospital be, however, a fait
accompli, and Mr Harding's acquiescence in that appointment be not
less so. nothing would induce Mr Harding to make a public appeal
against the bishop; and the Master of Lazarus quite approve of his
not do so.
'I donot know what have come to the Master,' say the archdeacon
over and over again. 'he use to be ready enough to stand up for
his order.'
'my dear archdeacon,' Mrs Grantly would say in reply, 'what be the
use of always fight? I really think the Master be right.' the
Master, however, have take step of his own, of which neither the
archdeacon nor his wife know anything.
'then Mr Slope's success be henbane to Dr Grantly; and Mrs
Bold's impropriety be as bad. what would be all the world to
Archdeacon Grantly if Mr Slope should become the Dean of Barchester
and marry his wife's sister! he talk of it, and talk of it till
he be nearly ill. Mrs Grantly almost wish that the marriage be
do and over, so that she might hear no more about it.
and there be yet another ground of misery which cut he to the
quick, nearly as closely as either of the two other. that paragon
of a clergyman, whom he have bestow upon St Ewold's, that college
friend of whom he have boast so loudly, that ecclesiastical knight
before whose lance Mr Slope be to fall and bite the dust, that
worthy bulwark of the church as it should be, that honour
representative of Oxford's good spirit, be--so at least his wife
have tell he half a dozen time--misconduct himself!
nothing have be see of Mr Arabin at Plumstead for the last week,
but a good deal have, unfortunately, be hear of he.
as soon as Mrs Grantly have find herself alone with the
archdeacon, on the evening of the Ullathorne party, she have
express herself very forcibly as to Mr Arabin's conduct on that
occasion. he have, she declare, look and act and talk very
unlike a decent parish clergyman. at first the archdeacon have
laugh at this, and assure she that she need not trouble herself;
that Mr Arabin would be find to be quite safe. but by degree he
begin to find out that his wife's eye have be sharp than his
own. other people couple the signora's name with that of Mr
Arabin. the meagre little prebendary who live in the close, tell
he to a nicety how often Mr Arabin have visit at Dr Stanhope's,
and how long he have remain on the occasion of each visit. he have
ask after Mr Arabin at the cathedral library, and an officious
little vicar choral have offer to go and see whether he could be
find at Dr Stanhope's. Rumour, when she have contrive to sound the
first note on her trumpet, soon make a loud peal audible enough.
it be too clear that Mr Arabin have succumb to the italian woman,
and that the archdeacon's credit would suffer fearfully if
something be not do to rescue the brand from the burning.
besides, to give the archdeacon his due, he be really attach to
Mr Arabin, and grieve greatly at his backsliding.
they be sit talk over their sorrow, in the drawing-room
before dinner on that day after Mr Slope's departure for London;
and on this occasion Mrs Grantly speak her mind freely. she have
opinion of her own about parish clergyman, and now think it
right to give vent to they.
'it you would have be lead by I, archdeacon, you would never have
put a bachelor into St Ewold's.'
'but, my dear, you donot mean to say that all bachelor clergyman
misbehave themselves.'
'I donot know that clergyman be so much well than other man,'
say Mrs Grantly. 'itbe all very well with a curate whom you have
under your own eye, and whom you can get rid of if he persist in
impropriety.'
'but Mr Arabin be a fellow, and couldnot have have a wife.'
'then I would have find some one who could.'
'but, my dear, be fellow never to get living?'
'yes, to be sure they be, when they get engage. I never would put
a young man into a living unless he be marry, or engage to be
marry. now here be Mr Arabin. the whole responsibility lie upon
you.'
'there be not at this moment a clergyman in all Oxford more
respect for moral and conduct than Arabin.'
'oh, Oxford!' say the lady, with a sneer. 'what man choose to do
at Oxford, nobody ever hear of. a man may do very well at Oxford
who would bring disgrace on a parish; and, to tell you the truth,
it seem to I that Mr Arabin be just such a man.'
the archdeacon groan deeply, but he have no further answer to
make.
'you really must speak to he, archdeacon. only think what the
Thornes will say if they hear that their parish clergyman spend
his whole time philander with this woman.'
the archdeacon groan again. he be a courageous man, and know
well enough how to rebuke the young clergyman of the diocese when
necessary. but there be that about Mr Arabin which make the doctor
feel that it would be very difficult to rebuke he with good
effect.
'you can advise he to find a wife for himself, and he will
understand well enough what that mean,' say Mrs Grantly.
the archdeacon have nothing for it but groan. there be Mr Slope;
he be go to be make dean; he be go to take a wife; he be
bout to achieve respectability and wealth; and excellent family
mansion, and a family carriage; he would soon be among the
comfortable elite of the ecclesiastical world of Barchester;
whereas his own protege, the true scion of the true church, by whom
he have swear, would still be a poor vicar, and that with a very
indifferent character for moral conduct! it might be all very well
recommend Mr Arabin to marry, but how would Mr Arabin when
married support a wife?
thing be order themselves thus at Plumstead drawing-room when
Dr and Mrs Grantly be disturb in their sweet discourse by the
quick rattle of a carriage and a pair of horse on the gravel
sweep. the sound be not that of visitor, whose private carriage
be generally bring up to country-house door with demure
propriety, but belong rather to some person or person who be
in a hurry to reach the house, and have not intention of immediately
leave it. guest invite to stay a week, and who be conscious
of arrive after the first dinner bell, would probably approach in
such a manner. so might arrive an attorney with the news of a
granduncle's death, or a son from college with all the fresh
honour of a double first. no one would have have himself drive to
the door of a country house in such a manner who have the slight
doubt of his own right to force an entry.
'who be it?' say Mrs Grantly, look at her husband.
'who on earth can it be?' say the archdeacon to his wife. he then
quietly get up and stand with the drawing-room door open in his
hand. 'why, it be your father!'
it be indeed Mr Harding, and Mr Harding alone. he have come by
himself in a post-chaise with a couple of horse from Barchester,
arrive almost after dark, and evidently full of news. his visit
have usually be make in the quiet manner; he have rarely
presume to come without notice, and have always be drive up in a
modest old green fly, with one horse, that hardly make itself hear
as it crawl up to the hall door.
'good gracious, Warden, be it you?' say the archdeacon, forget
in his surprise the event of the last few year. 'but come in;
nothing be the matter, I hope?'
'we be very glad you be come, papa,' say his daughter. 'Iwill go
and get your room ready at once.'
'I an't warden, archdeacon,' say Mr Harding. 'Mr Quiverful be
warden.'
oh, I know, I know,' say the archdeacon, petulantly. 'I forget all
about it at the moment. be anything the matter?'
'donot go at the moment, Susan,' say Mr Harding; 'I have something
to tell you.'
'the dinner bell will ring in five minute,' say she.
'will it?' say Mr Harding. 'then, perhaps I have well wait.' he
be big with news which he have come to tell, but which he know
could not be tell without much discussion. he have hurry away to
plumstead as fast as two horse could bring he, and now, find
himself there, he be willing to accept the reprieve which dinner
would give he.
'if you have anything of moment to tell we, say the archdeacon,
'pray let we hear it at once. have Eleanor go off?'
'no, she have not,' say Mr Harding, with a look of great
disclosure.
'has Slope be make dean?'
'no, he have not; but--'
'but what?' say the archdeacon, who be become very impatient.
'they have--'
'they have what?' say the archdeacon.
'they have offer it to I,' say Mr Harding, with a modesty which
almost prevent his speaking.
'Good heavens!' say the archdeacon, and sink back exhausted in an
easy-chair.
'my dear, dear, father,' say Mrs Grantly, and throw her arm
around his neck.
'so I think I have well come out and consult with you at once,'

'consult!' shout the archdeacon. 'but, my dear Harding, I

shake they as though he be go to shake they off, and walk

'But--' begin Mr Harding.
'but I no but,' say the archdeacon. 'I never be so happy in my
life. it be just the proper thing to do. upon my honour, Iwill
never say another word against Lord--the long day I have to
live.'
'thatbe Dr Gwynne's doing, you may be sure,' say Mrs Grantly, who
greatly like the master of Lazarus, he be an orderly married
man with a large family.
'I suppose it be,' say the archdeacon.
'oh, papa, I be so truly delighted,' say Mrs Grantly, get up
and kiss her father.
'but, my dear,' say Mr Harding. it be all in vain that he strove
to speak; nobody would listen to he.
'well, Mr Dean,' say the archdeacon, triumphing; 'the deanery

Quiverful! I willnot begrudge he his good fortune any long.'
no, indeed,' say Mrs Grantly. 'poor woman, she have fourteen
child. I be sure I be very glad they have get it.'
'so be I,' say Mr Harding.
'I would give twenty pound,' say the archdeacon, 'to see how Mr

at last Mr Harding be allow to go up-stair and wash his hand,

matter for discussion in the low region how Mr Harding, instead

various be the opinion on the matter. the cook and butler, who
be advance in year, think that it be just as it should be;
but the footman and lady's maid, who be young, think it be a
great shame that Mr Slope should lose his chance.
'hebe a mean chap all the same,' say the footman; 'and it an't
along of he that I say so. but I always do admire the missus's
sister; and shewould well become the situation.'
while these be the idea down-stair, a very great difference of

'I should think so,' say the archdeacon.

the decanter almost fall from the archdeacon's have upon the table;

and preferment amount to less than 200 pound a year, should

position which his profession have to afford!
'what!' say the archdeacon, gasp for breath, and star at his

'what!'
'I do not find myself fit for new duty,' urge Mr Harding.
'new duty! what duty?' say the archdeacon, with unintended

'oh, papa,' say Mrs Grantly, 'nothing can be easy that what a
dean have to do. surely you be more active than Dr Trefoil.'
'he willnot have half as much to do as at present,' say Dr Grantly.
'do you see what the Jupiter say the other day about young man?'
'yes; and I see that the Jupiter say all that it could to induce

make dean.'
Mr Harding make no reply to this rebuke, though he feel it

pass by.
'I know I cannot make you understand my feeling,' he say, 'for we
have be cast in different mould. I may wish that I have your
spirit and energy and power of combat; but I have not. every
day that be add to my life increase my wish for peace and rest.'
'and where on earth can a man have peace and rest if not in a
deanery?' say the archdeacon.
'People will say I be too old for it.'
'good heaven! what people? what need you care for any people?'
'but I think myself I be too old for any new place.'
'dear papa,' say Mrs Grantly, 'man ten year old than you have
be appoint to new situation day after day.'
'my dear,' say he, 'it be impossible that I should make you
understand my feeling, nor do I pretend to any great virtue in the

enable I to stand against the spirit of the time. the call on all
side now be for young man, and I have not the nerve to put myself
in opposition to the demand. be the Jupiter, when it hear of my
appointment, to write article after article, set forth my
incompetency, I be sure it would cost I my reason. I ought to be
able to bear with such thing, you will say. well, my dear, I own
that I ought. but I feel my weakness and I know that I cannot. and,
to tell you the truth, I know no more than a child what the dean
have to do.'
'Pshaw!' exclaim the archdeacon.
'donot be angry with I, archdeacon; donot let we quarrel about it,

disoblige you in this matter, you would not be angry with I.'
this be a dreadful blow to Dr Grantly. nothing could possibly have

have never look down on Mr Harding on account of his great
poverty, he do fully recognise the satisfaction of have those
belong to he in comfortable position. it would be much more
suitable that Mr Harding should be dean of Barchester than vicar of
St Cuthbert's and precentor to boot. and then the great
discomfiture of that arch enemy of all that be respectable in
Barchester, of that new low church clerical parvenu that have fall
amongst they, that alone would be worth more, almost than the
situation itself. it be frightful to think that such unhoped for
good fortune should be mar by the absurd crotchet and
unwholesome hallucination by which Mr Harding allow himself to
be lead astray. to have the cup so near his lip and then to lose
the drinking of it, be more than Dr Grantly could endure.
and yet it appear as though he would have to endure it. in vain he
threaten and in vain he coax. Mr Harding do not indeed speak
with perfect decision of refuse the proffered glory, but he would
not speak with anything like decision of accept it. when press
again and again, he would again and again allege that he be wholly
unfit to new duty. it be in vain that the archdeacon try to

new duty to perform. it be in vain he hint that in all case
of difficulty he, the archdeacon, be willing and able to guide a
weak-minded dean. Mr Harding seem to have a foolish idea, not
only that there be new duty to do, and that no one should
accept the place who be not himself prepared to do they.
the conference end in an understanding that Mr Harding should at
once acknowledge the letter he have receive from the minister's
private secretary, and should beg that he might be allow two day
to make up his mind; and that during those two day the matter
should be consider.
on the following morning the archdeacon be to drive Mr Harding
back to Barchester.
CHAPTER XLVIII
MISS thorne shows she talent for match-making
on Mr Harding's return to Barchester from Plumstead, which be
effect by he in due course in company with the archdeacon, some
tiding of a surprising nature meet he. he be, during the journey,
subject to such a weight of unanswerable argument, all of which
go to prove that it be his bounden duty not to interfere with
the paternal government that be so anxious to make he a dean,
that when he arrive at the chemist's door in High Street, he
barely know which way to turn himself in the matter. but, perplex
as he be, he be doom to further perplexity. he find a note
there from his daughter, beg he to most urgently to come to
she immediately. but we must again go back a little in our story.
Miss Thorne have not be slow to hear the rumour respect Mr
Arabin, which have so much disturb the happiness of Mrs Grantly.
and she, also, be unhappy to think that her parish clergyman
should be accuse of worship a strange goddess. she, also, be
of opinion, that rector and vicar should all be marry, and with
that good-natured energy which be characteristic of she, she put
her wit to work to find a fitting match for Mr Arabin. Mrs
Grantly, in this difficulty, could think of no well remedy than a
lecture from the archdeacon. Miss Thorne think that a young lady,
marriageable, and with a dowry, might be of more efficacy. in
look through the catalogue of her unmarried friend, who might
possibly be in want of a husband, and might also be fit for such a
promotion as a country parsonage afford, she could think of no one
more eligible than Mrs Bold; and, consequently, lose no time, she
go into Barchester on the day of Mr Slope's discomfiture, the
same day that her brother, have have his interesting interview with
the last of the Neros, and invite Mrs Bold to bring her nurse and
baby to Ullathorne and make a protract visit.
Miss Thorne suggest a month or two, intend to use she
influence afterwards in prolong it so as to last out the winter,
in order that Mr Arabin might have an opportunity of become
fairly intimate with his intended bride. 'wewill have Mr Arabin
too,' say Miss Thorne to herself; 'and before the spring theywill
know each other; and in twelve or eighteen month' time, if all
go well, Mrs Bold will be domicile at St Ewold's'; and then the
kind-hearted lady give herself some not undeserved praise for her
matching genius.
Eleanor be take a little by surprise, but the matter end in she
promise to go to Ullathorne, for at any rate a week or two; and
on the day previous to that on which her father drive out to

Miss Thorne would not perplex she with her embryo lord on that same
evening, think that she would allow she a few hour to make
herself at home; but on the follow morning Mr Arabin arrive.
'and now,' say Miss Thorne to herself,' I must contrive to throw
they in each other's way.' that same day, after dinner, Eleanor,
with an assumed air of dignity which she could no maintain, with

not conquer, and a joy which she could not hide, tell Miss Thorne
that she be engage to marry Mr Arabin, and that it behove she to
get back home to Barchester as quick as she could.
to say simply that Miss Thorne be rejoice at the success of the
scheme, would give a very faint idea of her feeling on the
occasion. my reader may probably have dream before now that they
have have before they some terrible long walk to accomplish, some
journey of twenty or thirty mile, an amount of labour frightful to
anticipate, and that immediately on start they have ingeniously
find some accommodate short cut which have bring they without
fatigue to their work's end in five minute. Miss Thorne's wake
feeling be somewhat of the same nature. my reader may perhaps
have have to do with child, and may on some occasion have
promise to their young charge some great gratification intend
to come off, perhaps at the end of the winter, or at the beginning
of summer. the impatient juvenile, however, will not wait, and
clamorously demand their treat before they go to bed. Miss Thorne
have a sort of feeling that an inexperienced gunner, who have ill
calculate the length of the train that he have lay. the gunpowder
explode much too soon and poor Miss Thorne feel that she be blow
up by the strength of her own petard.
Miss Thorne have have lover of her own, but they have be gentleman
of old-fashioned and deliberate habit. Miss Thorne's heart also
have not always be hard, though she be still a virgin spinster;
but it have never yield in this way at the first assault. she have
intend to bring together a middle-aged studious clergyman, and a
discreet matron who might possibly be induce to marry again; and
in do she have throw fire among tinder. well, it be all as it
should be, but she do feel perhaps a little put out by the
precipitancy of her own success; and perhaps a little vex at the
readiness of Mrs Bold to be woo.
she say, however, nothing about it to any one, and ascribe it all
to the altered manner of the new age. their mother and
grandmother be perhaps a little more deliberate; but, it be
admit on all side that thing be conduct very differently
now that in former time. for aught Miss Thorne know of the matter,
a couple of hour might be quite sufficient under the new regime to
complete that for which she in her ignorance have allot twelve
month.
but we must not pass over the wooing so cavalierly. it have be
tell, with perhaps tedious accuracy, how Eleanor dispose of two of
her lover at Ullathorne; and it must also be tell with equal
accuracy, and if possible with less tedium, how she encounter Mr
Arabin.
it cannot be deny that when Eleanor accept Miss Thorne's
invitation, she remember that Ullathorne be in the parish of St
Ewold's. since her interview with the signora she have do little
else than think about Mr Arabin, and the appeal that have be make

of it how she would, she could not but accept it as a fact that Mr
Arabin be fond of she; and then when she go far, and ask

that she be fond of he. if it be destine for she to be the
partner of his hope and sorrow, to whom she could she look for
friendship so properly as to Miss Thorne? this invitation be like
an ordain step towards the fulfilment of her destiny, and when
she also hear that Mr Arabin be expect to be at Ullathorne on

in her favour. well, do she not deserve it? in that affair of Mr
Slope, have not all the world conspire against she?
she could not, however, make herself easy and at home. when in the

Arabin's quality, she hint that any little rumour which might
be ill-naturedly spread abroad concern he really mean nothing,

little far and declare that she do not know a pretty
vicarage-house in the country than St Ewold's, Mrs Bold remembering
the project bow-window and the project priestess still hold she
tongue; though her ear tingle with the conviction that all the
world would know that she be in love with Mr Arabin. well; what
could that matter if they could only meet and tell each other what
each now long to tell?
and they do meet. Mr Arabin come early in the day, and find the
two lady together at work in the drawing-room. Miss Thorne, who
have she know all the truth would have vanish into air at once,
have no conception that her immediate absence would be a blessing,
and remain chat with they till luncheon-time. Mr Arabin could
talk about nothing but the Signora Neroni's beauty, would discuss
no people but the Stanhopes. this be very distressing to Eleanor,
and not very satisfactory to Miss Thorne. but yet there be
evidence of innocence in his open avowal of admiration.
and then they have lunch, and then Mr Arabin go out on parish
duty; and Eleanor and Miss Thorne be leave to take a walk

'do you think the Signora Neroni be so lovely as people say?'
Eleanor ask as they be come home.
'she be very beautiful certainly, very beautiful,' Miss Thorne
answer; 'but I do not know that any one consider her lovely. she
be a woman all man would like to look at; but few I imagine would
be glad to take she to their hearth, even be she unmarried and
not afflict as she be.'
there be some comfort in this. Eleanor make the most of it till
she get back to the house. she be then leave alone in the
drawing-room, and just as it be get dark Mr Arabin come in.
it be a beautiful afternoon in the beginning of October, and
Eleanor be sit in the window to get the advantage of the last
daylight for her novel. there be a fire in the comfortable room,
but the weather be not cold enough to make it attractive; and as
she could see the sun set from where she sit, she be not very
attentive to her book.
Mr Arabin when he enter stand awhile with his back to the fire in
his usual way, merely utter a few commonplace remark about the
beauty of the weather, while he pluck up courage for the more
interesting converse. it cannot probably be say that he have
resolve then and there to make an offer to Eleanor. man we believe
seldom make such resolve. Mr Slope and Mr Stanhope have do so, it
be true; but gentleman generally propose without any absolutely
define determination as to their do so. such be now the case
with Mr Arabin.
'it be a lovely sunset,' say Eleanor, answer he on the
dreadfully trite subject which he have choose.
Mr Arabin could not see the sunset from the hearth-rug, as he have
to go close to she.
'very lovely,' say he, stand modestly so far away from her s to
avoid touch the flounce of her dress. then it appear that he
have nothing furth to say; so after gaze for a moment in silence
at the brightness of the set sun, he return to the fire.
Eleanor find that it be quite impossible for herself to commence
a conversation. in the first place she could find nothing to say;
word, which be generally plenty enough with she, would not come

prevent herself from cry.
'do you like Ullathorne?' say Mr Arabin, speak from the safely
distant position which he have assume on the hearth-rug.
'yes, indeed, very much!'
'I donot mean Mr and Miss Thorne. I know you like they; but the
style of the house. there be something about old-fashioned
mansion, build as this be, and old-fashioned garden, that to I
be especially delightful.'
'I like everything old-fashioned,' say Eleanor; 'old-fashioned
thing be so much the honest.'
'I donot know about that,' say Mr Arabin, gently laugh. 'that
be an opinion on which very much may be say on either side. it be
strange how widely the world be divide on a subject which so
nearly concern we all, and which be so close beneath our eye.
some think that we be quickly progress towards perfection,
while other imagine that virtue be disappear from the earth.'
'and you, Mr Arabin, what do you think?' say Eleanor. she feel
somewhat surprised at the tone which this conversation be take,
and yet she be quite relieved at his say something which
enable herself to speak without show any emotion.
'what do I think, Mrs Bold?' and then he rumble his money with his
hand in his trouser pocket, and look and speak very little like
a thriving lover. 'it be the bane of my life that on important
subject I acquire no fix opinion. I think, and think, and go on
thinking; and yet my thought be run over in different
direction. I hardly know whether or no we do lead more confidently
than our father do on those high hope to which we profess to
aspire.'
'I think the world grow more worldly every day,' say Eleanor.
'that be because you see more of it than when you be young. but
we should hardly judge by what we see,--we see so very very

continue to turn over his shilling and half-crown. 'if we
believe in scripture, we can hardly think that mankind in general

Eleanor, whose mind be certainly engage otherwise than on the

but he do not look at she. instead of do so, he leave the

which may exist between myself and your connection, Dr Grantly,

be this to her doom after all? 'I therefore think it right to beg
your pardon in a humble spirit, and I now do so.'
what be Eleanor to say to this? she could not say much, because

she be now past speak calmly, though she still continue to

against all charity; so, rise from her seat, she gently touch

do forgive you. you know that I forgive you.'
he take the hand that have so gently touch his arm, and then gaze

be a sober and seriousness in his own countenance, which Eleanor

it be but for a minute that they stand so, but the duration of

Eleanor be sure now that she be love. no word, be their
