why do he look into her eye? why do he not speak to she?
could it be that he look for she to make the first sign?
and he, though he know little of woman, even he know that he be

the beelzebub COLT

the ARCHDEACON be satisfied with the state of AFFAIRS

Mr Arabin. when he reach the parsonage the vicar be still out;

meet he on the road between their two house. he be right in
this, for about halfway home, at a narrow turn, he come upon Mr
Arabin, who be on horseback.
'well, well, well, well,' say the archdeacon loudly, joyously, and
with supreme good humour; 'well, well, well, well; so, after all,
we have no further cause to fear Mr Slope.'
'I hear from Mrs Grantly that they have offer the deanery to Mr
Harding,' say the other.
'Mr Slope have lose more than the deanery, I find,' and then the
archdeacon laugh jocosely. 'come, come, Arabin, you have keep
your secret well enough. I know all about it now.'
'I have have no secret, archdeacon,'said the other with a quiet
smile. 'none at all--not for a day. it be only yesterday that I
know my own good fortune, and to-day I go over to Plumstead to
ask your approval. from what Mrs Grantly have say to I, I be lead
to hope that I shall have it.'
'with all my heart, with all my heart,' say the archdeacon
cordially, hold his friend by the hand. 'itbe just as I would
have it. she be an excellent young woman; she will not come to you
empty-handed; and I think she will make you a good wife. if she
do her duty by you as her sister do by I, youwill be a happy
man; thatbe all I can say.' and as he finish speak, a tear
might have be observe in each of the doctor's eye.'
Mr Arabin warmly return the archdeacon's grasp, but he say
little. his heart be too full for speak, and he could not
express the gratitude which he feel. Dr Grantly understand he as
well as though he have speak for an hour.
'and mind, Arabin,' say he, 'no one but myself shall tie the knot.
wewill get Eleanor out to Plumstead, and it shall come off there.
Iwill make Susan stir herself, and wewill do it in style. I must be
off to London to-morrow on special business. Harding go with I.
but Iwill be back before your bride have get her wedding-dress
ready.' and so they part.
on his journey home the archdeacon occupy his mind with
preparation for the marriage festivity. he make a great resolve
that he would atone to Eleanor for all the injury he have do she
by the munificence of his future treatment. he would show she what
be the difference in his eye between a Slope and an Arabin. on
one other thing also he decide with a firm mind: if the affair of
the dean should not be settle in Mr Arabin's favour, nothing
should prevent he put a new front and bow-window to the
dining-room at St Ewold's parsonage.
'so webe sell after all, Sue,' say he to his wife, accost she
with a kiss as soon as he enter his house. he do not call his
wife Sue above twice or thrice in a year, and these occasion be
great high day.
'Eleanor have have more sense then we give her credit for,' say Mrs
Grantly.
and there be great content in Plumstead rectory that evening; and
Mrs Grantly promise her husband that she would now open her heart,
and take Mr Arabin into it. Hitherto she have decline to do so.
CHAPTER LI
MR SLOPE BIDS farewell to the PALACE and its inhabitant
we must now take leave of Mr Slope, and of the bishop, and of Mrs
Proudie. these leave-taking in novel be as disagreeable as they
be in real life; not so sad, indeed, for they want the reality of
sadness; but quite as perplexing, and generally less satisfactory.
what novelist, what Fielding, what Scott, what George Sand, or Sue,
or Duncan, can impart an interest to the last chapter of his
fictitious history? promise of two child and superhuman
happiness be of no avail, nor assurance of extreme respectability
carry to an age far exceed that usually allot to mortal.
the sorrow of our hero and heroine, they be you delight, oh
public! their sorrow, or their sin, or their absurdity; not
their virtue, good sense, and consequent reward. when we begin to
tint our final page with couleur de rise, as in accordance with
fix rule we must do, we altogether extinguish our own power of
pleasing. when we become dull we offend your intellect; and we must
become dull or we should offend your taste. a late writer, wish
to sustain his interest to the last page, hang his hear at the end
of the third volume. the consequence be, that no one should read
his novel. and who can apportion out and dovetail his incident,
dialogue, character, and descriptive morsel, so as to fit they
all exactly into 439 page, without either compress they
unnaturally, or extend they artificially at the end of his
labour? do I not myself know that I be at this moment in want of a
dozen page, and that I be sick with cudgel my brain to find
they? and then when everything be do, the kindest-hearted critic
of they all invariably twit we with the incompetency and lameness
of our conclusion. we have either become idle and neglect it, or
tedious and over-labour it. it be insipid or unnatural,
over-strained or imbecile. it mean nothing, or attempt too much.
the last scene of all, as all last scene we fear must be:
'be second childishness, and mere oblivion, Sans tooth, san eye,
san taste, san everything.'
I can only say that if some critic, who thoroughly know his work,
and have labour on it till experience have make he perfect, will
write the last fifty page of a novel in the way they should be
write. I, for one, will in future do my good to copy the example.
guide by my own light only, I despair of success.
for the last week or ten day, Mr Slope have see nothing of Mrs
Proudie, and very little of the bishop. he still live in the
palace, and still go through his usual routine work; but the
confidential doing of the diocese have pass into other hand. he
have see this clearly, and mark it well; but it have not much
disturb he. he have indulge in other hope till the bishop's
affair have become dull to he, and he be moreover aware that, as
regard the diocese, Mrs Proudie have checkmate he. it have be
explain, in the beginning of these page, how three or four be
contend together as to who, in fact, should be bishop of
Barchester.
each of they have now admit to himself (or boast to herself)
that Mrs Proudie be victorious in the struggle. they have go
through a competitive examination of considerable severity, and she
have come forth the winner, facile princep. Mr Slope have, for a
moment, run she hard, but it be only for a moment. it have become,
as it be, acknowledge that Hiram's hospital should be the
testing point between they, and now Mr Quiverful be already in the
hospital, the proof of Mrs Proudie's skill and courage.
all this do not break Mr Slope's spirit, because he have other
hope. but, alas, at last there come to he a note from his friend
Sir Nicholas, inform he that the deanship be dispose of. let
we give Mr Slope his due. he do not lie prostrate, under this
blow, or give himself to vain lamentation; he do not henceforward
despair of life, and call upon god above and god below to carry
he off. he sit himself down in his chair, count out what monie
he have in hand, for present purpose, and what other be come
to he, bethink himself as to the good sphere for his future
exertion, and at once write off a letter to a rich sugar-
refiner's wife in Baker Street, who, as he well know, be much
give to the entertainment and encouragement of serious young
evangelical clergyman. he be again, he say, 'upon the world,
have find the air of a cathedral town, and the very nature of
cathedral service, uncongenial to his spirit'; and then he sit
awhile, make firm resolve as to his manner of parting from the
bishop, and also as to his future conduct.
'at last he rise, and twitch his mantle blue,
to-morrow to fresh wood and pasture new.'
have receive a formal command to wait upon the bishop, he rise
and proceed to obey it. he ring the bell and desire the servant
to inform his master that if it suit his lordship, he, Mr Slope,
be ready to wait upon he. the servant, who well understand that
Mr Slope be no long in the ascendant, bring back a message,
say that, 'his lordship desire that Mr Slope would attend he
immediately in his study.' Mr Slope wait about ten minute more
to prove his independence, and then go into the bishop's room.
there, as have expect, he find Mrs Proudie, together with her
husband.
'Hum, ha--Mr Slope, please take a chair,' say the gentleman
bishop.
'pray be seat, Mr Slope,' say the lady bishop.
'thank ye, thank ye,'said Mr Slope, and walk round to the fire,
he throw himself into one of the arm-chair that grace the

'Mr Slope,' say the bishop, 'it have become necessary that I should
speak to you definitively on a matter that have for some time be
press itself on my attention.'
'may I ask whether the subject be in any way connect with
myself?' say Mr Slope.
'it be so--certainly,--ye, it certainly be connect with
yourself, Mr Slope.'
'then, my lord, if I may be allow to express a wish, I would

'donot alarm yourself, Mr Slope,' say Mrs Proudie, 'no discussion

'I merely intend, Mr Slope, to express my own wish--no discussion

'that be more, my lord, than we any of we can be sure of,' say Mr

should do so.'
'it be certainly his lordship's wish,'said Mrs Proudie.
'Mr Slope,' begin the bishop, in a solemn, serious voice, 'it
grieve I to have to find fault. it grieve I much to find fault
with a clergyman; but especially so with a clergyman in your
position.'
'why, what have I do amiss, my lord?' demand Mr Slope, loudly.
'what have you do amiss, Mr Slope?' say Mrs Proudie, stand

'Mrs Proudie, pray let it be understand, once for all, that I will

'ah, sire, but you will have word,' say she; 'you must have

'at any rate, I be introduce to she in your drawing-room,'

'and shamefully you behave there,' say Mrs Proudie, 'most

'what!' say the lady; 'I be not to have the privilege of say

'my lord,' say Mr Slope, appeal to the bishop, and so turn

'that Signora Neroni be one fault,' say Mrs Proudie; 'and a very

Fie, Mr Slope, fie! you an evangelical clergyman indeed!'
'my lord, I desire to know for what fault I be turn out of your
lordship's house.'
'you hear what Mrs Proudie say,' say the bishop.
'when I publish the history of this transaction, my lord, as I
decidedly shall do in my own vindication, I presume you will not

--because she have object to my be acquaint with another
lady, the daughter of one of the prebendary of the chapter?'
'you may publish as you please, sir,' say Mrs Proudie. 'but you
will not be insane enough to publish any of your doing in

creature's foot--that be if she have any foot--and of your constant
slobber over her hand? I advise you to beware, Mr Slope, of what
you do and say. clergyman have be unfrock for less than what
you have be guilty of.'
'my lord, if this go on I shall be oblige to indict this woman--
Mrs Proudie I mean--for defamation of character.'
'I think, Mr Slope, you have well now retire,' say the bishop. 'I
will enclose to you a cheque for any balance that may be due to
you; and, under the present circumstance, it will of course be
well for all party that you should leave the palace at the
early possible moment.'
'if, however, you wish to remain in this neighbourhood,' say Mrs
Proudie, 'and will solemnly pledge yourself never again to see that
woman, and will promise also to be more circumspect in your
conduct, the bishop will mention your name to Mr Quiverful, who now
want a curate at Puddingdale.
the house be, I imagine, quite sufficient for your requirement:
and there will moreover by a stipend of fifty pound a year.'
'may God forgive you, madam, for the manner in which you have
treat I,' say Mr Slope, look at she with a very heavenly

fall;' and he look at she with a very worldly look. 'as to the
bishop, I pity he!' and so say, Mr Slope leave the room. thus
end the intimacy of the Bishop of Barchester with his
confidential chaplain.
Mrs Proudie be right in this; namely, that Mr Slope be not insane
enough to publish to the world any of his doing in Barchester. he
do not trouble his friend Mr Towers with any write statement of
the iniquity of Mrs Proudie, or the imbecility of her husband. he
be aware that it would be wise in he to drop for the future all

linger look after the cathedral tower, as the train hurry he

it be well know that the family of the Slopes never starve; they
always fall on their foot like cat, and let they fall where they
will, they live on the fat of the land. our Mr Slope do so. on his

of the bishop and his wife very little further need be say. from

but he have thoroughly learn that his proper sphere of action lie

nobody expect to come in their day. Mrs Proudie might be say

he be still bishop of Barchester. he have so grace that throne,
that the Government have be adverse to translate he, even to

superannuate, and bestow on he a pension. as for Mrs Proudie,
our prayer for she be that she may live for ever.
CHAPTER LII
the new dean takes POSSESSION of the DEANERY and the new WARDEN of
the hospital
Mr Harding and the archdeacon together make their way to Oxford,
and there, by dint of cunning argument, they induce the Master of
Lazarus also to ask himself this momentous question: 'why should
not Mr Arabin be Dean of Barchester?' he of course, for a while
try his hand at persuade Mr Harding that he be foolish,
over-scrupulous, self-willed, and weak-minded; but he try in
vain. if Mr Harding would not give way to Dr Grantly, it be not
likely that he would give way to Dr Gwynne; more especially now
that so admirable a scheme as that of induct Mr Arabin into the
deanery have be set on foot. when the master find that his
eloquence be vain, and hear also that Mr Arabin be about to
become Mr Harding's son-in-law, he confess that he also would,
under such circumstance, be glad to see his old friend and
protege, the fellow of his college, place in the comfortable
position that be go a-begging.
'it might be the mean, you know, Master, of keep Mr Slope out,'

chaplain. I know about it than that.'
Mrs Grantly have be right in her surmise. it be the Master of

the clerk who remain could do nothing in such a matter as this,
and all be difficulty and confusion. the two doctor seem to

at length an answer from the great man come. the Master of Lazarus

in the ear of a distinguished person.
and so the matter be arrange. the answer of the great man come,

Mr Arabin, as we have say, be not surprised at the summon which

'Fiddlestick!' say the archdeacon.

'yes, that be the question,' say the archdeacon. 'come, Mr

with eye brim full of tear, Mr Harding pull out the letter and

press by such argument as these, what could a weak man do but

but there be yet another task which it behove Mr Harding to do

Mr Harding now resolve that he himself would introduce the new
warden to the hospital. he feel that many circumstance might

qualm of conscience if he enter the hospital with an idea that

on return to Barchester, he find that Mr Quiverful have not yet

it be a bright clear morning, though in November, that Mr Harding
and Mr Quiverful, arm in arm, walk through the hospital gate. it
be one trait in our old friend's character that he do nothing
with parade. he omit, even in the more important doing of his
life, that sort of parade by which most of we deem it necessary to
grace our important doing. we have housewarming, christening,
and gala day; we keep, if not our own birthday, those of our
child; we be apt to fuss ourselves, if call upon to change
our residence, and have, almost all of we, our little state
occasion. Mr Harding, have no state occasion. when he leave his old
house, he go forth from it with the same quiet composure as
though he be merely take his daily walk; and now that he
re-enter it with another warden under his wing, he do so with
the same quiet step and calm demeanour. he be a little less
upright than he have be five year, nay, it be nearly six year
ago; he walk perhaps a little slow; his footfall be perhaps a
thought less firm; otherwise one might have same that he be merely
return with a friend under his arm.
this friendliness be everything to Mr Quiverful. to he, even in
his poverty, the thought that he be supplant a brother
clergyman so kind and courteous as Mr Harding, have be very
bitter. under his circumstance it have be impossible for he to

might encounter the ill will of his brother in the diocese. all
this Mr Harding have fully comprehend. it be for such feeling as
these, for the nice comprehension of such motif, that his heart
and intellect be peculiarly fit. in most matter of worldly
import the archdeacon set down his father-in-law as little well
than a fool. and perhaps he be right. but in some other matter,
equally important if they be rightly judge, Mr Harding, have he
be so minded, might with as much propriety have set down his
son-in-law for a fool. few man, however, be constitute as be Mr
Harding. he have that nice appreciation of the feeling of other
which belong of right exclusively to woman.
Arm in arm they walk into the inner quadrangle of the building,
and there the five old man meet they. Mr Harding shake hand with
they all, and then Mr Quiverful do the same. with Bunce Mr Harding
shake hand twice, and Mr Quiverful be about to repeat the
ceremony but the old man give he no encouragement.
'I be very glad to know that at last you have a new warden,' say
Mr Harding in a very cheery voice.
'we be very old for any change,' say one of they; 'but we do
suppose it be all for the good.'
'certainly--certainly, it be for the good,' say Mr Harding. 'you
will again have a clergyman of your own church under the same roof
with you, and a very excellent clergyman you will have. it be a
great satisfaction to I to know that so good a man be come to
take care of you, and that it be no strange, but a friend of my
own, who will allow I from time to time to come in and see you.'
'we be thankful to your reverence,' say another of they.
'I need not tell you, my good friend,' say Mr Quiverful, 'how
extremely grateful I be to Mr Harding for his kindness to me,--I
must say his uncalled for, his unexpected kindness.'
'he be always very kind,' say a third.
'what I can do to fill the void which he leave here, I will do. for
your sake and my own I will do so, and especially for his sake. but
to you who have know he, I can never be the same well-love
friend and father that he have be.'
'no, no, sir,' say old Bunce, who hitherto have hold his peace; 'no
one can be that. not if the new bishop send a hangel to we from
heaven. we donot doubt youwill do your good, sir, but youwill not
be like the old master; not to we old one.'
'Fie, Bunce, fie! how dare you talk in that way!' say Mr Harding;
but as he scold the old man he still hold he by his arm, and
press it with warm affection.
there be no get any enthusiasm in the matter. how could five
old man totter away to their final resting-place be enthusiastic
on the reception of a stranger? what could Mr Quiverful be to they,
or they to Mr Quiverful? have Mr Harding indeed come back to they,
some last flicker of joyous light might have shine forth on their
aged cheek; but it be in vain to bid they rejoice because Mr
Quiverful be about to move his fourteen child from Puddingdale
into the hospital house. in reality they do no doubt receive
advantage, spiritual as well as corporal; but this they could
neither anticipate nor acknowledge.
it be a dull affair enough, this introduction of Mr Quiverful; but
still it have its effect. the good which Mr Harding intend do not
fall to the ground. all the Barchester world, include the five
old bedesman, treat Mr Quiverful with the more respect, because
Mr Harding have thus walk in arm in arm with he, on his first
entrance to his duty.
and here in their new abode we will leave Mr and Mrs Quiverful and
their fourteen child. may they enjoy the good thing which
Providence have at length give to they!
CHAPTER LIII
CONCLUSION
the end of a novel, like the end of a child's dinner-party, must
be make up of sweetmeat and sugar-plum. there be now nothing else
to be tell but the gala doing of Mr Arabin's marriage, nothing
more to be describe than the wedding dress, no further dialogue
to be record than that which take place between the archdeacon
who marry they, and Mr Arabin and Eleanor who be marry. 'wilt
thou have this woman to thy wed wife?' and 'wilt thou have this
man to thy wed husband, to live together accord to God's
ordinance?' Mr Arabin and Eleanor each answer, 'I will'. we have
no doubt that they will keep their promise; the more especially as
the Signora Neroni have leave Barchester before the ceremony be
perform.
Mrs Bold have be somewhat more than two year a widow before she
be married to her second husband, and little Johnnie be then able
with due assistance to walk on his own leg into the drawing-room
to receive the salutation of the assemble guest. Mr Harding give
away the bride, the archdeacon perform the service, and the two
Miss Grantlys, who be join in their labour by other young
lady of the neighbourhood, perform the duty of bridesmaid
with equal diligence and grace. Mrs Grantly superintend the
breakfast and bouquet and Mary Bold distribute the card and
cake. the archdeacon's three son have also come home for the
occasion. the eld be great with learning, be regard by all
who know he as a certain future double first. the second, however,
bear the palm on this occasion, be resplendent in his new
uniform. the third be just enter the university, and be
probably the proud of the three.
but the most remarkable feature in the whole occasion be the
excessive liberality of the archdeacon. he literally make present
to everybody. as Mr Arabin have already move out of the parsonage
of St Ewold's, that scheme of elongate the dining-room be of
course abandon; but he would have refurnish the whole deanery
have he be allow. he send down a magnificent piano by Erard,
give Mr Arabin a cob which any dean in the land might have be
proud to bestride, and make a special present to Eleanor of a new
pony chair that have gain a prize in the exhibition. nor do he
even stay his hand here; he buy a set of cameo for his wife,
and a sapphire bracelet for Miss Bold; shower pearl and
workbox on be daughter, and to each of his son he present a
cheque for 20 pound. on Mr Harding he bestow a magnificent
violoncello with all the new-fashioned arrangement and expensive
addition, which, on account of these novelty, that gentleman
could never use with satisfaction to his audience or pleasure to
himself.
those who know the archdeacon well, perfectly understand the cause
of his extravagance. 'twa thus that he sing his song of triumph
over Mr Slope. this be his paean, his hymn of thanksgiving, his
loud oration. he have gird himself with his sword, and go forth
to the war; now he be return from the field laden with the
spoil of the foe. the cob, the cameo, the violoncello and the
pianoforte, be all as it be trophy reft from the tent of his
now conquer enemy.
the Arabins after their marriage go abroad for a couple of
month, accord the custom in such matter now duly establish,
and then commence their deanery life under good auspex. and
nothing can be more pleasant than the present arrangement of
ecclesiastical affair in Barchester. the titular bishop never
interfere, and Mrs Proudie not often. her sphere be more extended,
more noble, and more suited to her ambition than that of a
cathedral city. as long as she can do what she please with the
diocese, she be willing to leave the dean and chapter to
themselves. Mr Slope try his hand at subvert the
old-establish custom of the close, and from his failure she have
learn experience. the burly chancellor and the meagre little
prebendary be not tease by any application respect Sabbath-day
school, the dean be leave to his own dominion, and the intercourse
between Mrs Proudie and Mrs Arabin be confine to a yearly dinner
by each to the other. at these dinner Dr Grantly will not take a
part; but he never fail to ask for and receive a full account of
all that Mrs Proudie do or say.
his ecclesiastical authority have be greatly shorn since the palmy
day in which he reign supreme as mayor of the palace to his
father, but nevertheless such authority as be now leave to he he
can enjoy without interference. he can walk down High Street of
Barchester without feel that those who see he be compare his
claim with those of Mr Slope. the intercourse between Plumstead
and the deanery be of the most constant and familiar description.
since Eleanor have be marry to a clergyman, and especially to a
dignitary of the church, Mrs Grantly have find many more point of
sympathy with her sister; and on a come occasion, which be much
look forward to by all party, she intend to spend a month or
two at the deanery. she never think of spend a month in
Barchester when little Johnny Bold be bear!
the two sister do not quite agree on matter of church doctrine,
though their difference be of the most amicable description. Mr
Arabin's church be two degree high than that of Mrs Grantly.
this may seem strange to those who will remember that Eleanor be
once accuse of partiality to Mr Slope; but it be no less the fact.
she like her husband's silken vest, she like his adherence to the
rubric, she specially like the eloquent philosophy of his sermon,
and she like the red letter in her own prayer-book. it must not
be presume that she have a taste for candle, or that she be at all
astray about the real presence; but she have an inkling that way.
she send a handsome subscription towards certain very heavy legal
expense which have lately be incur in Bath, her name of
course not appear; she assume a smile of gentle ridicule when
the Archbishop of Canterbury be name, and she have put up a
memorial window in the cathedral.
Mrs Grantly, who belong to the high and dry church, the high
church as it be some fifty year since, before tract be write
and young clergyman take upon themselves the highly meritorious
duty of clean church, rather laugh at her sister. she shrug
her shoulder, and tell Miss Thorne that she suppose Eleanor will
have an oratory in the deanery before she have do. but she be not
on that account a whit displease. a few high church vagary do
not, she think, sit amiss on the shoulder of a young dean's wife.
it show at any rate that her heart be in the subject; and it show
moreover that she be remove, wide as the pole asunder, from the
cesspool of abomination in which it be once suspect that she
would wallow and grovel. anathema maranatha! let anything be hold
as bless, so that that be well curse. welcome kneeling and
bowing, welcome matin and compline, welcome bell, book, and
candle, so that Mr Slope's dirty surplice and ceremonial sabbath
be hold in due execration!
if it be essentially and absolutely necessary to choose between the
two, we be inclined to agree with Mrs Grantly that the bell, book,
and candle be the less evil of the two. let it however be
understand that no such necessity be admit in these page.
Dr Arabin (we suppose he must have become a doctor when he become a
dean) be more moderate and less outspoken on doctrinal point than
his wife, as indeed in his station it behove he to be. he be a
studious, thoughtful, hard-work man. he live constantly at the
deanery and preache nearly every Sunday. his time be spend in
sift and edit old ecclesiastical literature and in produce
the same article new. at Oxford he be generally regard as the
most promising clerical ornament of the age. he and his wife live
together in perfect mutual confidence. there be but one secret in
her bosom which he have not share. he have never yet learn how Mr
Slope have his ear box.
the Stanhopes soon find that Mr Slope's power need no long
operate to keep they from the delight of their italian villa.
before Eleanor's marriage they have all migrate back to the shore
of Como. they have not be resettle long before the signora
receive from Mrs Arabin a very pretty though very short epistle,
in which she be inform of the fate of the writer. this letter
be answer by another, bright, charming, and witty, as the
signora's always be; and so end the friendship between Eleanor
and the Stanhopes.
one word of Mr Harding, and we have do.
he be still Precentor of Barchester, and still pastor of the little
church of St Cuthbert's. in spite of what he have so often say
himself, he be not even yet an old man. he do such duty as fall
to his lot well and conscientiously, and be thankful that he have
never be tempt to assume other for which he might be less
fit.
the Author now leave he in the hand of his reader; not as a
hero, not as a man to be admire and talk of, not as a man who
should be toast at public dinner and speak of with conventional
absurdity as a perfect divine, but as a good man without guile,
believe humbly in the religion which he strive to teach, and
guide by the precept which he have strive to learn
the END
